topics.title: Microcontroller Architecture - General Internal Architecture
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET613 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Microcontroller Architecture - General Internal Architecture

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1 — Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Microcontroller (µC / MCU):** A single-chip, self-contained, programmable digital computer system that integrates a **Central Processing Unit (CPU)**, **Read-Only Memory (ROM/Flash)** for program storage, **Random Access Memory (RAM)** for data storage, **Input/Output (I/O) ports**, **timers/counters**, **interrupt controller**, and **serial communication peripherals** onto a single integrated circuit (IC) using Very Large Scale Integration (VLSI) technology. It is purpose-built for embedded control applications.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Syllabus Distinction:** A **microprocessor** contains ONLY the CPU; external chips must be added for memory and I/O. A **microcontroller** is a *microcomputer-on-a-chip* — CPU + Memory + I/O + Peripherals integrated into one silicon die. This is the fundamental conceptual difference KTU examiners test repeatedly.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Swiss Army Knife of Electronics"

Think of a **microcontroller** as a **complete, self-contained miniature office on a single desk**:

| Office Component | Microcontroller Equivalent | Function |
|---|---|---|
| The Manager / Brain | **CPU (ALU + Control Unit + Registers)** | Makes decisions and processes data |
| The Filing Cabinet (Permanent Records) | **ROM / Flash Memory** | Stores the program (instructions) permanently |
| The Whiteboard (Temporary Notes) | **RAM** | Stores variables and temporary data during execution |
| The Mailbox / Telephone | **I/O Ports / Communication Peripherals** | Interfaces with the outside world |
| The Wall Clock & Alarm | **Timers / Counters** | Tracks time and counts events |
| The Panic Button System | **Interrupt Controller** | Handles urgent external events immediately |
| The Office Power Switch | **Reset & Clock Circuit** | Initializes and synchronizes the system |

Without the microcontroller, you would need a **microprocessor + dozens of external chips** to build the same office — a complex, power-hungry, expensive solution. The microcontroller packs it all into a tiny IC, often the size of a fingernail.

## 1.3 The von Neumann vs. Harvard Architecture Debate

Modern microcontrollers (including the 8051, AVR, PIC, and ARM Cortex-M families) primarily use the **Harvard Architecture**, although some advanced variants use a **Modified Harvard** layout. KTU syllabus emphasizes this distinction.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Harvard Architecture:** Physically separate **program memory bus** and **data memory bus**, allowing simultaneous fetch of instruction and data. The 8051 is the canonical example studied under KTU OEAET613.
>
> **von Neumann Architecture:** A **single shared bus** for both program and data memory. Simpler hardware but creates a "von Neumann bottleneck" since instruction and data cannot be fetched at the same time.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Harvard Dominates Embedded:** In real-time control (e.g., anti-lock braking, motor control), instructions and sensor data must be read in the *same clock cycle*. Harvard architecture's dual-bus parallelism makes deterministic timing possible — a key KTU 2024 outcome for Module 1.

## 1.4 Standard Performance Metrics (KTU Expects Memorization)

- **Clock Frequency (f<sub>CLK</sub>):** Typically **1 MHz to 200 MHz** for modern MCUs; 8051 typically uses **11.0592 MHz** or **12 MHz** crystal.
- **Word Size:** **8-bit** (8051, PIC16) or **32-bit** (ARM Cortex-M).
- **Instruction Cycle Time (T):** 

$$T = \frac{1}{f_{\text{CLK}}}$$

- **Machine Cycle:** Multiple of clock period (8051 uses **12 clock periods per machine cycle**).
- **Program Memory Size:** 4 KB to 2 MB Flash (typical range).
- **RAM Size:** 128 B to 1 MB SRAM.
- **Power Supply:** **+5V** (classic 8051) or **+3.3V** (modern ARM-based).
- **Package Pin Count:** **8 to 176 pins** (DIP, QFP, BGA packages).

## 1.5 Why This Topic Matters (Engineering Context)

Microcontrollers are the invisible "brains" inside nearly every modern device:

- **Automotive:** Engine control units (ECUs), airbag systems, ABS
- **Consumer Electronics:** Washing machines, microwave ovens, TVs, remote controls
- **Medical:** Pacemakers, blood glucose meters, infusion pumps
- **Industrial:** PLCs, robotic arms, SCADA systems
- **IoT:** Smart sensors, wearable devices, home automation

KTU Module 1 lays the foundation for *all subsequent modules* — assembly programming, peripheral interfacing, and RTOS concepts are impossible to understand without mastering this internal architecture.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2 — Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Seven Building Blocks of a Generic Microcontroller

A microcontroller is not a single functional unit; it is an **orchestrated system of cooperating sub-modules** sharing a common bus backbone. The seven canonical blocks (the "Magnificent Seven" of MCU architecture) are:

### Block 1: Central Processing Unit (CPU)
The CPU is the *executive core*. It has three internal sub-units:
- **Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU):** Performs arithmetic (`ADD`, `SUB`, `MUL`, `DIV`) and logical (`AND`, `OR`, `XOR`, `NOT`, shifts, rotates) operations.
- **Register File:** A small, ultra-fast set of on-chip storage locations (8-bit, 16-bit, or 32-bit wide) directly accessible to the ALU. Includes the **Accumulator (A)**, **Program Counter (PC)**, **Stack Pointer (SP)**, **Instruction Register (IR)**, and **Program Status Word (PSW)**.
- **Control Unit (CU):** Decodes the fetched instruction (held in the IR) and generates the necessary **micro-control signals** (read/write strobes, register select, ALU mode select) to orchestrate the data flow through the buses.

### Block 2: Program Memory (ROM / Flash / EPROM)
- **Non-volatile** — retains contents when power is OFF.
- Stores the **machine code instructions** (the firmware).
- 8051 typically uses **4 KB on-chip ROM** (e.g., 8051); modern variants use **Flash** (reprogrammable in-circuit via ISP).
- Addressed by the **Program Counter (PC)**.

### Block 3: Data Memory (RAM / SRAM)
- **Volatile** — contents lost on power-off.
- Stores **runtime variables, the stack, and the register bank**.
- 8051 has **128 bytes of internal RAM** (expandable to 256 B externally).
- Organized as a **register bank (00H–1FH)**, **bit-addressable area (20H–2FH)**, and **general-purpose RAM (30H–7FH)**.

### Block 4: Input/Output Ports
- Bidirectional (or quasi-bidirectional, as in 8051) digital interfaces to external hardware.
- 8051 has **four 8-bit ports: P0, P1, P2, P3** → total 32 I/O lines.
- Each pin can typically drive **10–20 mA** (suitable for LEDs directly).
- Some pins have **alternate functions** (e.g., P3.0 = RxD, P3.1 = TxD for UART).

### Block 5: Timers / Counters
- Hardware modules that count clock pulses (Timer mode) or external events (Counter mode).
- 8051 has **two 16-bit timers/counters: T0 and T1**, each formed by two 8-bit Special Function Registers (THx, TLx).
- Essential for **time-delay generation**, **pulse-width measurement**, and **baud-rate generation** for serial communication.

### Block 6: Interrupt Controller
- Manages asynchronous, high-priority events from internal/external sources.
- 8051 supports **5 interrupt sources**: External 0, External 1, Timer 0 overflow, Timer 1 overflow, Serial port.
- Assigns priorities (high/low) and vector addresses to each interrupt.
- Enables **real-time responsiveness** without polling.

### Block 7: Serial Communication Interface (SCI / UART)
- Enables communication with other devices (PC, sensors, other MCUs) using protocols like **UART**, **SPI**, **I²C**.
- 8051 has an **on-chip full-duplex UART** (receive via RxD, transmit via TxD).
- Baud rate is programmable via Timer 1 overflow.

## 2.2 The Bus System — The "Highway Network"

Three parallel electrical pathways interconnect all blocks:

| Bus Type | Function | Width (8051) | Direction | Driven By |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Address Bus** | Carries the memory/I/O address to be accessed | 16-bit | Unidirectional (CPU → Memory/I/O) | CPU |
| **Data Bus** | Carries the actual data being transferred | 8-bit | Bidirectional | CPU ↔ Memory/I/O |
| **Control Bus** | Carries control signals (RD#, WR#, ALE, PSEN#) | ~6–10 lines | Mixed | CPU + Memory |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why bus width matters:** An 8-bit data bus means the CPU is an 8-bit processor. A 16-bit address bus means it can directly access $2^{16} = 65{,}536 = 64$ KB of memory. KTU frequently tests the relationship:

$$M_{\text{max}} = 2^{n}$$

where $n$ is the address bus width in bits.

## 2.3 The Fetch–Decode–Execute Cycle (Instruction Cycle)

This is the **heartbeat of the CPU** and the highest-weightage topic in Module 1.

**Step 1 — Fetch:**
- The **Program Counter (PC)** holds the address of the next instruction.
- The address is placed on the **address bus**.
- The **Control Unit** asserts the **PSEN#** (Program Store Enable) signal for ROM access.
- The instruction byte is read onto the **data bus** and loaded into the **Instruction Register (IR)**.
- The PC is then **incremented** to point to the next instruction.

**Step 2 — Decode:**
- The **Control Unit** decodes the opcode in the IR.
- It determines which operation is required and which internal resources must be activated.

**Step 3 — Execute:**
- The **ALU** performs the operation (add, move, compare, etc.).
- The result is stored in the **Accumulator** or another destination register.
- Status flags in the **PSW** (Carry, Auxiliary Carry, Overflow, Parity) are updated.

**Step 4 — Repeat:**
- The PC provides the next address and the cycle continues.

> [!NOTE]
> **8051-Specific Machine Cycle:** The 8051 divides its oscillator frequency by 12 to produce one machine cycle. So if f<sub>OSC</sub> = 12 MHz, then one machine cycle = 1 µs, and a single-byte, single-cycle instruction takes 1 µs. This is **THE most-asked KTU question** in Module 1.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Concept | Formula / Relation | Units | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Instruction cycle time (T) | $T = \dfrac{1}{f_{\text{CLK}}}$ | seconds (s) | For 8051, 1 machine cycle = $12/f_{\text{CLK}}$ |
| 2 | Machine cycle (8051) | $T_{\text{MC}} = \dfrac{12}{f_{\text{OSC}}}$ | seconds (s) | e.g., 12 MHz crystal → 1 µs MC |
| 3 | Max addressable memory | $M = 2^{n}$ | bytes | $n$ = address bus width |
| 4 | 8051 max memory (16-bit addr) | $2^{16} = 65{,}536 = 64$ KB | bytes | Split: 64 KB code + 64 KB data |
| 5 | Timer count value | $\text{Count} = 2^{N} - T_{\text{desired}} \cdot f_{\text{OSC}} \div 12$ | decimal | $N$ = timer bit width (16 for T0/T1) |
| 6 | Baud rate (8051 UART, Mode 1) | $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \cdot f_{\text{OSC}}}{32 \cdot 12 \cdot (256 - \text{TH1})}$ | bps | SMOD = 0 or 1 (PCON.7) |
| 7 | Power dissipation (CMOS MCU) | $P = C \cdot V_{DD}^{2} \cdot f$ | watts (W) | $C$ = switching capacitance |
| 8 | Interrupt latency (8051) | $3 \text{ to } 8$ machine cycles | µs | Fixed response time for level/edge triggered INT |
| 9 | Address bus width vs ROM | $n = \log_2(\text{ROM size in bytes})$ | bits | Inverse of formula 3 |
| 10 | Stack growth (8051) | SP starts at 07H, grows **upward** to 7FH | bytes | PUSH increments SP; POP decrements SP |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical LaTeX Rule:** In KTU answer sheets, always use $\vert x \vert$ or $\lvert x \rvert$ for absolute value — never just $\vert x \vert$ inside a table cell, as the pipes break markdown rendering in digital submissions.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive ECU:** Uses a 32-bit MCU running at 100+ MHz with Harvard architecture to execute PID control loops in <100 µs.
- **IoT Sensor Node:** Uses a low-power 8-bit MCU (e.g., ATmega328) with Harvard architecture and integrated ADC to sample temperature every second on a coin cell for years.
- **Medical Pacemaker:** Uses a deterministic Harvard-architecture MCU to deliver precisely timed electrical pulses — any timing violation could be fatal. This is *why* Harvard dominates safety-critical embedded systems.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3 — Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Maximum Addressable Memory from Address Bus Width

This derivation is the **#1 most-marked** KTU Module 1 question.

**Given:** A microcontroller has an $n$-bit address bus. Each addressable location holds 1 byte (8 bits).

**Find:** Maximum directly addressable memory $M$ in bytes.

**Step 1 — Count the unique binary addresses.**
A bus of width $n$ bits can represent $n$ independent binary digits. Each digit is binary (0 or 1). The total number of unique binary patterns of length $n$ is $2^n$.

**Step 2 — Map addresses to memory locations.**
Each unique binary pattern corresponds to **one unique memory address** pointing to one byte. Therefore, the number of addressable bytes equals the number of unique addresses.

**Step 3 — Formulate the result.**

$$M = 2^{n} \quad \text{(bytes)}$$

**Step 4 — Verify with a known case (8051).**
The 8051 has a 16-bit address bus ($n = 16$).

$$M = 2^{16} = 65{,}536 \text{ bytes} = 64 \text{ KB}$$

This matches the 8051 datasheet (64 KB code + 64 KB data). ✓

**Step 5 — Worked example: ARM Cortex-M0.**
ARM Cortex-M0 has a 32-bit address bus ($n = 32$).

$$M = 2^{32} = 4{,}294{,}967{,}296 \text{ bytes} = 4 \text{ GB}$$

This matches the 32-bit ARM memory map. ✓

## 3.2 Derivation 2: 8051 Machine Cycle and Instruction Execution Time

**Given:** An 8051 system uses a **12 MHz crystal oscillator**. We want to determine the time taken to execute a single-byte, single-cycle instruction.

**Step 1 — Recall the fundamental relationship.**
For any digital system, the clock period is the inverse of the frequency.

$$T_{\text{CLK}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{OSC}}}$$

**Step 2 — Plug in $f_{\text{OSC}} = 12$ MHz $= 12 \times 10^6$ Hz.**

$$T_{\text{CLK}} = \frac{1}{12 \times 10^{6}} = 83.33 \text{ ns}$$

**Step 3 — Apply the 8051 machine cycle formula.**
The 8051 divides the oscillator frequency by 12 internally to define one machine cycle.

$$T_{\text{MC}} = \frac{12}{f_{\text{OSC}}}$$

**Step 4 — Compute.**

$$T_{\text{MC}} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^{6}} = 1 \times 10^{-6} \text{ s} = 1 \; \mu\text{s}$$

**Step 5 — Conclude the execution time.**
A single-byte, single-cycle 8051 instruction (e.g., `INC A`, `CLR A`, `MOV A, R0`) takes **exactly one machine cycle** = **1 µs**.

**Step 6 — Worked variant: 11.0592 MHz crystal (UART-friendly).**
This frequency is chosen because it is exactly divisible by standard baud rates (9600, 19200, etc.).

$$T_{\text{MC}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} \approx 1.0851 \; \mu\text{s}$$

This is why **11.0592 MHz is the KTU textbook recommendation** for serial-communication projects.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Timer Reload Value for a Desired Delay

**Problem:** Using Timer 0 of an 8051 (16-bit mode) with a 12 MHz crystal, generate a **50 ms delay**.

**Step 1 — Relate timer tick to machine cycle.**
The timer increments once every machine cycle.

$$T_{\text{tick}} = T_{\text{MC}} = \frac{12}{f_{\text{OSC}}}$$

For 12 MHz:

$$T_{\text{tick}} = 1 \; \mu\text{s}$$

**Step 2 — Calculate the number of timer ticks required.**

$$N = \frac{T_{\text{desired}}}{T_{\text{tick}}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1 \times 10^{-6}} = 50{,}000$$

**Step 3 — Determine the initial (reload) timer value.**
Timer 0 in mode 1 is a 16-bit counter that overflows from FFFFH to 0000H (which sets the TF0 flag). Total counts available = $2^{16} = 65{,}536$.

$$\text{Reload Value} = 65{,}536 - N = 65{,}536 - 50{,}000 = 15{,}536$$

**Step 4 — Convert to hexadecimal.**

$$15{,}536 = 3\text{CB0H}$$

**Step 5 — Split into TH0 and TL0.**

$$TH0 = 3\text{CH}, \quad TL0 = \text{B0H}$$

**Step 6 — Final assembly code (KTU Model Answer).**
```
MOV TMOD, #01H      ; Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit)
MOV TH0, #3CH       ; Load high byte
MOV TL0, #B0H       ; Load low byte
SETB TR0            ; Start Timer 0
HERE: JNB TF0, HERE  ; Wait for overflow flag
CLR TR0             ; Stop timer
CLR TF0             ; Clear overflow flag
```

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Harvard vs. von Neumann (KTU Repeated Question)

| Parameter | von Neumann | Harvard | Modified Harvard |
|---|---|---|---|
| Memory buses | Single shared bus | Separate code + data buses | Separate caches, unified main memory |
| Simultaneous I+D fetch | ❌ No | ✅ Yes | ✅ Yes (via cache) |
| Hardware complexity | Low | Higher | Medium |
| Determinism (real-time) | Poor | Excellent | Very good |
| Bus contention | Yes (bottleneck) | None | Minimal |
| Example processors | x86, 68000 | 8051, AVR, PIC, ARM Cortex-M | ARM9, modern x86 with cache |
| KTU syllabus weightage | Low (conceptual) | **Very High** (Module 1 core) | Medium |

## 3.5 Exhaustive Comparison: Microprocessor vs. Microcontroller

| Parameter | Microprocessor (MPU) | Microcontroller (MCU) |
|---|---|---|
| Integration | CPU only | CPU + Memory + I/O + Peripherals |
| External components required | ROM, RAM, I/O controller, timer IC | None (single chip works) |
| Cost | High system cost | Very low system cost |
| Power consumption | High (watts) | Low (milliwatts to µW in sleep) |
| Clock speed | 1 GHz to 5 GHz | 1 MHz to 200 MHz |
| Typical use | Desktop PC, server, laptop | Embedded systems, IoT, appliances |
| Bit width | 32, 64-bit | 8, 16, 32-bit |
| KTU example | 8085, 8086 (studied in earlier sem) | **8051, ARM (this course)** |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4 — Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block Diagram — General Internal Architecture of a Microcontroller

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CPU["CENTRAL PROCESSING UNIT"]
        ALU["ALU\nArithmetic Logic Unit"]
        CU["Control Unit\nDecoder + Sequencer"]
        REG["Register File\nA B R0-R7 SP PC IR PSW"]
    end

    subgraph MEM["MEMORY SUBSYSTEM"]
        ROM["ROM / FLASH\nProgram Memory\nNon-Volatile"]
        RAM["RAM / SRAM\nData Memory\nVolatile"]
    end

    subgraph PER["ON-CHIP PERIPHERALS"]
        IO["I/O PORTS\nP0 P1 P2 P3"]
        TIM["TIMERS / COUNTERS\nT0 T1 T2"]
        INT["INTERRUPT\nCONTROLLER"]
        UART["SERIAL UART\nRxD TxD"]
    end

    subgraph SUP["SUPPORT CIRCUITRY"]
        CLK["CLOCK & RESET\nOscillator + POR"]
        BUS["INTERNAL BUS\nAddress + Data + Control"]
    end

    CPU <--> BUS
    BUS <--> MEM
    BUS <--> PER
    CLK -.-> CPU
    CLK -.-> MEM
    CLK -.-> PER
```

## 4.2 The Fetch–Decode–Execute Cycle Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    F1["PC --> Address Bus"] --> F2["Memory responds\nInstruction byte"]
    F2 --> F3["IR <- Instruction\nPC <- PC + 1"]
    F3 --> D1["Control Unit\ndecodes opcode"]
    D1 --> D2["Generate control\nsignals"]
    D2 --> E1["ALU executes\noperation"]
    E1 --> E2["Update flags\nin PSW"]
    E2 --> F1
```

## 4.3 8051-Specific Internal Architecture (KTU Most-Asked Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CORE["CPU CORE 8051"]
        ALU["ALU"]
        ACC["Accumulator A"]
        BREG["B Register"]
        PSW["PSW\nFlags"]
        SP["Stack Pointer SP"]
        PC["Program Counter PC\n16-bit"]
    end

    subgraph SFRS["SPECIAL FUNCTION REGISTERS"]
        TH0["TH0 TL0"]
        TH1["TH1 TL1"]
        SCON["SCON"]
        TCON["TCON"]
        TMOD["TMOD"]
        IE["IE IP"]
    end

    subgraph PORTS["I/O PORTS"]
        P0["Port 0\n8-bit"]
        P1["Port 1\n8-bit"]
        P2["Port 2\n8-bit"]
        P3["Port 3\n8-bit"]
    end

    ROM["On-chip ROM\n4 KB"] -.->|PSEN| CORE
    RAM["Internal RAM\n128 B"] <-->|MOVX| CORE
    EXTROM["External ROM\nup to 64 KB"] -.->|PSEN| CORE
    EXTRAM["External RAM\nup to 64 KB"] <-->|MOVX| CORE

    CORE <--> PORTS
    CORE <--> SFRS
```

## 4.4 Memory Map of 8051 (Logical Organization)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph CODE["PROGRAM MEMORY CODE SPACE"]
        A1["0000H - 002BH\nReset + Interrupt Vectors"]
        A2["002BH - 0FFFH\nOn-chip ROM 4 KB"]
        A3["1000H - FFFFH\nExternal ROM up to 64 KB"]
    end

    subgraph DATA["DATA MEMORY SPACE"]
        B1["00H - 1FH\nRegister Bank 0-3\n32 bytes"]
        B2["20H - 2FH\nBit Addressable Area\n16 bytes = 128 bits"]
        B3["30H - 7FH\nGeneral Purpose RAM\n80 bytes"]
        B4["80H - FFH\nSFRs Special Function\nRegisters 128 bytes"]
    end
```

## 4.5 Signal Flow During Instruction Fetch (8051)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant PC as Program Counter
    participant ABUS as Address Bus
    participant ROM as ROM/Flash
    participant DBUS as Data Bus
    participant IR as Instruction Register
    participant CU as Control Unit

    PC->>ABUS: Place address on bus
    CU->>ROM: Assert PSEN# signal
    ROM->>DBUS: Place instruction byte
    DBUS->>IR: Latch instruction
    PC->>PC: Increment by 1
    IR->>CU: Decode opcode
    CU->>CU: Generate control signals
```

## 4.6 Interrupt Handling Flow (Generic MCU)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Normal program execution"] --> B{"Interrupt\nRequest?"}
    B -- "No" --> A
    B -- "Yes" --> C["Complete current\ninstruction"]
    C --> D["Push PC to stack"]
    D --> E["Load ISR vector\naddress into PC"]
    E --> F["Execute ISR"]
    F --> G["RETI instruction"]
    G --> H["Pop PC from stack"]
    H --> A
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Compilation Note:** All Mermaid nodes above use purely alphanumeric IDs (e.g., `nodeA`, `step1`) with no reserved keywords. Labels contain only raw uppercase alphanumeric text — no bold (`**`), italics (`*`), or HTML tags — ensuring clean rendering in GitHub, VSCode, and KTU's learning management system.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

# SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, May 2024]**
> **Differentiate between a microprocessor and a microcontroller. Mention any three points.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| # | Microprocessor | Microcontroller |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Contains **only the CPU**; memory and I/O are **external**. | Contains **CPU + memory + I/O + peripherals** on a **single chip**. |
| 2 | **High** cost and complexity for embedded use. | **Low** cost and highly integrated. |
| 3 | **High** power consumption (watts). | **Low** power consumption (mW to µW). |
| 4 | Used in **general-purpose computing** (PCs, laptops). | Used in **embedded control** (appliances, IoT, automobiles). |
| 5 | Examples: 8085, 8086, Core i7. | Examples: 8051, ATmega328, STM32, PIC16F877. |

---

> **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]**
> **Explain the Harvard architecture of a microcontroller with a suitable diagram.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
In Harvard architecture, the microcontroller has **two physically separate memory spaces and two separate buses** — one for **program (code) memory** and another for **data memory**. This allows the CPU to **fetch an instruction and read/write data in the same clock cycle**, eliminating the von Neumann bottleneck. The 8051, AVR, and PIC families use Harvard architecture. Since code and data are in separate address spaces, the 8051 uses the **PSEN#** signal to access program ROM and the **RD#/WR#** signals to access data RAM. This dual-bus structure delivers deterministic, real-time performance essential for embedded control. *(Diagrammatic representation expected for full marks.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

---

### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Style]*

> **(a)** Draw the **general internal architecture (block diagram) of a microcontroller** and explain the function of **any five major blocks** in detail. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a neat diagram, explain the **fetch–decode–execute cycle** of a microcontroller. Calculate the **machine cycle time** and **execution time of a single-byte instruction** for a 8051 system with a **11.0592 MHz crystal**. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**
*(Refer to the diagram in Section 4.1 of these notes — student must reproduce a clean, labeled block diagram showing CPU, Memory, I/O, Timers, Interrupts, Bus, Clock.)*

**Explanation of five major blocks (4 marks = 4 × 1 mark each):**

1. **CPU (Central Processing Unit):** The "brain" of the microcontroller. It contains the **ALU** (performs arithmetic and logic operations), the **Control Unit** (decodes instructions and generates control signals), and the **Register File** (fast on-chip storage for operands and addresses).

2. **Program Memory (ROM/Flash):** **Non-volatile** memory that stores the **firmware** (machine code instructions). 8051 typically has 4 KB of on-chip ROM; modern MCUs use Flash. Instructions are fetched sequentially using the **Program Counter (PC)**.

3. **Data Memory (RAM):** **Volatile** memory used to store **runtime variables, the stack, and the register banks**. The 8051 has 128 B of internal RAM organized as Register Banks (00H–1FH), Bit-Addressable area (20H–2FH), and General Purpose RAM (30H–7FH).

4. **I/O Ports:** Bidirectional digital interfaces connecting the MCU to external hardware. The 8051 has four 8-bit ports (P0, P1, P2, P3) providing 32 I/O lines. They can source/sink ~10–20 mA, sufficient to drive LEDs directly.

5. **Timers/Counters:** Hardware counters that operate as **timers** (counting internal clock pulses for time delays) or **counters** (counting external events on a pin). The 8051 has two 16-bit timers (T0, T1) used for delay generation, event counting, and baud-rate generation.

6. **Interrupt Controller:** Manages **asynchronous high-priority events** from external (INT0, INT1) or internal (timer overflow, serial) sources. On detecting an interrupt, the CPU finishes the current instruction, pushes the PC onto the stack, and jumps to the corresponding **ISR (Interrupt Service Routine)**. After executing `RETI`, it resumes normal flow.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Fetch–Decode–Execute Cycle Diagram (3 marks):**
*(Refer to Section 4.2 of these notes.)*

**Step-by-step Explanation (2 marks):**
- **Fetch:** PC places address on address bus; PSEN# is asserted; instruction byte is read into IR; PC is incremented.
- **Decode:** Control Unit decodes the opcode held in IR.
- **Execute:** ALU performs the operation; result stored in accumulator; PSW flags updated; cycle repeats.

**Calculation (2 marks):**

$$T_{\text{CLK}} = \frac{1}{f_{\text{OSC}}} = \frac{1}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} \approx 90.42 \text{ ns}$$

$$T_{\text{MC}} = \frac{12}{f_{\text{OSC}}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^{6}} \approx 1.0851 \; \mu\text{s}$$

A single-byte, single-cycle 8051 instruction (e.g., `INC A`) takes **1 machine cycle**.

$$T_{\text{exec}} \approx 1.0851 \; \mu\text{s}$$

---

### **QUESTION B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)** — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Style]*

> **(a)** Compare **Harvard and von Neumann architectures** in detail. State **which one the 8051 uses** and justify your answer. **(7 marks)**
>
> **(b)** With a neat diagram, describe the **internal RAM organization of the 8051**. List the **four register banks** and explain the role of **PSW bits RS0 and RS1** in bank selection. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution for (a) — 7 marks

**Comparison Table (4 marks):**
*(Refer to Section 3.4 of these notes — student must reproduce the full comparison table.)*

**Justification of 8051 using Harvard (3 marks):**
The 8051 employs a **modified Harvard architecture** because:
- It has **physically separate program and data memory spaces**, each addressable up to 64 KB independently ($2^{16}$ bytes).
- The **PSEN#** (Program Store Enable) signal activates only during code fetches from ROM, while **RD# and WR#** activate only during data RAM access — these signals are mutually exclusive, confirming bus separation.
- This enables the 8051 to **simultaneously fetch an instruction and access data** in the same machine cycle, delivering deterministic real-time performance — critical for embedded control applications such as motor control, signal generation, and protocol handling.

#### Model Solution for (b) — 7 marks

**Internal RAM Diagram (3 marks):**
*(Refer to the 8051 RAM memory map in Section 4.4 of these notes.)*

**Register Banks and PSW (4 marks):**
The lower 32 bytes (00H–1FH) of internal RAM are divided into **four register banks**, each containing **eight 8-bit registers R0–R7**.

| Bank | Address Range | Selected by RS1, RS0 |
|---|---|---|
| Bank 0 | 00H – 07H | 0, 0 |
| Bank 1 | 08H – 0FH | 0, 1 |
| Bank 2 | 10H – 17H | 1, 0 |
| Bank 3 | 18H – 1FH | 1, 1 |

The **PSW (Program Status Word) register bits RS0 and RS1** (bits 3 and 4 of PSW) select the active register bank. The **default bank after reset is Bank 0**. By switching RS0/RS1, the programmer can rapidly switch context — useful for interrupt service routines that need their own private registers without stack push/pop overhead. The stack pointer (SP) is initialized to **07H** but must be moved if Bank 1 is used (otherwise stack writes clobber register bank 1).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout — Common Mark Losers:**
> 1. **Confusing "machine cycle" with "instruction cycle":** Machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods for 8051. Instruction cycle = 1, 2, or 4 machine cycles depending on the instruction. Don't mix them up.
> 2. **Forgetting PSEN# vs. RD#/WR#:** PSEN# is for *code* fetch from ROM; RD#/WR# are for *data* read/write from/to RAM. This distinction is worth 1–2 marks every KTU exam.
> 3. **Writing `|x|` in tables:** Use `\vert` or `\lvert \rvert` in LaTeX to avoid breaking table syntax — a digital-submission mistake that costs aesthetic marks.
> 4. **Forgetting the stack pointer default:** SP = 07H after reset. Many students write 08H, confusing it with the start of Bank 1.
> 5. **Not drawing the diagram in (a):** A 14-mark question without a diagram typically loses 2–3 marks. Always include a labeled block diagram.
> 6. **Mixing Harvard with von Neumann:** Modified Harvard (used by 8051) is *not* pure Harvard — the on-chip RAM is accessible via the same MOVX-style data bus, but the program fetch is independent. Be precise with terminology.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Must-Memorize" List for KTU Module 1 — Microcontroller Architecture:**

- ✅ A **microcontroller** = CPU + Memory + I/O + Peripherals on a **single IC**.
- ✅ **Microprocessor** = CPU only; needs external memory and I/O chips.
- ✅ **8051** uses **modified Harvard architecture** (separate code + data buses).
- ✅ **von Neumann** has a single shared bus; creates a bottleneck; not used by 8051.
- ✅ Three internal CPU sub-units: **ALU**, **Register File**, **Control Unit**.
- ✅ Key 8051 registers: **A (Accumulator)**, **B**, **R0–R7**, **SP**, **PC (16-bit)**, **DPTR**, **PSW**.
- ✅ **Bus types:** Address (unidirectional), Data (bidirectional), Control (mixed).
- ✅ **Max addressable memory formula:** $M = 2^{n}$ where $n$ = address bus width.
- ✅ 8051 has **16-bit address bus** → $2^{16} = 64$ KB per memory space (code + data).
- ✅ **8051 Machine Cycle:** $T_{\text{MC}} = 12 / f_{\text{OSC}}$. For 12 MHz → 1 µs. For 11.0592 MHz → 1.0851 µs.
- ✅ **Single-byte, single-cycle instruction** = 1 machine cycle.
- ✅ **Fetch–Decode–Execute** is the fundamental CPU operation cycle.
- ✅ **8051 Program Memory:** 4 KB on-chip ROM (8051) / 8 KB (8052) / up to 64 KB external.
- ✅ **8051 Data Memory:** 128 B internal RAM (256 B in 8052) + up to 64 KB external.
- ✅ **Internal RAM Layout:** 00H–1FH (Register Banks), 20H–2FH (Bit-Addressable), 30H–7FH (General Purpose), 80H–FFH (SFRs).
- ✅ **Bit-Addressable area** (20H–2FH) = 16 bytes × 8 bits = **128 individually addressable bits**.
- ✅ **Register Bank selection** via **PSW bits RS0 (PSW.3) and RS1 (PSW.4)**.
- ✅ **SP** initializes to **07H**; PUSH increments, POP decrements.
- ✅ **Four 8-bit I/O ports** (P0–P3) = **32 I/O lines**; P0 is open-drain, needs external pull-ups.
- ✅ **Two 16-bit timers/counters** (T0, T1), each formed by two 8-bit SFRs (THx, TLx).
- ✅ **Five interrupt sources** in 8051: INT0, INT1, T0 overflow, T1 overflow, Serial port.
- ✅ **Interrupt vector addresses:** 0003H (INT0), 000BH (T0), 0013H (INT1), 001BH (T1), 0023H (Serial).
- ✅ **11.0592 MHz crystal** is preferred for UART applications (exact baud-rate divisibility).
- ✅ **PSEN#** = Program Store Enable (active during code fetch from ROM).
- ✅ **ALE** = Address Latch Enable (demultiplexes address/data bus on P0).
- ✅ **EA#** = External Access (low → all code from external ROM; high → on-chip ROM priority).
- ✅ **RST** pin: applying a high pulse for ≥ 2 machine cycles resets the 8051 (PC = 0000H).
- ✅ **Power supply:** VCC = +5V; GND = 0V. Modern variants use +3.3V.
- ✅ **Package types:** DIP-40, QFP-44, and LCC-44 (leadless).

> **End of Module 1 Notes — Microcontroller Architecture: General Internal Architecture**
> *Next Module Preview: 8051 Instruction Set, Addressing Modes, and Assembly Language Programming.*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Address Bus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET613 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Address Bus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

The **Address Bus** is a *unidirectional* set of conducting wires (signal lines) used by the Central Processing Unit (CPU) of a microcontroller to transmit the **physical memory address** of a memory location, register, or I/O device that it wishes to read from or write to. The address bus is strictly a *selecting* bus — it does NOT carry the actual data payload.

In the context of the **Intel 8051 family** (the canonical microcontroller studied in OEAET613), the address bus is **16 bits wide**, organized as two distinct signal groups:
- **Lower Order Address Bus (A0 – A7)**: 8 lines.
- **Higher Order Address Bus (A8 – A15)**: 8 lines.

The maximum number of uniquely addressable locations is therefore mathematically constrained by the bus width, given by the fundamental relationship:

$$\text{Addressable Memory Locations} = 2^{n}$$

where **n** is the width (in bits) of the address bus.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** The address bus, data bus, and control bus form the **three-bus architecture** (System Bus) of the 8051. Students must clearly differentiate the *function* of each bus. Mixing up the address bus and data bus is a common cause of losing 2–3 marks in board examinations.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The House Number System"

Imagine a long street (the **memory**) containing 65,536 houses, each house holding one byte of data. The CPU is a postman who wants to deliver a letter (the **data**) to a specific house.

- The **postman's notebook** in which he writes the destination *house number* is the **Address Bus**.
- The number he writes (e.g., `4F2A` in hexadecimal) uniquely identifies one, and only one, house.
- The **letter itself** travels on a different path — the **Data Bus**.
- The street is *one-way* for house numbers: the postman can only write numbers **TO** the houses, never the reverse. This is precisely why the address bus is **unidirectional**.

If the postman's notebook only had two digits (00–99), he could only reach 100 houses, no matter how large the street actually was. Similarly, an 8-bit address bus is limited to 256 locations, while a 16-bit bus (8051) reaches 65,536 locations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Standard Metric (8051):** The 8051's 16-bit address bus can uniquely identify **2¹⁶ = 65,536 bytes = 64 KB** of external memory. This figure (64 KB) is one of the most frequently tested numbers in KTU examinations.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Value (8051 Standard) | Units |
|---|---|---|
| Address Bus Width | **16** | bits |
| Number of Address Lines | **16** | physical pins |
| Maximum Addressable Memory | **65,536** | bytes |
| Equivalent in KB | **64** | KB |
| Bus Direction | **Unidirectional (CPU → Memory/I/O)** | — |
| Lower Order Lines (A0–A7) Source | **Port 0** (multiplexed with data) | — |
| Higher Order Lines (A8–A15) Source | **Port 2** (dedicated) | — |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown — How the Address Bus Works in the 8051

The 8051 is an **8-bit microcontroller**, meaning its internal data processing unit is 8 bits wide. However, it requires 16 bits of address information to access the full 64 KB of external memory space. This creates a pin-count optimization challenge, solved elegantly through **multiplexing**.

### Step-by-Step Logic Flow

- **Step 1 — Address Generation:** The CPU internally computes a 16-bit memory address (e.g., for an instruction fetch from code memory or a `MOVX` data access).

- **Step 2 — Latching Signal (ALE):** The microcontroller asserts the **Address Latch Enable (ALE)** pin HIGH. This signal instructs an external latch (typically a **74LS373** or **74HC573** octal D-type transparent latch) to *capture and hold* whatever is currently on Port 0.

- **Step 3 — Multiplexed Phase:** During the first half of the memory cycle, **Port 0** outputs the **Lower Order Address (A0–A7)**, and **Port 2** outputs the **Higher Order Address (A8–A15)**. Together, these 16 lines form the complete 16-bit address bus.

- **Step 4 — De-multiplexing:** The external latch freezes the lower-order address on its outputs. After ALE goes LOW, Port 0 is *released* (goes to high-impedance) so it can later serve as the **8-bit bidirectional data bus (D0–D7)**.

- **Step 5 — Memory Access:** The now-stable 16-bit address (8 bits from the latch + 8 bits from Port 2) is presented to the external memory chip's address pins. The memory decodes this address and either drives data onto the data bus (READ) or accepts data from it (WRITE).

- **Step 6 — Control Signals:** The **PSEN̄** (Program Store Enable) signal activates ROM, while the **RD̄** and **WR̄** signals govern RAM read/write operations.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Multiplexing?** The 8051 is packaged in a 40-pin DIP. If 16 pins were reserved solely for addresses and 8 for data, only 16 pins would remain for the four I/O ports. Multiplexing allows Port 0 to serve *two roles* — first as address, then as data — saving 8 precious pins.

## 2.2 Internal vs. External Address Bus

The 8051 maintains two distinct address spaces:

- **Internal Address Bus:** Used to access on-chip memory: **128 bytes of Internal RAM** (address range `00H – 7FH`) and **4 KB of Internal ROM** (address range `0000H – 0FFFH`).
- **External Address Bus:** Used to access off-chip memory, addressable from `0000H` to `FFFFH` (64 KB code + 64 KB data, partially overlapping in the Harvard architecture of the original 8051).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Harvard vs. Von Neumann:** The 8051 uses a **modified Harvard architecture** with separate address buses for code memory (accessed via PSEN̄) and data memory (accessed via RD̄/WR̄). This is a classic KTU 14-mark question topic.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Rule | Description | Unit |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $M = 2^{n}$ | Max addressable memory locations, $n$ = address bus width | bytes |
| 2 | $M_{KB} = \dfrac{2^{n}}{1024}$ | Memory in kilobytes (for n ≥ 10) | KB |
| 3 | $M_{MB} = \dfrac{2^{n}}{2^{20}} = 2^{n-20}$ | Memory in megabytes (for n ≥ 20) | MB |
| 4 | $\text{Address Range} = 0 \text{ to } (2^{n} - 1)$ | Decimal range; last address = 2ⁿ − 1 | decimal |
| 5 | $\text{Address Range (Hex)} = 0000\text{H to FFFFH}$ | For 16-bit bus | hexadecimal |
| 6 | $\text{CPU Pin Overhead} = A + D + C$ | A = address lines, D = data lines, C = control lines | pins |

### Quick Reference Table — Address Bus Width vs. Addressable Memory

| Address Bus Width (n) | Max Memory (2ⁿ bytes) | In KB / MB | Common Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| 4 bits | 16 bytes | — | Tiny embedded controllers |
| 8 bits | 256 bytes | — | 8051 internal RAM (lower half) |
| 12 bits | 4,096 bytes | 4 KB | 8051 internal ROM |
| 16 bits | 65,536 bytes | **64 KB** | **8051 external memory** |
| 20 bits | 1,048,576 bytes | 1 MB | 8086 |
| 24 bits | 16,777,216 bytes | 16 MB | 80386 |
| 32 bits | 4,294,967,296 bytes | 4 GB | Modern ARM Cortex-M |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

The address bus concept governs every digital system — from a simple washing machine controller to advanced automotive ECUs. In modern 32-bit ARM Cortex-M microcontrollers, a 32-bit address bus permits up to **4 GB** of linear addressing, enabling complex operating systems like embedded Linux. In contrast, the 8051's 16-bit bus (64 KB) makes it ideal for **cost-sensitive, deterministic, real-time control applications** such as:
- Consumer appliance control
- Simple sensor interfacing
- Educational embedded platforms
- Industrial timer/counter modules

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Addressable Memory of the 8051

**Problem Statement:** Prove that the 8051 microcontroller, with its 16-bit address bus, can access exactly 64 KB of external memory.

### Step-by-Step Derivation

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given:} \quad & n = 16 \text{ bits (address bus width of 8051)} \\
\text{To find:} \quad & M_{\max} = \text{Maximum addressable memory in bytes} \\
\\
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Each address line can carry one of two states: logic 0 or logic 1.} \\
& \text{Therefore, the number of unique binary patterns} = 2^{n} \\
\\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Each unique pattern corresponds to exactly one addressable byte.} \\
& \therefore M_{\max} = 2^{n} \\
\\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Substituting } n = 16: \\
& M_{\max} = 2^{16} \\
\\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{Evaluating the power of 2:} \\
& 2^{10} = 1024 \\
& 2^{16} = 2^{10} \times 2^{6} = 1024 \times 64 \\
& 2^{16} = 65{,}536 \text{ bytes} \\
\\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{Converting to kilobytes (1 KB = 1024 bytes):} \\
& M_{\max} = \frac{65{,}536}{1024} = 64 \text{ KB} \\
\\
\therefore \quad & \boxed{M_{\max} = 64 \text{ KB}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Address Decomposition on the 8051

**Problem Statement:** A programmer writes `MOVX A, @DPTR` with `DPTR = 4F2AH`. Determine which bits appear on Port 0, which bits appear on the latch, and which bits appear on Port 2.

### Step-by-Step Solution

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Given:} \quad & \text{16-bit address} = 4F2A\text{H} \\
& \text{Convert to binary: } 4\text{F2A}_{16} \\
\\
\text{Step 1:} \quad & \text{Convert each hex digit to 4-bit binary:} \\
& 4 = 0100 \\
& F = 1111 \\
& 2 = 0010 \\
& A = 1010 \\
& \text{Combined 16-bit binary: } 0100\;1111\;0010\;1010 \\
\\
\text{Step 2:} \quad & \text{Identify lower byte (A0–A7) and upper byte (A8–A15):} \\
& A_{15}\text{–}A_{8} \;(\text{upper byte}) = 4\text{F}_{16} = 0100\;1111 \\
& A_{7}\text{–}A_{0} \;(\text{lower byte}) = 2\text{A}_{16} = 0010\;1010 \\
\\
\text{Step 3:} \quad & \text{Assign lines to physical pins during the first clock phase:} \\
& \text{Port 2 (pins P2.0–P2.7) outputs A8–A15} = 0100\;1111 \\
& \text{Port 0 (pins P0.0–P0.7) outputs A0–A7} = 0010\;1010 \\
\\
\text{Step 4:} \quad & \text{The latch (74LS373) captures and holds the lower byte:} \\
& \text{Latch outputs} = 2\text{A}_{16} = 0010\;1010 \\
\\
\text{Step 5:} \quad & \text{After ALE goes LOW, Port 0 is freed for data transfer:} \\
& \text{Stable 16-bit address to memory} = \underbrace{4\text{F}}_{\text{from P2}}\,\underbrace{2\text{A}}_{\text{from latch}}
\end{aligned}
$$

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Pin Count Optimization via Multiplexing

**Problem Statement:** Compare the pin count required for a non-multiplexed 8051 bus versus the actual multiplexed design.

### Step-by-Step Comparison

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Non-multiplexed Design:} \quad & \\
& \text{Address lines required} = 16 \\
& \text{Data lines required} = 8 \\
& \text{Control lines (ALE, PSEN̄, RD̄, WR̄, EĀ)} = 5 \\
& \text{I/O port pins remaining} = 40 - (16 + 8 + 5) = 11 \text{ pins (only 1.375 ports!)} \\
\\
\text{Multiplexed Design (Actual 8051):} \quad & \\
& \text{Shared address/data lines} = 8 \text{ (Port 0)} \\
& \text{Address-only lines} = 8 \text{ (Port 2)} \\
& \text{Data-only lines} = 0 \text{ (Port 0 is reused)} \\
& \text{Control lines} = 5 \\
& \text{Pins consumed by bus} = 8 + 8 + 5 = 21 \text{ pins} \\
& \text{I/O port pins remaining} = 40 - 21 = 19 \text{ pins (3 full ports + 3 lines)} \\
\\
\text{Pin Savings:} \quad & 29 - 21 = 8 \text{ pins saved through multiplexing}
\end{aligned}
$$

This 8-pin saving is precisely why Intel chose to multiplex Port 0 — it preserved a full 4-port I/O capability (P0, P1, P2, P3) at the cost of one external latch IC.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Why 8051 Uses Two Separate Signals for Data Memory

| Memory Type | Address Range | Control Signal | Address Source |
|---|---|---|---|
| Internal RAM | 00H – 7FH | (Internal) | Internal address bus |
| Internal ROM | 0000H – 0FFFH | (Internal) | Program Counter |
| External ROM | 0000H – FFFFH | **PSEN̄** | PC (16-bit) |
| External RAM | 0000H – FFFFH | **RD̄ / WR̄** | DPTR (16-bit) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Distinction:** In the *original* 8051 (not the 8052), the external code memory and external data memory **share the same 64 KB address space** but are activated by *different control signals* (PSEN̄ vs. RD̄/WR̄). This allows the combined external memory to be 64 KB + 64 KB = **128 KB**, but accessed through two *physically separate* address buses in the strictest sense of the Harvard architecture.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Address Bus Architecture of the 8051

```mermaid
graph TD
    CPU[CPU Core of 8051] -->|16-bit Internal Address| MUX[Address/Data Multiplexer]
    MUX -->|A0 to A7 lower byte| P0[Port 0 Pins P0.0 to P0.7]
    MUX -->|A8 to A15 upper byte| P2[Port 2 Pins P2.0 to P2.7]
    ALE[ALE Pin from 8051] -->|Enable Signal| LATCH[74LS373 Octal Latch]
    P0 -->|Captures A0 to A7| LATCH
    LATCH -->|Holds stable A0 to A7| MEMADD[Memory Address Bus 16 bits]
    P2 -->|Holds stable A8 to A15| MEMADD
    MEMADD --> ROM[External ROM Chip]
    MEMADD --> RAM[External RAM Chip]
    P0 -->|After ALE low becomes| DATABUS[8-bit Bidirectional Data Bus D0 to D7]
    DATABUS --> ROM
    DATABUS --> RAM
    PSEN[PSEN bar Signal] --> ROM
    RDWR[RD bar WR bar Signals] --> RAM
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Address Bus Operation During MOVX Instruction

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as 8051 CPU
    participant ALE as ALE Pin
    participant P0 as Port 0
    participant LT as 74LS373 Latch
    participant P2 as Port 2
    participant MEM as External Memory
    participant DB as Data Bus

    Note over CPU: MOVX A, @DPTR with DPTR = 4F2AH
    CPU->>P2: Drive A8-A15 = 4FH (0100 1111)
    CPU->>P0: Drive A0-A7 = 2AH (0010 1010)
    CPU->>ALE: Pulse HIGH
    ALE->>LT: Enable Latch Capture
    LT->>LT: Freeze A0-A7 = 2AH on outputs
    CPU->>ALE: Drive LOW
    P0->>P0: Release to high-impedance
    CPU->>MEM: Stable 16-bit address 4F2AH on address bus
    CPU->>DB: Initiate READ via RD bar
    MEM->>DB: Drive data byte onto data bus
    DB->>CPU: CPU latches data into Accumulator
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Hierarchy Diagram — Three-Bus System Overview

```mermaid
graph LR
    subgraph SystemBus[8051 System Bus Architecture]
        AB[Address Bus - 16 lines - Unidirectional]
        DB[Data Bus - 8 lines - Bidirectional]
        CB[Control Bus - ALE PSEN RD WR EA]
    end
    CPU8051[8051 Microcontroller] --> SystemBus
    SystemBus --> EXTROM[External ROM up to 64 KB]
    SystemBus --> EXTRAM[External RAM up to 64 KB]
    SystemBus --> EXTIO[External I/O Devices]
    CPU8051 --> INTROM[Internal ROM 4 KB]
    CPU8051 --> INTRAM[Internal RAM 128 bytes]
    CPU8051 --> SFR[SFR Registers 128 bytes]
```

## 4.4 Pin-Out Reference (For Board Exam Reference)

| Port Pin | Alternate Function in External Memory Mode |
|---|---|
| P0.0 – P0.7 | **AD0 – AD7** (Multiplexed Lower Address + Data) |
| P1.0 – P1.7 | General Purpose I/O (no bus function) |
| P2.0 – P2.7 | **A8 – A15** (Higher Order Address) |
| P3.0 | RxD (Serial) |
| P3.1 | TxD (Serial) |
| P3.2 | INT0̄ (Interrupt 0) |
| P3.3 | INT1̄ (Interrupt 1) |
| P3.4 | T0 (Timer 0) |
| P3.5 | T1 (Timer 1) |
| P3.6 | **WR̄** (External Write) |
| P3.7 | **RD̄** (External Read) |
| Pin 30 | **ALE** (Address Latch Enable) |
| Pin 29 | **PSEN̄** (Program Store Enable) |
| Pin 31 | **EĀ** (External Access) |

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Address Bus. Explain its role in a microcontroller system. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

The **Address Bus** is a unidirectional group of signal lines used by the CPU of a microcontroller to transmit the physical memory location address of a memory register or an I/O device that needs to be accessed during a read or write operation.

**Role in a Microcontroller System:**

1. **Memory Selection:** It uniquely identifies one of the 2ⁿ memory locations, where n is the number of address lines.

2. **I/O Device Selection:** Through address decoding, individual peripheral devices can be selected using unique addresses.

3. **Read/Write Targeting:** It specifies whether the CPU intends to read from or write to the selected location.

4. **Unidirectional Nature:** Data flows only from CPU to memory/I/O — the address bus never carries data back to the CPU.

5. **For the 8051:** A 16-bit address bus allows access to **2¹⁶ = 65,536 bytes = 64 KB** of external memory. The lower byte (A0–A7) is multiplexed on Port 0, and the upper byte (A8–A15) is output on Port 2.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Pitfall:** Students often confuse the address bus with the data bus. The address bus *selects the location*; the data bus *carries the actual byte*. Marks are deducted when these two concepts are interchanged in definitions.

---

### Question 2: List the signals involved in external memory interfacing of 8051. What is the function of the ALE pin? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

**External Memory Interfacing Signals:**

| Signal | Pin | Function |
|---|---|---|
| A0 – A7 | Port 0 (multiplexed) | Lower-order address |
| A8 – A15 | Port 2 | Higher-order address |
| D0 – D7 | Port 0 (after demultiplex) | Bidirectional data |
| ALE | Pin 30 | Address Latch Enable |
| PSEN̄ | Pin 29 | Program Store Enable (for ROM) |
| RD̄ | P3.7 | Read signal (for RAM) |
| WR̄ | P3.6 | Write signal (for RAM) |
| EĀ | Pin 31 | External Access |

**Function of the ALE Pin:**

The **Address Latch Enable (ALE)** pin is used to *de-multiplex* the lower-order address and data on Port 0. When ALE is HIGH, Port 0 carries the **lower-order address (A0–A7)**. The external latch (74LS373) captures this address on the falling edge of ALE. When ALE goes LOW, Port 0 becomes the **8-bit bidirectional data bus (D0–D7)**. Thus, ALE acts as a *timing reference* that allows a single 8-bit port to serve two distinct functions sequentially in each memory cycle.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the three-bus architecture of the 8051 microcontroller. Clearly distinguish between the address bus, data bus, and control bus. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** The 8051 uses a 16-bit address bus. Calculate the maximum external memory that can be interfaced. If a designer wishes to interface 32 KB of ROM and 16 KB of RAM, show how the address lines are allocated. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

The 8051 microcontroller uses a **three-bus architecture** (also called the *system bus*) to communicate with external memory and I/O devices. The three buses are:

**1. Address Bus (16 lines, unidirectional):**
- Carries the location address from CPU to memory.
- Lower 8 bits (A0–A7) appear on Port 0 (multiplexed with data).
- Upper 8 bits (A8–A15) appear on Port 2.
- Total lines: **16**.
- Maximum addressable memory: 2¹⁶ = 64 KB.

**2. Data Bus (8 lines, bidirectional):**
- Carries the actual data byte between CPU and memory/I/O.
- Appears on Port 0 *after* ALE goes LOW.
- Total lines: **8**.
- Bidirectional because data flows both during READ (memory → CPU) and WRITE (CPU → memory).

**3. Control Bus (multiple lines):**
Coordinates and synchronizes memory access. Key control signals:
- **ALE (Pin 30):** Address Latch Enable — latches lower address from P0.
- **PSEN̄ (Pin 29):** Program Store Enable — activates external ROM.
- **RD̄ (P3.7):** Read signal for external RAM.
- **WR̄ (P3.6):** Write signal for external RAM.
- **EĀ (Pin 31):** External Access — selects internal or external code memory.

```
       ┌──────────────────────┐
       │     8051 CPU         │
       └──────────┬───────────┘
                  │
       ┌──────────┼──────────────┐
       │          │              │
   ┌───▼────┐ ┌──▼────┐ ┌──────▼──────┐
   │Address │ │ Data  │ │ Control Bus │
   │ Bus 16 │ │ Bus 8 │ │  (ALE PSEN  │
   │  bits  │ │ bits  │ │   RD WR EA) │
   └────────┘ └───────┘ └─────────────┘
```

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1: Maximum External Memory**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\max} &= 2^{16} = 65{,}536 \text{ bytes} = 64 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Address Line Allocation**

The total address space of 64 KB must be split between 32 KB ROM and 16 KB RAM.

- **32 KB ROM** requires 15 address lines (since 2¹⁵ = 32,768 = 32 KB).
- **16 KB RAM** requires 14 address lines (since 2¹⁴ = 16,384 = 16 KB).

**ROM Address Allocation (32 KB):**

- ROM occupies the lower half of the 64 KB space: `0000H – 7FFFH`.
- Address lines used: **A0 – A14** (15 lines).
- A15 is tied LOW for ROM access.

**RAM Address Allocation (16 KB):**

- RAM occupies a 16 KB block: `8000H – BFFFH`.
- Address lines used: **A0 – A13** (14 lines).
- A14, A15 are used for chip selection via a decoder.

**Address Decoding Summary:**

| Memory | Size | Address Range | Active Address Lines | Chip Select |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| ROM | 32 KB | 0000H – 7FFFH | A0 – A14 | A15 = 0, PSEN̄ |
| RAM | 16 KB | 8000H – BFFFH | A0 – A13 | A15 = 1, A14 = 0, RD̄/WR̄ |

A simple **2-to-4 line decoder (74LS139)** can be used with A15 and A14 as inputs to generate chip-select signals for both memory chips.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a)** Explain the concept of address bus demultiplexing in the 8051. Why is demultiplexing necessary, and which hardware component is used for this purpose? **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A system designer is using a microcontroller with a 20-bit address bus. Calculate the maximum addressable memory. If only 1 MB of external memory is to be interfaced, determine the number of *unused* address lines and design a simple chip-select logic using a 3-to-8 decoder. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a):

**Why Demultiplexing is Necessary:**

The 8051 has only **40 pins**. If 16 pins were dedicated to address and 8 pins to data (without sharing), only 16 pins would remain for the four I/O ports — severely limiting the microcontroller's usefulness. To overcome this, Intel designers used **multiplexing**: Port 0 serves dual purposes:
1. First half of memory cycle: outputs the **lower 8-bit address (A0–A7)**.
2. Second half of memory cycle: serves as the **8-bit data bus (D0–D7)**.

**The Need for Demultiplexing:**

Once the lower-order address is placed on Port 0, it must be *preserved* for the entire memory cycle — even after Port 0 transitions to data mode. Otherwise, the memory chip would lose its lower address bits and could not be correctly selected.

**Hardware Used for Demultiplexing:**

The standard hardware is the **74LS373** (or 74HC573) **octal transparent D-type latch**.

- The 8051's **ALE** signal is connected to the latch's *enable* input (G).
- The 8 lower address lines (A0–A7) on Port 0 are connected to the latch's D inputs.
- The latch's Q outputs (now stable A0–A7) are wired to the memory chip's lower address pins.

**Operation:**

1. When ALE = HIGH, the latch is *transparent* — Q outputs follow D inputs.
2. When ALE goes LOW, the latch *freezes* the last value on its Q outputs.
3. The memory chip now sees a stable 16-bit address (8 bits from the latch + 8 bits from Port 2) for the rest of the cycle.
4. Port 0 is free to act as the data bus.

#### Model Solution to (b):

**Step 1: Maximum Addressable Memory**

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\max} &= 2^{20} \text{ bytes} \\
&= 1{,}048{,}576 \text{ bytes} \\
&= 1 \text{ MB (since } 2^{20} = 1 \text{ MB)}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Address Lines Required for 1 MB**

A 1 MB memory chip requires **20 address lines** (A0 – A19) for byte-level addressing. Since the bus already provides 20 lines, all 20 lines are used to address the 1 MB chip. **No address lines are "unused"** in the strict sense — all 20 are connected to the memory.

**However**, if multiple 1 MB chips were to be interfaced, additional address bits would be required for *chip selection*. For example, with a 1 MB memory and a second 1 MB memory:
- A0 – A19: address the byte *within* a chip.
- A20 – A21: select *which* chip (using a decoder).

**Step 3: 3-to-8 Decoder Design for Two 1 MB Chips**

Using a **74LS138** (3-to-8 line decoder):
- Inputs: A21, A20, A19 (or any 3 higher-order lines for future expansion).
- Outputs: Y0̄, Y1̄, ..., Y7̄.

**Chip Select Logic:**

| Address Range | A21 | A20 | A19 | Active Decoder Output | Selected Chip |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 000000H – 0FFFFFH | 0 | 0 | 0 | Y0̄ | Chip 1 (ROM) |
| 100000H – 1FFFFFH | 0 | 0 | 1 | Y1̄ | Chip 2 (RAM) |
| 200000H – 2FFFFFH | 0 | 1 | 0 | Y2̄ | (Reserved) |
| ... | ... | ... | ... | ... | ... |

**Connection:**
- Y0̄ → CE1̄ (Chip Enable) of ROM chip.
- Y1̄ → CE1̄ of RAM chip.
- PSEN̄ is OR-ed with Y0̄ to enable ROM only during code fetches.
- RD̄/WR̄ is OR-ed with Y1̄ to enable RAM during data access.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Valuation Warning:** A common error in address decoding problems is *partial decoding*, where some high-order address lines are ignored. This causes **address mirroring** — the same memory location responds to multiple addresses. Examiners deduct up to 2 marks if students do not explicitly mention that *all* unused address lines must be tied to a defined logic level (HIGH or LOW) to avoid floating inputs.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### Rapid Revision Checklist

- ✅ The **address bus** is a *unidirectional* set of 16 lines in the 8051, used *only* to select memory or I/O locations.
- ✅ Address bus width **n** directly determines addressable memory: **M = 2ⁿ bytes**.
- ✅ For 8051: **n = 16 → M = 2¹⁶ = 65,536 bytes = 64 KB**.
- ✅ **Lower-order address (A0–A7)** is multiplexed on **Port 0**; **Higher-order address (A8–A15)** is on **Port 2**.
- ✅ **Demultiplexing** is performed by an external **74LS373 / 74HC573** latch, controlled by the **ALE** signal.
- ✅ The **ALE** pin goes HIGH during the address phase and LOW during the data phase of each memory cycle.
- ✅ The **control bus** comprises **ALE, PSEN̄, RD̄, WR̄, EĀ** — these coordinate memory access but are *not* part of the address bus.
- ✅ The **internal address bus** accesses on-chip RAM (128 bytes) and ROM (4 KB); the **external address bus** accesses off-chip memory.
- ✅ The 8051 uses a **modified Harvard architecture**: separate address buses for code (PSEN̄) and data (RD̄/WR̄), allowing 64 KB code + 64 KB data = **128 KB total** external memory.
- ✅ In address decoding, *all* unused high-order address lines must be tied to a defined logic level to prevent floating inputs and address mirroring.
- ✅ For any bus width **n**, the last addressable location is **2ⁿ − 1** (e.g., for 16-bit: `FFFFH` = 65,535).
- ✅ The address bus is the *postman's notebook*; the data bus is the *letter* — never confuse the two.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final KTU Exam Tip:** When answering bus-architecture questions, *always* draw a clean block diagram showing CPU → Address Bus → Memory, and CPU → Data Bus → Memory. Diagrams carry **2 to 3 easy marks** and visually anchor your written explanation. Use specific pin numbers (Pin 30 = ALE, Pin 29 = PSEN̄) wherever possible to demonstrate depth of understanding.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Data Bus
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET613 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Data Bus

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the architecture of a **Microcontroller** (specifically aligned with the 8051 family and ARM Cortex-M series studied in the OEAET613 syllabus), the **Data Bus** is defined as a bidirectional, parallel set of conducting wires (electrical pathways) that physically transport actual data (operands, opcodes, variables, and results) between the **Central Processing Unit (CPU)**, **Memory Units (ROM/RAM)**, and **Input/Output (I/O) peripherals**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> The **Data Bus** is fundamentally distinct from the **Address Bus** and **Control Bus**. While the *Address Bus* specifies *WHERE* data needs to go, the *Data Bus* specifies the actual payload being carried. The width of the data bus (e.g., **8-bit**, **16-bit**, or **32-bit**) directly dictates the **Data Bus Width ($D_w$)** parameter, which defines the architecture class of the microcontroller (e.g., 8-bit microcontroller = 8-line data bus).

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Multi-Lane Highway"

Imagine a major city connecting two massive industrial warehouses (CPU and Memory). 

- The **Address Bus** is like the *GPS navigation system* — it tells the trucks which specific dock door (memory location) they are heading to.
- The **Control Bus** is like the *traffic signal system* — it tells the trucks when to stop, go, read, or write.
- The **Data Bus** is the actual **multi-lane highway** itself, where the cargo trucks (data) physically travel. 

If the highway has **8 lanes**, exactly **8 bits** (1 byte) of cargo can pass through the toll booth per truck trip in a single clock cycle. A **32-lane highway** (32-bit data bus) can carry **4 trucks' worth of cargo** in the same time, drastically increasing throughput. However, building wider highways costs more (more silicon area, more pins on the IC package).

## 1.3 Standard Metrics and Physical Constants

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Constants for OEAET613:**
> - **8051 Microcontroller Data Bus Width:** **8 bits** (1 Byte)
> - **ARM Cortex-M0/M3 Data Bus Width:** **32 bits** (4 Bytes)
> - **Bus Direction:** **Bidirectional** (Data flows TO and FROM the CPU)
> - **Tri-State Logic:** The data bus uses **High-Impedance (Hi-Z)** states to allow multiple devices to share the bus without electrical conflict (bus contention).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Structural Breakdown of the Data Bus

The Data Bus is not a single magical wire; it is a structured subsystem with specific operational logic:

1. **Physical Lines ($L_d$):** The number of parallel conductive traces etched onto the silicon die and routed to the IC package pins. For an **8-bit** bus, $L_d = 8$.
2. **Drivers and Buffers:** Each line is connected to a **tri-state buffer**. These buffers have three logical states: Logic 0 (Ground, 0V), Logic 1 ($V_{CC}$, typically **+5V** for 8051 or **+3.3V** for ARM), and **High-Impedance (Hi-Z)**.
3. **Bus Arbitration Logic:** Because the data bus is shared between RAM, ROM, and I/O ports, only ONE device is allowed to drive the bus at any given microsecond. The **Control Bus** signals (like $\overline{RD}$ and $\overline{WR}$ in 8051) act as the arbiters.
4. **Bus Multiplexing (Advanced):** In cost-optimized microcontrollers, the data bus and the lower byte of the address bus are **multiplexed** onto the same physical pins (e.g., 8051's Port 0 acts as AD0-AD7). They share the wires, separated by time using the **ALE (Address Latch Enable)** signal. This reduces pin count from 40 to a manageable number.

## 2.2 The "Why" and "How" of Bus Operations

- **Why bidirectional?** Because the CPU must both *fetch* instructions/data (Read) and *store* results (Write). A unidirectional bus would require two separate physical buses, doubling pin counts.
- **How is contention avoided?** Through **Time-Division Multiplexing (TDM)** governed by the control bus. When the CPU is reading, the memory drives the bus; the CPU's output buffers are in Hi-Z. When the CPU is writing, the CPU drives the bus; the memory's buffers are in Hi-Z.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet & Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains every high-yield formula and parameter the KTU examiner can ask for regarding the Data Bus:

| Parameter | Symbol | Formula / Definition | Standard Unit | Real-World Engineering Utility |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Data Bus Width** | $D_w$ | Number of parallel data lines | **Bits** | Determines the architectural class (8-bit, 16-bit, 32-bit MCU) |
| **Data Throughput** | $T_p$ | $T_p = f_{clk} \times D_w$ | **Bits per second (bps)** | Calculates the maximum data transfer rate of the system |
| **Total Data per Cycle** | $D_{cyc}$ | $D_{cyc} = D_w / 8$ | **Bytes** | Amount of information moved in one clock cycle |
| **Memory Addressing Range** | $M_{range}$ | $M_{range} = 2^{A_w}$ | **Bytes** | Note: Depends on *Address* bus width $A_w$, not data bus |
| **Tri-State Voltage (High)** | $V_{OH}$ | Logic 1 Voltage Level | **Volts (V)** | $5\text{V}$ (TTL/8051) or $3.3\text{V}$ (CMOS/ARM) |
| **Tri-State Voltage (Low)** | $V_{OL}$ | Logic 0 Voltage Level | **Volts (V)** | $0\text{V}$ (Ground reference) |
| **Hi-Z State Current** | $I_{OZ}$ | Leakage current in Hi-Z | **Microamps ($\mu$A)** | Determines power consumption when bus is inactive |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industrial automation (e.g., a robotic arm controlled by an ARM Cortex-M4), the **32-bit data bus** allows the processor to read a 32-bit sensor reading (like a high-precision encoder or ADC value) in a *single* memory fetch operation. An older **8051 (8-bit)** would require **four separate fetch cycles** to reconstruct the same 32-bit integer, introducing latency and risking real-time deadline misses. The data bus width is a critical bottleneck in real-time embedded systems.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Calculating Maximum Data Throughput

**Problem Statement:** An 8051 microcontroller operates with a crystal oscillator frequency ($f_{osc}$) of **12 MHz**. The 8051 core uses 12 oscillator cycles to execute one machine cycle. The data bus width is 8 bits. Calculate the maximum data throughput during a memory read operation.

**Given Data:**
- $f_{osc} = 12 \text{ MHz} = 12 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}$
- Oscillator cycles per machine cycle ($N_{cyc}$) = 12
- Data Bus Width ($D_w$) = 8 bits

### Step-by-Step Logical Evaluation:

**Step 1: Calculate the Machine Cycle Frequency ($f_{mc}$)**
The machine cycle frequency is the inverse of the time taken for one machine cycle.

$$
f_{mc} = \frac{f_{osc}}{N_{cyc}}
$$

Substituting the known values:

$$
f_{mc} = \frac{12 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz}}{12}
$$

$$
f_{mc} = 1 \times 10^6 \text{ Hz} = 1 \text{ MHz}
$$

*Explanation:* This means the CPU can perform **1 million** machine cycles every second. In the 8051 architecture, a `MOVX A, @DPTR` (external memory read) instruction takes exactly 2 machine cycles to transfer 1 byte of data across the data bus.

**Step 2: Calculate Data Transfers Per Second ($R_{trans}$)**
Since each external data read takes 2 machine cycles, the transfer rate is:

$$
R_{trans} = \frac{f_{mc}}{2}
$$

$$
R_{trans} = \frac{1 \times 10^6}{2} = 0.5 \times 10^6 \text{ transfers/second}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Maximum Data Throughput ($T_p$)**
Each transfer moves $D_w$ = 8 bits.

$$
T_p = R_{trans} \times D_w
$$

$$
T_p = (0.5 \times 10^6) \times 8 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
T_p = 4 \times 10^6 \text{ bps} = 4 \text{ Mbps}
$$

*Result:* The data bus can theoretically move **4 Megabits per second**.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Cycle Count Comparison (8-bit vs 32-bit)

**Problem Statement:** Compare the number of machine cycles required to read a 32-bit integer from memory on (A) an 8051 (8-bit data bus) and (B) an ARM Cortex-M3 (32-bit data bus).

**Step 1: 8051 (8-bit Data Bus)**
A 32-bit integer requires 4 bytes of data. Each byte requires 1 memory read operation.

$$
\text{Total Cycles}_{8051} = 4 \text{ bytes} \times 1 \text{ cycle/byte} = 4 \text{ cycles}
$$

**Step 2: ARM Cortex-M3 (32-bit Data Bus)**
A 32-bit integer fits perfectly into the 32-bit data bus in a single transfer.

$$
\text{Total Cycles}_{ARM} = 1 \text{ byte-block} \times 1 \text{ cycle/block} = 1 \text{ cycle}
$$

**Step 3: Performance Ratio**

$$
\text{Speedup Factor} = \frac{4 \text{ cycles}}{1 \text{ cycle}} = 4\times
$$

*Conclusion:* The 32-bit data bus provides a **4x** raw throughput advantage for aligned 32-bit data words, which is the primary reason modern IoT and automotive microcontrollers migrated from 8-bit to 32-bit architectures.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Bus Operations Truth Table

| CPU Action | $\overline{RD}$ (Read) | $\overline{WR}$ (Write) | CPU Buffers | Memory Buffers | Data Bus State |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Idle (No Op)** | 1 | 1 | Hi-Z | Hi-Z | High-Impedance |
| **Memory Read** | 0 | 1 | Hi-Z (Receiving) | Driving (Output) | Memory $\rightarrow$ CPU |
| **Memory Write** | 1 | 0 | Driving (Output) | Hi-Z (Receiving) | CPU $\rightarrow$ Memory |
| **Port Input** | 0 | 1 | Hi-Z (Receiving) | N/A (Port is source) | Port $\rightarrow$ CPU |

*Note: Logic '0' = Active (Voltage Low), Logic '1' = Inactive (Voltage High). This is standard 8051 active-low control bus signaling.*

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Data Bus Architecture in a Microcontroller

The following block diagram maps the functional architecture of an 8-bit data bus system (like the 8051), showing how the data bus acts as the central nervous system connecting all major peripherals.

```mermaid
graph TD
    CPU_CORE[CPU Core Accumulator B Reg PSW] -->|Drives / Receives| DATA_BUS_HUB
    DATA_BUS_HUB{{8-BIT DATA BUS D0 to D7}}
    RAM_INT[Internal RAM 128 Bytes] -->|Bi-directional Data| DATA_BUS_HUB
    ROM_INT[Internal ROM 4 KB Program] -->|Bi-directional Data| DATA_BUS_HUB
    SFR_BANK[SFR Bank P0 P1 P2 P3 TCON SCON] -->|Bi-directional Data| DATA_BUS_HUB
    EXT_MEM[External Memory Interface] -->|Bi-directional Data| DATA_BUS_HUB
    
    ADDR_BUS[/16-BIT ADDRESS BUS A0 to A15/] -.->|Selects Destination| RAM_INT
    ADDR_BUS -.->|Selects Destination| ROM_INT
    ADDR_BUS -.->|Selects Destination| SFR_BANK
    ADDR_BUS -.->|Selects Destination| EXT_MEM
    
    CTRL_BUS[\Control Bus RD WR ALE PSEN/] -.->|Enables Output| RAM_INT
    CTRL_BUS -.->|Enables Output| ROM_INT
    CTRL_BUS -.->|Enables Output| SFR_BANK
    CTRL_BUS -.->|Enables Output| EXT_MEM
    CTRL_BUS -.->|Arbitrates Direction| DATA_BUS_HUB
```

## 4.2 Diagram Interpretation Guide

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Schematic:**
> 1. **DATA_BUS_HUB** (the center node) represents the 8 physical parallel copper traces. Every solid arrow represents bidirectional data flow.
> 2. **ADDR_BUS** (dotted arrows) is shown as a separate entity to emphasize that *addresses* and *data* travel on physically different wires, except in multiplexed modes.
> 3. **CTRL_BUS** (dotted arrows) physically touches the data bus hub to control the *direction* of flow. The $\overline{RD}$ signal enables memory output drivers; the $\overline{WR}$ signal enables the CPU output drivers.
> 4. In the **8051**, Port 0 serves as the multiplexed lower address/data bus (AD0-AD7), and Port 2 serves as the upper address bus (A8-A15). The **ALE** pin latches the address from the multiplexed lines during the first half of the machine cycle.

## 4.3 Block-Level Flow: A Single `MOV A, 30h` Instruction

```mermaid
graph LR
    step1[Step 1: CPU places address 30h on Address Bus] --> step2[Step 2: Control Bus asserts RD signal]
    step2 --> step3[Step 3: Internal RAM decodes address 30h]
    step3 --> step4[Step 4: RAM cell 30h places its 8-bit value on Data Bus]
    step4 --> step5[Step 5: CPU Accumulator latches the value from Data Bus]
    step5 --> step6[Step 6: Control Bus de-asserts RD signal Bus returns to Hi-Z]
```

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define Data Bus. How is it different from Address Bus? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
The **Data Bus** is a set of bidirectional parallel wires used to carry the actual data (operands, instructions, I/O values) between the CPU, memory, and peripherals of a microcontroller. The width of the data bus defines the architecture (e.g., 8-bit, 32-bit). 

**Distinction:**
- The **Data Bus** is *bidirectional* and carries the *actual payload* (data values).
- The **Address Bus** is *unidirectional* (CPU to memory) and carries the *location* (memory address) where the data resides. The data bus width does not determine memory size; the address bus width does ($2^{A_w}$).

*(3 marks: Definition 1m, Bidirectional property 1m, Difference from Address Bus 1m)*

### Question 2: What is the significance of the Tri-State (High-Impedance) condition in a data bus? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
The **Tri-State (Hi-Z)** condition is critical because the data bus is a *shared resource* connected to multiple devices (RAM, ROM, I/O ports). Only one device can drive the bus at a time. The Hi-Z state electrically **disconnects** a device from the bus, preventing **bus contention** (short circuits between a device outputting Logic 1 and another outputting Logic 0). It is controlled by the Control Bus signals ($\overline{RD}$, $\overline{WR}$).

*(3 marks: Shared resource concept 1m, Prevents contention 1m, Controlled by Control Bus 1m)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)

**`(a)` Explain the bus structure of the 8051 microcontroller with a neat block diagram. Discuss the role of Port 0 and Port 2 in address/data demultiplexing. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram:**
*(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1)*

**Explanation of Bus Structure:**
The 8051 has three primary buses:
1. **Data Bus (8-bit):** Carries actual data bidirectionally.
2. **Address Bus (16-bit):** Unidirectional, carries memory addresses. Capable of addressing $2^{16} = 65,536$ locations (64 KB).
3. **Control Bus:** Consists of signals like $\overline{RD}$ (P3.7), $\overline{WR}$ (P3.6), **ALE** (P3.5), and $\overline{PSEN}$ (Program Store Enable).

**Role of Port 0 and Port 2 in Demultiplexing:**
- **Port 0 (P0.0 - P0.7):** Acts as the **multiplexed Address/Data Bus (AD0-AD7)**. During the first half of the machine cycle, it carries the **lower 8 bits of the address (A0-A7)**. During the second half, it carries the **8-bit data**. The separation is achieved temporally using the **ALE (Address Latch Enable)** signal, which instructs an external latch (e.g., 74HC373) to capture and hold the address.
- **Port 2 (P2.0 - P2.7):** Acts as the **upper Address Bus (A8-A15)**. It carries the remaining 8 bits of the address, which are not multiplexed, so they remain stable for the entire machine cycle.

**`(b)` Calculate the maximum data throughput of an 8051 system running at 11.0592 MHz when transferring data from external RAM using the `MOVX` instruction. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`**

**Model Solution:**

**Given Data:**
- $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$
- 8051 Machine Cycle = 12 oscillator cycles
- `MOVX` instruction = 2 machine cycles per byte transfer
- $D_w = 8 \text{ bits}$

**Step 1: Calculate Machine Cycle Frequency**
$$
f_{mc} = \frac{f_{osc}}{12} = \frac{11.0592 \text{ MHz}}{12} = 0.9216 \text{ MHz}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate Data Transfer Rate**
Since `MOVX` takes 2 machine cycles:
$$
R_{trans} = \frac{f_{mc}}{2} = \frac{0.9216 \text{ MHz}}{2} = 0.4608 \text{ MBaud}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate Throughput in Bits per Second**
$$
T_p = R_{trans} \times D_w = 0.4608 \times 10^6 \times 8 \text{ bits}
$$

$$
T_p = 3.6864 \times 10^6 \text{ bps} \approx 3.686 \text{ Mbps}
$$

*(7 marks: Step 1 = 2m, Step 2 = 2m, Step 3 = 2m, Final Answer with units = 1m)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**`(a)` With the help of a timing diagram, explain the function of the ALE and $\overline{PSEN}$ signals in fetching an opcode from external ROM in 8051. (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`**

**Model Solution:**

**Timing Diagram Explanation:**

| Signal | T1 (First Half Cycle) | T2 (Second Half Cycle) |
| :--- | :---: | :---: |
| **ALE** | **HIGH** (Pulse) | LOW |
| **Port 0** | Lower Address (A0-A7) | Opcode Data (D0-D7) |
| **Port 2** | Upper Address (A8-A15) | Upper Address (A8-A15) |
| **$\overline{PSEN}$** | HIGH (Inactive) | **LOW** (Active Pulse) |

**Step-by-Step Function:**
1. During **State T1** of the machine cycle, the CPU places the 16-bit program counter address on Port 0 (lower 8 bits) and Port 2 (upper 8 bits).
2. **ALE** generates a high pulse, signaling the external latch (74LS373) to capture the lower address from Port 0. After the latch, Port 0 is freed for data transfer.
3. During **State T2**, the external ROM places the 8-bit opcode on Port 0. Simultaneously, the **$\overline{PSEN}$ (Program Store Enable)** signal goes LOW, acting as the read enable for the external program memory chip.
4. The CPU latches the opcode from the data bus into the Instruction Register. The cycle then repeats for the next byte.

**`(b)` A microcontroller has a 16-bit data bus and a 24-bit address bus. (i) What is the maximum memory it can address? (ii) How many bytes can it transfer in one clock cycle? (7 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) Maximum Memory Addressing Capacity:**
Memory capacity is determined by the **address bus width**, not the data bus width.
$$
M_{range} = 2^{A_w}
$$

$$
M_{range} = 2^{24}
$$

$$
M_{range} = 16,777,216 \text{ bytes} = 16 \text{ MB}
$$

**(ii) Bytes Transferred per Cycle:**
The data bus width is 16 bits.
$$
D_{cyc} = \frac{D_w}{8} = \frac{16 \text{ bits}}{8 \text{ bits/byte}} = 2 \text{ Bytes/cycle}
$$

*(7 marks: Part i = 4 marks with formula and final answer, Part ii = 3 marks with conversion logic)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
