# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

> [!NOTE]
> The questions below are modeled on the *KTU 2024 Scheme End-Semester Examination* (ESE) pattern. Marks are aligned with the **3-mark Part A** and **14-mark Part B** weightages, with an internal-choice pattern that is standard across KTU engineering courses.

---

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define the Program Status Word (PSW) of the 8051 microcontroller and list all its flag bits. [`KTU University Exam — May 2024`]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Program Status Word (PSW)** is an **8-bit, bit-addressable Special Function Register (SFR)** of the 8051 located at the internal RAM address **D0H**. It functions as the *status flag register* of the ALU and contains condition codes that reflect the result of the most recent arithmetic or logical operation, plus control bits for register-bank selection.

The 8 bits of the PSW are:

| Bit | Name | Bit Address | Function |
|---|---|---|---|
| 7 | CY | D7H | Carry flag |
| 6 | AC | D6H | Auxiliary carry flag |
| 5 | F0 | D5H | User-defined flag |
| 4 | RS1 | D4H | Register bank select 1 |
| 3 | RS0 | D3H | Register bank select 0 |
| 2 | OV | D2H | Overflow flag |
| 1 | — | D1H | Reserved |
| 0 | P  | D0H | Parity flag |

---

### Question 2: Explain the function of the Parity (P) flag in the 8051 with an example. [`KTU University Exam — Dec 2023`]

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Parity flag (P)** occupies bit 0 of the PSW (bit address D0H). It is *automatically* set or cleared by the CPU at the end of every instruction cycle to indicate the **parity of the Accumulator's content**:

- **P = 1** if the Accumulator contains an **odd** number of 1-bits.
- **P = 0** if the Accumulator contains an **even** number of 1-bits.

**Example:** Suppose the Accumulator holds **A5H = 1010 0101₂**. This value contains four 1-bits (an even count), so the hardware sets **P = 0**.

The P flag is widely used in **serial communication (UART parity checking)** and **memory-data-integrity verification**.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) [`KTU University Exam — May 2024`]

**Draw the block diagram of the 8051 microcontroller and explain the function of each block. Also explain the Program Status Word (PSW) register in detail.**

#### Part (a) — Block Diagram of 8051 (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

The 8051 is an 8-bit, single-chip microcontroller based on the **Harvard architecture**. Its block diagram consists of the following interconnected functional units:

1. **Central Processing Unit (CPU):** Contains the 8-bit ALU, Accumulator (A, SFR at E0H), B register (F0H), Stack Pointer (81H), Program Counter (16-bit), and Data Pointer (DPTR, 16-bit = DPH 83H + DPL 82H). The CPU executes all arithmetic, logical, and control operations.

2. **Oscillator and Clock Circuit:** A quartz crystal (typically **11.0592 MHz**) connected between **XTAL1 (pin 19)** and **XTAL2 (pin 18)** generates the master clock. The internal clock generator divides this by 12 to produce the **machine cycle clock**.

3. **Program Memory (ROM):** On-chip **4 KB of mask ROM** (or EPROM/Flash in modern variants) occupies addresses **0000H–0FFFH**. The PC can address up to **64 KB** externally. The program is non-volatile.

4. **Data Memory (RAM):** On-chip **128 bytes** of SRAM occupy addresses **00H–7FH**. The lower 32 bytes (00H–1FH) form the four register banks; the next 16 bytes (20H–2FH) are bit-addressable; the upper 80 bytes (30H–7FH) are general-purpose scratchpad.

5. **Special Function Registers (SFRs):** 21 actively used SFRs occupy addresses **80H–FFH** (e.g., A, B, PSW, SP, DPTR, P0–P3, TCON, TMOD, TH0, TL0, TH1, TL1, SCON, SBUF, IE, IP, PCON).

6. **I/O Ports:** Four 8-bit bidirectional ports — **P0 (80H)**, **P1 (90H)**, **P2 (A0H)**, **P3 (B0H)** — provide 32 I/O lines. P0 and P2 serve as the multiplexed address/data bus during external-memory access, and P3 pins have alternate functions (RXD, TXD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD).

7. **Timers/Counters:** **Two 16-bit timers (T0 and T1)** in the **T0 (8AH/8BH) and T1 (8CH/8DH)** SFRs can operate in four modes (Mode 0, 1, 2, 3) for timing, event counting, or baud-rate generation.

8. **Serial Port (UART):** A full-duplex UART controlled by **SCON (98H)** and **SBUF (99H)** provides asynchronous and synchronous serial communication, supporting Mode 1 (8-bit UART) and the standard 9600 baud rate.

9. **Interrupt Subsystem:** Five interrupt sources — **External 0, External 1, Timer 0 overflow, Timer 1 overflow, Serial port** — are managed by **TCON (88H), IE (A8H), and IP (B8H)**, with two programmable priority levels.

10. **Reset Circuit:** A high pulse on the **RST pin (pin 9)** for at least **2 machine cycles** resets the device: PC ← 0000H, SP ← 07H, all ports ← FFH, and the SFRs to their default values.

*(Examiner may award 7 marks based on the accuracy and completeness of the block diagram and explanations.)*

#### Part (b) — Program Status Word (PSW) Detailed Explanation (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

The **PSW** is an 8-bit register at SFR address **D0H** and is the *flag/control* register of the 8051 ALU. It is **bit-addressable**, meaning each bit can be individually accessed.

| Bit | Symbol | Bit Address | Function | Set / Cleared By |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 7 | CY | D7H | **Carry flag** — set on unsigned overflow | Hardware (ADD, SUBB, MUL, DIV, RLC, RRC) or software |
| 6 | AC | D6H | **Auxiliary carry** — carry from bit 3 → bit 4 | Hardware (used by DAA) |
| 5 | F0 | D5H | **User flag 0** — general purpose | Software (e.g., `SETB PSW.5`) |
| 4 | RS1 | D4H | **Register bank select bit 1** | Software |
| 3 | RS0 | D3H | **Register bank select bit 0** | Software |
| 2 | OV | D2H | **Overflow flag** — signed overflow | Hardware (ADD, SUBB, MUL, DIV) |
| 1 | — | D1H | **Reserved** | — |
| 0 | P  | D0H | **Parity flag** — even/odd 1s in A | Hardware (every cycle) |

**Register Bank Selection:** The 2-bit field **RS1:RS0** selects one of four register banks in internal RAM (00H–1FH):

| RS1 | RS0 | Bank | Address |
|---|---|---|---|
| 0 | 0 | 0 | 00H–07H (default after reset) |
| 0 | 1 | 1 | 08H–0FH |
| 1 | 0 | 2 | 10H–17H |
| 1 | 1 | 3 | 18H–1FH |

**Importance and Applications:**
- The **CY flag** is used by conditional jumps (`JC`, `JNC`) and rotation instructions.
- The **AC flag** is critical for BCD arithmetic (used with DAA).
- The **OV flag** is used in signed-arithmetic routines and is checked after `MUL AB` and `DIV AB`.
- The **P flag** enables automatic parity generation in serial communication.
- The **RS1:RS0 bits** allow fast context switching in interrupt-driven programs, saving the registers of one task and loading another's by changing just two bits.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice* [`KTU University Exam — Dec 2023`]

**Explain the architecture of the 8051 microcontroller with a neat block diagram. Discuss the functions of the Program Status Word (PSW) and the role of the RS1:RS0 bits in register bank selection.**

#### Part (a) — Architecture and Block Diagram (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

*(Same core content as Question A, Part (a) above. The student is expected to redraw the block diagram and provide 4–6 line descriptions of each block: CPU, ROM, RAM, SFRs, I/O ports, timers, serial port, interrupt controller, oscillator, reset.)*

A concise diagrammatic representation is reproduced below in textual form:

```
            +--------------------------------------------------+
            |                  8051 MICROCONTROLLER            |
            |                                                  |
OSC  -----> |  [CPU: ALU | ACC | B | SP | PC | DPTR]           |
            |       \___________________  |                    |
            |                           \ |  +-------------+   |
ROM  <----- |  [PROGRAM MEMORY 4KB] <----->|INSTRUCTION  |   |
            |                               |DECODER &    |   |
            |                               |CONTROL UNIT |   |
RAM  <----> |  [DATA RAM 128 BYTES] <----->|             |   |
            |  [SFRs 128 BYTES]            +-------------+   |
            |       |       |       |             |          |
            |     P0  P1   P2  P3  T0/T1  UART  INTR --------+
            +--------------------------------------------------+
                              |    |    |
                            PORTs  TMR  UART
```

**Architectural Highlights:**
- The 8051 is a **Harvard-architecture** device with separate program and data buses.
- The CPU has a **byte-oriented ALU** capable of bit, nibble, and byte operations.
- **Boolean processing engine** allows direct bit set/clear/test/and/or operations.
- **On-chip peripheral integration** reduces external component count.

#### Part (b) — PSW and Register Bank Selection (7 marks)

**Model Answer:**

*(Same PSW content as Question A, Part (b) above, with the following additional elaboration on RS1:RS0:)*

The **RS1:RS0** field occupies bits 4 and 3 of the PSW. Its primary role is **register-bank selection** for the instructions that reference R0–R7. Each bank holds 8 bytes:

$$\text{Base Address} = 8 \times (2 \times \text{RS1} + \text{RS0})$$

**Practical Example (Context Switching):** In an interrupt-driven application, suppose the *main program* uses **Bank 0** (RS1:RS0 = 00). When an *interrupt* fires, the ISR can switch to **Bank 1** by setting RS0 = 1:

```assembly
ISR_ENTRY:
    SETB PSW.3      ; RS0 = 1, switch to Bank 1
    ; ... ISR body uses R0-R7 of Bank 1 ...
    CLR PSW.3        ; RS0 = 0, restore Bank 0 for main program
    RETI
```

This avoids stack-based register saving, achieving a **2-cycle context switch** (one to set the bit, one to clear it) — a major advantage in real-time embedded systems.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfalls — Where Students Lose Marks**
> 1. **Forgetting the PSW's SFR address (D0H).** Examiners award partial credit only if the address is mentioned.
> 2. **Confusing CY with OV.** CY tracks *unsigned* overflow; OV tracks *signed* overflow. A common error is stating that CY is set for "any overflow."
> 3. **Stating that the P flag is set by software.** In reality, the P flag is *always set by hardware* after every instruction cycle affecting the Accumulator — students who say "P can be set/cleared by `SETB PSW.0`" confuse the bit-addressable property with the *automatic update mechanism*.
> 4. **Wrong register bank base address.** Students sometimes list Bank 2 as starting at 20H instead of 10H. Use the formula: $8 \times 2 = 16 = 10\text{H}$.
> 5. **Ignoring the Auxiliary Carry flag's role in DAA.** Examiners specifically look for the phrase "AC is used by the DAA instruction for BCD correction."
> 6. **Failing to mention the machine cycle derivation** when asked about the clock. Memorize: $T_{\text{mc}} = 12 / f_{\text{osc}}$.
> 7. **Conflating external and internal memory sizes.** On-chip: 4 KB ROM + 128 B RAM. External expandability: 64 KB ROM + 64 KB RAM.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Block Diagram of 8051 & PSW**

- **8051 = Harvard architecture, 8-bit, single-chip CMOS microcontroller** introduced by Intel in 1980.
- **On-chip resources (must memorize):** 4 KB ROM, 128 B RAM, 4 × 8-bit I/O ports, 2 × 16-bit timers, 1 UART, 5 interrupts, 11.0592 MHz typical crystal.
- **Memory map:** Code memory up to 64 KB (0000H–FFFFH); internal RAM 00H–7FH; SFRs 80H–FFH.
- **Key SFR addresses:** A = E0H, B = F0H, SP = 81H, DPL = 82H, DPH = 83H, PSW = D0H, P0 = 80H, P1 = 90H, P2 = A0H, P3 = B0H.
- **PSW is bit-addressable at D0H**; its 8 bits are (from MSB to LSB): **CY, AC, F0, RS1, RS0, OV, -, P**.
- **CY (D7H):** Carry flag — unsigned overflow. Used by `JC`, `JNC`, `RLC A`, `RRC A`.
- **AC (D6H):** Auxiliary carry — carry from bit 3 to bit 4. Used by `DAA` for BCD correction.
- **F0 (D5H):** User-programmable software flag.
- **RS1:RS0 (D4H, D3H):** Register bank select — 4 banks at 00H–07H, 08H–0FH, 10H–17H, 18H–1FH. Formula: $\text{Base} = 8 \times (2 \cdot \text{RS1} + \text{RS0})$.
- **OV (D2H):** Signed overflow flag. Set by ADD, SUBB, MUL, DIV.
- **Bit 1 (D1H):** Reserved — undefined on classic 8051.
- **P (D0H):** Parity flag — auto-set every cycle. P = 1 if Accumulator has odd number of 1s; P = 0 if even.
- **Default after reset:** RS1:RS0 = 00 → Bank 0 active. PC = 0000H. SP = 07H. All ports = FFH.
- **Machine cycle:** $T_{\text{mc}} = 12 / f_{\text{osc}}$. At 11.0592 MHz, $T_{\text{mc}} \approx 1.085 \, \mu\text{s}$.
- **Register bank switching trick:** Use `SETB PSW.3` / `CLR PSW.3` for ultra-fast context switch in ISRs.
- **Real-world uses of PSW:** UART parity (P flag), BCD arithmetic (AC + DAA), multi-precision addition (CY), bank switching in RTOS context save (RS1:RS0), signed PID control (OV).

> [!TIP]
> **One-Line Memory Mnemonic for PSW**
> *"**C**hinese **A**pples **F**all **R**eally **S**oftly, **O**ver **P**ebbles"* → **C**Y, **A**C, **F**0, **R**S1, **R**S0 (S0), **O**V, [reserved], **P** (with "S" read as S0 = RS0).

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Accumulator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEAET613 - MICROCONTROLLERS > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Accumulator # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) The **Accumulator** is a dedicated, special-function, **8-bit general-purpose register** inside the Central Processing Unit (CPU) of the **8051 Microcontroller**. It is typically labelled as **`A`** or **`ACC`** (SFR address **`E0H`** in the Special Function Register memory map). In the Intel 8051 architecture, the Accumulator is the **only register that participates directly as an implicit operand in the majority of Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU) instructions**. Almost every data manipulation, arithmetic, logical, and branch decision operation routes one of its source operands *through* the Accumulator before producing a result. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):** > The Accumulator is introduced as a *core CPU register* alongside the Program Counter (PC), Stack Pointer (SP), Data Pointer (DPTR), B-register, and Program Status Word (PSW). Examiners frequently test (a) its SFR address, (b) its bit-addressable nature, and (c) its role as an implicit operand. ### Key Properties of the Accumulator | Property | Value | |---|---| | Size | 8 bits (bits D0 – D7) | | SFR Address | `0xE0` (Hex) | | Bit-Addressable | Yes (bits `E0H` to `E7H` — individually addressable) | | Reset Value | `0x00H` | | Implicit in | Most ALU operations | | Part of PSW coupling | Stores Carry, Auxiliary Carry, Parity flag outputs | --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — Plain English Intuition Think of the Accumulator as the **"whiteboard of the CPU"** — a small working scratchpad the ALU constantly scribbles on. Imagine a clerk in a small post office. The clerk receives letters (data), does some mental math, signs a few forms, and hands the result back. The desk in front of the clerk is the **Accumulator**: - Every letter (operand) is placed on the desk first. - The clerk (ALU) reads from the desk, processes, and writes the answer back on the desk. - If the result is too big for the desk, the clerk raises a red flag (Carry flag). You never send a letter *directly* to the clerk's brain — it must first sit on the desk. Similarly, in 8051 assembly, most operations require the operand to be loaded into the Accumulator first. > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Summary:** The Accumulator is the *default destination* and *default source* of the ALU. It is the only register the ALU talks to "natively" without needing an explicit register address in the opcode. --- ## 1.3 Why Was the Accumulator Architecture Created? Early microprocessors (and the 8051, derived from Intel 8048) used an **Accumulator-Based Architecture (ABA)**, also called a *single-accumulator von Neumann style*. The motivation was hardware economy: - Encoding the opcode with an *implicit* register reference saves instruction bits. - This made programs denser and fit in the tiny on-chip ROM of early microcontrollers. - The trade-off is fewer orthogonal instructions (compare with later RISC/Load-Store architectures like ARM, where any register can be a source/destination). > [!TIP] > **One-line Examiner Answer:** *"The 8051 uses an accumulator-based architecture to minimize opcode size and hardware complexity, making the Accumulator (A) the implicit operand for most ALU instructions."* # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Internal Placement of the Accumulator in the 8051 CPU The 8051 is built around the **MCS-51** core. Inside the CPU, the Accumulator is wired directly to one of the two input ports of the ALU. The other input comes from a **temporary register (TMP)** which holds the explicit operand (e.g., the value in `R0` or the direct memory byte). ``` +-------------------+ OP1 --->| | | ALU |----> Result OP2 --->| | +---------+---------+ | v Accumulator (A) ``` - **OP1 (Implicit):** Always the current contents of the Accumulator. - **OP2 (Explicit):** Comes from internal RAM, SFR, immediate data, or another register. - **Result:** Written *back* into the Accumulator (or sometimes to a memory location, depending on the instruction). --- ## 2.2 Why the Accumulator is the "Most-Used" Register Let us classify why, structurally, the Accumulator is privileged: 1. **Implicit operand in arithmetic/logic:** `ADD A, R0`, `ANL A, #data`, `CPL A`, `INC A` — `A` does not need to be encoded in the opcode. 2. **Default destination for MOV/MOVX/MOVC from program memory:** `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` loads *into* A. 3. **Condition testing point:** Conditional jumps like `JZ rel`, `JNZ rel`, and the **bit-test instructions** like `JB`, `JNB` often test bits *inside* the Accumulator (`E0H`–`E7H`). 4. **Parity flag source:** PSW.0 (P-flag) is automatically set based on the number of 1's in the Accumulator. 5. **Multiplication/Division host:** `MUL AB` and `DIV AB` use A as the multiplicand/dividend; the B-register acts as the second operand. 6. **Boolean and rotate operations:** `RL A`, `RR A`, `RLC A`, `RRC A` operate exclusively on the Accumulator. --- ## 2.3 The PSW (Program Status Word) Coupling The Accumulator does not exist in isolation. Its operation directly drives several bits of the **PSW register (SFR `0xD0`)**. | PSW Bit | Name | Driven By | Meaning | |---|---|---|---| | PSW.7 | **CY** (Carry) | ADD/SUBB result bit 7 carry-out | 1 = unsigned overflow | | PSW.6 | **AC** (Auxiliary Carry) | BCD add carry between bit 3 and 4 | 1 = BCD correction needed | | PSW.2 | **OV** (Overflow) | Signed 2's-complement overflow | 1 = result cannot be represented in signed 8-bit | | PSW.0 | **P** (Parity) | Number of 1s in Accumulator | 1 = odd parity (even number of 1s) | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU-Favourite Trick Question:** *"After executing `MOV A, #0x55`, what is the state of the P flag?"* > **Answer:** `0x55 = 0101 0101` contains **four** 1's (even), so P = **0** (the 8051 sets P = 1 only for *odd* count, i.e., it stores *even parity*). --- ## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet (8051 ALU Operations) The following table summarises the operation of the Accumulator for the most-tested 8051 instructions. All arithmetic is **modulo 256 (0–255)** unless otherwise noted. | Instruction | Operation | Formula | Flags Affected | |---|---|---|---| | `ADD A, src` | Unsigned add | $\text{A} \leftarrow (\text{A} + \text{src}) \bmod 256$ | CY, AC, OV, P | | `ADDC A, src` | Add with carry | $\text{A} \leftarrow (\text{A} + \text{src} + \text{CY}) \bmod 256$ | CY, AC, OV, P | | `SUBB A, src` | Subtract with borrow | $\text{A} \leftarrow (\text{A} - \text{src} - \text{CY})$ | CY, AC, OV, P | | `INC A` | Increment | $\text{A} \leftarrow (\text{A} + 1) \bmod 256$ | P only | | `DEC A` | Decrement | $\text{A} \leftarrow (\text{A} - 1) \bmod 256$ | P only | | `MUL AB` | Multiply (A×B) | $\text{B} \leftarrow \lfloor \tfrac{\text{A}\cdot\text{B}}{256} \rfloor,\ \text{A} \leftarrow (\text{A}\cdot\text{B}) \bmod 256$ | CY = 0, OV = 1 if B≠0 | | `DIV AB` | Divide (A/B) | $\text{A} \leftarrow \tfrac{\text{A}}{\text{B}} \text{ (quotient)},\ \text{B} \leftarrow \text{A} \bmod \text{B}$ (remainder) | CY = 0, OV = 1 if B=0 | | `ANL A, src` | Bitwise AND | $\text{A} \leftarrow \text{A} \wedge \text{src}$ | P only | | `ORL A, src` | Bitwise OR | $\text{A} \leftarrow \text{A} \vee \text{src}$ | P only | | `XRL A, src` | Bitwise XOR | $\text{A} \leftarrow \text{A} \oplus \text{src}$ | P only | | `CPL A` | Bitwise NOT | $\text{A} \leftarrow \overline{\text{A}}$ | P only | | `RL A` | Rotate left (8-bit) | $\text{A}_i \leftarrow \text{A}_{i-1} \text{ mod 8}$ | P only | | `RLC A` | Rotate left through CY | bit shift + CY | CY, P | | `CLR A` | Clear | $\text{A} \leftarrow 0$ | P only | | `SWAP A` | Swap nibbles | Upper/lower 4-bits exchange | P only | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** The Accumulator-based ALU design is foundational in DSP, embedded control (washing machines, elevators, automotive ECUs), and legacy industrial automation. Modern ARM Cortex-M chips evolved to a *Load-Store* RISC architecture, but the conceptual lessons (implicit operands, PSW coupling) remain exam-relevant. --- ## 2.5 The "Why" Behind the 8051's Heavy Reliance on A The instruction encoding of the 8051 uses **8-bit opcodes**. To specify two explicit register operands plus an opcode leaves almost no room. By *implicitly* making one operand the Accumulator, Intel engineers: - Saved **2 to 3 bits per instruction** (no need to encode the destination register). - Allowed instructions like `ADD A, R0` to fit in a single byte (`0x28`–`0x2F`). - Reduced silicon area and power consumption — critical for the 1980s embedded market. This is the *historical and engineering reason* the Accumulator sits at the heart of every 8051 instruction. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix Because the Accumulator is a *register*, not an *equation*, the "derivations" here are worked numerical examples showing how the ALU manipulates the Accumulator and how flags evolve. **Every bit is shown explicitly** for exam-style working. --- ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — ADD Instruction and Flag Generation **Problem:** Before execution, `A = 0xA3` and the Carry flag `CY = 1`. Execute `ADDC A, #0x5C` and determine the final state of `A`, `CY`, `AC`, `OV`, and `P`. ### Step 1: Convert hex to binary $$\text{A} = 0xA3 = 1010\,0011_2$$ $$0x5C = 0101\,1100_2$$ $$\text{CY}_\text{in} = 1$$ ### Step 2: Perform binary addition bit-by-bit (LSB → MSB) $$ \begin{aligned} \text{Bit 0:}\ & 1 + 0 + 1 = 10_2 \Rightarrow \text{sum} = 0,\ \text{carry} = 1 \\ \text{Bit 1:}\ & 1 + 0 + 1 = 10_2 \Rightarrow \text{sum} = 0,\ \text{carry} = 1 \\ \text{Bit 2:}\ & 0 + 1 + 1 = 10_2 \Rightarrow \text{sum} = 0,\ \text{carry} = 1 \\ \text{Bit 3:}\ & 0 + 1 + 1 = 10_2 \Rightarrow \text{sum} = 0,\ \text{carry} = 1 \\ \text{Bit 4:}\ & 1 + 1 + 1 = 11_2 \Rightarrow \text{sum} = 1,\ \text{carry} = 1 \quad \leftarrow \textbf{AC generated here} \\ \text{Bit 5:}\ & 0 + 0 + 1 = 01_2 \Rightarrow \text{sum} = 1,\ \text{carry} = 0 \\ \text{Bit 6:}\ & 1 + 1 + 0 = 10_2 \Rightarrow \text{sum} = 0,\ \text{carry} = 1 \\ \text{Bit 7:}\ & 0 + 0 + 1 = 01_2 \Rightarrow \text{sum} = 1,\ \text{carry} = 0 \quad \leftarrow \textbf{No final CY} \\ \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3: Assemble the result bits $$\text{A}_\text{new} = 1010\,0000_2 = 0xA0$$ ### Step 4: Compute the flags - **CY (PSW.7):** Carry out of bit 7 → **CY = 0** - **AC (PSW.6):** Carry out of bit 3 into bit 4 → **AC = 1** - **OV (PSW.2):** Use $\text{OV} = C_7 \oplus C_6$, where $C_7$ = carry into bit 7, $C_6$ = carry into bit 6. Here $C_7 = 1$, $C_6 = 1$, so $\text{OV} = 0$. - **P (PSW.0):** Count 1's in `0xA0 = 1010 0000` → two 1's (even) → **P = 0** ### Final Answer | Quantity | Value | |---|---| | A | `0xA0` | | CY | 0 | | AC | 1 | | OV | 0 | | P | 0 | > [!NOTE] > **Key Takeaway:** The Parity flag is set to **1 only for odd** number of 1s. The Auxiliary Carry is a half-carry specifically for **BCD (Binary-Coded Decimal)** correction via the `DA A` instruction. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — MUL AB and DIV AB ### Multiplication **Problem:** `A = 0x14` (20), `B = 0x06` (6). Execute `MUL AB`. ### Step 1: Compute $$\text{A} \times \text{B} = 20 \times 6 = 120$$ $$120 < 256 \Rightarrow \text{result fits in A only}$$ ### Step 2: Result placement - $\text{A} = 120 \bmod 256 = 0x78$ - $\text{B} = \lfloor 120 / 256 \rfloor = 0$ - **OV = 0** (high byte is zero) - **CY = 0** (always cleared by MUL) ### Division **Problem:** `A = 0x64` (100), `B = 0x09` (9). Execute `DIV AB`. ### Step 1: Compute $$100 \div 9 = 11 \text{ remainder } 1$$ ### Step 2: Result placement - $\text{A} = \text{quotient} = 11 = 0x0B$ - $\text{B} = \text{remainder} = 1 = 0x01$ - **OV = 0** (division by non-zero) - **CY = 0** > [!WARNING] > **KTU Pitfall:** In `DIV AB`, if `B = 0` **before** the instruction, the 8051 **does not trap** — it just sets `OV = 1` and leaves A and B undefined. Students often write "divide by zero error" in exams; the 8051 has no such hardware exception. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Common Exam Bit-Operations on the Accumulator | Initial A | Operation | Final A (Binary) | Final A (Hex) | P flag (PSW.0) | |---|---|---|---|---| | `0xF0` | `CPL A` | `0000 1111` | `0x0F` | 0 (four 1s) | | `0x0F` | `RL A` | `0001 1110` | `0x1E` | 0 (four 1s) | | `0x81` | `RLC A` (CY=0) | `0000 0010` plus CY becomes old bit 7 | `0x02`, CY=1 | 0 | | `0xA5` | `SWAP A` | `0101 1010` | `0x5A` | 0 | | `0x0F` | `ANL A, #0xF0` | `0000 0000` | `0x00` | 0 | | `0x0F` | `ORL A, #0xF0` | `1111 1111` | `0xFF` | 1 (eight 1s → odd count) | > [!TIP] > **Mnemonic for SWAP:** "Upper nibble dives, lower nibble rises." Useful for BCD-to-ASCII conversions such as `ADD A, #0x30` after `SWAP A`. --- ## 3.4 Detailed Bit-Trace for `RLC A` (Rotate Left through Carry) **Initial:** `A = 0b1000 0001`, `CY = 0`. ### Step 1: Capture bit 7 of A $$\text{CY}_\text{new} = \text{A}_7 = 1$$ ### Step 2: Shift A left by one, with old CY filling bit 0 $$ \begin{aligned} \text{A}_\text{new, 7..1} &= \text{A}_{6..0} = 000\,0000 \\ \text{A}_\text{new, 0} &= \text{CY}_\text{old} = 0 \\ \Rightarrow \text{A}_\text{new} &= 0000\,0000_2 = 0x00 \end{aligned} $$ ### Step 3: Final flag state - $\text{CY} = 1$, $\text{P} = 0$ (zero 1s in A → P = 0). > [!NOTE] > This instruction is heavily used in **multi-byte shift/serialisation** routines — e.g., transmitting 16-bit data over an 8-bit UART by rotating the high byte, then the low byte, through CY. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics Below are Mermaid diagrams that satisfy the **Mermaid Compilation Safeguards** — every node ID is alphanumeric and prefixed; labels are clean uppercase text without markdown formatting. ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Accumulator's Position in the 8051 CPU ```mermaid flowchart LR A[REGISTER BANK 0-3
R0 to R7] --> MUX1[INTERNAL DATA BUS
8 BIT] B[SFR AREA
PORT 0 TO 3 TIMER ETC] --> MUX1 C[IMMEDIATE DATA
FROM PROGRAM MEMORY] --> MUX1 MUX1 --> TMP[TEMPORARY REGISTER
TMP2] ACC[ACCUMULATOR
SFR E0H BIT ADDRESSABLE] --> ALU[ARITHMETIC LOGIC UNIT] TMP --> ALU ALU --> ACC ALU --> FLG[PROGRAM STATUS WORD
PSW D0H] ACC --> FLG ACC --> ROM[PROGRAM MEMORY
LOOKUP TABLE ACCESS] ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Data flows from the selected source into the temporary register, both inputs enter the ALU, the result lands in the Accumulator, and the ALU also updates flags in the PSW based on the result. The Accumulator itself can be a *source* for further operations (loop-back path). --- ## 4.2 Functional Flow — Execution of `ADD A, R5` ```mermaid flowchart TD ST1[START
FETCH OPCODE 0x2D FROM ROM] --> ST2[DECODE OPCODE
OP IS ADD A Rn
R5 SELECTED] ST2 --> ST3[READ R5 FROM
CURRENT REGISTER BANK
INTO TMP2] ST3 --> ST4[READ ACCUMULATOR
CURRENT VALUE INTO ALU INPUT A] ST4 --> ST5[ALU PERFORMS A PLUS TMP2] ST5 --> ST6{ANY CARRY OUT
FROM BIT 7} ST6 -- YES --> ST7[SET CARRY FLAG
CY EQUALS 1] ST6 -- NO --> ST8[CY EQUALS 0] ST7 --> ST9[WRITE 8 BIT RESULT
BACK INTO ACCUMULATOR] ST8 --> ST9 ST9 --> ST10[UPDATE PARITY FLAG
P BASED ON COUNT OF 1 BITS] ST10 --> ST11[END OF INSTRUCTION
PROGRAM COUNTER ADVANCES] ``` --- ## 4.3 Instruction-Class Tree — How the Accumulator Interacts with the Instruction Set ```mermaid flowchart TD ROOT[8051 INSTRUCTION SET] --> ARITH[ARITHMETIC
ADD ADDC SUBB MUL DIV INC DEC DA] ROOT --> LOGIC[LOGICAL
ANL ORL XRL CPL CLR SWAP] ROOT --> ROT[ROTATE
RL RR RLC RRC] ROOT --> DATA[DATA TRANSFER
MOV MOVX MOVC PUSH POP XCH XCHD] ROOT --> BR[BOOLEAN AND BRANCH
JB JNB JBC JC JNC JZ JNZ CJNE DJNZ] ARITH --> AA[ACCUMULATOR IS
IMPLICIT OPERAND
AND DESTINATION] LOGIC --> AA ROT --> AA DATA --> AB[ACCUMULATOR IS
FREQUENT DESTINATION
E G MOVC A DPTR] BR --> AC[CONDITION OFTEN TESTS
A EQUAL ZERO OR BIT
INSIDE SFR E0H] ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Visual Insight:** Notice the convergence — almost all instruction classes either *use* the Accumulator as operand/destination (Arithmetic, Logical, Rotate) or *test* it (Branch). This is exactly the architectural centralisation that the KTU syllabus emphasises. --- ## 4.4 Accumulator Bit Map ```mermaid flowchart LR B7[BIT 7
E7H] --- B6[BIT 6
E6H] --- B5[BIT 5
E5H] --- B4[BIT 4
E4H] --- B3[BIT 3
E3H] --- B2[BIT 2
E2H] --- B1[BIT 1
E1H] --- B0[BIT 0
E0H] ``` Each bit can be referenced individually by its SFR bit address, e.g.: $$\text{MOV } 0xE0, \;\#0\times80 \quad \text{(set bit 7 of accumulator)}$$ or $$\text{SETB } 0xE5 \quad \text{(set bit 5 of accumulator)}$$ This bit-addressability is unique among the 8051's general-purpose registers and is a *favourite* viva question. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap The following questions mirror the KTU End-Semester Evaluation (ESE) style, including mark distribution, internal-choice structure, and valuation focus areas. --- ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1. Define the Accumulator in the 8051 Microcontroller. Mention its SFR address and size. [KTU University Exam – May 2023]** **Model Answer:** The **Accumulator (`A` or `ACC`)** is a special-function, 8-bit register inside the 8051 CPU that serves as the *implicit operand* and *default destination* for most Arithmetic Logic Unit (ALU) operations such as `ADD`, `SUBB`, `ANL`, `ORL`, `XRL`, `RL`, and `MUL/DIV`. It occupies Special Function Register address `0xE0H` and is *bit-addressable* (bits `E0H`–`E7H`). The Parity flag (PSW.0) is automatically computed from the 1-bit count of the Accumulator after every instruction that writes to it. --- ### **Q2. Explain the role of the Accumulator in the `MUL AB` and `DIV AB` instructions. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]** **Model Answer:** In `MUL AB`, the **Accumulator holds the multiplicand (8-bit)** and the **B-register holds the multiplier**. Their unsigned product is 16 bits; the lower 8 bits are stored *back* in the Accumulator and the upper 8 bits in the B-register. The OV flag is set if the product exceeds 8 bits (i.e., B ≠ 0 after multiplication). In `DIV AB`, the Accumulator holds the **dividend (8-bit)** and B holds the divisor. After execution, the Accumulator stores the **quotient** and the B-register stores the **remainder**. The OV flag is set if B was zero at the start (undefined result); the CY flag is always cleared by both MUL and DIV. --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — Internal Choice > **KTU Pattern:** Part B carries 14 marks, split typically as (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks. Students must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- ### **Question A (14 Marks) — Choice 1** #### **Q.A (a)** Explain the internal architecture of the 8051 with reference to the role of the Accumulator. How is the Accumulator coupled to the Program Status Word (PSW)? (7 marks) **[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]** **Model Solution:** **1. Internal Architecture Overview (3 marks):** The 8051 is an 8-bit Harvard-architecture microcontroller. Its CPU contains an ALU, an Accumulator (A), a B-register, a Program Counter (PC), a Stack Pointer (SP), a Data Pointer (DPTR), and the Program Status Word (PSW). Internal RAM has 256 bytes (128 user + 128 SFR). The Accumulator is the central data register; all arithmetic and logical operations route operands through it. The ALU has two inputs — the Accumulator and a temporary register that buffers the explicit operand. The result is always written back into the Accumulator. **2. Coupling with the PSW (3 marks):** The ALU updates the PSW flags based on the result of every instruction involving the Accumulator: $$\begin{aligned} \text{CY (PSW.7)} &= \text{carry-out from bit 7 of A} \\ \text{AC (PSW.6)} &= \text{carry-out from bit 3 to bit 4 of A} \\ \text{OV (PSW.2)} &= C_7 \oplus C_6 \\ \text{P (PSW.0)} &= 1 \iff \text{Accumulator has an odd number of 1s} \end{aligned}$$ These flags are then used by branch instructions (`JC`, `JNC`, `JB`, `JNB`, `JBC`, `JNZ`, `JZ`). **3. Conclusion (1 mark):** The Accumulator–PSW pair forms the *decision-making core* of the 8051 — the ALU computes into A, the PSW summarises the result, and branches consume those flags to alter program flow. #### **Q.A (b)** Starting with `A = 0xC3` and `CY = 0`, trace the execution of `SUBB A, #0x58`. Determine the new values of `A`, `CY`, `AC`, `OV`, and `P`. Show all bit-level workings. (7 marks) **[KTU University Exam – Jul 2024]** **Model Solution:** `SUBB A, #0x58` computes $\text{A} = \text{A} - 0x58 - \text{CY}$. With $\text{CY} = 0$, it is a straight subtraction. **Step 1: Binary representations** $$\text{A} = 0xC3 = 1100\,0011_2,\quad 0x58 = 0101\,1000_2$$ **Step 2: Two's complement of the subtrahend (0x58)** $$\overline{0x58} + 1 = 1010\,0111 + 1 = 1010\,1000_2 = 0xA8$$ **Step 3: Add A and the two's complement (carry-in = 0):** $$ \begin{aligned} & 1100\,0011 \\ +& 1010\,1000 \\ =&\ 1\,0110\,1011 \end{aligned} $$ The 9-bit sum is `1 0110 1011`. Discarding the carry-out (bit 8), the 8-bit result is `0110 1011 = 0x6B`. **Step 4: Flags** - **CY:** A borrow is generated whenever the result has no final carry-out in the two's-complement view. Here final carry-out = 1, so **no borrow** → **CY = 0**. - **AC:** Look at the half-carry between bit 3 and bit 4 of the addition. The lower nibble of A is `0011`, the lower nibble of 0x58's complement is `1000`; sum = `1011` (no half-carry) → **AC = 0**. - **OV:** $\text{OV} = C_7 \oplus C_6$. Carry into bit 7: $C_7 = 1$ (from the chain). Carry into bit 6: $C_6 = 0$. So $\text{OV} = 1 \oplus 0 = 1$. - **P:** Count 1's in `0x6B = 0110 1011` → five 1's (odd) → **P = 1**. **Final Result:** | Register / Flag | Value | |---|---| | A | `0x6B` | | CY | 0 | | AC | 0 | | OV | 1 | | P | 1 | **Verification by direct subtraction:** $0xC3 - 0x58 = 195 - 88 = 107 = 0x6B$ ✓ --- ### **Question B (14 Marks) — Choice 2** #### **Q.B (a)** Discuss the following Accumulator-related instructions with one example each: `ADD`, `ADDC`, `SUBB`, `MUL AB`, `DIV AB`, `ANL`, `ORL`, `XRL`, `CPL`, `SWAP`, `RL`, `RLC`. (7 marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Model Solution (compact table form acceptable in exam):** | Instruction | Example | Effect | Result | |---|---|---|---| | `ADD A, R0` | A=10, R0=20 | A = A + R0 | A = 30 | | `ADDC A, R0` | A=10, R0=20, CY=1 | A = A + R0 + CY | A = 31 | | `SUBB A, R0` | A=30, R0=10, CY=0 | A = A − R0 − CY | A = 20 | | `MUL AB` | A=20, B=6 | A = (A·B) mod 256, B = high byte | A=120, B=0 | | `DIV AB` | A=100, B=9 | A=quotient, B=remainder | A=11, B=1 | | `ANL A, #0x0F` | A=0xA5 | A = A AND 0x0F | A = 0x05 | | `ORL A, #0x0F` | A=0xA0 | A = A OR 0x0F | A = 0xAF | | `XRL A, #0xFF` | A=0x0F | A = A XOR 0xFF | A = 0xF0 | | `CPL A` | A=0x0F | A = NOT A | A = 0xF0 | | `SWAP A` | A=0xA5 | Swap upper/lower nibble | A = 0x5A | | `RL A` | A=0b10000001 | Rotate left within A | A = 0b00000011 | | `RLC A` (CY=0) | A=0b10000001 | Rotate left through CY | A = 0b00000010, CY=1 | The P flag is updated by every instruction above; CY, AC, OV are updated only by arithmetic operations. #### **Q.B (b)** A system measures temperature using an 8-bit ADC. The raw ADC value is in the Accumulator. Write the sequence of 8051 instructions to: (i) Check if the temperature exceeds 100°C (threshold `0x64`). (ii) If it exceeds, set `P1.0` HIGH; else, set `P1.0` LOW. (7 marks) **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Model Solution:** ```assembly ; (i) Compare A with threshold 0x64 MOV A, #0x64 ; A = threshold (or load ADC value) ; We assume the raw ADC value is already in A CJNE A, #0x64, NOT_EQUAL ; If A != 0x64, jump SJMP EQUAL NOT_EQUAL: JC BELOW ; CY = 1 means A < 0x64 ; else A > 0x64 SETB P1.0 ; Temperature exceeds threshold SJMP DONE BELOW: CLR P1.0 ; Temperature below threshold SJMP DONE EQUAL: CLR P1.0 ; Equal treated as not exceeding DONE: SJMP DONE ; Infinite loop / next task ``` **Explanation (to write in the answer sheet):** 1. `CJNE A, #0x64, NOT_EQUAL` performs `A − 0x64`. If `A < 0x64`, the CY flag is set; if `A > 0x64`, CY is cleared. The instruction also forces a jump to `NOT_EQUAL` whenever the values differ. 2. Inside `NOT_EQUAL`, we test CY. `JC BELOW` jumps if `A < 0x64`. If execution falls through, `A > 0x64` and we set `P1.0` HIGH. 3. If `A == 0x64`, we reach `EQUAL` and set `P1.0` LOW (not exceeding). 4. `SJMP DONE` is an unconditional short jump used to skip the alternative branch and prevent "fall-through" execution. > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Marks Lost:** > 1. Forgetting that `CJNE` is the **only** direct compare instruction in the 8051 — using `SUBB` then `JZ` is valid but loses 1 mark for inefficiency. > 2. Forgetting that `CJNE` *always* jumps when values differ — students often write `JNC` thinking no jump occurs, causing the CY=1 case to be missed. > 3. Not setting the parity flag manually — but the 8051 updates P *automatically* on every A-modifying instruction. > 4. Treating `JC` as "jump if carry cleared" — opposite of the truth (JC = jump if carry **set**). > 5. Using `CLR`/`SETB` on `P0.0` (which is the open-drain data bus) instead of `P1.0` — a classic 8051 trap. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final 60-Second Revision Block — Memorise These:** - The **Accumulator** is an 8-bit SFR at address **`0xE0H`**, and is **bit-addressable** (`E0H`–`E7H`). - It is the **implicit operand** and **default destination** for most 8051 ALU instructions — this is the *defining property* of an accumulator-based architecture. - The ALU outputs to the Accumulator and *simultaneously* updates the **PSW flags** (CY, AC, OV, P). - **CY (PSW.7):** Carry/borrow out of bit 7. - **AC (PSW.6):** Half-carry between bits 3 and 4 — used by `DA A` for BCD correction. - **OV (PSW.2):** Signed overflow, computed as $C_7 \oplus C_6$. - **P (PSW.0):** Set to 1 when the Accumulator has an **odd** number of 1-bits (i.e., stores *even parity*). - `ADD` / `ADDC` / `SUBB` affect all four flags; `INC A` / `DEC A` affect **P only**; `MUL` and `DIV` affect only **CY and OV**. - `MUL AB` puts low byte of product in **A** and high byte in **B**; `DIV AB` puts quotient in **A** and remainder in **B**. - `RL` rotates within A; `RLC` rotates through CY — vital for multi-byte serial routines. - `SWAP A` exchanges the upper and lower nibbles; the foundation of BCD↔ASCII conversion. - The Accumulator is the source register for **lookup table access**: `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` and `MOVC A, @A+PC`. - Branch instructions that test Accumulator bits directly use the bit-addressable nature (`JB 0xE7, label` tests bit 7 of A). - Exam keywords to use: *"implicit operand," "SFR E0H," "bit-addressable," "ALU," "PSW coupling," "Parity = 1 for odd count of 1s."*

topics.title: Program Counter, Memory Organization - RAM & ROM
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET613 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Program Counter, Memory Organization - RAM & ROM

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The Program Counter (PC)

### Formal Definition
> [!NOTE]
> **Program Counter (PC)** is a **16-bit special function register** in the 8051 microcontroller that contains the **memory address of the next instruction to be fetched and executed** by the CPU. It is the only register in the 8051 that is **not part of the internal RAM/SFR memory map**; it operates as an independent pointer register.

The Program Counter is automatically incremented by the controller logic after every instruction fetch, so that the processor can execute instructions sequentially from program memory. The PC can hold values ranging from **0000H to FFFFH**, which corresponds to a maximum addressable program memory of **64 KB (65,536 bytes)**.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The Reading Bookmark:** Imagine you are reading a thick textbook (the program memory). You keep a **bookmark** (the Program Counter) on the page you are about to read *next*. Every time you finish a paragraph, you move the bookmark to the next page (increment the PC). If the book tells you to "jump to Chapter 5" (a branch/jump instruction), you directly move the bookmark to that chapter's first page (overwrite the PC with a new address). The bookmark itself is not the content; it only **points to where the content lives**.

### Key Properties of the 8051 PC
- **Width:** 16 bits (2 bytes) → can represent addresses from `0000H` to `FFFFH`.
- **Reset Value:** On power-on or reset, the PC is loaded with `0000H` (the very first instruction of any 8051 program is fetched from address `0000H` of program memory).
- **Auto-Increment:** The PC is incremented by the controller hardware according to the byte-length of the fetched instruction (1-byte, 2-byte, or 3-byte opcodes).
- **Modifiability:** The PC can be overwritten by branch instructions such as `LJMP`, `SJMP`, `AJMP`, `ACALL`, `LCALL`, `CJNE`, `DJNZ`, and `JMP @A+DPTR`.

## 1.2 Memory Organization — RAM and ROM

A microcontroller needs two fundamentally different kinds of memory:

### A. Read-Only Memory (ROM) — Program Memory
> [!NOTE]
> **ROM (Read-Only Memory)** is **non-volatile memory** used to permanently store the **executable program code** (the machine-language instructions) and constant lookup tables. The CPU *fetches* instructions from ROM; it does not write to it during normal operation.

In the 8051:
- On-chip ROM size is typically **4 KB** (addresses `0000H–0FFFH`).
- The 8051 can address up to **64 KB of external program memory** (addresses `0000H–FFFFH`).
- ROM is read using the **Program Store Enable (PSEN̅)** signal.

### B. Random-Access Memory (RAM) — Data Memory
> [!NOTE]
> **RAM (Random-Access Memory)** is **volatile memory** used to hold **temporary working data** — variables, the stack, intermediate computation results, and the register banks. Contents are lost when power is removed.

In the 8051:
- Internal RAM = **128 bytes** (addresses `00H–7FH`).
- On-chip Special Function Registers (SFRs) occupy the upper 128 bytes (`80H–FFH`) — *some 8052 variants have 256 bytes of internal RAM*.
- External data memory can extend up to **64 KB** (addresses `0000H–FFFFH`), accessed using the `MOVX` instruction and the **Read/Write (RD̅, WR̅)** strobe signals.

### Conceptual Analogy for RAM vs ROM
> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Analogy — The Classroom Whiteboard vs. The Printed Manual:**
> - **ROM** is like a **printed manual** in the teacher's desk — instructions are *etched in stone*, they survive forever, and students can only *read* from it. The teacher (CPU) reads instructions from the manual in order.
> - **RAM** is like a **whiteboard** — the teacher writes temporary notes, erases them, performs scratch calculations, and stacks papers on the desk. The whiteboard loses its contents at the end of class (power-off).
> - The **Program Counter** is the teacher's *finger pointing to the next line* in the printed manual.

## 1.3 Why Two Separate Memory Spaces?
The 8051 uses a **Harvard architecture** for program memory (separate address and data bus) but allows external Von Neumann-style expansion. The reasons are:

| Aspect | Harvard Separation (PC/ROM vs RAM) |
|---|---|
| Speed | Instruction fetch and data access can happen in parallel |
| Safety | Program memory is write-protected; accidental writes cannot corrupt code |
| Bandwidth | Doubles the effective memory bandwidth |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Program Counter — Operational Analysis

The PC in the 8051 always points to the **next** instruction (not the currently executing one). The fetch-decode-execute cycle works as follows:

### Step-by-Step Operation
1. **Reset** → PC ← `0000H`.
2. **Fetch Cycle Begins:** The 16-bit address on the PC is placed on the **address bus** (`A0–A15`).
3. **PSEN̅** (Program Store Enable) is asserted low → the external (or internal) ROM places the opcode byte at the PC's address onto the data bus.
4. **Decode:** The instruction is decoded by the controller.
5. **Auto-Increment:** The PC is **incremented internally** by 1, 2, or 3 depending on the instruction length:
   - **1-byte instructions** (e.g., `INC A`) → PC ← PC + 1
   - **2-byte instructions** (e.g., `MOV A, #data`) → PC ← PC + 2
   - **3-byte instructions** (e.g., `LJMP addr16`) → PC ← PC + 3
6. **Execute** the instruction; then return to step 2 with the updated PC.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Why:** Auto-increment is performed *while* the current instruction is executing. This pipeline-like behaviour is what makes the 8051 fast — by the time one instruction finishes, the PC is already pointing to the next.

### PC Behavior on Branch Instructions
| Instruction Type | Effect on PC | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Unconditional Long Jump | PC ← `addr16` (16-bit) | `LJMP 2000H` |
| Absolute Jump | PC[10:0] ← `addr11` (2 KB page) | `AJMP label` |
| Short Jump (relative) | PC ← PC + 2 + signed offset | `SJMP label` |
| Subroutine Call | Push PC+2/PC+3 to stack, then PC ← target | `LCALL addr16` |
| Return | Pop saved PC from stack | `RET` |

## 2.2 Memory Organization — Detailed Map of the 8051

### A. Program Memory (ROM) Map
$$\text{Program Memory Range} = 0000\text{H to FFFFH} = 2^{16} \text{ bytes} = \mathbf{64 \text{ KB}}$$

| Address Range | Size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `0000H` | 1 byte | Reset vector — execution always begins here |
| `0003H` | 8 bytes | External Interrupt 0 (INT0) ISR |
| `000BH` | 8 bytes | Timer 0 overflow ISR |
| `0013H` | 8 bytes | External Interrupt 1 (INT1) ISR |
| `001BH` | 8 bytes | Timer 1 overflow ISR |
| `0023H` | 8 bytes | Serial Port ISR |
| `002BH–0FFFH` | up to 4 KB | On-chip ROM user code area |
| `1000H–FFFFH` | up to 60 KB | External program memory |

### B. Internal Data Memory (RAM) Map
| Address Range | Size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|
| `00H–1FH` | 32 bytes | Four Register Banks (Bank 0–3), each with R0–R7 |
| `20H–2FH` | 16 bytes | Bit-addressable area (128 addressable bits, `00H–7FH`) |
| `30H–7FH` | 80 bytes | General-purpose scratchpad RAM |
| `80H–FFH` | 128 bytes | Special Function Registers (SFRs) — *not general RAM* |

> [!WARNING]
> **Address overlap trap:** The bit-addressable area (`20H–2FH`) shares its byte addresses with general RAM, but each of its 128 bits can be individually accessed using bit instructions like `SETB 05H` or `CLR 7FH`.

### C. External Data Memory (XRAM)
$$\text{XRAM Range} = 0000\text{H to FFFFH} = \mathbf{64 \text{ KB}}$$

Accessed only via the `MOVX` instruction family using a 16-bit data pointer (`DPTR` or `R0/R1` with `@`).

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet

| # | Quantity | Formula / Value | Units |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Addressable program memory | $2^{n}$ where $n$ = PC width | bytes |
| 2 | 8051 program memory | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ | bytes = **64 KB** |
| 3 | 8051 on-chip ROM (8051) | $4 \times 1024 = 4096$ | bytes = **4 KB** |
| 4 | 8051 internal RAM | $128$ | bytes |
| 5 | 8051 bit-addressable RAM | $16 \times 8 = 128$ | bits |
| 6 | 8051 register banks | $4$ banks $\times 8$ registers | — |
| 7 | 8051 external data memory | $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ | bytes = **64 KB** |
| 8 | 8051 SFRs (upper RAM) | $128$ | registers |
| 9 | Total 8051 address space (Harvard) | $64 \text{ KB code} + 64 \text{ KB XRAM} + 64 \text{ KB ext. code}$ | bytes = **192 KB logical** |
| 10 | PC after reset | $0$ | decimal `= 0000H` |
| 11 | PC increment per 1-byte instruction | $PC \leftarrow PC + 1$ | count |
| 12 | PC increment per 2-byte instruction | $PC \leftarrow PC + 2$ | count |
| 13 | PC increment per 3-byte instruction | $PC \leftarrow PC + 3$ | count |

> [!NOTE]
> **No-Table-Cell-Pipe Rule:** Where absolute value or set membership is needed inside a cell, use `\vert` or `\mid`. For example, the PC range is written as `PC $\in$ [0000H, FFFFH]` not `PC \in |0000H, FFFFH|`.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Embedded firmware development** requires precise understanding of code placement — placing ISR vectors in their reserved ROM slots (`0003H`, `000BH`, etc.).
- **Memory-mapped I/O** in the 8051 uses the SFR region (`80H–FFH`); misusing these addresses corrupts hardware control registers.
- **Bank-switching of registers** allows ultra-fast context switching in interrupt-driven systems, critical in **real-time control systems** (motor control, automotive ECUs, IoT nodes).

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Maximum Addressable Memory from PC Width

The Program Counter is an $n$-bit binary register. Each bit can hold one of two values (`0` or `1`). Therefore, the total number of unique address combinations is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Number of unique addresses} &= \underbrace{2 \times 2 \times 2 \cdots \times 2}_{n \text{ times}} \\
&= 2^{n} \text{ addresses}
\end{aligned}
$$

Since each address refers to one **byte** of memory:

$$
\boxed{\;M_{\max} = 2^{n} \text{ bytes}\;}
$$

For the 8051, $n = 16$ (because PC is 16 bits wide):

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\max} &= 2^{16} \text{ bytes} \\
&= 65{,}536 \text{ bytes}
\end{aligned}
$$

Converting to kilobytes using $1 \text{ KB} = 1024$ bytes:

$$
\begin{aligned}
M_{\max} &= \frac{65{,}536}{1024} \text{ KB} \\
&= 64 \text{ KB}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Conclusion:** A 16-bit Program Counter can address a maximum of **64 KB** of program memory.

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — PC Address Range Boundaries

For a $16$-bit counter, the smallest value is when all 16 bits are zero, and the largest when all bits are one:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Minimum address} &= 0000\,0000\,0000\,0000_{2} = 0_{10} = \text{0000H} \\
\text{Maximum address} &= 1111\,1111\,1111\,1111_{2} = 65{,}535_{10} = \text{FFFFH}
\end{aligned}
$$

Therefore the valid range is:

$$
\boxed{\; \text{PC} \in [\text{0000H},\ \text{FFFFH}] \;}
$$

The total number of distinct values in this range is:

$$
\begin{aligned}
N &= \text{FFFFH} - \text{0000H} + 1 \\
&= 65{,}535 - 0 + 1 \\
&= 65{,}536 \\
&= 2^{16}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — PC Sequence for a Linear Instruction Stream

Consider the following 8051 assembly fragment starting at address `0100H`:

```text
0100H:  MOV A, #25H      ; 2-byte instruction
0102H:  MOV R0, #40H      ; 2-byte instruction
0104H:  ADD A, @R0        ; 1-byte instruction
0105H:  SJMP 0100H        ; 2-byte instruction (relative jump)
```

**Step 1 — Reset and Initial Fetch**

The CPU starts with `PC = 0000H`. Assume the user has placed code at `0100H` using `ORG 0100H`. When the CPU jumps to `0100H` (via a `LJMP 0100H` placed at `0000H`):

$$
\begin{aligned}
PC_{0} &= \text{0100H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Fetch `MOV A, #25H` (2-byte instruction)**

During the fetch of this 2-byte opcode, the PC is incremented by 2 *in parallel with execution*:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PC_{1} &= PC_{0} + 2 \\
       &= 0100\text{H} + 0002\text{H} \\
       &= 0102\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Fetch `MOV R0, #40H` (2-byte instruction)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PC_{2} &= PC_{1} + 2 \\
       &= 0102\text{H} + 0002\text{H} \\
       &= 0104\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 4 — Fetch `ADD A, @R0` (1-byte instruction)**

$$
\begin{aligned}
PC_{3} &= PC_{2} + 1 \\
       &= 0104\text{H} + 0001\text{H} \\
       &= 0105\text{H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 5 — Fetch `SJMP 0100H` (2-byte instruction, relative jump)**

The relative offset is calculated as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Offset} &= \text{Target} - (PC_{\text{after fetch}} + \text{offset bytes}) \\
             &= 0100\text{H} - (0105\text{H} + 2) \\
             &= 0100\text{H} - 0107\text{H} \\
             &= -\text{07H} \; (\text{i.e., 0F9H in 2's complement 8-bit})
\end{aligned}
$$

During the jump, the PC is **overwritten** with the target:

$$
\begin{aligned}
PC_{4} &= \text{0100H}
\end{aligned}
$$

**The PC loop becomes:** `0100H → 0102H → 0104H → 0105H → 0100H → …` infinitely.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — ROM vs RAM at a Glance

| Parameter | Program Memory (ROM) | Data Memory (RAM) |
|---|---|---|
| **Volatility** | Non-volatile (retains without power) | Volatile (lost on power-off) |
| **Writability** | Read-only during normal operation | Read/Write |
| **Bus used** | Code bus (PSEN̅ strobe) | Data bus (RD̅/WR̅ strobes) |
| **Max size (8051)** | 64 KB (4 KB on-chip + 60 KB external) | 64 KB external + 128 B internal |
| **Pointer register** | Program Counter (PC) | DPTR, R0, R1 (for `MOVX`) |
| **Reset behavior** | PC ← 0000H | Internal RAM is undefined; SFRs set to defaults |
| **Used for** | Machine opcodes, constant tables | Variables, stack, register banks, bit memory |
| **Speed** | Same clock as CPU | Same clock (on-chip); slower for external |
| **Example IC** | Internal mask ROM, external EPROM/Flash | Internal SRAM, external SRAM |

## 3.5 Derivation 4 — Bit-Addressable RAM Count

The 16 bytes from `20H` to `2FH` form the bit-addressable region. Each byte contains 8 bits:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Total bit-addressable bits} &= 16 \text{ bytes} \times 8 \text{ bits/byte} \\
&= 128 \text{ bits}
\end{aligned}
$$

These 128 bits are given the bit addresses `00H` through `7FH`:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bit address range} &= 00\text{H} \text{ to } 7\text{FH} \\
&= 0 \text{ to } 127 \text{ (decimal)} \\
&= 2^{7} \text{ unique bit locations}
\end{aligned}
$$

So bit instructions like `SETB 05H` and `CLR 7FH` can manipulate any one of these **128 individual bits** directly — a powerful feature for hardware flag manipulation.

---
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 8051 Memory Architecture — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    CPU["CPU EXECUTION UNIT"]
    PC["PROGRAM COUNTER PC 16 BIT REGISTER"]
    DPTR["DATA POINTER DPTR 16 BIT"]
    PSEN["PSEN BAR PROGRAM STORE ENABLE"]
    RDWR["RD BAR / WR BAR DATA STROBES"]

    subgraph PROM["PROGRAM MEMORY ROM 64 KB MAX"]
        VEC["RESET AND ISR VECTORS 0000H 002BH"]
        ONROM["ON CHIP ROM 4 KB 0000H 0FFFH"]
        EXROM["EXTERNAL ROM 60 KB 1000H FFFFH"]
    end

    subgraph IRAM["INTERNAL DATA RAM 128 BYTES"]
        BANK["REGISTER BANKS 00H 1FH BANK 0 TO 3"]
        BIT["BIT ADDRESSABLE 20H 2FH 128 BITS"]
        GPR["GENERAL PURPOSE RAM 30H 7FH"]
    end

    subgraph SFR["SFR AREA 80H FFH 128 REGISTERS"]
        PORTS["PORTS P0 P1 P2 P3 80H 90H A0H B0H"]
        CONTROL["CONTROL REGISTERS TCON SCON PCON 88H 98H 87H"]
        TIMERS["TIMER REGISTERS TMOD TCON TH0 TL0 TH1 TL1 89H 8CH 8AH 8DH 8BH"]
        ACCB["ACC B PSW SP IE IP E0H F0H D0H 81H A8H B8H"]
    end

    subgraph XRAM["EXTERNAL DATA RAM 64 KB MAX"]
        EXRAM1["LOWER XRAM 0000H 7FFFH"]
        EXRAM2["UPPER XRAM 8000H FFFFH"]
    end

    CPU -- "PC OUT 16 BIT ADDRESS" --> PROM
    CPU -- "FETCH OPCODE BYTES" --> PROM
    PROM -- "PSEN BAR LATCH" --> CPU

    CPU -- "INTERNAL READ WRITE" --> IRAM
    CPU -- "SFR ACCESS" --> SFR

    CPU -- "MOVX DPTR OR R0 R1" --> XRAM
    XRAM -- "RD WR BARS" --> CPU

    PC -. "GENERATES ADDRESS" .-> PROM
    DPTR -. "POINTS TO" .-> XRAM
```

## 4.2 Program Counter Operational Flow — Fetch-Increment-Execute Loop

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    RST["RESET EVENT"] -- "PC EQUALS 0000H" --> F1["STEP 1 PLACE PC ON ADDRESS BUS"]
    F1 -- "A0 TO A15 DRIVEN" --> F2["STEP 2 ASSERT PSEN BAR"]
    F2 -- "ROM OUTPUTS OPCODE" --> F3["STEP 3 LATCH OPCODE INTO INSTRUCTION REGISTER"]
    F3 -- "DECODE BY CONTROLLER" --> F4{"STEP 4 BRANCH INSTRUCTION?"}
    F4 -- "YES" --> F5["STEP 5A OVERWRITE PC WITH TARGET ADDRESS"]
    F4 -- "NO" --> F6["STEP 5B PC EQUALS PC PLUS OPCODE LENGTH"]
    F5 --> F7["STEP 6 EXECUTE INSTRUCTION"]
    F6 --> F7["STEP 6 EXECUTE INSTRUCTION"]
    F7 -- "NEXT MACHINE CYCLE" --> F1
```

## 4.3 Internal RAM Memory Map (Detailed)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph IR["INTERNAL RAM 00H TO 7FH 128 BYTES"]
        direction TB
        subgraph B0["ADDRESS 00H 1FH 32 BYTES"]
            B0A["BANK 0 R0 R7 00H 07H"]
            B0B["BANK 1 R0 R7 08H 0FH"]
            B0C["BANK 2 R0 R7 10H 17H"]
            B0D["BANK 3 R0 R7 18H 1FH"]
        end
        subgraph B1["ADDRESS 20H 2FH 16 BYTES BIT ADDRESSABLE"]
            B1A["128 BITS ADDRESS 00H 7FH"]
        end
        subgraph B2["ADDRESS 30H 7FH 80 BYTES"]
            B2A["GENERAL PURPOSE SCRATCHPAD"]
        end
    end

    subgraph SF["SFR AREA 80H TO FFH 128 BYTES"]
        direction TB
        SFA["ACCUMULATOR A AT E0H"]
        SFB["B REGISTER AT F0H"]
        SFC["STACK POINTER SP AT 81H"]
        SFD["PSW PROGRAM STATUS WORD AT D0H"]
        SFE["PORTS P0 P1 P2 P3"]
        SFF["TIMERS SERIAL INTERRUPTS CONTROL"]
    end
```

## 4.4 Harvard Architecture — Separate Code and Data Buses

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph HA["8051 HARVARD ARCHITECTURE"]
        direction LR
        CBUS["CODE BUS 16 BIT ADDRESS 8 BIT DATA PSEN BAR"]
        DBUS["DATA BUS 16 BIT ADDRESS 8 BIT DATA RD WR BARS"]
    end
    CBUS -- "PROGRAM MEMORY ROM 64 KB" --> HA
    DBUS -- "DATA MEMORY RAM INTERNAL 128B AND EXTERNAL 64KB" --> HA
    HA -- "CPU FETCHES OPCODES VIA CBUS" --> CBUS
    HA -- "CPU READS WRITES DATA VIA DBUS" --> DBUS
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Mermaid and not physical drawings:** A full chip pinout or memory chip schematic would require electrical wiring details that cannot be cleanly rendered in Mermaid. The block-level functional architecture above shows *all* the logical connections between the CPU, PC, ROM, RAM, and SFRs, which is what the KTU examiner expects in a 14-mark answer.

# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**What is the Program Counter in the 8051 microcontroller? What is its width and what value does it assume on reset?**

**Model Answer:**
The Program Counter (PC) is a 16-bit register that holds the address of the next instruction to be fetched from program memory. Its width is **16 bits**, allowing it to address up to **64 KB** of program memory (addresses `0000H` to `FFFFH`). On power-on or reset, the PC is automatically loaded with `0000H`, so the first instruction is always fetched from the reset vector at `0000H` of program (ROM) memory. The PC is incremented automatically by the controller logic after each instruction fetch, depending on whether the instruction is 1, 2, or 3 bytes long. It can also be modified by branch and call instructions such as `LJMP`, `ACALL`, `LCALL`, and `RET`. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question 2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**Differentiate between program memory (ROM) and data memory (RAM) in the 8051 with respect to volatility, bus signals used, and access instructions.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Program Memory (ROM) | Data Memory (RAM) |
|---|---|---|
| **Volatility** | Non-volatile (retains code without power) | Volatile (data lost on power-off) |
| **Bus / Strobe** | Code bus, controlled by **PSEN̅** | Data bus, controlled by **RD̅** and **WR̅** |
| **Access Instruction** | Automatically fetched via PC | Read/written using `MOV`, `MOVX` (external) |
| **On-chip Size (8051)** | 4 KB | 128 bytes |
| **Maximum Size** | 64 KB | 64 KB (external) + 128 B (internal) |
| **Purpose** | Stores opcodes and constants | Stores variables, stack, register banks |

**(3 marks)**

---

## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a)** Explain the function of the Program Counter in the 8051 microcontroller with a neat diagram. How does it differ from the Data Pointer (DPTR)? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Draw and explain the 8051 internal RAM memory organization. List the addresses and sizes of the register banks, bit-addressable area, general-purpose RAM, and SFR area. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — Program Counter Function and Comparison with DPTR

**Definition and Function (4 marks):**
The Program Counter (PC) is a **16-bit register** in the 8051 that always holds the **address of the next instruction** to be fetched from program (ROM) memory. It is automatically initialized to `0000H` on reset. The PC is incremented by the controller hardware by an amount equal to the byte-length of the just-fetched instruction (1, 2, or 3). Branch and call instructions can directly overwrite the PC with a new address, enabling loops, subroutines, and conditional execution.

The PC controls the **program fetch bus** (signals `A0–A15` for address and `PSEN̅` for strobe). It is **not** mapped into the SFR space; it is a private CPU register.

**Diagram (2 marks):**
```
                  +-------------------+
  Address Bus  -->|  PROGRAM COUNTER  |--> A0..A15 to ROM
  A0..A15  <-----|       (16-bit)    |<-- (overwritten by LJMP/ACALL/RET)
  Reset    ----->|  loaded with 0000H|
                  +-------------------+
                              |
                              v
                       PSEN-bar strobes
                       ROM to place opcode
                       on data bus
```

**Difference from DPTR (1 mark):**

| Parameter | PC | DPTR |
|---|---|---|
| Purpose | Points to next **instruction** in ROM | Points to **data** in external RAM/ROM |
| SFR address | Not in SFR space | `82H` (DPL) + `83H` (DPH) |
| Used by | Automatic fetch, `LJMP`, `RET`, etc. | `MOVX`, `MOVC A,@A+DPTR` |
| Modifiable? | Indirectly, by control logic | Directly via `MOV DPTR, #data` |

---

#### Part (b) — 8051 Internal RAM Organization

**Block Diagram (3 marks):**

```
        8051 INTERNAL DATA MEMORY (128 bytes)
        +-----------------------------------+ FFH
        |   SFR AREA  (128 registers)      | 80H
        |   - Acc (E0H), B (F0H)            |
        |   - SP (81H), PSW (D0H)           |
        |   - P0 (80H), P1 (90H), P2(A0H),  |
        |     P3 (B0H)                      |
        |   - TCON, TMOD, TH0, TL0, etc.    |
        +-----------------------------------+ 7FH
        |   GENERAL PURPOSE RAM (80 bytes)  | 30H
        |   (Scratchpad / Stack)            |
        +-----------------------------------+ 2FH
        |   BIT-ADDRESSABLE AREA (16 bytes) | 20H
        |   128 individually addressable    |
        |   bits (bit addr 00H - 7FH)       |
        +-----------------------------------+ 1FH
        |   REGISTER BANK 3 (R0-R7)         | 18H
        +-----------------------------------+ 17H
        |   REGISTER BANK 2 (R0-R7)         | 10H
        +-----------------------------------+ 0FH
        |   REGISTER BANK 1 (R0-R7)         | 08H
        +-----------------------------------+ 07H
        |   REGISTER BANK 0 (R0-R7)         | 00H
        +-----------------------------------+
```

**Address Table (4 marks):**

| Region | Address Range | Size | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Register Bank 0 | `00H – 07H` | 8 bytes | Default R0–R7 |
| Register Bank 1 | `08H – 0FH` | 8 bytes | Alternate R0–R7 |
| Register Bank 2 | `10H – 17H` | 8 bytes | Alternate R0–R7 |
| Register Bank 3 | `18H – 1FH` | 8 bytes | Alternate R0–R7 |
| Bit-addressable | `20H – 2FH` | 16 bytes | 128 bits, bit-addr `00H–7FH` |
| General Purpose | `30H – 7FH` | 80 bytes | User variables, stack |
| SFR | `80H – FFH` | 128 bytes | Control / status / I/O |

**Note:** Active register bank is selected by bits RS0 and RS1 in the PSW register.

---

### Question B. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] — Alternative Choice

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the 8051 program memory (ROM) organization. Why is the program memory read using the PSEN̅ signal, and how is external program memory interfaced? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A 12 MHz crystal is used with the 8051. Calculate the time taken to execute a single-byte instruction and a three-byte `LJMP` instruction. Justify each step. **(7 marks)**

### Model Solution

#### Part (a) — 8051 Program Memory Organization

**Diagram (3 marks):**
```
        8051 PROGRAM MEMORY (ROM) - 64 KB MAX
        +-----------------------------------+ FFFFH
        |                                   |
        |   EXTERNAL ROM  (60 KB)           |
        |   Addresses 1000H - FFFFH         |
        |                                   |
        +-----------------------------------+ 0FFFH
        |   ON-CHIP ROM  (4 KB)             |
        |   Addresses 0000H - 0FFFH         |
        |                                   |
        +-----------------------------------+ 002BH
        |   Serial Port ISR vector          | 0023H
        +-----------------------------------+ 001BH
        |   Timer 1 ISR vector              | 0013H
        +-----------------------------------+ 0013H
        |   Ext Int 1 ISR vector            | 000BH
        +-----------------------------------+ 000BH
        |   Timer 0 ISR vector              | 0003H
        +-----------------------------------+ 0003H
        |   Ext Int 0 ISR vector            | 0000H
        +-----------------------------------+ 0000H
        |   RESET VECTOR (PC <- 0000H)      |
        +-----------------------------------+
```

**Explanation (3 marks):**
- The 8051 has a **16-bit program address bus** → `2^16 = 64 KB` maximum.
- On-chip ROM is **4 KB** (`0000H–0FFFH`); external ROM extends up to **64 KB**.
- The 8051 reads program memory using the **PSEN̅** (Program Store Enable) strobe — this signal goes LOW during a code fetch to enable the external ROM's output enable.
- **Interfacing:** When the EA̅ (External Access) pin is tied HIGH, the 8051 fetches from on-chip ROM first; if the address exceeds on-chip ROM, it automatically fetches from external ROM. If EA̅ is tied LOW, all fetches are from external ROM.

**Key Features (1 mark):**
- Interrupt vectors occupy reserved slots in the lower addresses.
- The `MOVC` instruction reads data from program memory (used for lookup tables).

---

#### Part (b) — Machine Cycle Calculations

**Given:** Crystal frequency $f_{osc} = 12 \text{ MHz}$.

**Step 1 — Oscillator Period**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{osc} &= \frac{1}{f_{osc}} \\
        &= \frac{1}{12 \times 10^{6}} \text{ s} \\
        &= 0.0833 \;\mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2 — Machine Cycle**

The 8051 divides the oscillator by 12 to produce the machine cycle:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{MC} &= \frac{12}{f_{osc}} \\
       &= 12 \times T_{osc} \\
       &= 12 \times 0.0833 \;\mu\text{s} \\
       &= 1 \;\mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3 — Single-Byte Instruction Execution Time**

Most 8051 single-byte instructions (e.g., `INC A`, `NOP`, `RR A`) execute in **1 machine cycle**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{1\text{-byte}} &= 1 \times T_{MC} \\
                  &= 1 \;\mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Note: A few single-byte instructions like `INC DPTR` and `MUL AB` take 2 machine cycles; students should specify this if the instruction is not the default case.

**Step 4 — Three-Byte `LJMP` Instruction Execution Time**

The `LJMP addr16` opcode is 3 bytes long. The 8051 fetches the 3 bytes across **3 machine cycles** and requires **2 more machine cycles** to complete the jump. Total = **4 machine cycles**:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{\text{LJMP}} &= 4 \times T_{MC} \\
               &= 4 \times 1 \;\mu\text{s} \\
               &= 4 \;\mu\text{s}
\end{aligned}
$$

> Justification: 3 machine cycles for fetching the 3 opcode bytes (PC incremented each cycle), plus 1 cycle to latch the new PC value from the opcode stream.

**Final Answer:**
- Single-byte instruction: **1 μs**
- `LJMP` instruction: **4 μs**

---

## KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks:**
> 1. **Confusing PC with DPTR** — The PC points to *code* in ROM; DPTR points to *data* in XRAM/code bytes. Mixing these up in a comparison question loses 1–2 marks.
> 2. **Forgetting PSEN̅ vs RD̅/WR̅** — Program memory reads use **PSEN̅**; data memory reads use **RD̅**. Writing program memory (impossible in normal Harvard mode) would not use PSEN̅.
> 3. **Calculating memory size wrong** — $2^{16} = 65{,}536$ **bytes**, not $65{,}536$ **bits**. Always specify units.
> 4. **Forgetting 256-byte boundary in 8052** — The 8052 has 256 bytes internal RAM, not 128. Mention the variant if the question allows.
> 5. **Skipping the address table** — A memory map *without* an address table is treated as incomplete by KTU examiners. Always pair the diagram with a numerical table.
> 6. **Not justifying the LJMP timing** — Simply writing "4 μs" without showing the breakdown (3 fetch + 1 internal) loses 2 marks.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Program Counter (PC)** = 16-bit register, points to next instruction in ROM, reset value `0000H`, can address **64 KB** ($2^{16}$ bytes) of program memory.
- **PC auto-increments** by 1, 2, or 3 depending on instruction length during the fetch cycle.
- **PC is overwritten** by branch/call instructions (`LJMP`, `AJMP`, `SJMP`, `ACALL`, `LCALL`, `RET`, `RETI`, `JMP @A+DPTR`).
- **ROM (Program Memory)** is non-volatile, accessed via **PSEN̅** strobe; on-chip 4 KB, expandable to 64 KB externally.
- **RAM (Data Memory)** is volatile, accessed via **RD̅/WR̅** strobes; 128 bytes internal + 64 KB external (`MOVX`).
- **Internal RAM structure** = 4 register banks (32 B) + 16 B bit-addressable (128 bits) + 80 B general-purpose + 128 B SFR.
- **Bit-addressable area** (`20H–2FH`) gives 128 individually addressable bits (`00H–7FH`) — unique 8051 feature.
- **Harvard architecture** separates program and data buses for speed and code safety.
- **Memory capacity formula** = $2^n$ bytes where $n$ = number of address bits.
- **Machine cycle** = $12 / f_{osc}$. For $f_{osc} = 12$ MHz, $T_{MC} = 1 \;\mu\text{s}$.
- **Reset behavior**: PC ← `0000H`, internal RAM undefined, SFRs set to defaults (`SP = 07H`, all ports = `FFH`).
- **EA̅ pin** controls whether program fetches start from on-chip ROM (`EA̅ = 1`) or external ROM (`EA̅ = 0`).
- **DPTR ≠ PC** — DPTR is a user-accessible 16-bit data pointer (SFR `82H/83H`); PC is a private CPU register.

---
