topics.title: Register Banks and Stack
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET613 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Register Banks and Stack

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Register Banks — Formal KTU Definition

In the **Intel 8051 Microcontroller architecture**, the **Register Banks** are four discrete, physically distinct sets of **eight general-purpose 8-bit registers** (named **R0, R1, R2, R3, R4, R5, R6, R7**) located in the **internal RAM (idata memory) from address 00H to 1FH**. Each bank occupies exactly **8 bytes**, and only one bank is "active" (i.e., directly accessible to instructions using the names R0–R7) at any given time. The active bank is selected by the **Register Bank Select bits RS0 (PSW.3) and RS1 (PSW.4)** of the **Program Status Word (PSW)** register.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memory Map of Register Banks (8051 Internal RAM, Lower 32 bytes):**
> - **Bank 0:** RAM addresses `00H – 07H` (R0–R7 of Bank 0)
> - **Bank 1:** RAM addresses `08H – 0FH` (R0–R7 of Bank 1)
> - **Bank 2:** RAM addresses `10H – 17H` (R0–R7 of Bank 2)
> - **Bank 3:** RAM addresses `18H – 1FH` (R0–R7 of Bank 3)
>
> **Default Active Bank after RESET:** **Bank 0** (RS1 = 0, RS0 = 0)

### 1.2 The Stack — Formal KTU Definition

The **Stack** in the 8051 is a **Last-In-First-Out (LIFO)** memory structure implemented in the **internal RAM** and managed by an 8-bit **Stack Pointer (SP) special function register (SFR at address 81H)**. The stack is used by the **CALL, ACALL, LCALL, RET, RETI** instructions, by **hardware interrupt** service routines, and explicitly by the programmer through the **PUSH** and **POP** instructions. The stack **grows upward** in memory (toward higher addresses).

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (KTU Frequently Asked):**
> - **Stack Pointer (SP)** is **8-bit wide** in standard 8051 → Maximum stack depth = **256 bytes**.
> - SP is initialized to **07H** on power-on reset (first PUSH writes to `08H`).
> - 8052 has SP effectively behaving the same way, but with 256 bytes internal RAM.

### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Desk Drawer" Intuition

**Register Banks** are like having **four identical sets of labeled sticky notes (R0–R7) on a single office desk**. A worker (the CPU) can only read/write from **one set at a time**, but the **manager (PSW bits RS0, RS1)** can instantly swap which set is on the desk. This avoids losing the current "scratch values" when an **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** barges in — the manager just swaps to a *different* bank so the ISR's R0–R7 cannot clobber the main program's R0–R7. After the ISR finishes, the manager swaps back, and the main program finds its values exactly as it left them.

**The Stack** is like a **vertical stack of papers in an inbox tray**. You can only:
- **PUSH**: place a new paper *on top* (SP increments first, then writes at the new SP address).
- **POP**: remove the *top* paper and read it (reads from SP, then decrements SP).

If you PUSH too many papers without popping, they overflow the tray (the tray isn't infinite — internal RAM is limited to 128 bytes in standard 8051).

---

### 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

- **Internal RAM size (8051):** **128 bytes** (`00H – 7FH`)
- **Internal RAM size (8052):** **256 bytes** (`00H – FFH`)
- **Register Bank total footprint:** **32 bytes** (`00H – 1FH`)
- **Bit-addressable region:** `20H – 2FH` (**16 bytes = 128 addressable bits**)
- **SFR region:** `80H – FFH`
- **Stack Pointer reset value:** **`07H`**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The SP = 07H reset pitfall:** The very first PUSH will write to address `08H`, which **overlaps with Bank 1's R0 register**. KTU examiners love asking why we typically reinitialize SP to a "safe" upper location such as `60H` or `70H` in initialization code.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Program Status Word (PSW) — Bank Selection Bits

The **PSW (SFR at address D0H)** is the **CPU's flag and bank-select register**. Its bits control which register bank is active and store ALU status flags.

| PSW Bit | Address Bit | Name | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **PSW.7** | D7H | **CY (Carry Flag)** | Set on arithmetic carry/borrow |
| PSW.6 | D6H | **AC (Auxiliary Carry)** | Set on BCD carry between bit 3 and bit 4 |
| PSW.5 | D5H | **F0 (User Flag 0)** | General-purpose user flag |
| PSW.4 | D4H | **RS1 (Register Bank Select 1)** | High-order bank select bit |
| **PSW.3** | D3H | **RS0 (Register Bank Select 0)** | Low-order bank select bit |
| PSW.2 | D2H | **OV (Overflow)** | Set on signed arithmetic overflow |
| PSW.1 | D1H | — | Reserved (undefined) |
| PSW.0 | D0H | **P (Parity)** | Hardware-set even parity of ACC |

> [!NOTE]
> **Active Bank Selection Logic (must memorize for KTU):**
> - **RS1 = 0, RS0 = 0** → **Bank 0** selected (addresses `00H–07H`)
> - **RS1 = 0, RS0 = 1** → **Bank 1** selected (addresses `08H–0FH`)
> - **RS1 = 1, RS0 = 0** → **Bank 2** selected (addresses `10H–17H`)
> - **RS1 = 1, RS0 = 1** → **Bank 3** selected (addresses `18H–1FH`)

### 2.2 Bank Selection Logic — Step-by-Step

1. The 8051 instruction decoder, when encountering a register name like `R5`, looks at the **3-bit register field** in the opcode and the **2-bit RS1:RS0** value.
2. The final **physical internal RAM address** is computed as:  

$$
\text{Physical Address} = (\text{RS1} \cdot 2 + \text{RS0}) \cdot 8 + \text{Register Index}
$$

3. Switching banks is a **single-instruction, single-cycle** operation (modifying PSW). No RAM copy is needed — the data is physically *already there*, just renamed.

### 2.3 The Stack — Mechanics in Depth

#### 2.3.1 PUSH Operation (2 machine cycles, 2 bytes of opcode)

**Micro-steps executed by hardware:**
1. **SP ← SP + 1** (Stack pointer incremented *first*).
2. **Internal RAM [SP] ← Source byte** (data written to the *new* SP address).

#### 2.3.2 POP Operation (2 machine cycles, 2 bytes of opcode)

**Micro-steps executed by hardware:**
1. **Destination ← Internal RAM [SP]** (read data from current SP).
2. **SP ← SP − 1** (Stack pointer decremented *after* read).

#### 2.3.3 Stack and Subroutine Calls

- **LCALL/ACALL:** On call, the 8051 hardware automatically PUSHes the **16-bit return address** (2 bytes — low byte first, then high byte) onto the stack, then jumps to the subroutine.
- **RET:** On return, the 8051 hardware POPs 2 bytes (high first, then low) into the PC.
- **Interrupts:** Hardware pushes the PC (and in some cases, the PSW is implicitly switched depending on the interrupt source — this is interrupt-priority-and-bank-switching behavior).

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Quantity | Formula / Value | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Total register banks in 8051** | **4** | Bank 0, 1, 2, 3 |
| **Registers per bank** | **8** | R0 through R7 (8-bit each) |
| **Total bytes used by register banks** | **32 bytes** | From `00H` to `1FH` |
| **Physical address of R_n in selected bank** | $\text{Addr} = (\text{RS1} \cdot 2 + \text{RS0}) \cdot 8 + n$ | where $n \in \{0,1,\dots,7\}$ |
| **Stack Pointer width** | **8 bits** | Range `00H` to `FFH` |
| **Max stack depth (bytes)** | **256** | Limited by 8-bit SP |
| **SP reset value** | `07H` | First PUSH writes to `08H` |
| **PUSH effect on SP** | $\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} + 1$ | Then write to new SP |
| **POP effect on SP** | $\text{SP}_{\text{new}} = \text{SP}_{\text{old}} - 1$ | After read at old SP |
| **LCALL/ACALL stack effect** | Pushes **2 bytes** | Low byte pushed first |
| **Internal RAM available for stack** | `08H – 7FH` (8051) | Avoid `00H–1FH` (banks) and `20H–2FH` (bit) if possible |

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Register Banks** enable **zero-overhead context switching** in interrupt-driven systems — the ISR simply selects Bank 1, 2, or 3, and its R0–R7 are isolated from the foreground program. This is **faster and smaller in code size** than saving registers to the stack, and is widely used in commercial 8051 firmware.
- **The Stack** is essential for **subroutine nesting, parameter passing (in C51), and local variable storage** when compiled with C. The Keil C51 compiler, for example, places the stack at `08H` upward by default — a strong reason to reinitialize SP explicitly at program start.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Insight:** In modern real-time embedded systems using 8051 cores (e.g., Cypress PSoC, Silicon Labs EFM8, Nuvoton N76E003), the register-bank-swap technique is still preferred for **fast ISRs** because it avoids the ~20+ machine cycles of PUSH/POP overhead per register.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation 1 — Physical Address of an R-register in a Selected Bank

We want to derive the exact internal-RAM byte address that the 8051 hardware accesses when the CPU executes an instruction like `MOV A, R5` and a particular bank is selected by RS1/RS0.

**Step 1 — Identify the bank base address.**  
Each bank is **8 bytes wide**. Banks begin at addresses `00H, 08H, 10H, 18H`.  
The base address of the selected bank is given by multiplying the bank index (0 to 3) by 8.

$$
\text{Bank\_Base} = \text{Bank\_Index} \times 8
$$

**Step 2 — Compute the Bank_Index from RS1 and RS0.**  
The two bits RS1 and RS0 form a 2-bit binary number that *is* the bank index.

$$
\text{Bank\_Index} = 2 \cdot \text{RS1} + 1 \cdot \text{RS0}
$$

**Step 3 — Add the register's offset within the bank.**  
Each register R_n has offset $n$ within its bank (R0 → 0, R1 → 1, …, R7 → 7).

$$
\text{Physical\_Address} = \text{Bank\_Base} + n
$$

**Step 4 — Combine.**

$$
\text{Physical\_Address} = (2 \cdot \text{RS1} + \text{RS0}) \cdot 8 + n
$$

$$
\text{Physical\_Address} = 16 \cdot \text{RS1} + 8 \cdot \text{RS0} + n
$$

**Numerical Example:**  
Suppose **RS1 = 0, RS0 = 1** (Bank 1 is active) and the instruction accesses **R3** (so $n = 3$).

$$
\text{Physical\_Address} = 16(0) + 8(1) + 3 = 0 + 8 + 3 = 11_{10} = 0\text{BH}
$$

So `R3` of Bank 1 lives at internal-RAM address `0BH`. ✓

### 3.2 Derivation 2 — Stack Pointer Behavior Across Nested Subroutine Calls

Let us trace the SP value when a program begins at `SP = 60H` (a typical user-initialized safe value) and the following sequence is executed:

```
LCALL SUB_A     ; pushes 2-byte return address
LCALL SUB_B     ; pushes 2-byte return address  
RET             ; from SUB_B, pops 2 bytes
RET             ; from SUB_A, pops 2 bytes
```

**Step 1 — Initial state.**

$$
\text{SP} = 60\text{H}
$$

**Step 2 — First LCALL SUB_A.**  
Hardware performs two PUSHes (low byte, then high byte of PC).

$$
\text{SP after 1st internal PUSH} = 60\text{H} + 1 = 61\text{H} \quad \text{(low byte of return addr written here)}
$$

$$
\text{SP after 2nd internal PUSH} = 61\text{H} + 1 = 62\text{H} \quad \text{(high byte of return addr written here)}
$$

**Step 3 — Second LCALL SUB_B** (executed inside SUB_A).

$$
\text{SP} = 62\text{H} + 1 = 63\text{H} \quad \text{(low byte)}
$$

$$
\text{SP} = 63\text{H} + 1 = 64\text{H} \quad \text{(high byte)}
$$

**Step 4 — RET inside SUB_B** (returns to SUB_A).  
Hardware performs two POPs.

$$
\text{Read high byte from } 64\text{H}, \quad \text{SP} = 64\text{H} - 1 = 63\text{H}
$$

$$
\text{Read low byte from } 63\text{H}, \quad \text{SP} = 63\text{H} - 1 = 62\text{H}
$$

**Step 5 — RET inside SUB_A** (returns to main).

$$
\text{Read high byte from } 62\text{H}, \quad \text{SP} = 62\text{H} - 1 = 61\text{H}
$$

$$
\text{Read low byte from } 61\text{H}, \quad \text{SP} = 61\text{H} - 1 = 60\text{H}
$$

**Final state:** SP is back to `60H`, exactly as it started. ✓

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix — SP Trace Through a Program

| Event | SP Before | SP After | Notes |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :--- |
| Power-on Reset | undefined | `07H` | Hardware default |
| User init: `MOV SP, #60H` | `07H` | `60H` | Programmer-initialized safe value |
| `PUSH ACC` | `60H` | `61H` | Write ACC at `61H` |
| `PUSH PSW` | `61H` | `62H` | Write PSW at `62H` |
| `POP PSW` | `62H` | `61H` | Read from `62H`, then SP–1 |
| `POP ACC` | `61H` | `60H` | Read from `61H`, then SP–1 |
| `LCALL SUB` | `60H` | `62H` | 2 internal pushes |
| `RET` (from SUB) | `62H` | `60H` | 2 internal pops |

### 3.4 Worked Example — Bank Switching for an ISR

> [!NOTE]
> **Problem:** A main program uses Bank 0. When `INT0` fires, the ISR needs its own private R0–R7. Write the assembly to switch banks and to restore at the end. Assume the ISR uses Bank 2.

**Solution (assembly):**

```assembly
; --- Main program initialization (Bank 0 active by default) ---
        MOV  PSW, #00H        ; Explicitly select Bank 0 (RS1=RS0=0)
        ; ... main code using R0–R7 of Bank 0 ...
        ; (R0–R7 of Bank 1, 2, 3 are preserved untouched)

; --- Interrupt Service Routine for INT0 ---
        ORG  0003H            ; INT0 vector address
INT0_ISR:
        ; Save current PSW if we need to preserve it later,
        ; but here we just need to switch the active bank.
        ; Hardware already pushed PC onto the stack (2 bytes).

        ; Select Bank 2 by setting RS1=1, RS0=0
        ; PSW bit pattern: leave other flags as-is, set only D4
        SETB PSW.4            ; RS1 = 1
        CLR  PSW.3            ; RS0 = 0  → Bank 2 active

        ; Now R0–R7 refer to addresses 10H–17H (Bank 2).
        ; The ISR can freely use R0–R7 without clobbering Bank 0.

        ; ... ISR body using Bank 2's R0–R7 ...
        MOV  R0, #00H         ; Writes to RAM address 10H
        MOV  R7, #0FFH        ; Writes to RAM address 17H

        ; Restore original bank (Bank 0) before RETI
        CLR  PSW.4            ; RS1 = 0
        CLR  PSW.3            ; RS0 = 0  → Bank 0 active again

        RETI                  ; Hardware pops 2 bytes (PC) from stack
```

**Explanation of correctness:**
- After `SETB PSW.4; CLR PSW.3`, the bits `RS1:RS0 = 10₂ = 2`. Per the address formula:  

$$
\text{Addr of R0 in Bank 2} = 16(1) + 8(0) + 0 = 16 = 10\text{H} \checkmark
$$

- Bank 0's R0–R7 (addresses `00H–07H`) were never touched, so when the ISR returns, the main program's R0–R7 are intact.
- The stack is used *only* for PC save/restore (and could be used by the programmer explicitly for further state saving).

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 8051 Internal RAM Memory Map (Lower 32 bytes — Register Banks)

The following block diagram maps the 8051 internal RAM from `00H` to `FFH`, highlighting the four register banks, the bit-addressable region, the general-purpose RAM, and the SFR region.

```mermaid
graph TB
    subgraph LOW32["Internal RAM: 00H to 1FH — Register Banks (32 bytes)"]
        B0["Bank 0\n00H to 07H\nR0 to R7"]
        B1["Bank 1\n08H to 0FH\nR0 to R7"]
        B2["Bank 2\n10H to 17H\nR0 to R7"]
        B3["Bank 3\n18H to 1FH\nR0 to R7"]
    end

    subgraph BITAR["Bit-Addressable Region: 20H to 2FH (16 bytes = 128 bits)"]
        BIT["20H to 2FH\nBit-addressable RAM\n(Bits 00H to 7FH)"]
    end

    subgraph GEN["General-Purpose RAM: 30H to 7FH (80 bytes)"]
        GP["30H to 7FH\nScratch / Stack area\n(user can place SP here)"]
    end

    subgraph SFRREG["SFR Region: 80H to FFH (128 bytes)"]
        SFR["80H to FFH\nSpecial Function Registers\n(SP at 81H, PSW at D0H, etc.)"]
    end

    LOW32 --> BITAR --> GEN --> SFRREG
```

### 4.2 PSW Register Bit Layout (Bank Select Bits Highlighted)

```mermaid
graph LR
    CY["PSW.7\nCY (Carry)"]
    AC["PSW.6\nAC (Aux Carry)"]
    F0["PSW.5\nF0 (User Flag)"]
    RS1["PSW.4\nRS1 (Bank Sel MSB)"]
    RS0["PSW.3\nRS0 (Bank Sel LSB)"]
    OV["PSW.2\nOV (Overflow)"]
    RES["PSW.1\nReserved"]
    P["PSW.0\nP (Parity)"]
    CY --> AC --> F0 --> RS1 --> RS0 --> OV --> RES --> P
```

### 4.3 Bank Selection Truth Table (Active Bank Mapping)

```mermaid
graph TB
    Q["PSW.4 RS1  /  PSW.3 RS0"]:::inp
    Z["Selected Active Bank"]:::out

    Q --> A["00  -> Bank 0  -> 00H to 07H"]
    Q --> B["01  -> Bank 1  -> 08H to 0FH"]
    Q --> C["10  -> Bank 2  -> 10H to 17H"]
    Q --> D["11  -> Bank 3  -> 18H to 1FH"]
    A --> Z
    B --> Z
    C --> Z
    D --> Z

    classDef inp fill:#cfe8ff,stroke:#0050b3,color:#000
    classDef out fill:#d9f7be,stroke:#389e0d,color:#000
```

### 4.4 Stack Operation Flow — PUSH and POP Sequence

```mermaid
graph TB
    PUSH1["PUSH src"]:::op
    PUSH1A["Step 1: SP <- SP + 1"]:::step
    PUSH1B["Step 2: RAM SP <- src byte"]:::step

    POP1["POP dst"]:::op
    POP1A["Step 1: dst <- RAM SP"]:::step
    POP1B["Step 2: SP <- SP - 1"]:::step

    PUSH1 --> PUSH1A --> PUSH1B
    POP1 --> POP1A --> POP1B

    classDef op fill:#fff1b8,stroke:#d48806,color:#000
    classDef step fill:#f0f5ff,stroke:#2f54eb,color:#000
```

### 4.5 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Register Bank Switching in an ISR

```mermaid
graph TB
    MAIN["Main Program\nUses Bank 0 (R0-R7 at 00H-07H)"]:::main
    INTRQ["INT0 Interrupt Request\n(Hardware Detected)"]:::hw
    HWSAVE["Hardware Auto-Action:\nPUSH PC-lo, then PC-hi\nSP increments twice"]:::hw
    JMP["Jump to ISR Vector 0003H"]:::hw
    ISRBODY["ISR Body\nSETB PSW.4 / CLR PSW.3\nNow active bank = Bank 2 (10H-17H)"]:::isr
    USEBANK["ISR Uses R0-R7 of Bank 2\n(Main's R0-R7 are SAFE)"]:::isr
    RESTORE["CLR PSW.4 / CLR PSW.3\nActive bank = Bank 0 again"]:::isr
    RETI["RETI Instruction:\nPOP PC-hi, POP PC-lo\nSP decrements twice"]:::hw
    RESUME["Main Program Resumes\nwith R0-R7 of Bank 0 intact"]:::main

    MAIN --> INTRQ --> HWSAVE --> JMP --> ISRBODY --> USEBANK --> RESTORE --> RETI --> RESUME

    classDef main fill:#d9f7be,stroke:#389e0d,color:#000
    classDef hw fill:#ffd6d6,stroke:#cf1322,color:#000
    classDef isr fill:#fff1b8,stroke:#d48806,color:#000
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024, Model Paper]**
**Explain the register banks in the 8051 microcontroller. How are they selected?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The 8051 microcontroller contains **four register banks** (Bank 0, 1, 2, 3), each consisting of **eight 8-bit registers R0–R7**. These banks occupy internal RAM addresses **00H–1FH** (32 bytes total):

- Bank 0: `00H–07H`
- Bank 1: `08H–0FH`
- Bank 2: `10H–17H`
- Bank 3: `18H–1FH`

Only **one bank is active at a time**, and the active bank is selected by the **RS1 (PSW.4)** and **RS0 (PSW.3)** bits of the **Program Status Word (PSW)** register. After reset, **Bank 0** is selected by default (RS1 = RS0 = 0). The active bank can be changed at any time by setting/clearing the RS1, RS0 bits of PSW. **(3 marks)**

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**What is the role of the Stack Pointer (SP) in the 8051? Why is SP initialized to a value other than 07H in most programs?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Stack Pointer (SP, SFR at 81H)** is an 8-bit register that points to the **top of the stack** in internal RAM. It is used by the **PUSH, POP, CALL, RET, RETI**, and **interrupt-handling** instructions. The stack grows upward (SP is incremented before a PUSH, decremented after a POP).

After power-on reset, SP is initialized to **07H**. If left unchanged, the first PUSH will write to RAM address `08H`, which **overlaps with Bank 1's R0 register**, and continued stack operations will collide with **Bank 1, Bank 2, Bank 3, and the bit-addressable region**. Therefore, programmers typically reinitialize SP (e.g., `MOV SP, #60H` or `MOV SP, #0CFH` in 8052) to relocate the stack into the safe general-purpose RAM area, preventing accidental corruption of register banks and bit variables. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Question on Register Banks**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Draw the **internal RAM memory map of the 8051** from `00H` to `FFH` and clearly label the **four register banks**, the **bit-addressable area**, the **general-purpose RAM**, and the **SFR region**. List the **PSW bits RS0 and RS1** and show the truth table for all four bank combinations. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A main program is executing with **Bank 0** active. An **external interrupt INT0** arrives, and you must service it using **Bank 2** (so that the main program's R0–R7 are preserved untouched). Write the complete **8051 assembly code** for the ISR, including the **vector address**, the **bank-switching instructions**, an example use of R0 and R7, the **bank-restoration instructions**, and the proper **return** instruction. Also explain *why* the main program's R0–R7 remain intact after the ISR returns. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Memory Map and Bank Selection Table (7 marks)**

The lower 128 bytes of internal RAM (`00H–7FH`) of the 8051 are organized as shown below:

| Address Range | Region | Purpose |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| `00H – 07H` | **Bank 0** | R0–R7 of Bank 0 |
| `08H – 0FH` | **Bank 1** | R0–R7 of Bank 1 |
| `10H – 17H` | **Bank 2** | R0–R7 of Bank 2 |
| `18H – 1FH` | **Bank 3** | R0–R7 of Bank 3 |
| `20H – 2FH` | **Bit-addressable RAM** | 128 individually addressable bits (bit addresses `00H–7FH`) |
| `30H – 7FH` | **General-purpose RAM** | Scratchpad, user data, **stack area** |
| `80H – FFH` | **SFR Region** | Special Function Registers (SP at `81H`, PSW at `D0H`, ACC, B, DPTR, etc.) |

**PSW Bank-Select Truth Table:**

| RS1 (PSW.4) | RS0 (PSW.3) | Selected Bank | Address Range |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 0 | 0 | **Bank 0** | `00H – 07H` |
| 0 | 1 | **Bank 1** | `08H – 0FH` |
| 1 | 0 | **Bank 2** | `10H – 17H` |
| 1 | 1 | **Bank 3** | `18H – 1FH` |

The active bank is therefore calculated as:

$$
\text{Bank\_Index} = 2 \cdot \text{RS1} + \text{RS0}, \quad \text{Base\_Addr} = 8 \cdot \text{Bank\_Index}
$$

**(b) — ISR Assembly Code (7 marks)**

```assembly
; ===========================================
; MAIN PROGRAM INITIALIZATION (Bank 0 active)
; ===========================================
        ORG   0000H
        JMP   MAIN

        ; ---- INT0 Interrupt Vector (0003H) ----
        ORG   0003H
        LJMP  ISR_INT0        ; Jump to actual ISR (3-byte long jump gives range)

; ===========================================
; MAIN PROGRAM
; ===========================================
MAIN:   MOV   PSW, #00H      ; Explicitly select Bank 0 (RS1=0, RS0=0)
        SETB  IT0             ; INT0 edge-triggered
        SETB  EX0             ; Enable INT0
        SETB  EA              ; Enable global interrupt

        ; ... main code uses R0-R7 of Bank 0 (00H-07H) ...
LOOP:   MOV   R0, #55H       ; R0 here is at internal RAM address 00H
        MOV   R7, #0AAH      ; R7 here is at internal RAM address 07H
        SJMP  LOOP

; ===========================================
; ISR for INT0 — uses Bank 2 (10H-17H)
; ===========================================
ISR_INT0:
        ; Note: hardware has already pushed 16-bit PC onto stack
        ;       (SP is now 2 greater than before the interrupt)

        ; Select Bank 2: RS1=1, RS0=0
        SETB  PSW.4          ; RS1 = 1
        CLR   PSW.3          ; RS0 = 0

        ; Now R0-R7 refer to RAM addresses 10H-17H
        MOV   R0, #11H       ; Writes to address 10H (Bank 2 R0)
        MOV   R1, #22H       ; Writes to address 11H (Bank 2 R1)
        MOV   R7, #0FFH      ; Writes to address 17H (Bank 2 R7)

        ; ... (Bank 0's R0-R7 at 00H-07H are untouched) ...

        ; Restore active bank to Bank 0 before returning
        CLR   PSW.4          ; RS1 = 0
        CLR   PSW.3          ; RS0 = 0

        RETI                 ; Pops 2-byte PC from stack; SP back to original
```

**Why the main program's R0–R7 are preserved:**

- The main program used **R0–R7 of Bank 0**, which live at internal RAM addresses `00H–07H`.
- The ISR explicitly changed **RS1:RS0 to 10₂ (Bank 2)**, so its `R0–R7` instructions accessed addresses `10H–17H` instead.
- Therefore, the ISR's PUSH/POP/MOV R7 instructions wrote to addresses `10H–17H` — *not* to `00H–07H`. The Bank 0 data at `00H–07H` is **physically untouched**.
- On RETI, the ISR restored RS1:RS0 to 00 (Bank 0) before returning. The main program resumes and finds its R0–R7 exactly as it left them.
- This technique is called **register bank switching** and is the standard 8051 method for **fast, zero-copy context preservation in ISRs**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Comprehensive Question on Stack**

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the **8051 Stack Pointer (SP)** register, its **reset value**, and the **direction of stack growth**. Describe the step-by-step hardware action for both the **PUSH** and **POP** instructions. Why must the programmer **reinitialize SP** in most applications? **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A program starts with `SP = 60H`. Trace the exact SP value and the contents of the relevant internal RAM locations after each step of the following instruction sequence: `PUSH ACC` (assume ACC = 0A5H), `PUSH 30H` (assume internal RAM `30H = 0F3H`), `POP 30H`, `POP ACC`. Then explain what value the **ACC** register will hold at the end. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(a) — Stack Pointer, PUSH/POP Internals, Reinitialization (7 marks)**

**Stack Pointer (SP):** The 8051 contains an 8-bit **Stack Pointer (SP)** special function register located at internal SFR address **`81H`**. SP always holds the address of the **current top of the stack** in internal data memory (`00H–7FH` for 8051, `00H–FFH` for 8052).

**Reset Value:** On power-on reset, **SP = 07H**. This means the very first PUSH would write to RAM address `08H`, which overlaps with Bank 1's R0. Subsequent pushes continue through Bank 1 → Bank 2 → Bank 3 → bit-addressable region, destroying critical data.

**Stack Growth Direction:** The 8051 stack grows **upward** (toward higher addresses). For PUSH, **SP is incremented first**, then the data byte is written at the *new* SP address. For POP, the data is read from the *current* SP address, then SP is decremented.

**PUSH instruction — Hardware steps:**
1. `SP ← SP + 1`
2. `RAM[SP] ← source_byte`

**POP instruction — Hardware steps:**
1. `destination ← RAM[SP]`
2. `SP ← SP − 1`

**Why reinitialize SP:** Because the reset value of 07H causes the stack to overwrite register banks and the bit-addressable area, nearly all production code immediately executes `MOV SP, #0XXH` (typically `60H`, `70H`, or higher) to relocate the stack into the **general-purpose RAM region (`30H–7FH`)** that is free of critical data. This prevents stack growth from clobbering active register banks. **(7 marks)**

**(b) — Trace of the Stack (7 marks)**

**Initial State:**  
`SP = 60H`, `ACC = 0A5H`, internal RAM address `30H` contains `0F3H`.

**Step 1 — `PUSH ACC` (pushes ACC = 0A5H):**
- SP becomes `60H + 1 = 61H`
- The byte `0A5H` is written to internal RAM address **`61H`**
- **SP after Step 1 = 61H**

**Step 2 — `PUSH 30H` (pushes the *value* at RAM[30H] = 0F3H):**
- SP becomes `61H + 1 = 62H`
- The byte `0F3H` is written to internal RAM address **`62H`**
- **SP after Step 2 = 62H**

**Step 3 — `POP 30H` (pops top-of-stack into RAM[30H]):**
- Read from RAM at current SP `62H` → value is `0F3H`
- This value is written into internal RAM address `30H` (no change, since the data was 0F3H originally)
- SP becomes `62H − 1 = 61H`
- **SP after Step 3 = 61H**

**Step 4 — `POP ACC` (pops top-of-stack into ACC):**
- Read from RAM at current SP `61H` → value is `0A5H`
- ACC becomes `0A5H`
- SP becomes `61H − 1 = 60H`
- **SP after Step 4 = 60H** (back to original)

**Final value of ACC:** `0A5H` (unchanged from start, as expected for a balanced PUSH/POP).

**Summary Table:**

| Step | Instruction | SP Before | SP After | RAM[61H] | RAM[62H] | ACC | RAM[30H] |
| :---: | :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| 1 | `PUSH ACC` | 60H | 61H | 0A5H | — | 0A5H | F3H |
| 2 | `PUSH 30H` | 61H | 62H | 0A5H | 0F3H | 0A5H | F3H |
| 3 | `POP 30H` | 62H | 61H | 0A5H | 0F3H | 0A5H | F3H |
| 4 | `POP ACC` | 61H | 60H | 0A5H | 0F3H | **0A5H** | F3H |

**(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Register Banks and Stack Questions:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting that PUSH increments SP *before* writing** — Many students write "PUSH first stores at SP, then SP becomes SP+1." This is the **opposite** of 8051 hardware behavior. Marks lost: ~1 per sub-question.
> 2. **Confusing POP order with PUSH order** — For multi-byte pops (e.g., RETI pops 2 bytes), the **high byte is read first, then the low byte**, because the stack grows upward. Mis-stating this is a frequent mistake.
> 3. **Wrong bit positions for RS0/RS1** — RS0 is **PSW.3** (NOT PSW.0), and RS1 is **PSW.4** (NOT PSW.7). PSW.7 is the **Carry flag**, and PSW.0 is the **Parity flag**. Confusing these loses full marks.
> 4. **Ignoring the SP = 07H reset pitfall** — A common 14-mark question asks "why do we reinitialize SP?" Students who answer vaguely "to avoid problems" lose marks. You must state the **specific reason**: first PUSH writes to `08H`, overlapping Bank 1 R0.
> 5. **Treating Bank 0, 1, 2, 3 as four *copies* of R0–R7** — They are not copies; they are four *physically distinct* byte locations in RAM, and the active bank is selected by RS bits. Wording this imprecisely loses conceptual marks.
> 6. **Stating the wrong default bank after reset** — It is **Bank 0**, not Bank 1, not Bank 3. (RS1 = RS0 = 0.)
> 7. **Forgetting to clear unused register-bank selection bits** when restoring PSW in an ISR — failing to do so may leave the bank in an undefined state.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist — Register Banks and Stack (8051):**
>
> - **Number of register banks in 8051:** **4** (Bank 0, 1, 2, 3)
> - **Each bank:** **8 registers** (R0–R7), each **8 bits** wide
> - **Memory footprint:** **32 bytes** total at `00H – 1FH`
> - **Bank 0:** `00H–07H`; **Bank 1:** `08H–0FH`; **Bank 2:** `10H–17H`; **Bank 3:** `18H–1FH`
> - **Active bank selection bits:** **RS1 = PSW.4**, **RS0 = PSW.3**
> - **Default active bank (after reset):** **Bank 0**
> - **Physical address formula:** $\text{Addr} = 16 \cdot \text{RS1} + 8 \cdot \text{RS0} + n$ where $n$ is the register index 0–7
> - **Stack Pointer (SP) SFR address:** **`81H`**, **8 bits wide**
> - **SP reset value:** **`07H`** → first PUSH writes to `08H` (clobbers Bank 1 R0)
> - **Recommended SP init in user code:** `MOV SP, #60H` (or similar safe value)
> - **Stack growth direction:** **Upward** (toward higher addresses)
> - **PUSH steps:** `SP = SP + 1` *first*, then `RAM[SP] = source`
> - **POP steps:** `dest = RAM[SP]` *first*, then `SP = SP − 1`
> - **CALL (LCALL/ACALL):** Hardware **pushes 2 bytes** (PC low first, then PC high)
> - **RET/RETI:** Hardware **pops 2 bytes** (PC high first, then PC low)
> - **Max stack depth:** **256 bytes** (limited by 8-bit SP)
> - **Primary uses of stack:** Subroutine return addresses, ISR context save, PUSH/POP scratch storage
> - **Primary use of register banks:** **Fast ISR context switching** without PUSH/POP overhead
> - **Bit-addressable region:** `20H – 2FH` (**128 addressable bits**)
> - **SFR region:** `80H – FFH` (note: only some addresses are valid SFRs)

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Special Function Registers (SFRs)
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET613 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Special Function Registers (SFRs)

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** are dedicated, addressable on-chip hardware registers in the **8051 microcontroller** that occupy the upper **128 bytes** of the internal RAM space (addresses **0x80 to 0xFF**). Each SFR is hard-wired to a specific peripheral or control function of the CPU (e.g., Accumulator, I/O Ports, Timers, Serial Port, Interrupt System, Stack Pointer, and Program Status Word).

In the 8051 architecture, the internal data memory is divided as follows:

| Address Range | Region | Purpose |
|:---:|:---:|:---|
| `0x00` – `0x1F` | Lower 32 Bytes | Four Register Banks (R0–R7 × 4) + Bit-addressable zone |
| `0x20` – `0x2F` | 16 Bytes | Bit-addressable scratch area (16 × 8 = 128 bits) |
| `0x30` – `0x7F` | 80 Bytes | General Purpose RAM (user scratchpad) |
| **`0x80` – `0xFF`** | **Upper 128 Bytes** | **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** |

Only **21 of the 128 SFR addresses are physically implemented** in the standard 8051. The remaining addresses are "shadow" or unused space and are not available for general storage.

> [!NOTE]
> **SFR Bit Addressing:** Out of the 21 implemented SFRs, **11 are bit-addressable** (addresses ending in `0x0` or `0x8`). This means individual bits within these registers can be directly set/cleared using bit-manipulation instructions.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — "The Control Panel"

> [!TIP]
> **Think of the 8051 as a Factory Manager and the SFRs as the Control Panel on the Manager's Desk.**

Imagine a factory supervisor's office. The supervisor (the **CPU**) is the brain, but he cannot directly see the machines, conveyor belts, or alarms. Instead, he has a **dedicated control panel** (the **SFRs**) mounted on his desk. Each switch, dial, and indicator on the panel controls ONE specific thing:

- One switch = Motor ON/OFF (Port 1 register)
- One dial = Conveyor belt speed (Timer register)
- One indicator light = Emergency alarm (Interrupt flag)
- One digital counter = Items produced (Counter register)

The supervisor cannot *store* spare parts on this panel — its only job is **control and observation**. Similarly, the SFRs are not for storing your variables; they are pre-wired windows through which your program talks to the 8051's internal hardware.

## 1.3 Physical & Standard Metrics

- **Total SFR Memory Space:** **128 bytes** (address range `0x80`–`0xFF`)
- **Implemented SFRs:** **21 registers** (rest are reserved/unused)
- **Bit-addressable SFRs:** **11 registers** (16 bits each, except 8-bit byte-addressable ones)
- **Data Bus Width:** **8 bits** (all SFRs are 8-bit registers)
- **Reset Value:** Most SFRs are reset to **0x00**, except `SP` (resets to `0x07`) and `P0–P3` (resets to `0xFF`)

---

## 1.4 Why SFRs Matter in KTU Examinations

In KTU's 8051 syllabus, SFRs form the **foundation of all peripheral programming**. Whether you are configuring a timer, reading an I/O pin, or enabling an interrupt, you are *always* manipulating an SFR. The 8051 has **NO dedicated MMIO instructions** — peripherals are controlled purely by reading/writing the SFR memory-mapped addresses. This is the **memory-mapped I/O** paradigm.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📗 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Complete SFR Map of 8051

The 21 implemented Special Function Registers are arranged in a specific layout. KTU examiners love asking students to "list the SFRs and their addresses" — so memorize the **bold-highlighted** ones below.

| SFR Symbol | Name | Address | Bit-Addressable? | Reset Value |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| **A (ACC)** | Accumulator | `0xE0` | ✅ Yes | `0x00` |
| **B** | B Register (for MUL/DIV) | `0xF0` | ✅ Yes | `0x00` |
| **PSW** | Program Status Word | `0xD0` | ✅ Yes | `0x00` |
| **SP** | Stack Pointer | `0x81` | ❌ No | `0x07` |
| **DPL** | Data Pointer Low | `0x82` | ❌ No | `0x00` |
| **DPH** | Data Pointer High | `0x83` | ❌ No | `0x00` |
| **DPTR** | (16-bit: DPH:DPL) | `0x82–0x83` | ❌ No | `0x0000` |
| **P0** | Port 0 Latch | `0x80` | ✅ Yes | `0xFF` |
| **P1** | Port 1 Latch | `0x90` | ✅ Yes | `0xFF` |
| **P2** | Port 2 Latch | `0xA0` | ✅ Yes | `0xFF` |
| **P3** | Port 3 Latch | `0xB0` | ✅ Yes | `0xFF` |
| **IP** | Interrupt Priority | `0xB8` | ✅ Yes | `0x00` |
| **IE** | Interrupt Enable | `0xA8` | ✅ Yes | `0x00` |
| **TMOD** | Timer Mode | `0x89` | ❌ No | `0x00` |
| **TCON** | Timer/Counter Control | `0x88` | ✅ Yes | `0x00` |
| **TH0** | Timer 0 High | `0x8C` | ❌ No | `0x00` |
| **TL0** | Timer 0 Low | `0x8A` | ❌ No | `0x00` |
| **TH1** | Timer 1 High | `0x8D` | ❌ No | `0x00` |
| **TL1** | Timer 1 Low | `0x8B` | ❌ No | `0x00` |
| **SCON** | Serial Control | `0x98` | ✅ Yes | `0x00` |
| **SBUF** | Serial Data Buffer | `0x99` | ❌ No | `0x00` |
| **PCON** | Power Control | `0x87` | ❌ No | `0x00` |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Bit-Addressable SFRs Trick:** SFR addresses ending in **0H or 8H** are bit-addressable. Example: `0xE0` (ACC) → `0xE0`, `0xE1`, `0xE2`... `0xE7` map to bits `ACC.0` through `ACC.7`.

## 2.2 The Program Status Word (PSW) — Most Examined SFR

PSW at address `0xD0` is **THE** most-asked SFR in KTU papers. It is an 8-bit status register whose bits are described below.

| Bit | Symbol | Address (Bit) | Name | Function |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|:---|
| PSW.7 | **CY** | `0xD7` | Carry Flag | Set on arithmetic carry/borrow |
| PSW.6 | **AC** | `0xD6` | Auxiliary Carry | Set on carry from bit 3 → bit 4 (BCD math) |
| PSW.5 | **F0** | `0xD5` | User Flag 0 | General purpose flag for user |
| PSW.4 | **RS1** | `0xD4` | Register Bank Select 1 | Bank selection MSB |
| PSW.3 | **RS0** | `0xD3` | Register Bank Select 0 | Bank selection LSB |
| PSW.2 | **OV** | `0xD2` | Overflow Flag | Set on signed arithmetic overflow |
| PSW.1 | — | `0xD1` | Reserved | User-definable (8052) |
| PSW.0 | **P** | `0xD0` | Parity Flag | Set if A has odd number of 1s |

## 2.3 Register Bank Selection (RS1, RS0)

The two bits **RS1** and **RS0** in PSW select which of the **four register banks** in the lower 32 bytes is currently active as R0–R7.

| RS1 | RS0 | Bank Selected | Address Range of R0–R7 |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| `0` | `0` | **Bank 0** | `0x00` – `0x07` |
| `0` | `1` | **Bank 1** | `0x08` – `0x0F` |
| `1` | `0` | **Bank 2** | `0x10` – `0x17` |
| `1` | `1` | **Bank 3** | `0x18` – `0x1F` |

> [!NOTE]
> **Default Bank on Reset:** RS1:RS0 = `00` → **Bank 0** is active after power-on. Stack Pointer defaults to `0x07`, so the first PUSH writes to `0x08` (Bank 1's R0).

## 2.4 Detailed Bit-by-Bit Working of Key Flags

### 🔹 Carry Flag (CY)
- Set to `1` when an addition produces a carry out of bit 7, or when a subtraction requires a borrow.
- Example: `0xFF + 0x01 = 0x100` → result in A is `0x00`, CY = `1`.
- Used heavily in multi-byte arithmetic and rotate instructions (`RLC A`, `RRC A`).

### 🔹 Auxiliary Carry (AC)
- Set when there is a carry from bit **3 to bit 4** during addition.
- Critical for **BCD (Binary-Coded Decimal) arithmetic** using the `DA A` (Decimal Adjust) instruction.

### 🔹 Parity Flag (P)
- Automatically set to `1` if the Accumulator contains an **odd number of 1-bits** (this is the "8051 odd-parity convention" — confusingly, `P=1` means *odd* parity).
- Hardware sets this flag after every instruction that modifies the Accumulator.

### 🔹 Overflow Flag (OV)
- Set on **signed arithmetic overflow** (e.g., adding two positive numbers and getting a negative result).
- Computed as: $OV = C_7 \oplus C_6$, where $C_7$ is the carry into bit 7 and $C_6$ is the carry out of bit 6.

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Engineering Utility |
|:---|:---|:---|
| SFR Address Range | $0x80 \leq \text{Address} \leq 0xFF$ | Identify whether a memory-mapped location is SFR or GPR |
| Bit Address of SFR bit `n` | $\text{BitAddr} = \text{ByteAddr} + n$ | Direct bit manipulation in C/ASM |
| Total SFRs implemented | **21 registers** | Standard 8051 (8052 has more) |
| Number of bit-addressable SFRs | **11 registers** | Confirms `128` user-addressable bits in SFR zone |
| Parity Bit Setting | $P = 1$ if $\text{popcount}(A) \mod 2 = 1$ | Serial communication error check |
| Overflow Flag | $OV = C_7 \oplus C_6$ | Signed math correctness |
| Register Bank Selection | $\text{Bank} = 2 \cdot RS1 + RS0$ | Context switching in RTOS-like code |
| Stack Pointer Reset | $SP_{reset} = 0x07$ | First PUSH writes to `0x08` |
| 16-bit DPTR | $DPTR = (DPH \times 256) + DPL$ | External memory addressing up to 64KB |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

In industrial automation, SFRs are how a 8051-based PLC (Programmable Logic Controller) controls a production line:
- `P1` reads sensor inputs (limit switches, photoelectric eyes)
- `P0` drives actuator relays
- `TMOD`/`TH0`/`TL0` configure a timer to schedule a conveyor pulse every 100 ms
- `SCON`/`SBUF` send diagnostic data to a host PC over RS-232
- `PSW` flags determine error-handling paths in the firmware

Without understanding SFRs, **no embedded firmware can be written** for the 8051.

# 📙 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Walkthrough — Effect of Instructions on PSW

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following derivations show **step-by-step** how PSW flags get set after arithmetic operations. Every step is explicitly expanded to satisfy KTU's "show your work" valuation standard.

### 🔢 Example 1: Addition `0xA5 + 0x58`

**Binary Representations:**

$$
0xA5 = 1010\,0101_2
$$

$$
0x58 = 0101\,1000_2
$$

**Step-by-step column addition (LSB → MSB):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bit 0:} & \quad 1 + 0 = 1 \quad (\text{no carry}) \\
\text{Bit 1:} & \quad 0 + 0 = 0 \quad (\text{no carry}) \\
\text{Bit 2:} & \quad 1 + 0 = 1 \quad (\text{no carry}) \\
\text{Bit 3:} & \quad 0 + 1 = 1 \quad (\text{no carry}) \\
\text{Bit 4:} & \quad 0 + 1 = 1 \quad (\text{no carry}) \\
\text{Bit 5:} & \quad 1 + 0 = 1 \quad (\text{no carry}) \\
\text{Bit 6:} & \quad 0 + 1 = 1 \quad (\text{no carry}) \\
\text{Bit 7:} & \quad 1 + 0 = 1 \quad (\text{no carry})
\end{aligned}
$$

**Resulting Sum:**
$$
1010\,0101 + 0101\,1000 = 1111\,1101_2 = 0xFD
$$

**PSW Flag Analysis:**

| Flag | Calculation | Value | Reasoning |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---|
| **CY** | Carry out of bit 7 = 0 | `0` | $1+0$ at MSB, no overflow past 8 bits |
| **AC** | Carry from bit 3→4 = 0 | `0` | $0+1$ at bit 3, no carry generated |
| **P** | popcount(0xFD) = 7 (odd) | `1` | 7 ones → odd → P=1 |
| **OV** | $C_7 \oplus C_6 = 0 \oplus 0$ | `0` | No signed overflow |

---

### 🔢 Example 2: Addition `0xC3 + 0x69` (Demonstrates CY and OV)

**Binary Representations:**

$$
0xC3 = 1100\,0011_2, \quad 0x69 = 0110\,1001_2
$$

**Step-by-step addition:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Bit 0:} & \quad 1 + 1 = 0, \text{ carry} = 1 \\
\text{Bit 1:} & \quad 1 + 0 + 1 = 0, \text{ carry} = 1 \\
\text{Bit 2:} & \quad 0 + 0 + 1 = 1, \text{ carry} = 0 \\
\text{Bit 3:} & \quad 0 + 1 + 0 = 1, \text{ carry} = 0 \\
\text{Bit 4:} & \quad 0 + 0 + 0 = 0, \text{ carry} = 0 \\
\text{Bit 5:} & \quad 0 + 1 + 0 = 1, \text{ carry} = 0 \\
\text{Bit 6:} & \quad 1 + 1 + 0 = 0, \text{ carry} = 1 \\
\text{Bit 7:} & \quad 1 + 0 + 1 = 0, \text{ carry} = 1
\end{aligned}
$$

**Sum in A:** $0011\,1100_2 = 0x3C$
**Carry Out ($C_7$):** `1` → **CY = 1**
**Carry from bit 6→7 ($C_6$):** `1`

**Overflow Calculation:**

$$
OV = C_7 \oplus C_6 = 1 \oplus 1 = 0
$$

**Auxiliary Carry:** Carry from bit 3→4 = 0 (since bit-3 sum was $0+1+0=1$ with no carry out). So **AC = 0**.

**Parity:** popcount(0x3C) = popcount(0011 1100) = 4 (even) → **P = 0**

**Final PSW = 0xD0 + 0x00 = 0xC0** (CY=1, AC=0, F0=0, RS1=0, RS0=0, OV=0, –, P=0) → binary: `1100 0000` → `0xC0` ✅

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Register Bank Switching Derivation

> [!NOTE]
> KTU often asks: *"Write instructions to switch from Bank 0 to Bank 3. Show PSW state before and after."*

**Initial State (after RESET):**
- RS1 = 0, RS0 = 0 → Bank 0 active
- PSW = 0x00 (assuming no flags set)

**Required Final State:**
- RS1 = 1, RS0 = 1 → Bank 3 active

**Step-by-Step Derivation:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{PSW}_{\text{new}} & = \text{PSW}_{\text{old}} \; \vert \; (1 \ll 4) \; \vert \; (1 \ll 3) \\
& = 0x00 \; \vert \; 0x10 \; \vert \; 0x08 \\
& = 0x18
\end{aligned}
$$

**Assembly equivalent (using SETB instructions):**

```asm
SETB PSW.4      ; Set RS1 = 1
SETB PSW.3      ; Set RS0 = 1
; Now PSW = 0x18 → Bank 3 is active
; R0 is now at 0x18, R1 at 0x19, ..., R7 at 0x1F
```

**C equivalent:**

```c
PSW = PSW | 0x18;   // Sets bits 4 and 3 of PSW
```

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix — Stack Pointer (SP) Behavior

The 8051 stack grows **upward** (toward higher addresses). Default SP = `0x07`.

**Trace of PUSH instructions:**

| Instruction | Action | SP After | Memory Written |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|
| `MOV SP, #0x60` | Initialize SP manually | `0x60` | — |
| `PUSH ACC` | $\text{SP} \leftarrow \text{SP}+1$ ; Write A to [SP] | `0x61` | `[0x61] ← A` |
| `PUSH 0xF0` | Push B register | `0x62` | `[0x62] ← B` |
| `POP 0xF0` | Read [SP] into B, then SP–1 | `0x61` | `B ← [0x62]` |
| `POP ACC` | Read [SP] into A, then SP–1 | `0x60` | `A ← [0x61]` |

**Key Derivation (general PUSH behavior):**

$$
\text{SP}_{new} = \text{SP}_{old} + 1
$$

$$
\text{Memory}[\text{SP}_{new}] = \text{Source Register}
$$

**Key Derivation (general POP behavior):**

$$
\text{Destination} = \text{Memory}[\text{SP}_{old}]
$$

$$
\text{SP}_{new} = \text{SP}_{old} - 1
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** Stack is **Full Descending Ascent** terminology confusion. Remember: 8051 stack grows **UP** (toward higher addresses), not down. Many students lose marks by writing "stack grows downward."

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Data Pointer (DPTR) Operation

DPTR is a **16-bit register** formed by concatenating DPH (high byte) and DPL (low byte). It is used to access external data memory (XDATA) up to **64 KB**.

**Address formed by DPTR:**

$$
\text{External Address} = (DPH \times 256) + DPL
$$

**Worked Example:** Load `DPTR = 0x3F2A` to read external RAM at address `0x3F2A`.

**Step-by-step:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Step 1: } & DPL = 0x2A, \; DPH = 0x3F \\
\text{Step 2: } & \text{Combined address} = (0x3F \times 0x100) + 0x2A \\
\text{Step 3: } & = 0x3F00 + 0x2A = 0x3F2A \\
\text{Step 4: } & MOVX A, @DPTR \;\Rightarrow\; A \leftarrow \text{XRAM}[0x3F2A]
\end{aligned}
$$

**Assembly:**

```asm
MOV DPL, #0x2A    ; Low byte
MOV DPH, #0x3F    ; High byte
MOVX A, @DPTR     ; Read external RAM[0x3F2A] into A
```

# 📕 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### ❓ Question 1: `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`
**"List any five Special Function Registers of 8051 along with their addresses and functions."**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):

The five SFRs of 8051 are:

1. **Accumulator (A/ACC), Address: 0xE0** — The primary working register for all arithmetic and logical operations; serves as one of the operands and the destination for most instructions.

2. **B Register, Address: 0xF0** — Used exclusively as a second operand in `MUL AB` and `DIV AB` instructions; can be used as a general-purpose scratch register otherwise.

3. **Program Status Word (PSW), Address: 0xD0** — Contains the arithmetic flags (CY, AC, OV, P), user flag (F0), and register bank select bits (RS1, RS0).

4. **Stack Pointer (SP), Address: 0x81** — Points to the top of the stack in internal RAM; reset to 0x07 and grows upward on PUSH.

5. **Data Pointer (DPTR), Address: 0x82–0x83** — A 16-bit register (DPH:DPL) used to access external data memory up to 64 KB.

> Each SFR with name + address + function → **1 mark**, total 5 × 0.6 ≈ 3 marks.

---

### ❓ Question 2: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`
**"Explain the function of the Parity flag and Auxiliary Carry flag in the PSW register of 8051."**

#### ✅ Model Answer (3 Marks):

**Parity Flag (P) — PSW.0:**
- The Parity flag is **automatically set to 1 by hardware** whenever the Accumulator contains an **odd number of 1-bits** (this is the 8051's "odd-parity" convention).
- Example: If `A = 0xA5 = 1010 0101`, the popcount is 4 (even) → `P = 0`. If `A = 0xA3 = 1010 0011`, popcount is 5 (odd) → `P = 1`.
- **Utility:** It is primarily used for **error detection in serial communication** protocols.

**Auxiliary Carry Flag (AC) — PSW.6:**
- AC is set to 1 when there is a **carry from bit 3 to bit 4** of the Accumulator during an addition (or borrow in subtraction).
- Example: `0x08 + 0x09 = 0x11` → carry from D3 to D4 occurs → `AC = 1`.
- **Utility:** It is essential for **BCD (Binary-Coded Decimal) arithmetic** when used with the `DA A` (Decimal Adjust Accumulator) instruction.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — With Internal Choice)

### ❓ Question A: `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2024]`

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the internal RAM organization of 8051 microcontroller. Clearly differentiate the SFR area from the general-purpose RAM. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the PSW register of 8051 in detail. List all its bits, their addresses, and describe the Register Bank selection mechanism with an example. **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:

**Internal RAM Organization:**

The 8051 has 256 bytes of internal data memory (IDATA), divided as:

| Region | Address Range | Size | Function |
|:---|:---:|:---:|:---|
| Register Banks | `0x00` – `0x1F` | 32 bytes | Four banks of R0–R7 |
| Bit-Addressable | `0x20` – `0x2F` | 16 bytes | 128 individually addressable bits |
| General Purpose RAM | `0x30` – `0x7F` | 80 bytes | User scratchpad area |
| **SFR Region** | **`0x80` – `0xFF`** | **128 bytes** | **21 implemented hardware control registers** |

**Key Differences:**

| Feature | General Purpose RAM (`0x00`–`0x7F`) | SFR Region (`0x80`–`0xFF`) |
|:---|:---|:---|
| Purpose | User variable storage | Hardware peripheral control |
| Addressing | Direct or indirect | Direct only (by name in C) |
| Bit-addressing | Only `0x20`–`0x2F` (16 bytes) | Only SFRs at `0x80`, `0x88`, `0x90`, etc. (11 SFRs) |
| On-reset value | Indeterminate | Mostly `0x00`; `SP = 0x07`; `P0–P3 = 0xFF` |

**Diagram (as previously shown in Section 4.1):** *(3 marks for diagram + 4 marks for explanation)*

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:

**PSW (Program Status Word) at Address 0xD0:**

PSW is an 8-bit **bit-addressable** SFR that holds the status flags resulting from arithmetic and logical operations.

| Bit | Symbol | Bit Address | Description |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 7 | CY | `0xD7` | Carry Flag |
| 6 | AC | `0xD6` | Auxiliary Carry Flag |
| 5 | F0 | `0xD5` | User-Defined Flag 0 |
| 4 | RS1 | `0xD4` | Register Bank Select 1 |
| 3 | RS0 | `0xD3` | Register Bank Select 0 |
| 2 | OV | `0xD2` | Overflow Flag |
| 1 | — | `0xD1` | Reserved (User bit on 8052) |
| 0 | P | `0xD0` | Parity Flag |

**Register Bank Selection:**

The lower 32 bytes of internal RAM are organized as **four register banks** of 8 registers each (R0–R7). The two bits **RS1 (PSW.4)** and **RS0 (PSW.3)** determine which bank is currently active:

| RS1 | RS0 | Bank | R0 Address | R7 Address |
|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|
| 0 | 0 | Bank 0 | `0x00` | `0x07` |
| 0 | 1 | Bank 1 | `0x08` | `0x0F` |
| 1 | 0 | Bank 2 | `0x10` | `0x17` |
| 1 | 1 | Bank 3 | `0x18` | `0x1F` |

**Example — Switching from Bank 0 to Bank 3:**

```asm
SETB PSW.4      ; RS1 = 1
SETB PSW.3      ; RS0 = 1
; Now Bank 3 is active. R0 is at 0x18, R7 is at 0x1F.
```

This mechanism is used to provide **separate register contexts** in interrupt service routines (Bank 1 is conventionally used for this). *(7 marks: 2 for bit list + 2 for bank table + 3 for switching example and use-case explanation)*

---

### ❓ Question B (Alternative Choice): `[KTU University Exam – May 2023]`

**(a)** What are Special Function Registers in 8051? List and briefly explain the function of the following SFRs: ACC, B, SP, DPTR, PSW. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** Explain the function of the Stack Pointer and DPTR registers. Show the contents of SP and the stack memory after a sequence of PUSH and POP operations starting from SP = 0x60, where ACC = 0xAB and B = 0xCD. **(7 Marks)**

#### ✅ Model Solution for (a) — 7 Marks:

**Definition (1 mark):** Special Function Registers are on-chip registers located in the upper 128 bytes of internal RAM (`0x80`–`0xFF`) that provide **memory-mapped control** over the 8051's internal peripherals and CPU functions. Only 21 of the 128 bytes are physically implemented.

**SFR Explanations (6 marks — 1.2 marks each):**

1. **ACC (Accumulator, 0xE0):** The most-used register; it is the implicit operand A in most arithmetic/logic instructions and the destination for results. All data movement to/from external memory passes through ACC.

2. **B Register (0xF0):** Used as a second operand in `MUL AB` and `DIV AB`; otherwise serves as a temporary scratch register.

3. **SP (Stack Pointer, 0x81):** Holds the address of the top of the stack. After reset, SP = `0x07`. Increments before each PUSH, decrements after each POP.

4. **DPTR (Data Pointer, 0x82–0x83):** A 16-bit register formed by DPL (`0x82`) and DPH (`0x83`). Used to access external data memory (XDATA) up to 64 KB and as the base register for `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` lookup-table reads.

5. **PSW (Program Status Word, 0xD0):** Holds status flags (CY, AC, OV, P), user flag F0, and the register bank select bits (RS1, RS0).

---

#### ✅ Model Solution for (b) — 7 Marks:

**Stack Pointer (SP):** A pointer to the "top" of the 8051 stack. The 8051 stack **grows upward** (toward higher addresses). On PUSH, SP is incremented first, then data is written at [SP]. On POP, data is read from [SP], then SP is decremented.

**DPTR (Data Pointer):** A 16-bit register (DPH:DPL) used to address external data memory (XDATA) up to 64 KB. For 8051, the XDATA address space is `0x0000` to `0xFFFF`.

**Trace of PUSH/POP Operations (initial SP = 0x60, ACC = 0xAB, B = 0xCD):**

| Step | Instruction | SP (Before) | SP (After) | Memory Address | Value Stored | Effect |
|:---:|:---|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---|
| 1 | `PUSH ACC` | `0x60` | `0x61` | `0x61` | `0xAB` | Stack now has 1 byte |
| 2 | `PUSH B` | `0x61` | `0x62` | `0x62` | `0xCD` | Stack now has 2 bytes |
| 3 | `POP ACC` | `0x62` | `0x61` | — | — | A = `0xCD` (LIFO!) |
| 4 | `POP B` | `0x61` | `0x60` | — | — | B = `0xAB` |

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical LIFO Observation:** Because the stack is LIFO, popping into ACC first gives it the value of B (`0xCD`), and popping into B gives it the value of ACC (`0xAB`). **KTU students commonly lose 1–2 marks for not recognizing this order-swap.**

**Final Register State:**
- ACC = `0xCD` (the value of B that was pushed second)
- B = `0xAB` (the value of ACC that was pushed first)
- SP = `0x60` (back to start)

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Confusing stack growth direction:** The 8051 stack grows **UP** (higher addresses). Writing "grows down" loses 1 mark.
> 2. **Parity flag misinterpretation:** P=1 means *odd* parity (odd number of 1s), NOT even parity. Many students get this backwards.
> 3. **Bit-addressable memory range confusion:** Students often think all of `0x20`–`0x7F` is bit-addressable — only `0x20`–`0x2F` is.
> 4. **DPTR total address:** DPTR is **16-bit**, accessing **64 KB** of XDATA, not 32 KB.
> 5. **SFR vs GPR overlap confusion:** SFRs and GPRs are NOT in the same address space — SFRs are at `0x80–0xFF` (upper 128) and GPRs are at `0x00–0x7F` (lower 128), despite being referenced by the same MOV instructions. This is a **key 8051 architectural quirk** and is frequently tested.
> 6. **Reset values of P0–P3:** Students write `0x00` for ports on reset — correct value is `0xFF` (all bits high).

---

## 5.3 🎯 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must-Memorize for KTU Exams**

- ✅ **SFRs occupy `0x80`–`0xFF`** of internal data memory; **21 are implemented** in the standard 8051.
- ✅ **21 implemented SFRs** include: A, B, PSW, SP, DPL, DPH, P0, P1, P2, P3, IP, IE, TMOD, TCON, TH0, TL0, TH1, TL1, SCON, SBUF, PCON.
- ✅ **11 SFRs are bit-addressable** — those with addresses ending in `0H` or `8H`: `0x80, 0x88, 0x90, 0x98, 0xA0, 0xA8, 0xB0, 0xB8, 0xD0, 0xE0, 0xF0`.
- ✅ **PSW is at `0xD0`** and is the most-asked SFR — know all 8 bits: `CY, AC, F0, RS1, RS0, OV, -, P`.
- ✅ **Parity bit P = 1** when Accumulator has an **ODD** number of 1s (counter-intuitive 8051 convention).
- ✅ **OV = C7 ⊕ C6** (carry into bit 7 XOR carry out from bit 6).
- ✅ **Register Bank selection** is via `RS1:RS0`: `00→Bank 0`, `01→Bank 1`, `10→Bank 2`, `11→Bank 3`.
- ✅ **Default bank after RESET is Bank 0**; **default SP = `0x07`** (first PUSH writes to `0x08`).
- ✅ **8051 stack grows UP** (toward higher addresses), is **LIFO**, and PUSH increments SP first.
- ✅ **DPTR is 16-bit** (DPH:DPL), can address **64 KB** of external data memory.
- ✅ **On RESET:** All SFRs → `0x00` **except** `SP = 0x07` and `P0–P3 = 0xFF`.
- ✅ **Memory-mapped I/O:** 8051 has **no dedicated I/O instructions** — all peripheral control is done by reading/writing SFR addresses directly.
- ✅ **TMOD is NOT bit-addressable** but TCON IS — a common KTU trick question.
- ✅ **Bit Address Formula:** For bit `n` of byte-addressed SFR at `Addr`, the bit's address = `Addr + n`.
- ✅ **AC flag is set on carry from bit 3 → bit 4** — critical for BCD `DA A` instruction.
- ✅ **P3 has alternate functions:** P3.0 (RXD), P3.1 (TXD), P3.2 (INT0), P3.3 (INT1), P3.4 (T0), P3.5 (T1), P3.6 (WR), P3.7 (RD).
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: I/O Port Organization
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET613 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** I/O Port Organization

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

In the **8051 Microcontroller** architecture, the **I/O (Input/Output) Port Organization** refers to the hardware structure, internal circuitry, and functional grouping of the **four bidirectional 8-bit parallel ports** — **Port 0 (P0)**, **Port 1 (P1)**, **Port 2 (P2)**, and **Port 3 (P3)** — collectively occupying **Special Function Registers (SFRs)** at addresses `80H`, `90H`, `A0H`, and `B0H` respectively. Each port is a latch-backed, byte-addressable interface that mediates all data transfer between the **CPU** and the **external world** (sensors, actuators, memory, or peripheral ICs).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Definition Box**
> A port in the 8051 is **NOT a single pin** — it is a **package of 8 identical I/O lines**, each consisting of a **D-type latch**, an **output driver stage**, an **input buffer**, and a **read control multiplexer**. Every bit of the port SFR can be independently programmed as Input or Output.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Think of an I/O port as a **mailroom counter at a corporate office**:
- The **counter desk (D-latch)** stores the letter (1-bit data: 0 or 1) until the next instruction comes.
- The **delivery boy (output driver)** physically carries the letter to the external customer.
- The **incoming tray (input buffer)** receives letters arriving from outside.
- The **manager (CPU)** decides whether to *read incoming mail* or *send outgoing mail* using a switch — this is the **read-pin vs read-latch** concept.

Without these ports, the CPU would have no standardized "windows" to communicate with the outside world — just as an office cannot function without a reception desk.

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Value | Significance |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Number of I/O Ports | **4 (P0, P1, P2, P3)** | Total 32 I/O lines |
| Port Width | **8 bits** | Each port is byte-wide |
| Total I/O Lines | **32 lines** | Across all four ports |
| Port 0 Output Type | **Open-drain** | Needs **10 kΩ external pull-up** |
| Ports 1, 2, 3 Output Type | **Quasi-bidirectional** | Have internal pull-ups |
| SFR Address of P0 | **`80H`** | Bit-addressable |
| SFR Address of P1 | **`90H`** | Bit-addressable |
| SFR Address of P2 | **`A0H`** | Bit-addressable |
| SFR Address of P3 | **`B0H`** | Bit-addressable |
| TTL Compatibility | **Logic 1 ≥ 2.0 V, Logic 0 ≤ 0.8 V** | Industry standard interfacing |

> [!NOTE]
> **Syllabus Highlight:** KTU frequently asks students to **compare the four ports** and identify which one is **"open-drain"** and requires external pull-ups. Memorize: **Only P0 is true open-drain.**

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Breakdown of a Single Port Pin

Each of the 32 port lines follows an identical internal logic structure consisting of **one D-latch**, **two output transistors (FETs)**, **an input buffer**, and **control logic**. Let us dissect the role of each component:

### 2.1.1 The D-Type Latch (Storage Element)
- Every port bit has a **D-type flip-flop (D-FF)** that retains the value written by the CPU.
- The output `Q` of the latch drives the internal circuitry.
- The latch is written to during a **"write-to-port"** instruction (e.g., `MOV P1, A`).

### 2.1.2 The Output Driver Stage
- Comprises two **MOSFETs** — a **pull-up FET** (Q1) and a **pull-down FET** (Q2).
- **When Q = 1:** Pull-up FET ON, pull-down FET OFF → Pin driven HIGH.
- **When Q = 0:** Pull-up FET OFF, pull-down FET ON → Pin driven LOW.
- In **Port 0**, the pull-up FET is **genuinely absent** → hence the term *open-drain*. In Ports 1, 2, 3, a **weak internal pull-up** (≈ 10 kΩ to 40 kΩ) is present → *quasi-bidirectional*.

### 2.1.3 The Input Buffer
- A high-impedance buffer that reads the **physical logic level** on the external pin.
- Routes this value back to the internal bus when the CPU executes a **"read-pin"** instruction.

### 2.1.4 The Read Control Multiplexer
- Critical and often overlooked: the 8051 has **two distinct read operations**:
  - **Read Latch (Read SFR):** Reads the value held in the D-FF (i.e., what *we wrote*). Triggered by instructions like `ANL P1, A` or `INC P1`.
  - **Read Pin:** Reads the actual external voltage on the pin. Triggered by most ordinary MOV instructions (e.g., `MOV A, P1`).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Two Read Modes?**
> The "read-latch" mode prevents a common hazard — if a port pin is driving an external load (say, a transistor base), the external voltage at the pin may be momentarily altered. Reading the *latch* instead of the *pin* ensures the logical operation (AND, OR, XOR) is performed on the **intended internal value**, avoiding read-modify-write race conditions.

## 2.2 Port-by-Port Functional Deep-Dive

### 2.2.1 Port 0 (P0, SFR `80H`)
- **Dual Role:** Serves as a **general-purpose I/O port** OR as the **multiplexed low-order address/data bus (AD0–AD7)** when external memory is accessed.
- **Structure:** True **open-drain**. Has no internal pull-up resistors.
- **External Requirement:** Needs **external 10 kΩ pull-up resistors** when used as a simple I/O port.
- **During External Memory Access:**
  - First, it outputs the **low-byte of the address (A0–A7)** from the internal latch.
  - Then, it transitions to **read/write the data byte (D0–D7)**.
  - The **ALE (Address Latch Enable)** signal from the 8051 is used to latch A0–A7 into an **external 74HC373** latch IC.

### 2.2.2 Port 1 (P1, SFR `90H`)
- **Simplest port** — purely a **general-purpose I/O** port.
- Has **internal pull-ups**, so it does not require external resistors.
- **No alternate function** — used only for I/O.
- Ideal for interfacing **LEDs, switches, keypads, and simple sensors**.

### 2.2.3 Port 2 (P2, SFR `A0H`)
- **Dual Role:** General-purpose I/O OR **high-order address bus (A8–A15)** during external memory access.
- When external code memory (ROM) is used beyond 4 KB, P2 emits the high byte of the address.
- When external data memory (RAM) is accessed using `MOVX` instruction, P2 emits A8–A15.
- Has **internal pull-ups**.

### 2.2.4 Port 3 (P3, SFR `B0H`) — The Alternate Function Port
- Each of the 8 pins has a **dedicated alternate function** essential for microcontroller operation:

| Port 3 Pin | Alternate Function | Description |
| :---: | :---: | :--- |
| **P3.0** | **RXD** | Serial Data Receive (UART input) |
| **P3.1** | **TXD** | Serial Data Transmit (UART output) |
| **P3.2** | **INT0** | External Interrupt 0 |
| **P3.3** | **INT1** | External Interrupt 1 |
| **P3.4** | **T0** | Timer 0 External Input |
| **P3.5** | **T1** | Timer 1 External Input |
| **P3.6** | **WR (active LOW)** | External Data Memory Write Strobe |
| **P3.7** | **RD (active LOW)** | External Data Memory Read Strobe |

> [!NOTE]
> These alternate functions are **automatically activated** by setting the corresponding SFRs (e.g., `SCON` for UART, `TCON` for interrupts, `TMOD` for timers). The user does **NOT** need to manually configure the port pin direction for alternate functions.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Equation / Rule | Unit / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Total I/O lines available | $N_{IO} = 4 \text{ ports} \times 8 \text{ bits}$ | $N_{IO} = 32$ lines |
| External address bus size (P0 + P2) | $A_{total} = A_{low} + A_{high}$ | $A_{total} = 8 + 8 = 16 \text{ bits}$ |
| Maximum addressable external memory | $M_{max} = 2^{16}$ | $M_{max} = 65536 \text{ bytes} = 64 \text{ KB}$ |
| External pull-up for P0 (typical) | $R_{pull-up} \approx 10 \text{ k}\Omega$ | Pull-up to $V_{CC} = 5 \text{ V}$ |
| Fan-out (TTL loads per pin) | $F_{out} \approx 8$ | Standard TTL load |
| Source current per pin (HIGH) | $I_{OH} \approx -60 \text{ \mu A}$ (P0, P1, P2, P3) | Negative indicates source |
| Sink current per pin (LOW) | $I_{OL} \approx 1.6 \text{ mA}$ (P0) or $1.6 \text{ mA}$ (P1–P3) | Strong sink capability |
| TTL Logic HIGH threshold | $V_{IH} \geq 2.0 \text{ V}$ | Recognized as logic 1 |
| TTL Logic LOW threshold | $V_{IL} \leq 0.8 \text{ V}$ | Recognized as logic 0 |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Embedded Industrial Control:** P0/P2 connect to external memory chips (e.g., 32 KB ROM + 32 KB RAM) for storing large lookup tables in motor control firmware.
- **Communication Interfaces:** P3.0 and P3.1 are used for **UART-based communication** with PCs (via MAX232 level shifter) and with other microcontrollers.
- **Sensor Networks:** P1 is the workhorse port for **GPIO-based sensor reading** (e.g., DHT11 temperature sensor, IR obstacle sensor) because of its internal pull-ups and simplicity.
- **Automotive ECUs:** Port 3 alternate functions (interrupts, timers) are critical for handling real-time events such as **crank-shaft position sensors** and **wheel-speed ABS inputs**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Total Addressable External Memory via P0 + P2

When the 8051 accesses external memory, the 16-bit external address is formed by combining the low-byte from **Port 0** and the high-byte from **Port 2**.

### Step 1 — Identify the Width of Each Address Port
Port 0 contributes the **low-order address lines**:
$$A_{low} = 8 \text{ bits} \rightarrow \text{AD0 through AD7}$$
Port 2 contributes the **high-order address lines**:
$$A_{high} = 8 \text{ bits} \rightarrow \text{A8 through A15}$$

### Step 2 — Concatenate to Form the Full Address Bus
The total address bus width is the sum:
$$A_{total} = A_{low} + A_{high}$$
$$A_{total} = 8 + 8 = 16 \text{ bits}$$

### Step 3 — Compute Maximum Addressable Memory
Using the binary addressing power formula:
$$M_{max} = 2^{A_{total}}$$
$$M_{max} = 2^{16}$$

### Step 4 — Evaluate the Numerical Result
$$M_{max} = 65536 \text{ bytes}$$
$$M_{max} = 64 \text{ KB}$$

This is precisely why the **classic 8051 is called a "16-bit address bus, 8-bit data bus"** machine, and why it supports exactly **64 KB of code memory + 64 KB of data memory** (Harvard architecture with separate address spaces).

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Port Direction Configuration

The 8051 ports are **NOT configured via a DDR (Data Direction Register)** like in AVR/PIC microcontrollers. Instead, port direction is set **implicitly** by the instruction used.

| Operation Type | Instruction Example | Direction | Mechanism |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Write `1` to a pin | `SETB P1.0` | **Input** (high-impedance) | Pull-up FET ON, pull-down OFF, pin floats HIGH |
| Write `0` to a pin | `CLR P1.0` | **Output LOW** | Pull-down FET ON, drives 0 V |
| Read a pin | `MOV A, P1` | **Input read** | Activates "read-pin" path |
| Read-modify-write | `ANL P1, #0FH` | **Read-latch** | Reads from D-FF, not pin |
| Set bit logic | `SETB P1.0` | **Input** | Standard practice: write 1 before reading |
| Reset bit logic | `CLR P1.0` | **Output LOW** | Standard practice: drives the pin LOW |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Standard Rule:** Before reading an external input on any port pin, **always write a `1` to that pin** (e.g., `SETB P1.0`). This makes the pin an input (high-impedance) so the external device can drive it without contention. Forgetting this rule is the **#1 cause of "why my switch doesn't work"** in lab viva questions.

## 3.3 Numerical Evaluation: Driving an LED via P1.0

Suppose an LED (forward voltage $V_f = 2 \text{ V}$, forward current $I_f = 10 \text{ mA}$) is connected from P1.0 to ground through a current-limiting resistor.

**Step 1 — Apply Kirchhoff's Voltage Law (KVL):**
$$V_{CC} = V_R + V_f$$
$$5 \text{ V} = V_R + 2 \text{ V}$$
$$V_R = 3 \text{ V}$$

**Step 2 — Apply Ohm's Law to find resistor value:**
$$V_R = I_f \times R$$
$$R = \frac{V_R}{I_f} = \frac{3 \text{ V}}{10 \text{ mA}}$$
$$R = 300 \text{ }\Omega$$

**Step 3 — Use nearest standard E12 resistor:**
$$R_{standard} = 330 \text{ }\Omega$$

This derivation validates the 8051's sink-current capability of **1.6 mA per pin** being sufficient for low-current LEDs.

## 3.4 Detailed Pin-by-Pin Functional Architecture

Below is the comprehensive mapping of all 32 port pins, their directions, and recommended use cases:

| Port | Pin | Direction Default | Recommended Use | Alternate Function |
| :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| **P0.0 – P0.7** | All 8 | Open-drain I/O | External Memory Bus (AD0–AD7) | Address/Data multiplexed |
| **P1.0 – P1.7** | All 8 | Quasi-bidirectional I/O | LEDs, Switches, Keypad rows, Generic GPIO | **None** |
| **P2.0 – P2.7** | All 8 | Quasi-bidirectional I/O | High-order Address Bus (A8–A15) | Address only |
| **P3.0** | RXD | I/O or Serial Input | UART Receive line | **Serial Input** |
| **P3.1** | TXD | I/O or Serial Output | UART Transmit line | **Serial Output** |
| **P3.2** | INT0 | I/O or Interrupt 0 | External interrupt source | **Hardware INT0** |
| **P3.3** | INT1 | I/O or Interrupt 1 | External interrupt source | **Hardware INT1** |
| **P3.4** | T0 | I/O or Timer 0 Input | Counter input for Timer 0 | **Timer 0 clock** |
| **P3.5** | T1 | I/O or Timer 1 Input | Counter input for Timer 1 | **Timer 1 clock** |
| **P3.6** | WR | I/O or Write Strobe | External RAM write enable | **Memory WR strobe** |
| **P3.7** | RD | I/O or Read Strobe | External RAM read enable | **Memory RD strobe** |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Block-Level Architecture of a Single 8051 Port Pin

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    cpuBus["Internal CPU Bus"]
    dLatch["D Type Latch (D-FF)"]
    qOut["Q Output of Latch"]
    pullUp["Pull-Up FET (Q1)"]
    pullDown["Pull-Down FET (Q2)"]
    pinNode["External Pin (P.x)"]
    inputBuf["Input Buffer"]
    readMux["Read Mux Selector"]
    readLatchPath["Read Latch Path"]
    readPinPath["Read Pin Path"]
    altFunc["Alternate Function Mux"]

    cpuBus --> dLatch
    dLatch --> qOut
    qOut --> pullUp
    qOut --> pullDown
    pullUp --> pinNode
    pullDown --> pinNode
    pinNode --> inputBuf
    inputBuf --> readMux
    dLatch --> readMux
    readMux --> readLatchPath
    readMux --> readPinPath
    readMux --> cpuBus
    pinNode --> altFunc
    altFunc --> cpuBus

    style cpuBus fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style dLatch fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style pullUp fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style pullDown fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style pinNode fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style readMux fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Interpretation:** The Internal CPU Bus writes data into the D-FF. The Q output drives two complementary FETs (Q1 pull-up, Q2 pull-down) which jointly control the external pin voltage. The same pin feeds back through an Input Buffer into a Read MUX, which selects between "Read Latch" (used by read-modify-write instructions) and "Read Pin" (used by ordinary read instructions). For Port 3 pins, an additional Alternate Function MUX routes peripheral signals (RXD, T0, INT0, etc.) back to the CPU.

## 4.2 Port 0 Internal Structure — Open-Drain Configuration

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    p0CPU["CPU Bus to P0 SFR"]
    p0Latch["D Latch (8-bit register)"]
    p0PullDown["Pull-Down FET Q2"]
    p0ExternalPull["External 10 kOhm Pull-Up Resistor"]
    p0Pin["Port 0 Pins P0.0 to P0.7"]
    p0ExtMem["External Address Data Bus AD0-AD7"]
    p0ALE["ALE Signal to 74HC373 Latch"]

    p0CPU --> p0Latch
    p0Latch --> p0PullDown
    p0PullDown --> p0Pin
    p0ExternalPull --> p0Pin
    p0Pin --> p0ExtMem
    p0ExtMem --> p0ALE

    style p0ExternalPull fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style p0PullDown fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style p0ALE fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Insight:** Port 0 is the **only port** with a *genuine open-drain* output (no internal pull-up). When used as a simple GPIO output HIGH, current *must* flow through the **external pull-up resistor**. This is why KTU repeatedly asks: *"Why does Port 0 need external pull-ups?"*

## 4.3 Port 3 Alternate Function Routing Diagram

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    p3Pin0["P3.0 / RXD"]
    p3Pin1["P3.1 / TXD"]
    p3Pin2["P3.2 / INT0"]
    p3Pin3["P3.3 / INT1"]
    p3Pin4["P3.4 / T0"]
    p3Pin5["P3.5 / T1"]
    p3Pin6["P3.6 / WR"]
    p3Pin7["P3.7 / RD"]

    uartBlock["UART Serial Block (SCON)"]
    int0Block["External Interrupt 0 (TCON.IT0)"]
    int1Block["External Interrupt 1 (TCON.IT1)"]
    t0Block["Timer 0 Counter Input (TMOD)"]
    t1Block["Timer 1 Counter Input (TMOD)"]
    wrBlock["External Data Memory Write Control"]
    rdBlock["External Data Memory Read Control"]

    p3Pin0 --> uartBlock
    p3Pin1 --> uartBlock
    p3Pin2 --> int0Block
    p3Pin3 --> int1Block
    p3Pin4 --> t0Block
    p3Pin5 --> t1Block
    p3Pin6 --> wrBlock
    p3Pin7 --> rdBlock

    style uartBlock fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style int0Block fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style int1Block fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style t0Block fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style t1Block fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style wrBlock fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style rdBlock fill:#FF6B6B,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

## 4.4 System-Level View: 8051 Connected to External Memory via P0 and P2

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    mcu8051["8051 Microcontroller"]
    p0Bus["Port 0 (AD0-AD7)"]
    p2Bus["Port 2 (A8-A15)"]
    p3Bus["Port 3 (RD, WR, ALE)"]
    latch74373["74HC373 Address Latch"]
    extROM["External ROM (e.g., 27C256)"]
    extRAM["External RAM (e.g., 6264)"]
    addrLatch["Latched A0-A7 Bus"]

    mcu8051 --> p0Bus
    mcu8051 --> p2Bus
    mcu8051 --> p3Bus
    p0Bus --> latch74373
    p3Bus --> latch74373
    latch74373 --> addrLatch
    addrLatch --> extROM
    addrLatch --> extRAM
    p2Bus --> extROM
    p2Bus --> extRAM
    p3Bus --> extROM
    p3Bus --> extRAM

    style mcu8051 fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style latch74373 fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style extROM fill:#90EE90,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style extRAM fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

> [!NOTE]
> **System Insight:** The **ALE (Address Latch Enable)** signal from P3 is not part of the data flow diagram above; it is shown as a control input to the 74HC373 latch. The 74373 captures the address byte from P0 at the falling edge of ALE, freeing P0 to carry the data byte during the next clock phase. This is the **heart of the 8051's multiplexed bus architecture**.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Explain the I/O port organization of the 8051 microcontroller. Why is Port 0 called an "open-drain" port?**

**Model Answer:**
The 8051 microcontroller has **four 8-bit bidirectional I/O ports — P0, P1, P2, and P3** — each occupying a Special Function Register (SFR) at addresses `80H`, `90H`, `A0H`, and `B0H` respectively, providing a total of **32 I/O lines**. Each port pin contains a **D-type latch** for data storage, an **output driver** with two MOSFETs, and an **input buffer** with a read multiplexer.

Port 0 is called an **open-drain port** because it **lacks an internal pull-up transistor**. It contains only the **pull-down FET (Q2)**; the pull-up must be provided externally (typically a **10 kΩ resistor** to $V_{CC}$). This is required when P0 is used as a general-purpose I/O port. The other three ports (P1, P2, P3) have internal pull-ups, making them "quasi-bidirectional".

### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**List the alternate functions of Port 3 pins in the 8051 microcontroller.**

**Model Answer:**

| Pin | Alternate Function | Purpose |
| :---: | :---: | :--- |
| P3.0 | **RXD** | Serial data receive (UART input) |
| P3.1 | **TXD** | Serial data transmit (UART output) |
| P3.2 | **INT0** | External hardware interrupt 0 |
| P3.3 | **INT1** | External hardware interrupt 1 |
| P3.4 | **T0** | Timer/Counter 0 external input |
| P3.5 | **T1** | Timer/Counter 1 external input |
| P3.6 | **WR (active LOW)** | External data memory write strobe |
| P3.7 | **RD (active LOW)** | External data memory read strobe |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

### Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Draw the internal structure of a single port pin of the 8051 microcontroller and explain the role of the D-latch, output driver, and read multiplexer in detail.

**(b) [7 Marks]** Compare the four I/O ports of the 8051 in terms of internal structure, pull-up resistors, and alternate functions.

---

### **Model Solution for Question A:**

#### Part (a) — Internal Structure of a Single Port Pin [7 Marks]

**Diagram (3 marks):**

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    intBus["CPU Internal Bus"]
    dFF["D Type Latch"]
    qNode["Q Output"]
    fet1["Pull-Up MOSFET (Q1)"]
    fet2["Pull-Down MOSFET (Q2)"]
    pX["External Pin P.x"]
    inBuf["Input Buffer"]
    rdMux["Read Multiplexer"]
    rL["Read Latch Output"]
    rP["Read Pin Output"]
    altFn["Alternate Function Mux"]

    intBus --> dFF
    dFF --> qNode
    qNode --> fet1
    qNode --> fet2
    fet1 --> pX
    fet2 --> pX
    pX --> inBuf
    inBuf --> rdMux
    dFF --> rdMux
    rdMux --> rL
    rdMux --> rP
    rdMux --> intBus
    pX --> altFn
    altFn --> intBus

    style intBus fill:#FFD700,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style dFF fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style pX fill:#FFA500,stroke:#000,color:#000
    style rdMux fill:#DDA0DD,stroke:#000,color:#000
```

**Explanatory Text (4 marks):**

1. **D-Type Latch (D-FF):** Each port bit is backed by a D-type flip-flop. The CPU writes the desired logic value into this latch using SFR write instructions. The latch's Q output drives the output driver stage and also feeds the read-latch path. The latch is essential because it **remembers the value** between successive CPU operations.
