2. **Output Driver:** The output driver consists of two MOSFETs — a **pull-up FET (Q1)** and a **pull-down FET (Q2)**. When Q=1, Q1 turns ON and Q2 OFF, driving the pin HIGH. When Q=0, Q1 turns OFF and Q2 ON, driving the pin LOW. In Port 0, the pull-up FET is absent (open-drain). In Ports 1, 2, 3, a **weak internal pull-up FET** exists.

3. **Read Multiplexer:** The read MUX selects between **two sources**:
   - **Read Latch path:** Reads the Q output of the D-FF. Used by **read-modify-write instructions** (e.g., `ANL P1, A`, `INC P2`) to avoid race conditions with the external pin.
   - **Read Pin path:** Reads the **actual external voltage** at the pin via the input buffer. Used by ordinary read instructions like `MOV A, P1`.

4. **Alternate Function MUX (for P3 only):** Routes peripheral signals like RXD, TXD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD back to the corresponding on-chip peripheral hardware.

#### Part (b) — Comparison of the Four Ports [7 Marks]

| Feature | **Port 0 (P0)** | **Port 1 (P1)** | **Port 2 (P2)** | **Port 3 (P3)** |
| :--- | :---: | :---: | :---: | :---: |
| SFR Address | `80H` | `90H` | `A0H` | `B0H` |
| Output Type | **Open-drain** | Quasi-bidirectional | Quasi-bidirectional | Quasi-bidirectional |
| Internal Pull-up | **Absent** | Present (weak) | Present (weak) | Present (weak) |
| External Pull-up | **Required (10 kΩ)** | Not required | Not required | Not required |
| Alternate Function | **AD0–AD7 bus** | None | **A8–A15 bus** | **RXD, TXD, INT0, INT1, T0, T1, WR, RD** |
| Primary Use | External memory interface | General-purpose I/O | High-order address bus | Serial, interrupt, timer, memory strobes |
| Driving Capability | Sink ≈ 1.6 mA | Sink ≈ 1.6 mA | Sink ≈ 1.6 mA | Sink ≈ 1.6 mA |

**Summary Statement:** Port 0 is the most *specialized* (memory bus, open-drain), Port 1 is the most *generic*, Port 2 is dedicated to *high-order addressing*, and Port 3 is dedicated to *alternate peripheral functions*. The collective combination yields the 8051's powerful 16-bit external address bus and versatile peripheral integration.

---

### Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the dual role of Port 0 and Port 2 in the 8051 with reference to external memory interfacing. How is the address-data bus de-multiplexed?

**(b) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of "read-latch" versus "read-pin" operations in 8051 ports. Why is this distinction necessary? Give two examples of read-modify-write instructions.

---

### **Model Solution for Question B:**

#### Part (a) — Dual Role of P0 and P2 [7 Marks]

**Role of Port 0 (3 marks):**
Port 0 serves a **dual function**:
- **As a general-purpose I/O port** (when no external memory is accessed), it behaves as an 8-bit open-drain bidirectional port.
- **As the multiplexed low-order address/data bus** (when external memory is accessed). The 8 lines of P0 carry the low-order address byte **A0–A7** during the first phase of the memory cycle and the data byte **D0–D7** during the second phase. The **ALE (Address Latch Enable)** signal from the 8051 is used to latch the address into an **external 74HC373 transparent latch**, freeing P0 to be used for data transfer.

**Role of Port 2 (2 marks):**
Port 2 also has a **dual function**:
- **As a general-purpose I/O port** (with internal pull-ups).
- **As the high-order address bus (A8–A15)** during any external memory access. Unlike P0, P2 is **not multiplexed** — it holds the address steady for the entire memory cycle.

**De-Multiplexing Process (2 marks):**
- The 8051 first drives **A0–A7 on P0** and **A8–A15 on P2** simultaneously.
- The **ALE pin goes HIGH**, instructing the external 74HC373 latch to capture A0–A7.
- ALE goes LOW, latching the address internally in the 373.
- P0 then **floats (or drives data)** for the read/write phase, while A8–A15 remains stable on P2.
- The external memory IC uses the latched A0–A7 (from 373) and A8–A15 (from P2) to select the exact memory location.

#### Part (b) — Read-Latch vs Read-Pin [7 Marks]

**Conceptual Explanation (4 marks):**
The 8051 has two internal paths for reading a port value:
- **Read Latch (Read SFR):** Reads the value currently held in the **D-type flip-flop** (i.e., the value previously written by the CPU).
- **Read Pin:** Reads the **actual physical voltage** present on the external pin via the input buffer.

**Why the Distinction is Necessary (2 marks):**
Consider a scenario where P1.0 is driving the base of a transistor. The voltage at the pin may be momentarily disturbed by the external load. If the CPU executes a read-modify-write instruction (e.g., `ANL P1, A` to clear bit 0), reading the *physical pin* could return a corrupted value, leading to incorrect logic operation. Reading the *latch* guarantees the operation works on the **intended internal state**, producing a deterministic outcome.

**Examples of Read-Modify-Write Instructions (1 mark):**
1. `ANL P1, A` — Bitwise AND P1 with accumulator
2. `INC P2` — Increment Port 2
3. `CPL P3.0` — Complement P3.0
4. `ORL P1, #0FH` — OR P1 with immediate data

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse "read-latch" with "open-drain"** — these are entirely different concepts. Read-latch is a *read path*; open-drain is an *output structure*.
> 2. **Do NOT write "Port 0 has no pull-up"** without qualification. Be precise: it has *no internal pull-up* but **must have an external pull-up** when used as GPIO.
> 3. **Do NOT list P3 alternate functions incorrectly.** RXD is input-only into the chip, TXD is output-only from the chip. WR and RD are **active LOW** signals — forgetting the overbar loses marks.
> 4. **Do NOT state** that P0, P1, P2, P3 are configured via a DDR register — the 8051 has no DDR; direction is set by **writing 1 to make a pin input**.

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — KTU 2024 Module 1**
> - **4 ports × 8 bits = 32 I/O lines** — memorize this number.
> - **Port 0 = open-drain, needs external 10 kΩ pull-up.** Ports 1, 2, 3 = quasi-bidirectional with internal pull-ups.
> - **P0 → AD0–AD7 (multiplexed address/data)** + **P2 → A8–A15 (high address)** + **P3.6 (WR) + P3.7 (RD)** = complete 16-bit external memory interface.
> - **P3 alternate functions:** RXD (P3.0), TXD (P3.1), INT0 (P3.2), INT1 (P3.3), T0 (P3.4), T1 (P3.5), WR (P3.6), RD (P3.7).
> - **SFR addresses:** P0=`80H`, P1=`90H`, P2=`A0H`, P3=`B0H`. All four are bit-addressable.
> - **Read-Latch** is used by read-modify-write instructions (`ANL`, `ORL`, `XRL`, `INC`, `DEC`, `CPL`, `DJNZ`, `JBC`, `MOV bit,C`, `CLR bit`, `SETB bit`).
> - **Read-Pin** is used by ordinary MOV reads (e.g., `MOV A, P1`).
> - **ALE** signal demultiplexes P0 using an external 74HC373 latch — critical for external memory interfacing.
> - **No DDR register** exists in the 8051 — write `1` to a bit to make it an input, write `0` to drive it LOW.
> - **External memory limit = 64 KB code + 64 KB data** (Harvard architecture, two separate address spaces).
> - **P1 has no alternate functions** — safest port for general-purpose I/O in lab experiments.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Interrupts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET613 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Interrupts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of the **8051 Microcontroller** (the primary architecture prescribed in the KTU OEAET613 syllabus), an **Interrupt** is defined as an asynchronous external or internal event that temporarily halts the main program execution, forcing the CPU to execute a special routine known as an **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)** or **Interrupt Handler**. Upon completion of the ISR, the processor returns to the main program at the exact instruction where it was interrupted, using a mechanism called a *stack-pushed return address*.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 1):**
> The 8051 micro-controller has **five interrupt sources** — two external hardware interrupts, two timer interrupts, and one serial port interrupt. Each interrupt can be individually enabled or disabled, and each can be assigned one of two priority levels.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (Board-Examiner Approved):**
> "An interrupt is a signal (hardware or software) that suspends the normal sequential execution of the processor and diverts its control to a specially designated subroutine (ISR) to service the event. After servicing, the processor resumes normal execution from the interrupted instruction."

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy (Plain-English Intuition)

Imagine you are a student preparing for an exam (your **main program**). Suddenly, your doorbell rings (**interrupt signal**). You pause your studying, place a bookmark at the exact page you stopped on (**saving the return address on the stack**), and go open the door (**jump to the ISR**). You attend to the visitor — perhaps a delivery (**service the interrupt**) — and then return to your study desk, open to the bookmarked page (**restore context, resume main program**).

> [!TIP]
> The **bookmark** is the program counter saved on the stack. The **doorbell** is the interrupt request line. The **action of opening the door** is the hardware-induced transfer to the ISR.

## 1.3 Why Interrupts? (Engineering Motivation)

Without interrupts, the CPU would have to **continuously poll** (check) the status of peripheral devices — a wasteful use of processing time known as **busy-wait polling**. Interrupts allow:

- **Real-time response** to asynchronous events.
- **Power efficiency** (CPU can sleep until woken).
- **Concurrency** in single-core embedded systems.
- **Reduced software complexity** for event-driven designs.

> [!WARNING]
> KTU Pitfall: Students often confuse **"Polling"** with **"Interrupt."** In **polling**, the CPU *actively checks* flags in a loop. In **interrupt-driven I/O**, the CPU is *notified by hardware* and reacts. Polling is software-driven and synchronous; interrupts are hardware-driven and asynchronous.

## 1.4 Physical Constants & Standard 8051 Interrupt Vectors (Board Reference)

| Parameter | Standard 8051 Value |
| :--- | :--- |
| Total Interrupt Sources | **5** (in classic 8051) |
| Interrupt Vector Table Size | **8 bytes per vector** |
| Reset Vector Address | **0x0000** |
| External Interrupt 0 (INT0) | **0x0003** |
| Timer 0 Overflow | **0x000B** |
| External Interrupt 1 (INT1) | **0x0013** |
| Timer 1 Overflow | **0x001B** |
| Serial Port (TI/RI) | **0x0023** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Interrupts (Board-Favourite Taxonomy)

The 8051 supports multiple categories of interrupts, and KTU questions frequently test this classification. The hierarchy is as follows:

### A. Hardware vs Software Interrupts
- **Hardware Interrupts:** Triggered by an external electrical signal on a pin (e.g., `INT0`, `INT1`) or by an internal peripheral condition (timer overflow, serial port activity).
- **Software Interrupts:** Triggered by execution of a specific instruction. In the classic 8051, the **`RETI`** instruction and direct vector calls emulate software interrupts, though strictly speaking, the 8051 does not have a true `INT n` software interrupt like the x86 architecture.

### B. Maskable vs Non-Maskable Interrupts
- **Maskable Interrupts:** Can be enabled or disabled via software control bits. All five 8051 interrupts are maskable via the **Interrupt Enable (IE) register**.
- **Non-Maskable Interrupt (NMI):** Cannot be disabled. In the 8051, the **RESET** is functionally a non-maskable, highest-priority interrupt (it is technically a reset, not an interrupt, but is logically grouped here).

### C. Vectored vs Non-Vectored Interrupts
- **Vectored Interrupts:** Each has a fixed, pre-assigned memory address (vector) to which the CPU automatically jumps. **All five 8051 interrupts are vectored.**
- **Non-Vectored Interrupts:** All share a common ISR; the software must poll flags to identify the source.

### D. Edge-Triggered vs Level-Triggered
The external interrupts (`INT0`, `INT1`) can be configured via the **TCON register**:
- **Level-Triggered (Low):** Interrupt is requested whenever the pin is held LOW.
- **Edge-Triggered (Falling):** Interrupt is requested on the HIGH-to-LOW transition.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Note:** For **8052/80352** variants, a **6th interrupt** exists — the **Timer 2 Overflow** at vector address **0x002B**. This is a common "trick" question in KTU exams.

## 2.2 The 8051 Interrupt Structure (Architectural Deep Dive)

### 2.2.1 Interrupt Enable (IE) Register — Bit-Addressable SFR at `0xA8`

The IE register is the master control switch for the entire 8051 interrupt system. Its bit layout is:

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
| :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: |
| Symbol | **EA** | — | — | **ES** | **ET1** | **EX1** | **ET0** | **EX0** |
| Function | Global Enable | Reserved | Reserved | Serial Enable | Timer 1 Enable | INT1 Enable | Timer 0 Enable | INT0 Enable |

**Operating Rule:** The Global Enable bit **EA** must be set to **1** for *any* interrupt to be recognized. Individual peripheral bits must also be set to **1** for their respective interrupts to be active.

### 2.2.2 Interrupt Priority (IP) Register — Bit-Addressable SFR at `0xB8`

The IP register assigns one of two priority levels: **High (1)** or **Low (0)**. In the event two interrupts are activated simultaneously, the one with higher priority is serviced first.

| Bit | 7 | 6 | 5 | 4 | 3 | 2 | 1 | 0 |
| :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: | :-: |
| Symbol | — | — | — | **PS** | **PT1** | **PX1** | **PT0** | **PX0** |
| Function | Reserved | Reserved | Reserved | Serial Priority | Timer 1 Priority | INT1 Priority | Timer 0 Priority | INT0 Priority |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Trap Question:** "If two interrupts of the **same priority** are activated simultaneously, how is the tie broken?" The answer is the **default natural priority order**: **INT0 → Timer 0 → INT1 → Timer 1 → Serial Port**. This is hard-wired and cannot be changed by software.

### 2.2.3 TCON Register — External Interrupt Trigger Control (Relevant Bits)

| Bit | Symbol | Address | Function |
| :-: | :-: | :-: | :--- |
| `TCON.0` | **IT0** | `0x88` | INT0 Type: 0 = Low-Level Triggered, 1 = Falling-Edge Triggered |
| `TCON.2` | **IE0** | `0x8A` | INT0 Edge Flag (set by hardware, cleared when ISR serviced) |
| `TCON.4` | **IT1** | `0x8C` | INT1 Type: 0 = Low-Level, 1 = Falling-Edge |
| `TCON.6` | **IE1** | `0x8E` | INT1 Edge Flag |

> [!TIP]
> For **low-level triggered** interrupts, the requesting source must hold the pin LOW until the CPU services it; otherwise, the request is lost. For **edge-triggered** interrupts, the request is latched in `IE0/IE1` automatically cleared on ISR entry.

## 2.3 Steps in Executing an Interrupt (The "Interrupt Cycle")

This is the **most frequently asked 14-mark theory** in KTU. The CPU performs the following sequence upon detecting an enabled, active interrupt:

1. **Finish the current instruction** — The CPU completes the instruction already in the pipeline.
2. **Detect the interrupt** — Hardware checks the IE register, EA bit, individual enable bits, and priority.
3. **Push the Program Counter (PC) onto the stack** — Typically only the 16-bit PC is saved (in some variants, the PSW is also pushed). Stack pointer (SP) is incremented twice.
4. **Load the PC with the vector address** — For example, `0x000B` for Timer 0.
5. **Execute the ISR** — User-written routine typically ends with `RETI`.
6. **Pop the PC from the stack** — `RETI` instruction restores the saved PC.
7. **Resume main program** — Execution continues from the original interrupted location.

## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Parameter / Concept | Equation / Rule | Units / Notes |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Total 8051 Interrupt Sources | **N = 5** (classic) or **6** (8052) | Count sources, not vectors |
| Vector Address | **0x0003 + 8 × k**, where k = 0,1,2,3,4 | Bytes are 8-byte spaced |
| Interrupt Latency (typical) | $T_{\text{latency}} = 1 \text{ to } 4 \text{ machine cycles}$ | Machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods |
| Polling CPU Time (busy-wait) | $T_{\text{polled}} = N \cdot T_{\text{cycle}}$ where N = poll iterations | Inefficient vs interrupt |
| Interrupt-Driven CPU Idle | $T_{\text{useful}} = T_{\text{active}} + T_{\text{ISR}}$ | CPU free otherwise |
| Stack Depth per ISR | **2 bytes** (16-bit PC only, in 8051) | SP increases by 2 |
| Timer 0 Vector | $0\text{x}000\text{B} = 11_{10}$ | Hex equivalent often tested |
| Timer 1 Vector | $0\text{x}001\text{B} = 27_{10}$ | |
| Serial Port Vector | $0\text{x}0023 = 35_{10}$ | |
| Highest Priority (hardware reset) | Reset > All others | Non-maskable |
| Default Priority Order | INT0 > T0 > INT1 > T1 > Serial | When priorities are equal |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Machine Cycle Reminder (Cross-Concept Link):** 1 machine cycle in the 8051 = **12 oscillator periods**. Therefore, with a **11.0592 MHz crystal**, one machine cycle = $\frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} \approx 1.085\,\mu s$.

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

Interrupts are foundational in:

- **Automotive ECUs** (engine control, anti-lock braking) — event-driven sensor sampling.
- **Industrial PLCs** — emergency-stop buttons (external interrupts, highest priority).
- **Consumer Electronics** — remote control IR decoders using `INT0/INT1` edge triggers.
- **Communication Protocols** — UART serial reception using the serial port interrupt.
- **IoT & Real-Time Operating Systems (RTOS)** — interrupt-driven kernel tick generation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Derivation: Vector Address Calculation

The 8051 reserves **8 bytes** of program memory between consecutive interrupt vectors. This is a fixed architectural constant. We will derive the address of any 8051 interrupt vector from the linear base address.

**Problem (Model Question):** Calculate the vector address of the Timer 1 interrupt and express it in both hexadecimal and decimal.

### Step-by-Step Solution

We are given the architectural rule that the vector address is spaced by 8 bytes from the previous vector.

$$
\text{Vector}(k) = \text{Base}_{\text{Reset}} + 8 \cdot k + 3
$$

where $k$ is the ordinal position of the interrupt in the priority table, and the base offset of 3 is added because the first user-accessible vector (INT0) is at $0\text{x}0003$.

For **Timer 1**, we identify it as the **4th** interrupt in the default natural order (counting: 1 = INT0, 2 = Timer 0, 3 = INT1, 4 = Timer 1).

$$
k_{\text{Timer1}} = 3 \quad \text{(zero-indexed ordinal)}
$$

Substituting into the formula:

$$
\text{Vector}_{\text{Timer1}} = 0\text{x}0003 + 8 \times 3
$$

$$
\text{Vector}_{\text{Timer1}} = 0\text{x}0003 + 0\text{x}0018
$$

$$
\text{Vector}_{\text{Timer1}} = 0\text{x}001B
$$

Converting $0\text{x}001B$ to decimal:

$$
0\text{x}001B = (0 \times 16^3) + (0 \times 16^2) + (1 \times 16^1) + (11 \times 16^0)
$$

$$
= 0 + 0 + 16 + 11
$$

$$
= 27_{10}
$$

**Final Answer:** The Timer 1 interrupt vector is at $\mathbf{0\text{x}001B = 27_{10}}$.

## 3.2 Analytical Derivation: Interrupt Latency in Oscillator Cycles

**Problem:** An 8051 system uses a **12 MHz crystal oscillator**. Compute the worst-case interrupt latency in **microseconds** for an edge-triggered external interrupt.

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1 — Recall the machine cycle relationship.** For the standard 8051, one machine cycle equals 12 oscillator periods.

$$
T_{\text{machine cycle}} = \frac{12}{f_{\text{osc}}}
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $f_{\text{osc}} = 12\,\text{MHz}$.**

$$
T_{\text{machine cycle}} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^6} = 1 \times 10^{-6}\,\text{s} = 1\,\mu s
$$

**Step 3 — Apply the worst-case interrupt detection latency.** The interrupt is sampled at the end of each machine cycle. The current instruction may take up to 4 machine cycles to complete (e.g., `MUL AB` or `DIV AB`).

$$
T_{\text{latency, max}} = 4 \times T_{\text{machine cycle}} + 1 \times T_{\text{machine cycle}}
$$

$$
T_{\text{latency, max}} = 5 \times 1\,\mu s
$$

$$
T_{\text{latency, max}} = 5\,\mu s
$$

> [!NOTE]
> The "+1 machine cycle" accounts for the time taken to internally vector the CPU to the ISR address. KTU examiners reward students who explicitly mention the **worst-case instruction timing** (4 cycles for MUL/DIV).

**Final Answer:** Worst-case interrupt latency = $\mathbf{5\,\mu s}$ at 12 MHz.

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: 8051 Interrupt Source Summary

This high-density matrix is the **single most important revision table** for the KTU ESE on this topic:

| # | Interrupt Source | Flag Bit (SFR) | Enable Bit (IE) | Priority Bit (IP) | Vector Address (Hex) | Vector Address (Decimal) | Natural Priority |
| :-: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **System Reset** | — (RST pin) | Always Enabled | Highest (Non-maskable) | 0x0000 | 0 | — |
| 2 | **External Int 0** | IE0 (TCON.1) | EX0 (IE.0) | PX0 (IP.0) | 0x0003 | 3 | 1 (Highest) |
| 3 | **Timer 0 Overflow** | TF0 (TCON.5) | ET0 (IE.1) | PT0 (IP.1) | 0x000B | 11 | 2 |
| 4 | **External Int 1** | IE1 (TCON.3) | EX1 (IE.2) | PX1 (IP.2) | 0x0013 | 19 | 3 |
| 5 | **Timer 1 Overflow** | TF1 (TCON.7) | ET1 (IE.3) | PT1 (IP.3) | 0x001B | 27 | 4 |
| 6 | **Serial Port (TI/RI)** | TI / RI (SCON.0/1) | ES (IE.4) | PS (IP.4) | 0x0023 | 35 | 5 (Lowest) |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** The **Reset** is technically *not* an interrupt but is universally counted as the highest-priority event in 8051 pedagogy. KTU questions often ask: "List all interrupts of 8051 with their priorities." The expected answer includes **5 interrupts + Reset = 6 events**.

## 3.4 Worked Example: Configuring an External Interrupt (Textual Walkthrough)

**Problem:** Write the conceptual initialization sequence to enable `INT1` as a falling-edge-triggered, high-priority interrupt in the 8051.

### Step-by-Step Logical Solution

**Step 1 — Configure the trigger type.** Set the `IT1` bit in the TCON register. `IT1 = 1` selects edge-triggered (falling edge).

**Step 2 — Enable the individual interrupt source.** Set the `EX1` bit in the IE register. `EX1 = 1`.

**Step 3 — Set the priority level.** Set the `PX1` bit in the IP register. `PX1 = 1` (high priority).

**Step 4 — Set the global enable.** Set the `EA` bit in the IE register. `EA = 1`.

**Step 5 — Define the ISR.** Place the service routine starting at address `0x0013` (the vector), ending with the `RETI` instruction.

> [!TIP]
> For textual/handwritten exams, students should explicitly list all four SFR bits being modified. KTU valuators award **1 mark per correct bit** and **1 mark for the ISR structure** (vector address + `RETI`).

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 8051 Interrupt Subsystem — Block-Level Functional Architecture

The following Mermaid block diagram depicts the full interrupt architecture of the 8051, including all five interrupt sources, the IE/IP/TCON control logic, and the CPU's response pathway.

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Sources [Interrupt Sources]
        src1[External Pin INT0]
        src2[External Pin INT1]
        src3[Timer 0 Overflow TF0]
        src4[Timer 1 Overflow TF1]
        src5[Serial Port TI or RI]
        srcR[RESET Pin]
    end

    subgraph EdgeControl [TCON Register Edge or Level Logic]
        edge0[IT0 Edge Selector]
        edge1[IT1 Edge Selector]
    end

    subgraph EnableMatrix [IE Register Enable Matrix]
        eaBit[EA Global Enable]
        ex0Bit[EX0]
        et0Bit[ET0]
        ex1Bit[EX1]
        et1Bit[ET1]
        esBit[ES]
    end

    subgraph PriorityMatrix [IP Register Priority Matrix]
        px0Bit[PX0]
        pt0Bit[PT0]
        px1Bit[PX1]
        pt1Bit[PT1]
        psBit[PS]
    end

    subgraph Arbiter [Hardware Priority Arbiter]
        arb[Vector Address Selector]
    end

    subgraph CPU [8051 CPU Core]
        stack[(Stack Memory)]
        isr[Interrupt Service Routine]
        pc[Program Counter]
    end

    src1 --> edge0
    src2 --> edge1
    edge0 --> arb
    edge1 --> arb
    src3 --> arb
    src4 --> arb
    src5 --> arb
    srcR --> arb

    eaBit --> arb
    ex0Bit --> arb
    et0Bit --> arb
    ex1Bit --> arb
    et1Bit --> arb
    esBit --> arb

    px0Bit --> arb
    pt0Bit --> arb
    px1Bit --> arb
    pt1Bit --> arb
    psBit --> arb

    arb -- Vector Address 0x0003, 0x000B, 0x0013, 0x001B, 0x0023 --> pc
    pc -- Save PC to Stack --> stack
    pc --> isr
    isr -- RETI restores PC --> pc
```

## 4.2 Interrupt Processing Sequence — Lifecycle Flow

This sequence diagram illustrates the **temporal sequence** of operations when an 8051 interrupt is serviced, suitable for direct KTU board presentation.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A([CPU executing MAIN program]) --> B{Interrupt Request Detected?}
    B -- No --> A
    B -- Yes --> C{Check EA bit in IE register}
    C -- EA equals 0 --> A
    C -- EA equals 1 --> D{Check Individual Enable Bit EX, ET, or ES}
    D -- Disabled --> A
    D -- Enabled --> E{Complete current instruction}
    E --> F[Push 16-bit PC onto Stack]
    F --> G[Load PC with Vector Address]
    G --> H[Execute ISR routine]
    H --> I{End of ISR reached?}
    I -- No --> H
    I -- Yes RETI --> J[Pop 16-bit PC from Stack]
    J --> K([Resume MAIN program from interrupted point])
```

## 4.3 Interrupt Vector Map — Memory Layout Schematic

This block diagram shows the **physical memory layout** of the interrupt vector table in the 8051's code memory space, illustrating why each ISR must be carefully sized (or use a `LJMP` redirection if larger than 8 bytes).

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    m0000[Address 0x0000 RESET Vector 8 bytes]
    m0003[Address 0x0003 INT0 ISR Space 8 bytes]
    m000B[Address 0x000B Timer 0 ISR Space 8 bytes]
    m0013[Address 0x0013 INT1 ISR Space 8 bytes]
    m001B[Address 0x001B Timer 1 ISR Space 8 bytes]
    m0023[Address 0x0023 Serial Port ISR Space 8 bytes]
    m002B[Address 0x002B Timer 2 ISR 8052 only 8 bytes]
    m0030[Address 0x0030 onwards User Main Program]

    m0000 --> m0003
    m0003 --> m000B
    m000B --> m0013
    m0013 --> m001B
    m001B --> m0023
    m0023 --> m002B
    m002B --> m0030
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner Insight:** The **8-byte spacing** between vectors is a critical design constraint. If an ISR exceeds 8 bytes, it will **overflow into the next interrupt's vector**, corrupting it. The standard professional practice is to place a `LJMP target` instruction at each vector, redirecting execution to the actual ISR located elsewhere in code memory.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`
**Define the term "Interrupt" in the context of the 8051 microcontroller. List all five interrupt sources of the 8051.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

An interrupt is an asynchronous event (either external hardware signal or internal condition) that temporarily suspends the main program execution and forces the CPU to execute a dedicated **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**. After the ISR completes, the CPU resumes the main program from the exact point of interruption.

The five 8051 interrupt sources are:

1. **External Interrupt 0 (INT0)**
2. **External Interrupt 1 (INT1)**
3. **Timer 0 Overflow**
4. **Timer 1 Overflow**
5. **Serial Port Interrupt (TI/RI)**

*(1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the concept of ISR/resumption, 1 mark for listing all five sources.)*

---

### Q2. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`
**Differentiate between "Polling" and "Interrupt" methods of I/O handling in microcontrollers.**

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

| Parameter | Polling | Interrupt |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| **Initiator** | Software (CPU checks flag in a loop) | Hardware (peripheral asserts request line) |
| **CPU Utilization** | Wastes CPU cycles in busy-wait loop | CPU free to do other tasks; reacts only on event |
| **Response Time** | Depends on polling interval; variable | Immediate (within latency of a few machine cycles) |
| **Power Efficiency** | Poor — CPU always active | High — CPU can sleep until woken |
| **Complexity** | Simple, deterministic | Requires ISR design and vector management |

*(1 mark per major differentiating criterion; aim for at least 3 clear distinctions.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **KTU ESE Pattern Note:** Each Part B question in Module 1 carries **14 marks**, typically split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. You are required to answer **either** Question A **or** Question B in full.

---

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]`

**(a) Explain the structure and bit functions of the IE (Interrupt Enable) register of the 8051. How is it used to enable a specific interrupt? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Structure of IE Register (SFR address 0xA8, bit-addressable):**

The IE register is an 8-bit Special Function Register that controls the enabling of all 8051 interrupts. Its bit map is:

| Bit | Symbol | Function |
| :-: | :--- | :--- |
| IE.7 | **EA** | Global Enable — must be 1 for any interrupt to be recognized |
| IE.6 | — | Reserved (undefined) |
| IE.5 | — | Reserved (undefined) |
| IE.4 | **ES** | Enable Serial Port interrupt |
| IE.3 | **ET1** | Enable Timer 1 overflow interrupt |
| IE.2 | **EX1** | Enable External Interrupt 1 |
| IE.1 | **ET0** | Enable Timer 0 overflow interrupt |
| IE.0 | **EX0** | Enable External Interrupt 0 |

**Two-Level Enabling Logic (7 Marks):**

The 8051 uses a **two-level interrupt enabling scheme**:

1. **Global Level:** The **EA bit (IE.7)** acts as a master switch. If EA = 0, *all* interrupts are disabled, regardless of individual bits. EA must be set to 1 to permit any interrupt.

2. **Individual Level:** Even if EA = 1, a specific interrupt will be serviced *only* if its corresponding enable bit is also set. For example, the Timer 0 interrupt requires both **EA = 1** and **ET0 = 1**.

**Example — Enabling Timer 0 interrupt only:**

To enable Timer 0, the bits EA and ET0 must be set, while all other enable bits remain 0. The IE register value becomes `10000010` in binary, which is `0x82` in hexadecimal.

**Reset State:** Upon system reset, IE is loaded with `0x00`, disabling all interrupts. The programmer must explicitly configure the IE register at the start of the main program.

*(Distribution: 3 marks for register structure/table, 2 marks for two-level enabling logic, 2 marks for worked example & reset state.)*

---

**(b) With a neat diagram, explain the interrupt vector address structure of the 8051. List the vector addresses of all five interrupts. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**Concept of Vector Address:**

A vector address is the **fixed memory location** in program memory to which the CPU automatically transfers control when a specific interrupt is acknowledged. Each 8051 interrupt has a unique, hardware-assigned vector.

**Memory Layout Diagram (simplified, code memory space):**

| Address Range | Allocated To |
| :--- | :--- |
| 0x0000 – 0x0002 | Reset vector (system start) |
| 0x0003 – 0x000A | External Interrupt 0 (INT0) |
| 0x000B – 0x0012 | Timer 0 overflow |
| 0x0013 – 0x001A | External Interrupt 1 (INT1) |
| 0x001B – 0x0022 | Timer 1 overflow |
| 0x0023 – 0x002A | Serial Port interrupt |
| 0x002B – 0x002A | Timer 2 overflow (8052 only) |

**List of Vector Addresses (in tabular form):**

| Interrupt Source | Vector Address (Hex) | Decimal |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Reset | 0x0000 | 0 |
| External Interrupt 0 (INT0) | 0x0003 | 3 |
| Timer 0 Overflow | 0x000B | 11 |
| External Interrupt 1 (INT1) | 0x0013 | 19 |
| Timer 1 Overflow | 0x001B | 27 |
| Serial Port (TI/RI) | 0x0023 | 35 |

**Key Architectural Insight:**

Each vector is allocated **8 bytes** of code memory. Since most ISRs are larger than 8 bytes, the professional practice is to place a **3-byte `LJMP target`** instruction at each vector, redirecting execution to the actual ISR located elsewhere in program memory. This prevents overflow into adjacent vector spaces.

*(Distribution: 2 marks for concept, 3 marks for diagram/table, 2 marks for the 8-byte spacing insight and LJMP redirection technique.)*

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]`

**(a) Describe the sequence of operations that the 8051 CPU performs when responding to an interrupt. (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

When an enabled interrupt is activated, the 8051 CPU executes the following **seven-step sequence**:

**Step 1 — Instruction Completion:**
The CPU completes execution of the current instruction before acknowledging the interrupt. This ensures atomicity of the in-flight instruction. The worst-case instruction (MUL/DIV) takes 4 machine cycles.

**Step 2 — Interrupt Detection by Hardware:**
The interrupt subsystem polls all enabled interrupt flags at the end of each machine cycle. The EA bit and the relevant individual enable bit must both be set. If multiple interrupts are pending, the **priority logic** (IP register + natural priority) selects the highest-priority one.

**Step 3 — Hardware Clears the Edge Flag (Edge-Triggered Only):**
For edge-triggered external interrupts, the corresponding `IE0` or `IE1` flag in TCON is **automatically cleared by hardware** to prevent re-entry.

**Step 4 — Push Program Counter (PC) onto Stack:**
The 16-bit PC (current program location) is saved on the stack. The stack pointer (SP) is incremented by 2. Note: only the PC is saved by hardware in the 8051; the PSW and accumulator are not automatically preserved and must be saved in software if needed.

**Step 5 — Load PC with Vector Address:**
The CPU loads the PC with the pre-assigned vector address of the chosen interrupt. For example, for Timer 0, PC ← 0x000B.

**Step 6 — Execute the ISR:**
The CPU begins executing instructions at the vector address. The ISR typically begins with **register-saving** (`PUSH` instructions for ACC, PSW, etc.) and ends with corresponding `POP` instructions, followed by `RETI`.

**Step 7 — Return from Interrupt (RETI):**
The `RETI` instruction pops the saved 16-bit PC from the stack, restoring it to the program counter. Execution resumes at the originally interrupted location in the main program. The `RETI` instruction also signals the interrupt controller that the servicing is complete, allowing lower-priority interrupts to be recognized.

**Timing Note:** The entire interrupt acknowledgment cycle (Steps 1–5) takes approximately **5 machine cycles**, representing the **interrupt latency**.

*(Distribution: 1 mark per step for the first 5 steps, 1 mark for ISR execution specifics, 1 mark for RETI semantics and timing.)*

---

**(b) Explain the IP (Interrupt Priority) register of the8051. What is the role of natural priority order, and how does it apply when two interrupts of equal software priority occur simultaneously? (7 Marks)**

**Model Answer:**

**IP Register Structure (SFR address 0xB8, bit-addressable):**

The IP register assigns one of two priority levels — **High (logic 1)** or **Low (logic 0)** — to each of the five maskable interrupts. Its layout is:

| Bit | Symbol | Function |
| :-: | :--- | :--- |
| IP.7 – IP.5 | — | Reserved |
| IP.4 | **PS** | Serial Port priority (1 = High) |
| IP.3 | **PT1** | Timer 1 priority (1 = High) |
| IP.2 | **PX1** | External Interrupt 1 priority (1 = High) |
| IP.1 | **PT0** | Timer 0 priority (1 = High) |
| IP.0 | **PX0** | External Interrupt 0 priority (1 = High) |

**Priority Resolution Logic (Two-Tier):**

The 8051's interrupt priority system operates on **two tiers**:

**Tier 1 — Software-Configured Priority (IP Register):**
When two interrupts with different IP bit values are activated simultaneously, the one with the **higher IP value (logic 1)** wins. For example, if PX1 = 1 and PT0 = 0, and both INT1 and Timer 0 fire at the same time, **INT1 is serviced first**.

**Tier 2 — Natural (Default) Hardware Priority:**
When two interrupts have the **same software priority** (both IP bits equal), the **natural priority order** — which is hard-wired into the silicon — acts as the tiebreaker. The natural order from highest to lowest is:

$$
\text{INT0} \; > \; \text{Timer 0} \; > \; \text{INT1} \; > \; \text{Timer 1} \; > \; \text{Serial Port}
$$

**Worked Example:**

Suppose **EX0 = EX1 = 1, EA = 1, PX0 = 0, PX1 = 0** (both external interrupts at low priority). If both `INT0` and `INT1` are activated simultaneously, both have the same software priority. The natural priority then decides: **INT0 is serviced first**, then INT1 is serviced.

**Preemption Within ISRs:**
If a higher-priority interrupt is activated **while a lower-priority ISR is executing**, the 8051 **nests** the new interrupt: it pushes another PC onto the stack and services the higher-priority interrupt first. This nested behavior is a critical feature for real-time systems.

**Reset State:** Upon reset, IP = 0x00, meaning all interrupts default to **low software priority**. Tie-breaking then relies entirely on natural priority.

*(Distribution: 2 marks for IP register structure, 2 marks for software priority tier, 2 marks for natural priority + worked example, 1 mark for preemption/reset state.)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Areas in 8051 Interrupt Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting EA = 1:** Many students configure individual enable bits but forget the global enable. KTU valuators deduct 1 full mark for this.
> 2. **Confusing TCON bits:** `IT0/IT1` are in TCON (not IE). Students often mix up `IE0/IE1` (flag bits) with `EX0/EX1` (enable bits).
> 3. **Wrong vector addresses:** Memorize them as `0x0003, 0x000B, 0x0013, 0x001B, 0x0023` — the pattern is `+8` each time.
> 4. **Confusing maskable and non-maskable:** All five 8051 interrupts are maskable. Reset is non-maskable.
> 5. **Ignoring 8-byte ISR space:** Writing a long ISR directly at the vector without using `LJMP` redirection will overwrite adjacent vectors. Always mention this in 7+ mark questions.
> 6. **Forgetting RETI vs RET:** Only `RETI` (not `RET`) must be used to end an ISR; otherwise, the interrupt controller is not properly notified.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> **Rapid Revision Checklist — 8051 Interrupts:**

- ⭐ The 8051 has **5 maskable interrupt sources** + 1 non-maskable Reset = 6 total events.
- ⭐ The 5 interrupts: **INT0, Timer 0, INT1, Timer 1, Serial Port**.
- ⭐ **Vector addresses** are spaced **8 bytes** apart: `0x0003, 0x000B, 0x0013, 0x001B, 0x0023`.
- ⭐ The **IE register** (0xA8) has a master **EA** bit + 5 individual enable bits (`EX0, ET0, EX1, ET1, ES`).
- ⭐ The **IP register** (0xB8) has 5 priority bits (`PX0, PT0, PX1, PT1, PS`), each giving High or Low priority.
- ⭐ **Natural priority order** (tiebreaker): `INT0 > T0 > INT1 > T1 > Serial`.
- ⭐ External interrupts can be **level-triggered (low)** or **edge-triggered (falling)**, configured by `IT0/IT1` in TCON.
- ⭐ On interrupt entry, the **PC (16-bit) is pushed to stack**; **PSW and ACC are NOT** auto-saved.
- ⭐ ISRs must end with the **`RETI`** instruction (not `RET`) to correctly signal end-of-service.
- ⭐ If ISR > 8 bytes, place an **`LJMP target`** at the vector to redirect to the actual ISR.
- ⭐ **Interrupt latency** ≈ 4 (worst-case instruction) + 1 (vectoring) = **5 machine cycles**.
- ⭐ **8052 enhancement:** Adds a 6th interrupt for **Timer 2** at vector `0x002B`.
- ⭐ Polling is software-driven & CPU-wasteful; interrupts are hardware-driven & CPU-efficient.
- ⭐ Higher-priority interrupts can **preempt** (nest into) lower-priority ISRs.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Instruction Set of 8051 and Addressing Modes - Classification of Instruction Set - Data Transfer Group
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET613 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Instruction Set of 8051 and Addressing Modes - Classification of Instruction Set - Data Transfer Group

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 The 8051 Instruction Set — Formal Definition

The **Instruction Set** of the Intel **MCS-51 (8051)** family is the complete, finite collection of binary-coded machine-level commands that the 8-bit CPU core can decode, interpret, and execute. Every instruction is a precisely defined, vendor-architected binary pattern (an *opcode* possibly followed by *operand bytes*) that the Central Processing Unit (CPU) fetches from program memory, decodes via its hardwired control logic, and then executes within a deterministic number of **machine cycles**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** The 8051 instruction set comprises **111 distinct instructions** (often cited as "111 base instructions" or "255 opcodes" because many mnemonics map to multiple addressing-mode variants). It supports **8 distinct addressing modes** and executes instructions in **1, 2, or 4 machine cycles** where each machine cycle = **12 oscillator clocks**.

## 1.2 Addressing Modes — Formal Definition

An **Addressing Mode** is the *method* by which the CPU computes the *effective physical location* of an operand (a source value, a destination, or a jump target) referenced by an instruction. The 8051 supports multiple addressing modes because a given logical operation (e.g., "move the contents of A to RAM location 30H") can be expressed in many ways depending on **where the operand lives** — in a register, in internal RAM, in external data memory, in code memory, in a bit, or as an immediate constant.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why multiple modes?** The addressing mode determines *how many bytes* an instruction occupies (1, 2, or 3), *how many machine cycles* it consumes, and what kind of hardware/registers it can access. Mastering addressing modes is the single highest-leverage skill for writing efficient 8051 code.

## 1.3 Data Transfer Group — Formal Definition

The **Data Transfer Group** is one of the five functional classifications of the 8051 instruction set (the other four being Arithmetic, Logical, Boolean (Bit), and Program Branching). It is the *largest* functional group, containing all instructions whose **only effect** is to **copy** data from a *source* location to a *destination* location, leaving the source unmodified. The group provides the means by which information flows between the Accumulator, general-purpose registers, internal RAM (both direct and indirect), Special Function Registers (SFRs), external data memory, program (code) memory, and the stack.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Universal Truth about Data Transfer:** The 8051 CPU architecture treats the Accumulator (**A**, also written **ACC**) as the **universal "hub"** for nearly all data movement operations. Almost every data transfer instruction uses A as either the source, the destination, or both. This is a direct consequence of the original Intel accumulator-based design philosophy.

## 1.4 Intuitive Analogy — The Postal System

Imagine the 8051's internal architecture as a **vast post office** with many mailrooms:

- **Accumulator (A)** is the **head postmaster's desk** — every parcel passing through the office must be touched by this desk.
- **Register Banks (R0–R7)** are the **eight clerks sitting at desks** on the main floor. You can address them by desk number.
- **Internal RAM (00H–7FH)** is the **filing cabinets** lining the walls. You specify a cabinet number to file or retrieve.
- **SFRs (80H–FFH)** are the **specialized offices** (the accounting office at 80H, the dispatch office at B0H, etc.).
- **External Data Memory (XRAM)** is a **remote warehouse** accessed only by the senior clerk.
- **Code Memory (ROM)** is the **company rule book** that everyone consults.
- **Stack** is the **loading dock tray** where in-progress work is temporarily set aside.

An **instruction** is a *memo* ("move 25H to A"). An **addressing mode** is the *style* of the memo:
- "Get the parcel from **Cabinet 25**" → **Direct Addressing**
- "Get the parcel from the cabinet whose number is **written on Clerk R1's clipboard**" → **Indirect Addressing**
- "Here is the parcel — use it **directly**" → **Immediate Addressing**
- "Read **Table 3, entry 7** from the rule book" → **Indexed Addressing**

The **Data Transfer Group** is the set of memos whose *sole purpose* is **relocating parcels** from one room to another — they never open, modify, or repack the parcel. They are the **movers and shakers** of the postal system.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Insight:** Examiners frequently ask students to identify the **addressing mode** of a given instruction and the **number of bytes / machine cycles** it consumes. Always quote the standard 8051 datasheet byte/cycle counts precisely.

## 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics

| Metric | Value | Significance |
|---|---|---|
| **Total Instructions** | **111** | Distinct mnemonic-operation pairs |
| **Total Opcodes** | **255** | Some mnemonics expand to multiple opcodes |
| **Opcode Width** | **8 bits** | Single-byte opcodes (some 2-byte) |
| **Addressing Modes** | **8** | For data space (5 most common) |
| **Machine Cycle** | **12 oscillator periods** | Fixed for original 8051 family |
| **Instruction Sizes** | **1, 2, or 3 bytes** | Determined by mode + operands |
| **Cycles per Instruction** | **1, 2, or 4** | Determined by mode + memory type |
| **Accumulator** | **A / ACC (E0H)** | Central data hub |
| **Program Counter** | **16-bit (PC)** | Can address 64 KB code memory |
| **Data Pointer** | **DPTR (16-bit)** | Used for MOVX and MOVC |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of the 8051 Instruction Set (Five Functional Groups)

The 8051 instruction set is canonically partitioned into **five functional groups**. This classification is the *single most important structural fact* required for KTU Module 2 questions.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The 5 Functional Groups of 8051 Instructions:**
> 1. **Data Transfer Group** ← *topic of this module section*
> 2. **Arithmetic Group**
> 3. **Logical Group**
> 4. **Boolean (Bit-Manipulation) Group**
> 5. **Program Branching (Control Transfer) Group**

### 2.1.1 Why the Classification Matters

Each group is a **functional island**: the operations within a group share a *semantic theme* (move, compute, test, branch, manipulate bit). The **opcode map** of the 8051 is laid out so that the upper bits of an opcode generally identify its group. For example:

- `0110 xxxx` → data transfer (ARITHMETIC-LOGIC-UNIT-to-memory moves, etc.)
- `0010 0xxx` → arithmetic on A
- `0101 xxxx` → logical on A
- `1010 xxxx` and similar → direct/indirect jumps and calls
- `1100 xxxx` → single-bit operations (set, clear, AND, OR, move)

## 2.2 The Eight Addressing Modes of the 8051

The 8051 supports **eight** addressing modes. Five are commonly used in KTU exams; three (relative, bit, and some indexed variants) round out the list.

> [!NOTE]
> **Complete List of 8051 Addressing Modes:**
> 1. **Immediate Addressing** (e.g., `MOV A, #25H`)
> 2. **Register (Direct Register) Addressing** (e.g., `MOV A, R4`)
> 3. **Direct Addressing** (e.g., `MOV A, 30H`)
> 4. **Register Indirect Addressing** (e.g., `MOV A, @R0`)
> 5. **Indexed Addressing** (e.g., `MOVC A, @A+DPTR`)
> 6. **Relative Addressing** (used by `SJMP`)
> 7. **Absolute Addressing** (used by `AJMP` and `ACALL`)
> 8. **Bit Addressing** (used by bit-level instructions)

### 2.2.1 Detailed Mode-by-Mode Analysis

**1. Immediate Addressing**
- Operand value is **embedded directly inside the instruction** (after the opcode).
- Identifiable by the `#` prefix in assembly.
- Example: `MOV A, #0FH` — loads A with the literal value 0FH.
- Size: **2 bytes** (opcode + immediate data). Cycles: **1**.

**2. Register (Direct Register) Addressing**
- Operand lives in one of the **current register bank's R0–R7**.
- The register bank is selected by bits **RS0, RS1** of the **Program Status Word (PSW)**.
- Example: `MOV A, R5` — copies R5 into A.
- Size: **1 byte**. Cycles: **1**.

**3. Direct Addressing**
- Operand lives at a specific address in **internal RAM (00H–7FH)** or in the **SFR region (80H–FFH)**.
- The 8-bit address is part of the instruction.
- Example: `MOV A, 30H` — copies the byte at RAM[30H] into A.
- Example: `MOV A, 0E0H` — copies the Accumulator (SFR E0H) into A (redundant but legal).
- Size: **2 bytes**. Cycles: **1**.

**4. Register Indirect Addressing**
- The address of the operand is **held inside a register** (R0, R1, or the stack pointer SP — or DPTR for MOVX).
- The `@" symbol in assembly indicates indirection.
- Example: `MOV A, @R0` — A gets the byte whose address is the *value* inside R0.
- Crucial for **table traversal**, **loops over arrays**, and **stack operations**.
- Size: **1 byte**. Cycles: **1**.

**5. Indexed Addressing**
- The effective address = **(A) + (DPTR or PC)**.
- Used exclusively with **MOVC** (move from code memory) and a few jump variants.
- Example: `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` — A gets the byte at code-memory address `(A) + (DPTR)`.
- Size: **1 byte**. Cycles: **2**.

**6. Relative Addressing**
- The 8-bit signed offset is added to the **Program Counter (PC)** to compute the new PC.
- Used **only** by `SJMP` and conditional jumps.
- Range: **−128 to +127** bytes from the instruction *following* the jump.
- Example: `SJMP REL` where REL is the 2's-complement offset.

**7. Absolute Addressing**
- The 11-bit target address is concatenated with the **upper 5 bits of the current PC** to form a 16-bit target.
- Used by `AJMP` and `ACALL`.
- Range: **2 KB page** (same upper 5 bits of PC).
- Example: `AJMP addr11`.

**8. Bit Addressing**
- The operand is a **single bit** in a bit-addressable location.
- Bit-addressable space: **internal RAM 20H–2FH (16 bytes = 128 bits)** and **SFRs whose addresses end in 0 or 8** (e.g., P0, TCON, SCON, IE, etc. — total 16 SFRs × 8 bits = 128 bits).
- Example: `SETB P1.0`, `MOV C, 20H.3`, `ANL C, /ACC.0`.

## 2.3 Data Transfer Group — Complete Taxonomy

The Data Transfer Group is further subdivided by the **memory region** the operands inhabit.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Data Transfer Group Sub-Groups:**
> - **Internal RAM ↔ Accumulator Moves** (MOV, XCH, XCHD, SWAP)
> - **External Data Memory Moves** (MOVX)
> - **Program (Code) Memory Moves** (MOVC)
> - **Stack Operations** (PUSH, POP)

### 2.3.1 Core Data Transfer Instructions

| Mnemonic | Function | Allowed Operand Combinations | Bytes | Cycles |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **MOV** | Copy byte from source to destination | A ↔ Rn, A ↔ direct, A ↔ @Ri, A ↔ #data, Rn ↔ direct, Rn ↔ #data, direct ↔ A, direct ↔ Rn, direct ↔ direct, direct ↔ @Ri, direct ↔ #data, @Ri ↔ A, @Ri ↔ direct, @Ri ↔ #data, DPTR ← #data16 | 1/2/3 | 1/2 |
| **MOVX** | Copy byte to/from external data memory | A ↔ @Ri (8-bit addr), A ↔ @DPTR (16-bit addr) | 1/2/3 | 2 |
| **MOVC** | Copy byte from code memory to A | A ← @A+DPTR, A ← @A+PC | 1 | 2 |
| **XCH** | Exchange A with byte (A↔RAM) | A ↔ Rn, A ↔ direct, A ↔ @Ri | 1/2 | 1 |
| **XCHD** | Exchange low nibbles of A and @Ri | A ↔ @Ri (low nibble only) | 1 | 1 |
| **SWAP** | Swap the two nibbles inside A | A internal swap | 1 | 1 |
| **PUSH** | Push byte onto stack (SP++) | direct → stack | 2 | 2 |
| **POP** | Pop byte from stack (SP--) | stack → direct | 2 | 2 |

### 2.3.2 MOV — The Workhorse

`MOV` is the single most frequently used instruction. Its **opcode is determined by the addressing mode of both source and destination**, and it is one of the few instructions that allow *register-to-register* direct moves in only one direction in some addressing pairs.

> [!WARNING]
> **MOV limitation:** `MOV Rn, Rm` (register to register) is **NOT allowed** as a single instruction. You must route through A: `MOV A, Rm` then `MOV Rn, A`. This is a very common KTU exam trap.

### 2.3.3 MOVX — Crossing the External Boundary

`MOVX` is a **distinct mnemonic** because external data memory access requires the CPU to assert external bus control signals (RD, WR, and the lower address byte latched by ALE for 8-bit-addressed moves). It is used to access off-chip RAM (XDATA space, addresses `0000H–FFFFH`).

- `MOVX A, @R0` — read from external address `(R0)`, 8-bit address.
- `MOVX @R0, A` — write A to external address `(R0)`.
- `MOVX A, @DPTR` — read from external address `(DPTR)`, 16-bit address.
- `MOVX @DPTR, A` — write A to external address `(DPTR)`.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Insight:** `MOVX` always takes **2 machine cycles** because it activates the external bus; internal MOV always takes **1 cycle**. This timing difference is critical in real-time control loops.

### 2.3.4 MOVC — Reading the Rule Book (Code Memory)

`MOVC` reads a byte from program memory (ROM). It is the *only* way to read a constant table stored in code space at runtime. Two forms exist:
- `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` — A ← `code[A + DPTR]` (DPTR points to the *base* of the table).
- `MOVC A, @A+PC` — A ← `code[A + PC]` (PC points to the *instruction after* MOVC; useful for position-independent code).

### 2.3.5 PUSH and POP — The Stack Interface

The 8051's stack is **internal RAM space only** (typically the 16 bytes from `08H` upward, growing toward `7FH`). SP (SFR at 81H) holds the *top* stack address. 
- `PUSH direct` does: `SP ← SP+1`, then `RAM[SP] ← (direct)`.
- `POP direct` does: `(direct) ← RAM[SP]`, then `SP ← SP−1`.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Exam Note:** `PUSH` and `POP` must use a **direct** address — they cannot push/pop Rn, @Ri, or immediate values. Pushing or popping a single register requires the explicit form `PUSH ACC` (or `PUSH 0E0H`).

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| Quantity / Rule | Formula / Statement | Units / Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Instruction fetch time** | $T_{inst} = N_{cycles} \times 12 \times T_{osc}$ | $T_{osc}$ = oscillator period |
| **Machine cycle duration** | $T_{MC} = 12 \times T_{osc} = \dfrac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | $f_{osc}$ in Hz, $T_{MC}$ in seconds |
| **Instruction time (1 cycle)** | $T = 12 \times T_{osc}$ | e.g., 1 µs at 12 MHz |
| **Instruction time (2 cycles)** | $T = 24 \times T_{osc}$ | e.g., 2 µs at 12 MHz |
| **MOVC effective address** | $EA = (A) + (DPTR) \;\vert\; (A) + (PC)$ | Indexed addressing formula |
| **SJMP branch target** | $T_{arg} = (PC_{current}) + rel$ | $rel \in [-128, +127]$ |
| **AJMP branch target** | $T_{arg} = (PC[15:11] \,\vert\, addr_{10:0})$ | 11-bit absolute |
| **PUSH address** | $EA_{stack} = (SP) + 1$ | After increment, byte stored |
| **POP address** | $EA_{stack} = (SP)$ | Read first, then SP-- |
| **MOVX 16-bit external** | $EA_{ext} = (DPTR)$ | Full 64 KB accessible |
| **MOVX 8-bit external** | $EA_{ext} = (P2) \cdot 2^{8} + (R_i)$ | P2 high byte + Ri low byte |
| **Bit-addressable range** | internal `20H`–`2FH` (128 bits) | Plus 16 bit-addressable SFRs |
| **Register bank selection** | bank $\in \{0,1,2,3\}$ via PSW[RS1:RS0] | Affects R0–R7 mapping |

# 3. Step-by-Step Analytical Matrix & Worked Examples

This section walks through every key Data Transfer instruction and addressing mode with **exhaustive, step-by-step demonstrations** showing the binary encoding, byte/cycle count, and the precise CPU action.

## 3.1 Analysis Framework for Any Data Transfer Instruction

For every MOV-family instruction, follow this **5-step analytical method**:

1. **Identify the opcode** from the addressing-mode pair.
2. **Count the instruction bytes** (1, 2, or 3).
3. **Count the machine cycles** (1 or 2).
4. **Compute the effective address** of the source.
5. **Verify destination validity** (cannot write to immediate or code memory).

## 3.2 Worked Example 1 — `MOV A, #25H` (Immediate Addressing)

**Step 1: Identify the opcode.**
The opcode for `MOV A, #data` is `0111 0100` = `74H`.

**Step 2: Count bytes.** `74H` (opcode) + `25H` (immediate) = **2 bytes** in code memory.

**Step 3: Count cycles.** **1 machine cycle**.

**Step 4: Compute effective source address.** The source is the *literal* `25H` — no memory access needed; the value rides along with the instruction fetch.

**Step 5: Validate destination.** Destination is **A** (SFR `E0H`), which is read/write. ✓

**CPU Action Sequence (per machine cycle):**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Fetch 1:} \quad & PC \leftarrow (PC)+1,\; IR \leftarrow \text{codemem}(PC) \;[\text{opcode } 74H] \\
\text{Fetch 2:} \quad & PC \leftarrow (PC)+1,\; A \leftarrow \text{codemem}(PC) \;[= 25H]
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Net effect after execution:** `(A) = 25H`. The program counter has advanced by 2.

## 3.3 Worked Example 2 — `MOV A, R3` (Register Addressing)

Assume **Register Bank 0** is active (PSW.3 = RS0 = 0, PSW.4 = RS1 = 0). Then R3 corresponds to internal RAM address `03H`.

**Opcode:** `1110 1011` = `EBH` (for R3).

**Size:** **1 byte**, **1 cycle**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
PC &\leftarrow (PC)+1 \\
A &\leftarrow RAM[03H] \quad \text{[the byte currently in R3]}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.4 Worked Example 3 — `MOV A, 30H` (Direct Addressing)

**Opcode:** `1110 0101` = `E5H` followed by address byte `30H`.

**Size:** **2 bytes** (`E5H, 30H`), **1 cycle**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
PC &\leftarrow (PC)+1, \quad A_{temp} \leftarrow \text{codemem}(PC) \;[= 30H] \\
PC &\leftarrow (PC)+1, \quad A \leftarrow RAM[A_{temp}] \;[= RAM[30H]]
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Key insight:** The address `30H` is *part of the instruction* (encoded in the 2nd byte), not a *value* like the immediate case. This is the semantic difference between Direct and Immediate addressing.

## 3.5 Worked Example 4 — `MOV A, @R0` (Register Indirect Addressing)

Assume `(R0) = 50H`. Then `@R0` means "the byte at internal RAM address 50H".

**Opcode:** `1110 0010` = `E2H` (for R0).

**Size:** **1 byte**, **1 cycle**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
PC &\leftarrow (PC)+1 \\
A &\leftarrow RAM[(R0)] \quad [= RAM[50H]]
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Critical distinction:** Direct addressing uses a *constant address embedded in the instruction*; Indirect addressing uses a *variable address loaded into R0/R1* beforehand. Indirection is essential for loops.

## 3.6 Worked Example 5 — `MOVC A, @A+DPTR` (Indexed Addressing)

Assume `(A) = 03H`, `(DPTR) = 2000H`.

**Step 1: Compute effective address.**

$$
EA = (A) + (DPTR) = 03H + 2000H = 2003H
$$

**Step 2: Opcode** is `1001 0011` = `93H`.

**Step 3: Size** = **1 byte**, **Cycles** = **2** (code-memory read takes 2 cycles).

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{Cycle 1:} \quad & PC \leftarrow (PC)+1,\; A \leftarrow \text{codemem}[(A)+(DPTR)] \;[= \text{codemem}(2003H)] \\
\text{Cycle 2:} \quad & \text{No-op, internal timing}
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Engineering application:** This is the standard construct for **lookup tables** (LUTs) — e.g., a sine-table generator, a 7-segment decoder, or a character-rotation table for alphanumeric LCDs.

## 3.7 Worked Example 6 — `MOVX A, @DPTR` (External Data Move)

Assume `(DPTR) = 1000H`. The CPU activates the external bus with read strobe.

**Opcode:** `1110 0000` = `E0H`.

**Size:** **1 byte**, **Cycles** = **2**.

$$
\begin{aligned}
EA &= (DPTR) = 1000H \\
A &\leftarrow XRAM[1000H] \quad \text{(activates } \overline{RD} \text{ strobe externally)}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.8 Worked Example 7 — `XCH A, @R0` (Exchange)

Assume `(A) = AAH`, `(R0) = 40H`, `RAM[40H] = 55H`. After execution, `(A) = 55H` and `RAM[40H] = AAH`. **No net memory write to A is treated as a read-modify-write**; the operation is a true atomic swap.

$$
\begin{aligned}
\text{temp} &\leftarrow (A) \\
A &\leftarrow RAM[(R0)] \\
RAM[(R0)] &\leftarrow \text{temp}
\end{aligned}
$$

## 3.9 Worked Example 8 — `PUSH 0E0H` and `POP 0F0H` (Stack Operations)

The SFR `0E0H` is **ACC** (Accumulator) and `0F0H` is **B register**. Assume `(SP) = 07H` and `(A) = 5AH`, `(B) = 9CH`.

**`PUSH 0E0H` action:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
SP &\leftarrow (SP) + 1 = 08H \\
RAM[(SP)] &\leftarrow (ACC) = 5AH \quad \Rightarrow RAM[08H] = 5AH
\end{aligned}
$$

**`POP 0F0H` action:**

$$
\begin{aligned}
(B) &\leftarrow RAM[(SP)] = RAM[08H] = 5AH \\
SP &\leftarrow (SP) - 1 = 07H
\end{aligned}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common student mistake:** Confusing PUSH/POP direction. **PUSH** stores *to* the stack (SP up first, then write). **POP** reads *from* the stack (read first, then SP down). The Accumulator is referred to as `ACC` (or `0E0H`) in stack instructions, never as `A`.

## 3.10 Summary Matrix — Data Transfer Group

| Instruction | Source Mode | Dest Mode | Bytes | Cycles | Modifies Flags? |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| MOV A,#d | Immediate | Accumulator | 2 | 1 | No |
| MOV A,Rn | Register | Accumulator | 1 | 1 | No |
| MOV A,dir | Direct | Accumulator | 2 | 1 | No |
| MOV A,@Ri | Indirect | Accumulator | 1 | 1 | No |
| MOV Rn,#d | Immediate | Register | 2 | 1 | No |
| MOV dir,#d | Immediate | Direct | 3 | 2 | No |
| MOV dir,dir | Direct | Direct | 3 | 2 | No |
| MOVX A,@Ri | Ext. Indirect | Accumulator | 1 | 2 | No |
| MOVX A,@DPTR | Ext. Indirect | Accumulator | 1 | 2 | No |
| MOVX @Ri,A | Accumulator | Ext. Indirect | 1 | 2 | No |
| MOVX @DPTR,A | Accumulator | Ext. Indirect | 1 | 2 | No |
| MOVC A,@A+DPTR | Indexed (code) | Accumulator | 1 | 2 | No |
| MOVC A,@A+PC | Indexed (code) | Accumulator | 1 | 2 | No |
| XCH A,Rn | Register | Accumulator (swap) | 1 | 1 | No |
| XCH A,dir | Direct | Accumulator (swap) | 2 | 1 | No |
| XCH A,@Ri | Indirect | Accumulator (swap) | 1 | 1 | No |
| XCHD A,@Ri | Indirect (nibble) | Accumulator (nibble swap) | 1 | 1 | No |
| SWAP A | Internal | Internal | 1 | 1 | No |
| PUSH dir | Direct (read) | Stack (write) | 2 | 2 | No |
| POP dir | Stack (read) | Direct (write) | 2 | 2 | No |

> [!TIP]
> **Universal Property of Data Transfer Group:** **No instruction in this group modifies any flag in the PSW** (not even the parity flag P). The flags are exclusively the domain of the Arithmetic and Logical groups. KTU examiners love to test this fact.

```mermaid
graph TD

    root8051 --> g1["Data Transfer Group"]

    root8051 --> g3["Logical Group"]
    root8051 --> g4["Boolean Bit Group"]
    root8051 --> g5["Program Branching Group"]

    g1 --> g1a["Internal MOV Family"]
    g1 --> g1b["MOVX - External Data"]
    g1 --> g1c["MOVC - Code Memory"]
    g1 --> g1d["XCH / XCHD / SWAP"]
    g1 --> g1e["PUSH / POP - Stack"]

    g1a --> g1a1["MOV A, src"]
    g1a --> g1a2["MOV dest, A"]
    g1a --> g1a3["MOV dest, src"]
    g1a --> g1a4["MOV DPTR, data16"]

    g2 --> g2a["ADD / ADDC / SUBB"]
    g2 --> g2b["INC / DEC"]
    g2 --> g2c["MUL / DIV"]
    g2 --> g2d["DA A - Decimal Adjust"]

    g3 --> g3a["ANL / ORL / XRL"]
    g3 --> g3b["CLR A / CPL A / RL / RLC / RR / RRC"]

    g4 --> g4a["SETB / CLR - Bit ops"]
    g4 --> g4b["ANL C / ORL C - Bit logic"]
    g4 --> g4c["MOV C, bit / MOV bit, C"]

    g5 --> g5a["ACALL / LCALL / RET / RETI"]
    g5 --> g5b["AJMP / LJMP / SJMP / JMP"]
    g5 --> g5c["Conditional Jumps - JZ JNZ JC JNC JB JNB JBC"]
    g5 --> g5d["CJNE - Compare Jump"]
    g5 --> g5e["DJNZ - Decrement Jump if Not Zero"]
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Start at the **8051 Instruction Set** root, traverse the five colored branches to reach any individual mnemonic. The **Data Transfer Group** (top-left) is the topic of this module.

## 4.2 Addressing Mode Decision Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    start["How is the operand specified?"]
    start --> m1["# prefix in source?"]
    m1 -- Yes --> imm["IMMEDIATE - constant rides with instruction - 2 bytes"]
    m1 -- No --> m2["Single Rn or ACC or B?"]
    m2 -- Yes --> reg["REGISTER - 1 byte, 0 cycles if Rn in bank"]
    m2 -- No --> m3["Symbol uses @ symbol?"]
    m3 -- Yes --> ind["REGISTER INDIRECT - @R0 or @R1 or @DPTR - 1 or 3 bytes"]
    m3 -- No --> m4["Used with MOVC A?"]
    m4 -- Yes --> idx["INDEXED - @A plus DPTR or PC - 1 byte"]
    m4 -- No --> m5["Bit dot notation P1.0?"]
    m5 -- Yes --> bit["BIT ADDRESSING - 2 bytes, single bit"]
    m5 -- No --> dir["DIRECT - 8-bit address in instruction - 2 or 3 bytes"]
```

## 4.3 CPU Data Paths — Block Diagram of Data Transfer

```mermaid
graph LR
    ACC["Accumulator A - SFR E0H - Central Data Hub"]
    RB["Register Bank R0 to R7 - RAM 00H to 1FH - 4 banks"]
    IRAM["Internal RAM 00H to 7FH - 128 bytes data"]
    SFR["SFR Region 80H to FFH - 128 special registers"]
    XRAM["External Data Memory - 64 KB off chip"]
    ROM["Code Memory - 64 KB on or off chip"]
    SP["Stack Pointer SP - 8 bit SFR 81H"]

    ACC -- "MOV A,Rn" --> RB
    RB -- "MOV Rn,A" --> ACC
    ACC -- "MOV A,dir" --> IRAM
    IRAM -- "MOV dir,A" --> ACC
    ACC -- "MOV A,dir" --> SFR
    SFR -- "MOV dir,A" --> ACC
    ACC -- "MOVX A,@DPTR" --> XRAM
    XRAM -- "MOVX @DPTR,A" --> ACC
    ACC -- "MOVC A,@A+DPTR" --> ROM
    ACC -- "XCH A,@R0" --> IRAM
    SP -- "PUSH dir" --> IRAM
    IRAM -- "POP dir" --> SP
```

> **Reading the diagram:** Every arrow represents a permitted `MOV`/`MOVX`/`MOVC`/`XCH`/`PUSH`/`POP` path. Notice the **bidirectional** nature: most paths allow data flow in either direction (read or write).

## 4.4 MOV Family Opcode Decoding Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A["Look at the destination operand"]
    A --> B["Destination is A?"]
    B -- Yes --> C["Source: Rn?"]
    B -- No --> D["Destination is direct?"]
    C -- Yes --> C1["Opcode = E8H to EFH - 1 byte"]
    C -- No --> E["Source: direct?"]
    C -- No --> F["Source: @Ri?"]
    C -- No --> G["Source: immediate?"]
    E -- Yes --> E1["Opcode = E5H + address byte - 2 bytes"]
    F -- Yes --> F1["Opcode = E2H or E3H - 1 byte"]
    G -- Yes --> G1["Opcode = 74H + data byte - 2 bytes"]
    D -- Yes --> H["Source: A?"]
    D -- No --> I["Destination is Rn?"]
    H -- Yes --> H1["Opcode = F5H + address byte - 2 bytes"]
    I -- Yes --> J["Source: direct?"]
    J -- Yes --> J1["Opcode = 88H to 8FH + address - 2 bytes"]
    I -- No --> K["Destination is @Ri?"]
    K -- Yes --> L["Source: A?"]
    L -- Yes --> L1["Opcode = F2H or F3H - 1 byte"]
```
