For Timer 0 in Mode 1, software-controlled, timer mode:
- M1 = 0, M0 = 1
- C/T̄ = 0 (Timer mode)
- GATE = 0 (Software control)
- Timer 1 bits: typically 0000 (not used in this question)

$$\text{TMOD} = 0000\,0001_b = 01H$$

**Step 6: Write the 8051 Assembly Subroutine.**

```assembly
DELAY_5MS:
    MOV TMOD, #01H     ; Timer 0, Mode 1, software control, timer mode
    MOV TH0, #0EEH     ; Load high byte of initial value
    MOV TL0, #00H      ; Load low byte of initial value
    SETB TR0           ; Start Timer 0
WAIT:
    JNB TF0, WAIT      ; Wait until TF0 is set (overflow)
    CLR TR0            ; Stop Timer 0
    CLR TF0            ; Clear overflow flag (must be done in software)
    RET                ; Return from subroutine
```

**Actual Delay Verification:**

$$T_{actual} = (65536 - 60928) \times 1.085\,\mu s = 4608 \times 1.085\,\mu s = 4999.68\,\mu s \approx 5\,ms \;\checkmark$$

---

#### **Worked Example 2: Mode 2 Auto-Reload Problem**

**Question:** Using **Timer 1 in Mode 2** with a **12 MHz crystal**, generate a **250 μs delay**. Find TH1.

**Step 1: Calculate the Timer Tick Period.**

$$T_{tick} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^6} = 1.0\,\mu s$$

**Step 2: Calculate the Required Number of Counts.**

$$N_{count} = \frac{250 \times 10^{-6}}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 250 \text{ counts}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the Reload Value.**

$$N_{reload} = 256 - 250 = 6$$

**Step 4: Hexadecimal Conversion.**

$$6_{10} = 06H$$

$$\text{TH1} = 06H$$

**Step 5: TMOD Value for Timer 1, Mode 2:**

- M1 = 1, M0 = 0
- C/T̄ = 0 (Timer mode)
- GATE = 0

$$\text{TMOD (upper nibble, Timer 1)} = 0010_b = 2H$$

If Timer 0 is not used (e.g., Mode 0), TMOD = $0000\,0010_b$ = 02H.

---

#### **Worked Example 3: Counter Application**

**Question:** A sensor outputs 1000 pulses to pin **P3.4 (T0)**. Using Timer 0 in **Mode 1 as a Counter**, determine the initial value and TMOD. How many pulses cause the overflow flag to set?

**Solution Analysis:**

- Pin **P3.4** is the external counter input for **Timer 0**.
- C/T̄ bit = 1 (Counter mode).
- The timer increments once per external pulse on T0.
- The counter counts from the initial value up to FFFFH (65,535) and overflows.
- If loaded with $0000H$, it overflows after **65,536 pulses**.

> [!WARNING]
> If you load $N_{initial} = 65536 - 1000 = 64536 = FC18H$, the overflow flag sets after exactly 1000 pulses. TH0 = FCH, TL0 = 18H.

**TMOD for Timer 0 Counter Mode 1, software-controlled:**

- M1 = 0, M0 = 1
- C/T̄ = 1
- GATE = 0

$$\text{TMOD} = 0000\,0101_b = 05H$$

---

### 3.3 Comparison Matrix: Timer vs. Counter

| Feature | Timer Mode | Counter Mode |
|---|---|---|
| **C/T̄ bit in TMOD** | 0 | 1 |
| **Clock Source** | Internal (oscillator ÷ 12) | External (T0/P3.4 or T1/P3.5) |
| **Trigger Edge** | N/A (continuous) | **Negative edge (HIGH-to-LOW transition)** |
| **Max Count Rate** | $f_{OSC} / 12$ | $f_{OSC} / 24$ (one full cycle of external pin) |
| **Typical Use** | Delay generation, baud rates | Event counting, frequency measurement |
| **Pin Used** | None | T0 (P3.4) for Timer 0; T1 (P3.5) for Timer 1 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Timer/Counter Functional Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    nodeA[Oscillator Crystal XTAL] --> nodeB[Divide by 12 Prescaler]
    nodeExtExt[External Pin T0 P3.4 or T1 P3.5] --> nodeMux
    nodeB --> nodeMux{MUX Selected by C/T-bar bit in TMOD}
    nodeMux --> nodeC[Selected Clock Source]
    nodeC --> nodeD[Increment Logic]
    nodeD --> nodeE[TLx Low Byte 8-bit]
    nodeE --> nodeF[THx High Byte 8-bit]
    nodeF --> nodeG{Overflow Detection FFFF H to 0000 H}
    nodeG -->|Overflow| nodeH[Set TFx Flag in TCON]
    nodeG -->|No overflow| nodeD
    nodeH --> nodeI[CPU Interrupt if Enabled]
    nodeI --> nodeJ[ISR or Polled Service Routine]
    nodeCtrl[TMOD Register and TCON Register] --> nodeMux
    nodeCtrl --> nodeD
    nodeCtrl --> nodeG
```

**Caption:** Functional flow of an 8051 Timer/Counter. The oscillator drives a divide-by-12 prescaler. The C/T̄ bit in TMOD selects between internal (timer) and external (counter) clock sources. The selected pulses increment TLx; when TLx overflows, THx increments. When THx:TLx rolls from FFFFH to 0000H, the TFx flag is set.

---

### 4.2 Mermaid Diagram — TMOD Register Bit Layout

```mermaid
graph LR
    nodeBit7[BIT 7 GATE1] --> nodeBit6[BIT 6 C/T-bar 1]
    nodeBit6 --> nodeBit5[BIT 5 M1 Timer 1]
    nodeBit5 --> nodeBit4[BIT 4 M0 Timer 1]
    nodeBit4 --> nodeBit3[BIT 3 GATE0]
    nodeBit3 --> nodeBit2[BIT 2 C/T-bar 0]
    nodeBit2 --> nodeBit1[BIT 1 M1 Timer 0]
    nodeBit1 --> nodeBit0[BIT 0 M0 Timer 0]
```

| Timer 1 (Upper Nibble) | Timer 0 (Lower Nibble) |
|---|---|
| GATE1, C/T̄1, M1.1, M0.1 | GATE0, C/T̄0, M1.0, M0.0 |

---

### 4.3 Mermaid Diagram — TCON Register Bit Layout (Timer-Relevant Bits)

```mermaid
graph LR
    tconBit7[BIT 7 TF1 Timer 1 Overflow] --> tconBit6[BIT 6 TR1 Timer 1 Run]
    tconBit6 --> tconBit5[BIT 5 TF0 Timer 0 Overflow]
    tconBit5 --> tconBit4[BIT 4 TR0 Timer 0 Run]
    tconBit4 --> tconBit3[BIT 3 IE1 Ext Int 1 Edge]
    tconBit3 --> tconBit2[BIT 2 IT1 Ext Int 1 Type]
    tconBit2 --> tconBit1[BIT 1 IE0 Ext Int 0 Edge]
    tconBit1 --> tconBit0[BIT 0 IT0 Ext Int 0 Type]
```

| Bit | Name | Function |
|---|---|---|
| TF1 (8FH) | Timer 1 Overflow | Set on overflow; cleared by software |
| TR1 (8EH) | Timer 1 Run | 1 = Run, 0 = Stop |
| TF0 (8DH) | Timer 0 Overflow | Set on overflow; cleared by software |
| TR0 (8CH) | Timer 0 Run | 1 = Run, 0 = Stop |

---

### 4.4 Mermaid Sequence Diagram — Mode 1 Delay Generation Flow

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CPU as 8051 CPU
    participant TMOD as TMOD Register
    participant THTL as THx and TLx Registers
    participant TCON as TCON Register
    participant TFx as TFx Flag

    CPU->>TMOD: Configure mode (e.g., MOV TMOD, #01H)
    CPU->>THTL: Load initial value (MOV TH0, #0EEH)
    CPU->>THTL: Load low byte (MOV TL0, #00H)
    CPU->>TCON: Start timer (SETB TR0)
    loop Every Machine Cycle
        THTL->>THTL: Increment TLx
        alt TLx overflows
            THTL->>THTL: Increment THx
        end
    end
    THTL->>TFx: On FFFFH to 0000H, set TFx = 1
    TFx->>CPU: Signal overflow (JNB TF0, WAIT loop exits)
    CPU->>TCON: Stop timer (CLR TR0)
    CPU->>TFx: Clear flag (CLR TF0)
```

**Caption:** Step-by-step flow of a typical Mode 1 delay subroutine. The CPU configures TMOD, loads initial values, starts the timer via TR0, and polls TF0 until overflow occurs. Note: TFx **must be cleared in software** — this is a common KTU exam pitfall.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A: Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
*Explain the function of the TMOD and TCON registers in the 8051 Timer/Counter system. List the four operating modes of Timer 0.*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **TMOD (Timer Mode) register** (SFR address 89H) is an **8-bit non-bit-addressable** register that configures the operating mode and clock source of Timer 0 and Timer 1. Each timer's 4-bit group contains: **GATE** (hardware/software control), **C/T̄** (Counter/Timer select), **M1** and **M0** (mode selection bits).

The **TCON (Timer Control) register** (SFR address 88H) is an **8-bit bit-addressable** register that controls the timer run state and holds overflow flags. The timer-relevant bits are **TR0, TR1** (run control — 1 starts the timer), and **TF0, TF1** (overflow flags — set by hardware on overflow, must be cleared by software).

**Four Operating Modes of Timer 0:**
1. **Mode 0:** 13-bit timer (8048 compatibility)
2. **Mode 1:** 16-bit timer
3. **Mode 2:** 8-bit auto-reload
4. **Mode 3:** Split timer (two 8-bit timers from Timer 0)

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
*Differentiate between Timer and Counter operations in 8051. What is the role of the C/T̄ bit?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Timer Mode | Counter Mode |
|---|---|---|
| Clock Source | Internal (oscillator ÷ 12) | External pin (T0/P3.4 or T1/P3.5) |
| C/T̄ bit value | 0 | 1 |
| Trigger | Automatic, every machine cycle | External negative-edge pulse |
| Purpose | Time delay generation | Event counting |

**Role of C/T̄ bit:** Located in the TMOD register (one bit per timer), the **C/T̄ bit** is the master selector that switches the timer's clock source. When **C/T̄ = 0**, the timer uses the **internal machine cycle** clock (derived from the crystal oscillator divided by 12). When **C/T̄ = 1**, the timer counts **external events** arriving on the T0 (P3.4) or T1 (P3.5) pin. It effectively converts the same hardware block between time-measurement and event-counting functions.

---

### 5.2 Part B: Long-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Paper Module 4]**

**(a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the architecture and operation of the 8051 Timer/Counter in **Mode 1**. List the functions of the TMOD register bits. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An 8051 system uses an **11.0592 MHz crystal**. Write an 8051 assembly language program using **Timer 0 in Mode 1** to generate a delay of **exactly 50 ms**. Calculate the values to be loaded into TH0, TL0, and TMOD. Show all calculations. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question A**

**(a) Block Diagram and Mode 1 Operation (7 marks):**

In **Mode 1**, the Timer operates as a **16-bit counter** formed by cascading TLx (low byte, 8-bit) and THx (high byte, 8-bit). The complete 16-bit count rolls over from **FFFFH to 0000H**, at which point the **TFx overflow flag** in TCON is set by hardware.

**TMOD Register Bit Functions:**

| Bit | Function |
|---|---|
| **GATE** | When set, timer runs only if INTx pin is HIGH and TRx is set (hardware gating). When cleared, timer runs as long as TRx is set (software control). |
| **C/T̄** | Selects Counter (1) or Timer (0) mode. |
| **M1, M0** | Mode select. For Mode 1: M1 = 0, M0 = 1. |

**Block Diagram:**

```
       ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────┐
       │                                             │
OSC ──►│ ÷12 Prescaler ──► MUX (C/T̄) ──► 16-bit    │
       │                       ▲        Counter      │
EXT ──►│                       │        (THx:TLx)    │
P3.4/  │                       │             │        │
P3.5   └───────────────────────┘             ▼        │
                                       FFFFH→0000H?  │
                                             │        │
                                             ▼        │
                                          Set TFx     │
                                                     │
       TMOD ────► Mode/Source Config                 │
       TCON ────► TRx (Run) / TFx (Flag)             │
       └─────────────────────────────────────────────┘
```

**Mode 1 Operation Steps:**
1. Configure **TMOD** to select Mode 1, timer or counter mode, and software control.
2. Load the initial 16-bit value into **THx** and **TLx**.
3. Set **TRx = 1** in TCON to start the timer.
4. Timer increments every machine cycle (or every external pulse).
5. When THx:TLx rolls from FFFFH to 0000H, **TFx is set**.
6. Software must **clear TFx** and clear TRx to stop the timer.

---

**(b) 50 ms Delay Calculation and Program (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Timer Tick Period.**

$$T_{tick} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} = 1.085\,\mu s$$

**Step 2: Required Number of Counts.**

$$N_{count} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} = 46082.9 \approx 46083 \text{ counts}$$

**Step 3: Initial Value.**

$$N_{initial} = 65536 - 46083 = 19453$$

**Step 4: Hexadecimal Conversion of 19453.**

$$19453 \div 16 = 1215 \text{ rem } 13 \,(D)$$
$$1215 \div 16 = 75 \text{ rem } 15 \,(F)$$
$$75 \div 16 = 4 \text{ rem } 11 \,(B)$$
$$4 \div 16 = 0 \text{ rem } 4$$

Reading bottom-up: $19453_{10} = 4BFDH$

$$\text{TH0} = 4BH \quad \text{TL0} = FDH$$

**Step 5: TMOD Value.**

Timer 0, Mode 1, Timer mode, Software control:

$$\text{TMOD} = 01H$$

**Step 6: Assembly Program.**

```assembly
        ORG 0000H
        LJMP MAIN
        ORG 0050H
MAIN:   MOV TMOD, #01H      ; Timer 0, Mode 1
        MOV TH0, #4BH       ; High byte of initial value
        MOV TL0, #0FDH      ; Low byte of initial value
        ACALL DELAY_50MS    ; Call the 50 ms delay
HERE:   SJMP HERE           ; Stay here forever (trap loop)

DELAY_50MS:
        SETB TR0            ; Start Timer 0
WAIT:   JNB TF0, WAIT       ; Loop until overflow
        CLR TR0             ; Stop Timer 0
        CLR TF0             ; Clear overflow flag
        RET
        END
```

**Verification:**

$$T_{actual} = (65536 - 19453) \times 1.085\,\mu s = 46083 \times 1.085 = 50000.06\,\mu s \approx 50\,ms \;\checkmark$$

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice**

**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Supplementary Paper]**

**(a)** Explain the operation of the 8051 Timer in **Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload)**. How does it differ from Mode 1? List two applications of Mode 2. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** An 8051 system uses a **12 MHz crystal**. Configure **Timer 1 in Mode 2 as a counter** to count **150 external pulses** on pin **P3.5**. Find TH1, TL1, and TMOD. Write the assembly subroutine to read the count and stop the counter. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Model Solution for Question B**

**(a) Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload) Operation (7 marks):**

In **Mode 2**, the Timer operates as an **8-bit counter** using only **TLx**. The reload value is stored in **THx**. When TLx overflows from **FFH to 00H**, the **TFx flag is set** by hardware, AND **THx is automatically copied into TLx** — this is the "auto-reload" action. The timer then continues counting from the reloaded value without software intervention.

**Differences from Mode 1:**

| Feature | Mode 1 | Mode 2 |
|---|---|---|
| Width | 16-bit (THx:TLx) | 8-bit (TLx only) |
| Reload | Manual (software reloads) | Automatic (THx copied to TLx) |
| Max Delay | ~71 ms (at 12 MHz) | ~255 μs (at 12 MHz) |
| Precision | Higher (16-bit) | Lower (8-bit) |
| Use | Long delays | Periodic short intervals, baud rates |

**Two Applications of Mode 2:**
1. **Baud Rate Generation for Serial Communication** — Timer 1 in Mode 2 generates the standard UART baud rates (e.g., 9600 bps with TH1 = FDH at 11.0592 MHz).
2. **Periodic Interrupts** — Producing fixed-frequency interrupts (e.g., every 250 μs) without software reload overhead.

---

**(b) Counter Configuration for 150 Pulses (7 marks):**

**Step 1: TMOD Configuration.**

For Timer 1, Mode 2, **Counter mode** (C/T̄ = 1), software control:
- M1 = 1, M0 = 0 (Mode 2)
- C/T̄ = 1 (Counter)
- GATE = 0

$$\text{TMOD (upper nibble, Timer 1)} = 1100_b = CH$$

Assuming Timer 0 is unused (Mode 0, timer, software control): TMOD = 0CH.

**Step 2: Calculate the Reload Value.**

$$N_{count} = 150 \text{ pulses}$$
$$N_{reload} = 256 - 150 = 106$$
$$106_{10} = 6AH$$

$$\text{TH1} = 6AH \quad \text{TL1} = 6AH \text{ (initial value before counting starts)}$$

**Step 3: Assembly Subroutine.**

```assembly
COUNT_150:
        MOV TMOD, #0CH      ; Timer 1, Mode 2, Counter mode
        MOV TH1, #6AH       ; Reload value
        MOV TL1, #6AH       ; Initial count value
        SETB TR1            ; Start counter (begins counting on P3.5 pulses)
WAIT_CNT:
        JNB TF1, WAIT_CNT   ; Wait for overflow after 150 pulses
        CLR TR1             ; Stop counter
        CLR TF1             ; Clear overflow flag
        RET
```

**Verification:** The counter increments on each **falling edge** of P3.5. After exactly 150 pulses, TL1 overflows from FFH to 00H, TF1 is set, and TH1 (= 6AH) is automatically reloaded into TL1.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Exams:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting to clear TFx in software** — The TF0/TF1 flag is set by hardware but must be cleared by software. If missed, the program "thinks" the timer has already overflowed and exits the wait loop immediately. **Loss: 1 mark.**
>
> 2. **Using wrong oscillator frequency in delay calculation** — Students often forget that 11.0592 MHz gives a 1.085 μs tick (not 1 μs). Substituting 1 μs when the question says 11.0592 MHz will give the wrong TH/TL values. **Loss: 2 marks.**
>
> 3. **Confusing Timer 0 and Timer 1 bit positions in TMOD** — TMOD's upper nibble (bits 4–7) controls Timer 1; the lower nibble (bits 0–3) controls Timer 0. A common error is setting the mode for the wrong timer. **Loss: 1–2 marks.**
>
> 4. **Not splitting the initial value into THx and TLx** — KTU examiners expect to see both THx and TLx calculated separately, with the high byte and low byte identified. **Loss: 1 mark.**
>
> 5. **Confusing C/T̄ bit logic** — Students sometimes write "C/T = 1 for Timer" — the **bar** (overline) is critical. C/T̄ = 1 means **Counter**, not Timer. **Loss: 1 mark.**
>
> 6. **Using software delay loops instead of hardware timers** — When the question explicitly asks for a timer-based delay, using DJNZ/NOP loops will earn partial credit at best. **Loss: up to 3 marks.**
>
> 7. **Mode 3 description error** — Mode 3 is supported **only by Timer 0**, not Timer 1. Writing that Timer 1 also has Mode 3 is incorrect. **Loss: 1 mark.**

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- ✅ The 8051 has **two 16-bit Timer/Counters** (Timer 0 and Timer 1), each formed by an 8-bit THx and an 8-bit TLx.
- ✅ **TMOD** (89H) configures mode and clock source — **not bit-addressable**.
- ✅ **TCON** (88H) controls run state and overflow flags — **bit-addressable**.
- ✅ The **C/T̄ bit** selects Timer mode (0 = internal clock) or Counter mode (1 = external pin).
- ✅ The **GATE bit** enables hardware gating via the INTx pin.
- ✅ **Mode 1** is 16-bit — best for long delays. Max delay ≈ 71.1 ms at 12 MHz.
- ✅ **Mode 2** is 8-bit auto-reload — best for baud rates and periodic tasks.
- ✅ The **TFx flag must be cleared in software** after it is set by hardware.
- ✅ Timer increments every **machine cycle = 12 oscillator periods**.
- ✅ Standard tick: **1.085 μs @ 11.0592 MHz** and **1.0 μs @ 12 MHz**.
- ✅ **Counter mode** uses pin **T0/P3.4** (Timer 0) or **T1/P3.5** (Timer 1); triggered on **falling edge**.
- ✅ Maximum external counting rate = $f_{OSC} / 24$ (one full external pin cycle per count).
- ✅ **Baud rate formula:** $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{SMOD} \cdot f_{OSC}}{32 \times 12 \times (256 - TH1)}$.
- ✅ **Mode 3** is available **only on Timer 0** (splits it into two 8-bit timers).
- ✅ Delay formula for Mode 1: $T_{delay} = (65536 - N_{initial}) \times \dfrac{12}{f_{OSC}}$.
- ✅ Delay formula for Mode 2: $T_{delay} = (256 - N_{reload}) \times \dfrac{12}{f_{OSC}}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Mnemonic — "TMODE-1" for Mode 1 Configuration:**
> **T** = TMOD = 01H (Timer 0, Mode 1)
> **M** = MOV TH0, then MOV TL0
> **O** = Open the timer (SETB TR0)
> **D** = Detect TF0 (JNB TF0, loop)
> **E** = Erase TF0 and TR0 (CLR)
> **1** = One flag, one source, one mode — keep it simple!

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Timer Registers
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEAET613 - MICROCONTROLLERS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Timer Registers # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Timer Registers** are Special Function Registers (SFRs) within a microcontroller that are dedicated to configuring, controlling, and monitoring the operation of the on-chip hardware **Timer/Counter** units. In the 8051 architecture, these registers include the **Timer Mode register (TMOD)**, the **Timer Control register (TCON)**, and the four **Timer data registers (TH0, TL0, TH1, TL1)** that hold the 16-bit count values for the two internal timers, Timer 0 and Timer 1. The 8051 microcontroller contains **two 16-bit Timer/Counters** — **Timer 0** and **Timer 1** — which can be used either as timers (counting machine cycles from the on-chip oscillator) or as event counters (counting external pulses on pin T0/P3.4 or T1/P3.5). The behavior of these timers is entirely governed by a small set of hardware registers that the programmer manipulates through software. ### The Four Primary Timer Register Groups 1. **TMOD (Timer Mode Register)** — Selects the **mode of operation** (Mode 0, 1, 2, or 3) and decides whether the unit functions as a **Timer** or a **Counter**. 2. **TCON (Timer Control Register)** — Controls the **Run/Stop** state, flags the **overflow**, and manages the **interrupt request flags** for both timers. 3. **TH0, TL0** — The **high byte** and **low byte** of Timer 0's 16-bit count register. 4. **TH1, TL1** — The **high byte** and **low byte** of Timer 1's 16-bit count register. > [!NOTE] > **Why are these called "Special Function Registers (SFRs)"?** > In the 8051, the upper 128 bytes of internal RAM (addresses 0x80 to 0xFF) are reserved for SFRs. TMOD, TCON, TH0, TL0, TH1, and TL1 are all mapped into this SFR space, making them directly addressable by name in C and assembly. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Stopwatch Inside the Chip > [!TIP] > **Think of Timer Registers as the control panel of a digital stopwatch embedded inside the microcontroller.** Imagine you bought a stopwatch with two independent timers (Timer 0 and Timer 1). On its front panel: - A **Mode Selector Dial** lets you choose how the stopwatch counts — is it counting internal machine pulses (timer mode) or external button presses (counter mode)? This dial is exactly what the **TMOD register** does in software. - A **Start/Stop Button** with a small **red overflow LED** lets you start the counting process and indicates when the count has exceeded its maximum value. This is precisely the role of the **TCON register**. - The **LCD display** showing the current count is split into a **high digit** and a **low digit** — together forming a 16-bit number. These are the **THx and TLx** register pairs. Just as you would set the dial (write to TMOD), press Start (set the TR0/TR1 bit in TCON), and watch the digits increment (THx/TLx auto-increment from the machine cycle), the microcontroller uses the same conceptual workflow through its timer registers. ## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics - **Oscillator Frequency ($f_{osc}$):** The crystal frequency connected to pins XTAL1 and XTAL2, typically **11.0592 MHz** or **12 MHz** in standard 8051 systems. - **Machine Cycle Frequency ($f_{mc}$):** Equals $f_{osc} / 12$. For 11.0592 MHz, this gives **0.9216 MHz** (a 1.085 µs machine cycle period). - **Timer Increment Rate:** The timer register increments **once every machine cycle** in timer mode. - **SFR Address Map (Key Timer Registers):** | Register | SFR Address | Bit-Addressable | |----------|-------------|-----------------| | TMOD | **0x89** | No | | TCON | **0x88** | Yes | | TL0 | **0x8A** | No | | TL1 | **0x8B** | No | | TH0 | **0x8C** | No | | TH1 | **0x8D** | No | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Bit-addressable vs. Non-bit-addressable:** Registers in the SFR region whose addresses end in **0** or **8** (like TCON at 0x88) are bit-addressable. This means individual bits (such as TR0) can be set or cleared directly using bit-manipulation instructions like `SETB TR0`. TMOD, however, is **not** bit-addressable — the entire byte must be written at once. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The TMOD Register (Timer Mode Register) — Address 0x89 TMOD is an **8-bit non-bit-addressable** register divided into two identical 4-bit nibbles: - **Lower nibble (bits 0–3):** Controls **Timer 0** - **Upper nibble (bits 4–7):** Controls **Timer 1** ### Bit-by-Bit Structure of TMOD | Bit | Name | Function | |-----|------|----------| | 7 | **GATE1** | When set, Timer 1 is controlled by hardware (INT1 pin). When cleared, controlled by software (TR1). | | 6 | **C/T1̄** | 1 = Counter mode, 0 = Timer mode for Timer 1. | | 5 | **M11** | Mode select bit 1 for Timer 1. | | 4 | **M01** | Mode select bit 0 for Timer 1. | | 3 | **GATE0** | When set, Timer 0 is controlled by hardware (INT0 pin). When cleared, controlled by software (TR0). | | 2 | **C/T0̄** | 1 = Counter mode, 0 = Timer mode for Timer 0. | | 1 | **M10** | Mode select bit 1 for Timer 0. | | 0 | **M00** | Mode select bit 0 for Timer 0. | ### The Four Timer Modes - **Mode 0 (M1=0, M0=0):** **13-bit Timer Mode** — Uses an 8-bit timer (TLx) with a 5-bit prescaler (THx upper 5 bits). Legacy mode retained for 8048 compatibility. - **Mode 1 (M1=0, M0=1):** **16-bit Timer Mode** — The most commonly used mode. THx and TLx form a full 16-bit counter (0 to 65535). - **Mode 2 (M1=1, M0=0):** **8-bit Auto-Reload Mode** — TLx counts from the reload value to 0xFF, overflows, and TLx is **automatically reloaded** from THx. - **Mode 3 (M1=1, M0=1):** **Split Timer Mode** — Timer 0 splits into two independent 8-bit timers; Timer 1 in this mode simply stops (its SFRs can still be used as a baud rate generator for the serial port). > [!NOTE] > **Why is Mode 1 the most popular?** Because it provides the full 16-bit range (65,536 counts), allowing the longest possible delay before overflow and the highest precision for time measurement. ## 2.2 The TCON Register (Timer Control Register) — Address 0x88 TCON is an **8-bit bit-addressable** register that serves **dual purposes** — it controls the timers **and** manages the external interrupt flags. The upper 4 bits are dedicated to timer control; the lower 4 bits handle external interrupts. | Bit | Name | Address | Function | |-----|------|---------|----------| | 7 | **TF1** | 0x8F | Timer 1 Overflow Flag — set by hardware on overflow, cleared by software. | | 6 | **TR1** | 0x8E | Timer 1 Run Control — 1 = start, 0 = stop (when GATE1=0). | | 5 | **TF0** | 0x8D | Timer 0 Overflow Flag — set by hardware on overflow, cleared by software. | | 4 | **TR0** | 0x8C | Timer 1 Run Control — 1 = start, 0 = stop (when GATE0=0). | | 3 | IE1 | 0x8B | External Interrupt 1 edge flag. | | 2 | IT1 | 0x8A | External Interrupt 1 type (edge/level). | | 1 | IE0 | 0x89 | External Interrupt 0 edge flag. | | 0 | IT0 | 0x88 | External Interrupt 0 type (edge/level). | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Hardware-Set, Software-Cleared Convention:** The TFx (Timer Flag) bits are set **automatically by hardware** when the timer overflows from its maximum value (0xFFFF in Mode 1). The CPU is then expected to **clear the flag in software** inside the interrupt service routine. Forgetting to clear TFx causes the interrupt to re-trigger immediately — a classic KTU viva question. ## 2.3 The Timer Data Registers (THx, TLx) These four 8-bit registers are the actual **counting elements**. In Mode 1, the 16-bit count is formed by concatenating the high and low bytes: $$ \text{Count} = (\text{THx} \ll 8) \;\vert\; \text{TLx} $$ - The count **increments by 1** on every machine cycle (in timer mode) or every external pulse (in counter mode). - The count **overflows** when it transitions from **0xFFFF to 0x0000**, which sets the corresponding **TFx** flag. - The count **underflows** to 0x0000 only after reaching the maximum value of 0xFFFF. ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | Formula / Concept | Equation | Units / Notes | |-------------------|----------|---------------| | Machine Cycle Frequency | $f_{mc} = f_{osc} / 12$ | Hz | | Machine Cycle Period | $T_{mc} = 12 / f_{osc}$ | seconds | | Timer Increment Interval | $\Delta t = 1 / f_{mc}$ | seconds per count | | Total Delay (Mode 1) | $T_{delay} = (65536 - N_{initial}) \times T_{mc}$ | seconds | | Reload Value Calculation (Mode 1) | $N_{initial} = 65536 - (T_{delay} / T_{mc})$ | integer count | | Auto-Reload Value (Mode 2) | $N_{reload} = 256 - (T_{delay} / T_{mc})$ | 8-bit value for THx | | Maximum Delay (Mode 1) | $T_{max} = 65536 \times T_{mc}$ | at 11.0592 MHz: ≈ 71.1 ms | | Maximum Delay (Mode 2) | $T_{max} = 256 \times T_{mc}$ | at 11.0592 MHz: ≈ 277.8 µs | | Timer Pin Mapping | T0 → P3.4, T1 → P3.5 | counter mode input pins | | Gate Control Source | INT0 (P3.2) for T0, INT1 (P3.3) for T1 | hardware start/stop | > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** Timer registers are the foundation of every time-critical embedded system. They are used to generate precise delays (e.g., blinking an LED at exactly 1 Hz), measure pulse widths in ultrasonic distance sensors (HC-SR04), generate baud rates for UART communication, produce PWM waveforms for motor control, and schedule real-time operating system (RTOS) tasks. ## 2.5 Step-by-Step Timer Programming Workflow The standard KTU-lab approach to using timer registers follows this sequence: 1. **Load TMOD** with the desired mode value (e.g., 0x01 for Timer 0 in Mode 1, software-controlled). 2. **Load THx and TLx** with the initial count value calculated from the desired delay. 3. **Set the TRx bit** in TCON to start the timer (`SETB TR0`). 4. **Wait and poll** the TFx flag, or enable the timer interrupt via the IE register. 5. **Clear the TFx flag** in software. 6. **Stop the timer** by clearing the TRx bit if a single-shot delay is needed. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Reload Value for a Given Delay (Mode 1) This is the single most important derivation for KTU university exam questions on timer registers. The goal is to find the **initial 16-bit value** that must be loaded into `THx:TLx` so that the timer overflows after exactly $T_{delay}$ seconds. ### Step 1: Establish the Time Per Count The 8051 oscillator drives the timer at the **machine cycle rate**, which is 1/12th of the crystal frequency. $$ T_{mc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} $$ Each count therefore takes exactly one machine cycle to complete: $$ \Delta t = T_{mc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} \text{ seconds} $$ ### Step 2: Relate the Delay to the Number of Counts If the timer starts at initial value $N_{initial}$ and overflows at 0xFFFF (rolling over to 0x0000), the total number of increments before overflow is: $$ N_{counts} = 65536 - N_{initial} $$ The total elapsed time is therefore: $$ T_{delay} = N_{counts} \times T_{mc} = (65536 - N_{initial}) \times \frac{12}{f_{osc}} $$ ### Step 3: Solve for the Initial Count Value Rearranging for $N_{initial}$: $$ 65536 - N_{initial} = \frac{T_{delay} \times f_{osc}}{12} $$ $$ N_{initial} = 65536 - \frac{T_{delay} \times f_{osc}}{12} $$ ### Step 4: Split into THx and TLx Since $N_{initial}$ is a 16-bit number, it must be split into the high and low bytes: $$ \text{TLx} = N_{initial} \mod 256 $$ $$ \text{THx} = \lfloor N_{initial} / 256 \rfloor $$ ## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Standard) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Problem:** An 8051 system uses an **11.0592 MHz** crystal. Configure **Timer 0 in Mode 1** to generate a delay of exactly **50 ms**. Calculate the values of TMOD, TH0, and TL0. ### Step 1: Compute the Machine Cycle Period $$ T_{mc} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} = 1.085\,\mu s $$ ### Step 2: Compute the Required Number of Counts $$ N_{counts} = \frac{T_{delay}}{T_{mc}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} = 46080 \text{ counts} $$ ### Step 3: Compute the Initial 16-bit Value $$ N_{initial} = 65536 - 46080 = 19456 $$ ### Step 4: Convert 19456 to Hexadecimal $$ 19456 = 0x4C00 $$ ### Step 5: Split into TH0 and TL0 $$ \text{TH0} = 0x4C, \quad \text{TL0} = 0x00 $$ ### Step 6: Determine the TMOD Value For Timer 0 in Mode 1, software-controlled, timer mode: - GATE0 = 0, C/T0̄ = 0, M10 = 0, M00 = 1 - Lower nibble = **0x01** - Upper nibble (Timer 1 unused) = **0x00** $$ \text{TMOD} = 0x01 $$ ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Choosing the Correct Mode for a Use-Case | Application | Required Delay | Best Mode | Reason | |-------------|----------------|-----------|--------| | 1 ms delay for keypad debounce | Short, precise | **Mode 2** | 8-bit auto-reload, no reload code needed in ISR | | 50 ms LED blink | Medium, one-shot | **Mode 1** | 16-bit range covers 50 ms comfortably | | Continuous 100 µs sampling | Short, repeated | **Mode 2** | Auto-reload is ideal for periodic interrupts | | Generating 9600 baud for UART | 1/9600 ≈ 104 µs | **Mode 2** (Timer 1) | Standard textbook method uses auto-reload | | Measuring external event width | Variable, long | **Mode 1** with GATE=1 | Hardware gating via INTx pin measures pulse width | ## 3.4 Derivation: Maximum Achievable Delay (Mode 1) The maximum delay occurs when the timer counts through its entire 16-bit range from 0x0000 to 0xFFFF (i.e., $N_{initial} = 0$): $$ T_{max} = 65536 \times \frac{12}{f_{osc}} $$ For the most common KTU-lab crystal of 11.0592 MHz: $$ T_{max} = 65536 \times \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} = 71.11 \text{ ms} $$ For a 12 MHz crystal: $$ T_{max} = 65536 \times \frac{12}{12 \times 10^6} = 65.536 \text{ ms} $$ > [!NOTE] > **For delays longer than $T_{max}$:** Use a **software loop counter** that calls the timer overflow routine multiple times. For example, two overflows yield 2 × 71.11 ms = 142.22 ms. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 SFR Memory Map (Timer Registers in Context) The following block diagram shows how the timer-related SFRs are positioned within the 8051's special function register space: ```mermaid graph TD SFR_BASE["SFR Memory Space
0x80 to 0xFF"] P0["P0 - Port 0
0x80"] TCON_REG["TCON - Timer Control
0x88 - BIT ADDR"] P1["P1 - Port 1
0x90"] SCON["SCON - Serial Control
0x98"] TMOD_REG["TMOD - Timer Mode
0x89 - BYTE ONLY"] TL0_REG["TL0 - Timer 0 Low
0x8A"] TL1_REG["TL1 - Timer 1 Low
0x8B"] TH0_REG["TH0 - Timer 0 High
0x8C"] TH1_REG["TH1 - Timer 1 High
0x8D"] IE_REG["IE - Interrupt Enable
0xA8"] SFR_BASE --> P0 SFR_BASE --> TCON_REG SFR_BASE --> P1 SFR_BASE --> SCON SFR_BASE --> TMOD_REG SFR_BASE --> TL0_REG SFR_BASE --> TL1_REG SFR_BASE --> TH0_REG SFR_BASE --> TH1_REG SFR_BASE --> IE_REG TCON_REG -.controls.-> TMOD_REG TMOD_REG -.configures.-> TL0_REG TMOD_REG -.configures.-> TH0_REG TMOD_REG -.configures.-> TL1_REG TMOD_REG -.configures.-> TH1_REG TCON_REG -.starts stops.-> TL0_REG TCON_REG -.starts stops.-> TH0_REG TCON_REG -.starts stops.-> TL1_REG TCON_REG -.starts stops.-> TH1_REG ``` ## 4.2 Timer Register Functional Block Diagram This diagram shows the **internal data flow** between the timer registers and the counting hardware: ```mermaid graph LR OSC["Oscillator
f_osc"] DIV12["Divide by 12
Prescaler"] MC["Machine Cycle
Pulse"] TMOD_BOX["TMOD Register
0x89"] TCON_BOX["TCON Register
0x88"] TH0_BOX["TH0 - 0x8C"] TL0_BOX["TL0 - 0x8A"] TH1_BOX["TH1 - 0x8D"] TL1_BOX["TL1 - 0x8B"] INCREMENT["16-bit
Incrementor"] OVERFLOW["Overflow
Detector"] TF0_FLAG["TF0 Flag"] TF1_FLAG["TF1 Flag"] T0_PIN["T0 - P3.4
External Pulse"] T1_PIN["T1 - P3.5
External Pulse"] MUX0["Mode MUX
Timer or Counter"] MUX1["Mode MUX
Timer or Counter"] OSC --> DIV12 DIV12 --> MC MC --> MUX0 T0_PIN --> MUX0 MUX0 --> TH0_BOX MUX0 --> TL0_BOX TH0_BOX --- TL0_BOX TL0_BOX --> INCREMENT INCREMENT --> TL0_BOX INCREMENT --> OVERFLOW OVERFLOW --> TF0_FLAG OVERFLOW --> TL0_BOX MC --> MUX1 T1_PIN --> MUX1 MUX1 --> TH1_BOX MUX1 --> TL1_BOX TH1_BOX --- TL1_BOX TL1_BOX --> INCREMENT INCREMENT --> TL1_BOX INCREMENT --> OVERFLOW OVERFLOW --> TF1_FLAG OVERFLOW --> TL1_BOX TMOD_BOX -.configures mode.-> MUX0 TMOD_BOX -.configures mode.-> MUX1 TCON_BOX -.TR0 gate.-> MUX0 TCON_BOX -.TR1 gate.-> MUX1 ``` ## 4.3 TMOD Bit Layout Diagram The following diagram visualizes the two-nibble structure of the TMOD register: ```mermaid graph TB TMOD_FULL["TMOD Register - 8 bits at address 0x89"] TIMER1_NIB["Upper Nibble - Timer 1"] TIMER0_NIB["Lower Nibble - Timer 0"] GATE1["Bit 7
GATE1"] CT1["Bit 6
C/T1 bar"] M11["Bit 5
M11"] M01["Bit 4
M01"] GATE0["Bit 3
GATE0"] CT0["Bit 2
C/T0 bar"] M10["Bit 1
M10"] M00["Bit 0
M00"] TMOD_FULL --> TIMER1_NIB TMOD_FULL --> TIMER0_NIB TIMER1_NIB --> GATE1 TIMER1_NIB --> CT1 TIMER1_NIB --> M11 TIMER1_NIB --> M01 TIMER0_NIB --> GATE0 TIMER0_NIB --> CT0 TIMER0_NIB --> M10 TIMER0_NIB --> M00 ``` ## 4.4 TCON Bit Layout Diagram The TCON register is bit-addressable, allowing direct access to each flag and control bit: ```mermaid graph TB TCON_FULL["TCON Register - 8 bits at address 0x88 - BIT ADDR"] TIMER_BITS["Upper Nibble - Timer Functions"] INT_BITS["Lower Nibble - External Interrupts"] TF1_BIT["Bit 7 - 0x8F
TF1 Overflow"] TR1_BIT["Bit 6 - 0x8E
TR1 Run"] TF0_BIT["Bit 5 - 0x8D
TF0 Overflow"] TR0_BIT["Bit 4 - 0x8C
TR0 Run"] IE1_BIT["Bit 3 - 0x8B
IE1 Edge"] IT1_BIT["Bit 2 - 0x8A
IT1 Type"] IE0_BIT["Bit 1 - 0x89
IE0 Edge"] IT0_BIT["Bit 0 - 0x88
IT0 Type"] TCON_FULL --> TIMER_BITS TCON_FULL --> INT_BITS TIMER_BITS --> TF1_BIT TIMER_BITS --> TR1_BIT TIMER_BITS --> TF0_BIT TIMER_BITS --> TR0_BIT INT_BITS --> IE1_BIT INT_BITS --> IT1_BIT INT_BITS --> IE0_BIT INT_BITS --> IT0_BIT ``` # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1: Define the TMOD register of the 8051 microcontroller. Explain the function of the GATE bit. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer:** The **TMOD (Timer Mode) register** is an 8-bit special function register located at SFR address **0x89**. It is **not bit-addressable**, so the entire byte must be written as a single operation. TMOD is divided into two 4-bit nibbles: the upper nibble (bits 4–7) configures **Timer 1**, and the lower nibble (bits 0–3) configures **Timer 0**. Each nibble contains four control bits: **GATE**, **C/T̄**, **M1**, and **M0**. - **GATE bit function:** When the GATE bit is set to **1**, the corresponding timer is controlled by an **external hardware signal** applied to the INTx pin (INT0 for Timer 0, INT1 for Timer 1). The timer runs only while the INTx pin is high AND the TRx bit is set. This enables **pulse-width measurement** applications. When GATE = 0, the timer is controlled purely by software through the **TRx** bit in the TCON register. ### Question 2: What is the function of the TF0 and TF1 flags in the TCON register? How are they cleared? `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer:** The **TF0 (Timer 0 Flag)** at bit position 5 (0x8D) and **TF1 (Timer 1 Flag)** at bit position 7 (0x8F) of the TCON register are **overflow indicator flags** for Timer 0 and Timer 1 respectively. - These flags are **set automatically by hardware** when the corresponding timer's counter register overflows from its maximum value (0xFFFF in Mode 1) to 0x0000. - They serve **two purposes**: (1) to be polled by software in a "wait-until-overflow" loop, and (2) to generate a timer interrupt request to the CPU when interrupts are enabled. - The flags **must be cleared by software**, typically at the start or end of the timer interrupt service routine, to prevent re-entry or repeated polling. The standard clearing method is the instruction `CLR TF0` or `CLR TF1` in assembly. --- ## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently lose marks on timer-register problems by: **(1)** confusing the role of TMOD vs. TCON, **(2)** forgetting to clear the TFx flag in polled-mode programs, **(3)** computing the reload value with the wrong formula (using $256 - N$ instead of $65536 - N$ for Mode 1), and **(4)** failing to split the 16-bit initial value correctly into THx and TLx bytes. Always show **all intermediate steps** and **explicit hex conversions**. ### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - May 2023]` **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the TMOD and TCON registers of the 8051 microcontroller. Describe all four modes of operation of the timers. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** An 8051 system uses a **12 MHz** crystal. Write a program to generate a delay of **25 ms** using Timer 0 in Mode 1. Calculate the values to be loaded into TH0, TL0, and TMOD. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) **TMOD Register (0x89) — Bit Layout:** | Bit 7 | Bit 6 | Bit 5 | Bit 4 | Bit 3 | Bit 2 | Bit 1 | Bit 0 | |-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------|-------| | GATE1 | C/T1̄ | M11 | M01 | GATE0 | C/T0̄ | M10 | M00 | **TCON Register (0x88) — Bit Layout (Timer-Relevant Bits):** | Bit 7 | Bit 6 | Bit 5 | Bit 4 | |-------|-------|-------|-------| | TF1 | TR1 | TF0 | TR0 | **Mode Descriptions:** - **Mode 0 — 13-bit Timer Mode:** TLx acts as an 8-bit counter and the lower 5 bits of THx act as a 5-bit prescaler, giving a 13-bit total (8192 counts max). This is a legacy mode inherited from the 8048 microcontroller. - **Mode 1 — 16-bit Timer Mode:** THx and TLx together form a full 16-bit counter, allowing values from 0x0000 to 0xFFFF (65,536 counts). This is the **most commonly used mode** because it provides the longest delays. - **Mode 2 — 8-bit Auto-Reload Mode:** TLx is the actual 8-bit counter (0x00 to 0xFF). When TLx overflows, the value in **THx is automatically copied into TLx** by hardware. This mode is ideal for **periodic, repeating** interrupts such as baud-rate generation. - **Mode 3 — Split Timer Mode:** Available only for **Timer 0**. Timer 0 splits into two independent 8-bit timers (TL0 controlled by TR0, TH0 locked into a timer function). Timer 1 in this mode stops incrementing, but its SFRs can still be used for the serial port baud rate generator. #### Model Solution for (b) **Step 1: Machine Cycle Period** $$ T_{mc} = \frac{12}{12 \times 10^6} = 1.0\,\mu s $$ **Step 2: Number of Counts Required** $$ N_{counts} = \frac{T_{delay}}{T_{mc}} = \frac{25 \times 10^{-3}}{1.0 \times 10^{-6}} = 25000 $$ **Step 3: Initial 16-bit Value** $$ N_{initial} = 65536 - 25000 = 40536 $$ **Step 4: Hexadecimal Conversion** $$ 40536_{10} = 0x9E58 $$ **Step 5: Split into TH0 and TL0** $$ \text{TH0} = 0x9E = 158_{10}, \quad \text{TL0} = 0x58 = 88_{10} $$ **Step 6: TMOD Value** For Timer 0, Mode 1, software-controlled, timer mode: lower nibble = 0x01; upper nibble = 0x00. $$ \text{TMOD} = 0x01 $$ --- ### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2022]` **(a)** Differentiate between **Timer mode** and **Counter mode** of operation in the 8051. How is the mode selected using the TMOD register? **(7 Marks)** **(b)** An 8051 microcontroller is clocked by a **11.0592 MHz** crystal. Configure **Timer 1 in Mode 2** to generate a time delay of **200 µs**. Calculate the TH1, TL1, and TMOD values. **(7 Marks)** #### Model Solution for (a) **Timer Mode vs. Counter Mode:** | Parameter | Timer Mode | Counter Mode | |-----------|------------|--------------| | **Clock Source** | Internal oscillator (divided by 12) | External pulse on T0 (P3.4) or T1 (P3.5) | | **Increment Rate** | Once per machine cycle | Once per external pulse edge | | **C/T̄ bit in TMOD** | Set to **0** | Set to **1** | | **Typical Use** | Generating time delays, scheduling | Counting external events (objects, pulses) | | **Pin Dependency** | None (uses internal clock) | Requires the T0/T1 pin | **Mode Selection Mechanism:** The C/T̄ bit (bit 2 of TMOD for Timer 0, bit 6 for Timer 1) acts as a multiplexer select line. When C/T̄ = 0, the internal machine cycle is routed to the incrementor. When C/T̄ = 1, the corresponding T0 or T1 pin signal is routed to the incrementor instead. #### Model Solution for (b) **Step 1: Machine Cycle Period** $$ T_{mc} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} = 1.085\,\mu s $$ **Step 2: Number of Counts Required (Mode 2 is 8-bit, range 0–256)** $$ N_{counts} = \frac{T_{delay}}{T_{mc}} = \frac{200 \times 10^{-6}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} \approx 184.33 $$ Rounding to the nearest integer: $N_{counts} = 184$. **Step 3: Reload Value (8-bit Mode 2 formula)** $$ N_{reload} = 256 - 184 = 72 $$ **Step 4: Hexadecimal Conversion** $$ 72_{10} = 0x48 $$ **Step 5: TH1 and TL1 Values** In Mode 2, **TH1 holds the reload value** and **TL1 is the active counter**. $$ \text{TH1} = 0x48, \quad \text{TL1} = 0x48 \text{ (initial load = reload value)} $$ **Step 6: TMOD Value** For Timer 1, Mode 2, software-controlled, timer mode: upper nibble = 0x20; lower nibble = 0x00. $$ \text{TMOD} = 0x20 $$ --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!TIP] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist for Timer Registers:** - The 8051 has **two 16-bit timers** (Timer 0 and Timer 1) controlled by **six SFRs**: TMOD, TCON, TH0, TL0, TH1, TL1. - **TMOD (0x89)** is **not bit-addressable** — it must be written as a complete byte. - **TCON (0x88)** is **bit-addressable** — its upper 4 bits (TF1, TR1, TF0, TR0) directly control the timers. - **TFx flags are set by hardware** on overflow and **must be cleared by software** to prevent repeated interrupts. - **TRx bits in TCON start/stop** the timers (when GATE = 0). - **GATE = 1** enables hardware control via the INTx pin — used for **pulse-width measurement**. - **C/T̄ = 0** selects timer mode (internal clock); **C/T̄ = 1** selects counter mode (external pin T0/T1). - **Mode 1 (16-bit)** is the most common general-purpose mode. - **Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload)** is used for **periodic events** and **UART baud-rate generation**. - **Mode 3** splits Timer 0 into two 8-bit timers and **halts Timer 1**. - The fundamental delay formula for **Mode 1** is: $$ T_{delay} = (65536 - N_{initial}) \times \frac{12}{f_{osc}} $$ - The fundamental delay formula for **Mode 2** is: $$ T_{delay} = (256 - N_{reload}) \times \frac{12}{f_{osc}} $$ - The **maximum delay in Mode 1** at 11.0592 MHz is approximately **71.11 ms**; for longer delays, use a software loop counter. - TMOD and TCON must be **configured before** setting TRx to start the timer. - Always **reload THx and TLx** at the start of every Mode 1 ISR to maintain a precise periodic delay.

topics.title: Timer0 and Timer1, Configuration of Timer Registers, Timer Mode Programming, Counter Mode, Serial Communication in 8051
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** OEAET613 - MICROCONTROLLERS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Timer0 and Timer1, Configuration of Timer Registers, Timer Mode Programming, Counter Mode, Serial Communication in 8051 ## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Timers/Counters in 8051 — Formal Definition The **Intel 8051 microcontroller** is equipped with two internal **16-bit programmable Timers/Counters** designated as **Timer0** and **Timer1**. These hardware peripherals operate by incrementing an internal register on every clock pulse (timer mode) or on every external transition (counter mode), and can be programmed to generate precise time delays or count external events without CPU intervention. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Timer0 and Timer1 share most of their architecture but Timer1 has the special additional role of acting as the **Baud Rate Generator** for the serial port. Timer0 is generally used for delay generation and timing applications. Each timer in the 8051 is built from **two 8-bit Special Function Registers (SFRs)**: - **Timer0:** `TH0` (high byte, address 0x8C) and `TL0` (low byte, address 0x8A) - **Timer1:** `TH1` (high byte, address 0x8D) and `TL1` (low byte, address 0x8B) ### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Stopwatch and the Tally Counter > [!NOTE] > **Intuitive Analogy — Two Workers in a Factory:** > Imagine the 8051 CPU as a busy factory manager. He cannot keep watching the clock all the time because he has production work to do. So he hires two smart workers (**Timer0** and **Timer1**) and gives them each a **stopwatch (timer mode)** or a **tally counter (counter mode)**. > - **Stopwatch Mode (Timer):** The worker counts the internal ticks of a wall clock. After a fixed number of ticks, he rings a bell to inform the manager. This is used for **delay generation** (e.g., blinking an LED every 1 second). > - **Tally Counter Mode (Counter):** The worker stands at the door and counts every car that passes. This is used for **event counting** (e.g., counting how many times a button is pressed). > - The manager only configures the worker once (set the start time) and goes back to his main job. When the bell rings, the worker sends an **interrupt signal**. ### 1.3 The Four Critical SFRs of the Timer Subsystem | Register | Address | Function | |----------|---------|----------| | `TMOD` | 0x89 | Timer **MO**de register (selects mode, timer/counter, start method) | | `TCON` | 0x88 | Timer **CON**trol register (run/stop flags, overflow flags, edge flags) | | `IE` | 0xA8 | Interrupt **E**nable register (enables timer interrupts to CPU) | | `THx/TLx`| 0x8A-0x8D | Timer **H**igh/**L**ow byte registers (the actual count values) | ### 1.4 Why Timers are Essential in Embedded Systems > [!IMPORTANT] > Without timers, the CPU would have to do **software delay loops** (e.g., `for(i=0;i<30000;i++);`) which: > - Waste CPU cycles doing nothing (CPU is fully blocked) > - Are imprecise (compiler optimization can break timing) > - Cannot run in the background > > Hardware timers allow **multitasking** — the CPU can execute other instructions while the timer counts independently in the background. ## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The TMOD Register (Timer Mode Register) — Address 0x89 The TMOD register is a **bit-addressable control register** that is split into two identical 4-bit nibbles. The **upper nibble** controls **Timer1** and the **lower nibble** controls **Timer0**. | Bit 7 | Bit 6 | Bit 5 | Bit 4 | Bit 3 | Bit 2 | Bit 1 | Bit 0 | |:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:| | GATE1 | C/T1 | T1M1 | T1M0 | GATE0 | C/T0 | T0M1 | T0M0 | | *(Timer 1)* | | | | *(Timer 0)* | | | | #### Bit-by-Bit Analysis of TMOD **1. GATE (Gate Control Bit)** - **GATE = 0:** Timer runs as long as the `TRx` software bit in TCON is set (software-controlled start). - **GATE = 1:** Timer runs only when `TRx = 1` AND the external `INTx` pin is HIGH (hardware-controlled start — used for pulse width measurement). **2. C/T̄ (Counter/Timer Select Bit)** - **C/T̄ = 0:** Acts as a **Timer** (counts internal oscillator pulses). - **C/T̄ = 1:** Acts as a **Counter** (counts external pulses on `T0` pin P3.4 or `T1` pin P3.5). **3. M1 and M0 (Mode Select Bits)** — These determine the operating mode: | M1 | M0 | Mode | Description | Max Count | |:--:|:--:|:----:|:------------|:---------:| | 0 | 0 | **Mode 0** | 13-bit Timer (8048 compatibility mode) | 8192 (2¹³) | | 0 | 1 | **Mode 1** | 16-bit Timer | 65536 (2¹⁶) | | 1 | 0 | **Mode 2** | 8-bit Auto-Reload Timer | 256 (2⁸) | | 1 | 1 | **Mode 3** | Split Timer (only for Timer0) | 256 each | ### 2.2 The TCON Register (Timer Control Register) — Address 0x88 The TCON register is **bit-addressable** and contains the run control bits, overflow flags, and external interrupt edge flags. | Bit 7 | Bit 6 | Bit 5 | Bit 4 | Bit 3 | Bit 2 | Bit 1 | Bit 0 | |:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:| | TF1 | TR1 | TF0 | TR0 | IE1 | IT1 | IE0 | IT0 | - **TF1, TF0 (Timer Overflow Flags):** Set to **1 by hardware** when the timer overflows from 0xFFFF to 0x0000. Must be **cleared by software**. - **TR1, TR0 (Timer Run Control Bits):** Set to **1 by software** to start the timer, cleared to **0** to stop the timer. - **IE1, IE0 (External Interrupt Edge Flags):** Set by hardware on detecting an edge. - **IT1, IT0 (Interrupt Type Control):** 0 = level-triggered, 1 = edge-triggered. ### 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master these formulas — they appear in nearly every KTU numerical problem on timers.** | Parameter | Formula | Description | |:----------|:--------|:------------| | Machine Cycle Period | $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{OSC}}$ | The 8051 divides the crystal frequency by 12 | | Timer Frequency | $f_{timer} = \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{12}$ | Frequency of increment pulses | | Timer Tick Duration | $T_{tick} = \dfrac{12}{f_{OSC}} = 1\,\mu s$ | For 11.0592 MHz crystal: ≈ 1.085 µs | | Required Count (Delay) | $Count = \dfrac{T_{delay}}{T_{tick}}$ | How many ticks are needed for the desired delay | | Timer Initial Value (Mode 1) | $N = 65536 - Count$ | 16-bit reload value for Mode 1 | | Timer Initial Value (Mode 0) | $N = 8192 - Count$ | 13-bit reload value for Mode 0 | | Timer Initial Value (Mode 2) | $N = 256 - Count$ | 8-bit auto-reload value for Mode 2 | | Hex Split | `THx = N >> 8;` `TLx = N & 0xFF;` | Separate high and low bytes for THx and TLx | | Auto-Reload Copy (Mode 2) | `THx = ReloadValue;` | The reload value is copied back automatically on overflow | | Serial Baud Rate (Mode 1, 3) | $\text{Baud} = \dfrac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$ | Timer1 in auto-reload (Mode 2) for baud generation | | Timer1 Reload Value | $TH1 = 256 - \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{12 \times 32 \times 2^{SMOD-1} \times \text{Baud}}$ | Solving the baud rate equation for TH1 | ### 2.4 Engineering Utility - **Industrial Process Control:** Generating precise actuator timing. - **PWM Generation:** Combined with interrupts, timers generate pulse-width modulated signals for motor speed control. - **Real-Time Clocks (RTC):** Cascade Timer0 and Timer1 to count seconds. - **Communication Protocols:** UART baud rate generation in serial communication. - **Debouncing:** Timer-based software debouncing of mechanical switches. ### 2.5 Counter Mode — Detailed Operation > [!NOTE] > When the **C/T̄ bit in TMOD is set to 1**, the timer becomes an **event counter**. It counts external pulses (1-to-0 or 0-to-1 transitions) on pins **P3.4 (T0)** for Timer0 and **P3.5 (T1)** for Timer1. **Key Differences from Timer Mode:** 1. The clock source is **external**, not the internal oscillator. 2. The increment is triggered by an **external pin transition**, not by the machine cycle. 3. Maximum count rate is limited to **f_OSC / 24** (one transition per 2 machine cycles). 4. The initial value must still be loaded into THx/TLx to set the count threshold. **Count Formula:** $\text{Events to overflow} = 65536 - N$ (for Mode 1) where $N$ is the value initially loaded. ## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Delay Calculation for Timer0 in Mode 1 **Problem Statement (KTU Typical Numerical):** The 8051 uses an **11.0592 MHz crystal**. Write an 8051 assembly program to generate a delay of **50 ms** using Timer0 in **Mode 1**. Show all calculations and write the complete program. #### Step 1: Compute the Machine Cycle Period The 8051 divides the oscillator frequency by 12 to produce the machine cycle clock. $$ T_{MC} = \frac{12}{f_{OSC}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} $$ $$ T_{MC} = 1.085\,\mu s \text{ (approx)} $$ This is the time taken for one timer increment (one tick). #### Step 2: Calculate the Number of Ticks Required The number of timer ticks needed to produce a 50 ms delay is: $$ \text{Count} = \frac{T_{delay}}{T_{MC}} = \frac{50 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} $$ $$ \text{Count} = \frac{50000}{1.085} = 46{,}082.95 \text{ ticks} $$ We round down to **46,082** ticks (rounding up could cause overflow — we will check this). #### Step 3: Calculate the Initial Value to Load In **Mode 1**, the timer is 16-bit, so it counts from the loaded value up to **65,536 (0xFFFF)** and overflows on the next tick. The number of ticks from value $N$ to overflow is $(65536 - N)$. $$ 65536 - N = 46{,}082 $$ $$ N = 65536 - 46{,}082 = 19{,}454 $$ Converting 19,454 to hexadecimal: $$ 19{,}454 \div 16 = 1215 \text{ remainder } 14 \quad (\text{LSB} = 0xE) $$ $$ 1215 \div 16 = 75 \text{ remainder } 15 \quad (0xF) $$ $$ 75 \div 16 = 4 \text{ remainder } 11 \quad (0xB) $$ $$ 4 \div 16 = 0 \text{ remainder } 4 \quad (\text{MSB} = 0x4) $$ Reading remainders from MSB to LSB: $$ N = 0x4BFE $$ #### Step 4: Split the Value into TH0 and TL0 $$ TH0 = \lfloor 0x4BFE / 256 \rfloor = 0x4B = 75 $$ $$ TL0 = 0x4BFE \mod 256 = 0xFE = 254 $$ > [!NOTE] > In C language: `TH0 = (65536 - 46082) >> 8;` and `TL0 = (65536 - 46082) & 0xFF;` #### Step 5: Configure TMOD Register For Timer0 in **Mode 1** as a **Timer** with **software start**: | GATE1 | C/T̄1 | T1M1 | T1M0 | GATE0 | C/T̄0 | T0M1 | T0M0 | |:-----:|:-----:|:----:|:----:|:-----:|:-----:|:----:|:----:| | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 1 | Thus: $\text{TMOD} = 0x01$ In C: `TMOD = 0x01;` #### Step 6: Complete 8051 C Program (Using Keil Syntax) ```c #include void Timer0_Delay_50ms(void) { TMOD = 0x01; // Timer0, Mode 1 (16-bit), software start TH0 = 0x4B; // Load high byte of initial value TL0 = 0xFE; // Load low byte of initial value TR0 = 1; // Start Timer0 while (TF0 == 0); // Wait here until TF0 becomes 1 (overflow) TR0 = 0; // Stop Timer0 TF0 = 0; // Manually clear overflow flag } void main(void) { while (1) { P1 = 0x00; // Turn ON all LEDs (active low assumed) Timer0_Delay_50ms(); P1 = 0xFF; // Turn OFF all LEDs Timer0_Delay_50ms(); } } ``` --- ### 3.2 Step-by-Step Derivation: Serial Communication Baud Rate Calculation **Problem Statement (KTU Typical):** The 8051 serial port is to communicate at **9600 baud, 8-bit data, 1 stop bit** using **Timer1 in auto-reload mode (Mode 2)**. The crystal frequency is **11.0592 MHz**. Calculate the TH1 reload value, with **SMOD = 0**. #### Step 1: Identify the Serial Mode For 8-bit data, 1 stop bit, no parity → **Serial Mode 1** of the 8051. The baud rate for Mode 1 is generated by **Timer1 overflow** with the formula: $$ \text{Baud Rate} = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \frac{f_{OSC}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)} $$ #### Step 2: Substitute Known Values Given: $\text{Baud} = 9600$, $f_{OSC} = 11.0592 \times 10^6$ Hz, $SMOD = 0$. $$ 9600 = \frac{2^0}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12 \times (256 - TH1)} $$ $$ 9600 = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12 \times (256 - TH1)} $$ #### Step 3: Solve for (256 − TH1) Rearranging: $$ (256 - TH1) = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{32 \times 12 \times 9600} $$ $$ (256 - TH1) = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{3{,}686{,}400} $$ $$ (256 - TH1) = 3 $$ #### Step 4: Solve for TH1 $$ TH1 = 256 - 3 = 253 = 0xFD $$ So `TH1 = 0xFD`. #### Step 5: Configure SCON Register For **Mode 1, 8-bit UART, Reception Enabled** (SM0=0, SM1=1, REN=1): | SM0 | SM1 | SM2 | REN | TB8 | RB8 | TI | RI | |:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:|:---:| | 0 | 1 | 0 | 1 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | $\text{SCON} = 0x50$ #### Step 6: Configure PCON for SMOD $$ \text{PCON} = 0x00 \quad (\text{since SMOD} = 0) $$ #### Step 7: Complete Serial Initialization Code ```c #include void Serial_Init_9600(void) { SCON = 0x50; // Mode 1, 8-bit UART, REN enabled TMOD = 0x20; // Timer1, Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload) TH1 = 0xFD; // Reload value for 9600 baud PCON = 0x00; // SMOD = 0 TR1 = 1; // Start Timer1 (baud rate generator) } void Serial_Tx(unsigned char ch) { SBUF = ch; // Load character into serial buffer while (TI == 0); // Wait until transmission complete TI = 0; // Clear transmit interrupt flag } void main(void) { Serial_Init_9600(); Serial_Tx('A'); // Transmit character 'A' while(1); } ``` --- ### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: All Four Timer Modes Compared | Feature | Mode 0 (13-bit) | Mode 1 (16-bit) | Mode 2 (8-bit Auto-Reload) | Mode 3 (Split Timer) | |:--------|:----------------|:----------------|:----------------------------|:---------------------| | **Size** | 13 bits (THx = 8 + TLx = 5) | 16 bits (THx + TLx) | 8 bits (TLx only) | 8+8 bits (TL0 + TH0) | | **Max Count** | 8192 | 65536 | 256 | 256 (each) | | **Reload** | Manual by software | Manual by software | **Automatic** from THx | Manual | | **Use Case** | Legacy 8048 code | General purpose delays | **Baud rate generator**, periodic interrupts | Two 8-bit timers (T0 only) | | **Formula** | $N = 8192 - \text{Count}$ | $N = 65536 - \text{Count}$ | $N = 256 - \text{Count}$ | Two independent 8-bit timers | | **Max Delay @11.0592 MHz** | ≈ 8.88 ms | ≈ 71.1 ms | ≈ 277 µs | ≈ 277 µs each | --- ### 3.4 Step-by-Step: Generating 1 Second Delay Using Nested Timer0 Loops Since the maximum delay in Mode 1 is ~71.1 ms, we need a software loop to repeat the timer overflow. **Delay per overflow (using FF FF):** $(65536 - 0) \times 1.085\,\mu s = 71.11\,ms$ **Number of overflows for 1 second:** $1/0.0711 \approx 14.06 \rightarrow 14$ times **Adjusted delay per overflow:** $14 \times 71.11\,ms = 995.6\,ms \approx 1\,s$ (with small error) **Alternative (preferred for exact 1 s):** Use a value that gives exactly 50 ms (as shown in Section 3.1) and loop it 20 times. ```c void Delay_1_Second(void) { unsigned char i; for (i = 0; i < 20; i++) { TMOD = 0x01; TH0 = 0x4B; TL0 = 0xFE; TR0 = 1; while (TF0 == 0); TR0 = 0; TF0 = 0; } } ``` --- ### 3.5 Step-by-Step: Counter Mode Programming **Problem:** Count 100 external pulses on pin P3.4 (T0) using Timer0 in Mode 1. #### Step 1: TMOD Configuration - C/T̄0 = 1 (counter mode) - T0M1 = 0, T0M0 = 1 (Mode 1, 16-bit) - GATE0 = 0 (software start) | GATE1 | C/T̄1 | T1M1 | T1M0 | GATE0 | C/T̄0 | T0M1 | T0M0 | |:-----:|:-----:|:----:|:----:|:-----:|:-----:|:----:|:----:| | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | 0 | **1** | 0 | **1**| $\text{TMOD} = 0x06$ (Actually for Mode 2 counter it's 0x06; for Mode 1 it's 0x05) For Mode 1 Counter: $\text{TMOD} = 0x05$ #### Step 2: Load Initial Value We need to count **100** pulses. Mode 1 overflows at 65,536. $$ N = 65536 - 100 = 65{,}436 $$ Convert to hex: $65{,}436 = 0xFF9C$ $$ TH0 = 0xFF \quad ; \quad TL0 = 0x9C $$ #### Step 3: C Program ```c #include sbit LED = P1^0; void main(void) { TMOD = 0x05; // Timer0, Mode 1, Counter mode TH0 = 0xFF; // Initial value high TL0 = 0x9C; // Initial value low (counts 100) TR0 = 1; // Start counter while (TF0 == 0); // Wait for 100 external pulses TR0 = 0; TF0 = 0; LED = 0; // Turn ON LED to indicate 100 pulses counted while(1); } ``` ## SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 Block Diagram — 8051 Timer/Counter Internal Architecture ```mermaid graph TD A["OSCILLATOR
f_OSC = 11.0592 MHz"] --> B["÷12 PRESCALER
Machine Cycle Generator"] B --> C["MUX SELECT
Based on C/T bit in TMOD"] C --> D{"C/T = 0?
Timer Mode"} C --> E{"C/T = 1?
Counter Mode"} D --> F["INTERNAL
CLOCK SOURCE
1.085 us per tick"] E --> G["EXTERNAL
T0 PIN P3.4 or
T1 PIN P3.5"] F --> H["AND GATE
with GATE and INTx"] G --> H H --> I["TRx CONTROL
Bit in TCON"] I --> J["16-BIT COUNTER
THx and TLx"] J --> K{"OVERFLOW
0xFFFF to 0x0000?"} K --> L["SET TFx FLAG
in TCON"] L --> M["GENERATE
INTERRUPT
to CPU"] K --> J ``` ### 4.2 Flow Diagram — Steps to Program a Timer in Mode 1 ```mermaid graph TD S1["STEP 1: Set TMOD
to choose Timer,
Mode, GATE"] --> S2["STEP 2: Calculate
Initial Value N
N = 65536 - Count"] S2 --> S3["STEP 3: Load THx
with high byte of N"] S3 --> S4["STEP 4: Load TLx
with low byte of N"] S4 --> S5["STEP 5: Set TRx = 1
to START timer"] S5 --> S6["STEP 6: WAIT
for TFx to become 1"] S6 --> S7["STEP 7: Clear TFx
to 0 manually"] S7 --> S8["STEP 8: Set TRx = 0
to STOP timer"] S8 --> S9["STEP 9: Return
from delay routine"] ``` ### 4.3 Block Diagram — 8051 Serial Communication System ```mermaid graph TD A["CPU writes data
to SBUF"] --> B["TRANSMIT SHIFT
REGISTER"] B --> C["TxD Pin P3.1
Serial Output"] C --> D["RECEIVING
8051 RxD Pin P3.0"] D --> E["RECEIVE SHIFT
REGISTER"] E --> F["SBUF READ
by CPU"] G["TIMER 1 OVERFLOW
at 16x Baud Rate"] --> H["BAUD RATE
GENERATOR"] H --> I["CLOCK THE
SHIFT REGISTERS"] J["SCON Register
Controls Mode
and Flags"] --> B J --> E K["TI and RI Flags
Signal Completion"] --> A K --> F ``` ### 4.4 Block Diagram — Serial Frame Format (Mode 1, 8-Bit UART) ```mermaid graph LR A["START BIT
0
1 bit"] --> B["D0 LSB"] B --> C["D1"] C --> D["D2"] D --> E["D3"] E --> F["D4"] F --> G["D5"] G --> H["D6"] H --> I["D7 MSB"] I --> J["STOP BIT
1
1 bit"] J --> K["IDLE
High
1"] ``` ### 4.5 Block Diagram — Four Timer Modes of 8051 ```mermaid graph TD A["8051 TIMER MODES"] --> B["MODE 0
13-bit Timer
THx 8-bit + TLx 5-bit"] A --> C["MODE 1
16-bit Timer
THx 8-bit + TLx 8-bit
MOST USED"] A --> D["MODE 2
8-bit Auto-Reload
THx reloads TLx
USED FOR BAUD RATE"] A --> E["MODE 3
Split Timer
Timer0 only
TL0 and TH0 act
as two 8-bit timers"] C --> F["Application:
Long Delays"] D --> G["Application:
Serial Baud Rate
Generation"] E --> H["Application:
Extra 8-bit Timer"] ``` ## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ### 📝 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 3 Marks]** *Explain the function of the TMOD register in 8051. List all four timer modes.* **Model Answer:** The **TMOD (Timer Mode) register** is an **8-bit non-bit-addressable SFR** located at address **0x89**. It is divided into two **4-bit nibbles**: - The **upper nibble** configures **Timer1** - The **lower nibble** configures **Timer0** Each nibble contains three control fields: 1. **GATE:** Hardware/software control of timer start 2. **C/T̄:** Selects Counter (1) or Timer (0) mode 3. **M1, M0:** Selects one of four operating modes **The Four Timer Modes:** | Mode | M1 | M0 | Description | |:----:|:--:|:--:|:------------| | **Mode 0** | 0 | 0 | 13-bit Timer (compatible with 8048) | | **Mode 1** | 0 | 1 | 16-bit Timer | | **Mode 2** | 1 | 0 | 8-bit Auto-Reload Timer | | **Mode 3** | 1 | 1 | Split Timer (Timer0 only) | --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Model Question, 3 Marks]** *What is the role of Timer1 in serial communication? Why is Mode 2 of Timer1 preferred for baud rate generation?* **Model Answer:** **Role of Timer1 in Serial Communication:** The 8051 uses **Timer1 overflow** as the clock source for the on-chip **UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver-Transmitter)**. Every time Timer1 overflows, the serial port receives one clock pulse. For a 9600 baud rate, Timer1 must overflow at **9600 × 16 = 153,600 times per second** because the serial port samples each bit 16 times. **Why Mode 2 is Preferred:** - **Mode 2 is 8-bit auto-reload**, meaning once configured, the timer **automatically reloads** the initial value from `TH1` into `TL1` on every overflow. - This provides a **continuous, jitter-free** clock to the UART. - A 16-bit Mode 1 timer would require software intervention to reload on every overflow, causing **baud rate errors**. - Mode 2 eliminates software overhead and ensures **stable, accurate serial communication**. --- ### 📝 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) --- #### ❓ Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 14 Marks]** **(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the **TMOD and TCON registers** of 8051. Show how Timer0 is configured for **Mode 1, software-triggered timer mode** with crystal frequency **11.0592 MHz**. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **TMOD Register (0x89) — Mode Register:** | Bit 7 | Bit 6 | Bit 5 | Bit 4 | Bit 3 | Bit 2 | Bit 1 | Bit 0 | |:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:| | GATE1 | C/T̄1 | T1M1 | T1M0 | GATE0 | C/T̄0 | T0M1 | T0M0 | | Timer 1 Nibble | | | | Timer 0 Nibble | | | | - **GATE0 = 0:** Software-controlled start - **C/T̄0 = 0:** Timer mode (internal clock) - **T0M1 = 0, T0M0 = 1:** Mode 1 (16-bit timer) **Result:** $\text{TMOD} = 0x01$ **TCON Register (0x88) — Control Register:** | Bit 7 | Bit 6 | Bit 5 | Bit 4 | Bit 3 | Bit 2 | Bit 1 | Bit 0 | |:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:|:-----:| | TF1 | TR1 | TF0 | TR0 | IE1 | IT1 | IE0 | IT0 | - **TR0 = 1:** Starts Timer0 - **TF0 = 1:** Set by hardware on overflow (cleared by software) **Configuration Code:** ```c TMOD = 0x01; // Timer0, Mode 1, Software start, Timer mode TH0 = ; TL0 = ; TR0 = 1; // Start Timer0 ``` --- **(b)** An 8051 system uses an **11.0592 MHz crystal**. Write an 8051 C program to generate a **delay of 25 ms** using Timer0 in Mode 1. Show all calculations and verify the delay. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Machine Cycle Period** $$ T_{MC} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} = 1.085\,\mu s $$ **Step 2: Required Count for 25 ms** $$ \text{Count} = \frac{25 \times 10^{-3}}{1.085 \times 10^{-6}} = 23{,}041 \text{ ticks} $$ **Step 3: Initial Value (Mode 1)** $$ N = 65536 - 23041 = 42{,}495 $$ **Hex Conversion of 42,495:** - 42,495 = 0xA5FF (calculation: 42,495 = 10×4096 + 5×256 + 15×16 + 15 = 40,960 + 1,280 + 240 + 15 = 42,495) **Splitting:** - $\text{TH0} = 0xA5 = 165$ - $\text{TL0} = 0xFF = 255$ **Step 4: Verification** $$ \text{Delay} = (65536 - 42495) \times 1.085\,\mu s = 23041 \times 1.085 = 24.999\,\mu s \approx 25\,ms $$ **Step 5: Complete C Program** ```c #include void Timer0_25ms_Delay(void) { TMOD = 0x01; // Timer0, Mode 1, Software start TH0 = 0xA5; // Load high byte TL0 = 0xFF; // Load low byte TR0 = 1; // Start Timer0 while (TF0 == 0); // Wait for overflow TR0 = 0; // Stop Timer0 TF0 = 0; // Clear overflow flag } void main(void) { while (1) { P2 = 0x00; // Turn ON Timer0_25ms_Delay(); P2 = 0xFF; // Turn OFF Timer0_25ms_Delay(); } } ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Students often forget to **manually clear the TF0 flag** in polling mode. The flag is set by hardware on overflow but must be reset by software, otherwise the next `while(TF0==0)` loop will exit immediately, producing **wrong (much shorter) delays**. --- #### ❓ Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2 — Internal Choice) **[KTU University Exam – Model Question, 14 Marks]** **(a)** Explain the **four serial communication modes of 8051**. With a neat sketch, describe the **serial frame format of Mode 1**. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **The 8051 has four serial communication modes, controlled by the SM0 and SM1 bits of the SCON register:** | Mode | SM0 | SM1 | Description | Baud Rate | |:----:|:---:|:---:|:------------|:----------| | **Mode 0** | 0 | 0 | 8-bit Shift Register (synchronous) | f_OSC/12 (fixed) | | **Mode 1** | 0 | 1 | 8-bit UART (asynchronous) | Timer1 overflow (variable) | | **Mode 2** | 1 | 0 | 9-bit UART (asynchronous) | f_OSC/32 or f_OSC/64 | | **Mode 3** | 1 | 1 | 9-bit UART (asynchronous) | Timer1 overflow (variable) | **Serial Frame Format for Mode 1 (8-bit UART):** ``` |--------|--------|--------|--------|--------|--------|--------|--------|--------|--------| | IDLE | START | D0 | D1 | D2 | D3 | D4 | D5 | D6 | D7 | | 1 | 0 | LSB | | | | | | | MSB | |--------|--------|--------|--------|--------|--------|--------|--------|--------|--------| |---8 DATA BITS---| | | v STOP BIT (1) ``` **Detailed Frame Description:** - **Idle State:** Line held HIGH (1) when no transmission - **Start Bit:** 1 bit of logic 0 (marks beginning of frame) - **Data Bits:** 8 bits, transmitted **LSB first** (D0 → D7) - **Stop Bit:** 1 bit of logic 1 (marks end of frame) - **Total Frame:** 10 bits (1 start + 8 data + 1 stop) **Pin Functions:** - **P3.0 (RxD):** Serial data **receive** pin - **P3.1 (TxD):** Serial data **transmit** pin --- **(b)** Write an 8051 C program to **transmit the string "KTU" serially at 9600 baud** using **Timer1 Mode 2** and **11.0592 MHz crystal**. Show all calculations. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **Step 1: Calculate TH1 for 9600 Baud** Using the baud rate formula with SMOD = 0: $$ \text{Baud} = \frac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \frac{f_{OSC}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)} $$ $$ 9600 = \frac{1}{32} \times \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{12 \times (256 - TH1)} $$ $$ (256 - TH1) = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{32 \times 12 \times 9600} = 3 $$ $$ TH1 = 256 - 3 = 253 = 0xFD $$ **Step 2: TMOD Value for Timer1 Mode 2** - GATE1 = 0, C/T̄1 = 0, T1M1 = 1, T1M0 = 0 - Timer0 nibble = 0000 (not used) - $\text{TMOD} = 0x20$ **Step 3: SCON Value for Mode 1** - SM0 = 0, SM1 = 1, REN = 1 - $\text{SCON} = 0x50$ **Step 4: Complete C Program** ```c #include void Serial_Init(void) { SCON = 0x50; // Mode 1, 8-bit UART, REN enabled TMOD = 0x20; // Timer1, Mode 2 (8-bit auto-reload) TH1 = 0xFD; // Reload value for 9600 baud PCON = 0x00; // SMOD = 0 TR1 = 1; // Start Timer1 (baud rate generator) } void Serial_Tx(unsigned char ch) { SBUF = ch; // Load character into SBUF while (TI == 0); // Wait until transmission completes TI = 0; // Clear TI flag manually } void main(void) { unsigned char i; unsigned char msg[] = "KTU"; Serial_Init(); for (i = 0; msg[i] != '\0'; i++) { Serial_Tx(msg[i]); // Transmit each character } while(1); } ``` > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:** Two common mistakes in this question: > 1. **Forgetting to clear the TI flag** after each transmission — without this, only the **first character** of "KTU" will be transmitted correctly; the rest will be missed because TI remains 1. > 2. **Using 12 MHz crystal assumptions** — if the student assumes 12 MHz, the TH1 value becomes -6.06, which is impossible. The **11.0592 MHz crystal is specifically chosen** because it gives integer division for standard baud rates. Always verify TH1 is an **unsigned 8-bit value** (0–255). --- ### 📋 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **Rapid Revision Checklist — Must Memorize for KTU Exam:** #### Timer Registers - ✅ **TMOD (0x89):** Non-bit-addressable, sets Mode, C/T̄, GATE for both timers - ✅ **TCON (0x88):** Bit-addressable, has TR0/TR1 (run) and TF0/TF1 (overflow flag) - ✅ **TH0, TL0, TH1, TL1:** 8-bit registers holding the timer count values #### Timer Modes - ✅ **Mode 0:** 13-bit timer (legacy) → $N = 8192 - \text{Count}$ - ✅ **Mode 1:** 16-bit timer (general purpose) → $N = 65536 - \text{Count}$ - ✅ **Mode 2:** 8-bit auto-reload (baud rate) → $N = 256 - \text{Count}$ - ✅ **Mode 3:** Split timer (Timer0 only) — TL0 and TH0 as two independent 8-bit timers #### Key Formulas - ✅ $T_{MC} = \dfrac{12}{f_{OSC}}$ (Machine cycle period) - ✅ $T_{MC} = 1.085\,\mu s$ for **11.0592 MHz** crystal - ✅ For 12 MHz crystal: $T_{MC} = 1\,\mu s$ - ✅ $\text{Baud Rate} = \dfrac{2^{SMOD}}{32} \times \dfrac{f_{OSC}}{12 \times (256 - TH1)}$ #### Counter Mode - ✅ C/T̄ = 1 selects counter mode - ✅ Counts external events on **P3.4 (T0)** or **P3.5 (T1)** - ✅ Max count rate = $f_{OSC}/24$ #### Serial Communication - ✅ **SCON (0x98):** Bit-addressable, contains SM0, SM1, SM2, REN, TB8, RB8, TI, RI - ✅ **SBUF (0x99):** Serial buffer for both transmit and receive - ✅ **PCON (0x87):** Contains SMOD bit (doubles baud rate when 1) - ✅ **Mode 1:** 8-bit UART, baud rate from Timer1 - ✅ **TI flag:** Set after transmission — must be cleared by software - ✅ **RI flag:** Set after reception — must be cleared by software - ✅ Standard **9600 baud reload value** = 0xFD with 11.0592 MHz, SMOD=0 #### Programming Steps (Timer Delay) - ✅ Step 1: Set TMOD - ✅ Step 2: Calculate N - ✅ Step 3: Load THx, TLx - ✅ Step 4: Set TRx = 1 (start) - ✅ Step 5: Wait for TFx = 1 - ✅ Step 6: Clear TFx, clear TRx

topics.title: Serial Communication
topics.content:
<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET613 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Serial Communication

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

**Serial Communication** is a digital data transmission technique in which bits of a data word are transmitted *sequentially* over a *single communication line* (or channel) one after another, in contrast to parallel communication where multiple bits are sent simultaneously across multiple lines.

In the context of the KTU 8051 Microcontroller syllabus, **Serial Communication** specifically refers to the **UART (Universal Asynchronous Receiver-Transmitter)** interface built into the 8051 core, which supports both **asynchronous** (start/stop bit framed, no shared clock) and **synchronous** (clocked, half-duplex) modes of operation, conforming to the **RS-232 standard** for PC-to-MCU communication.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 4)**
> The 2024 Scheme OEAET613 syllabus explicitly covers:
> - 8051 Serial Port architecture (SBUF, SCON, PCON registers)
> - Operating modes 0, 1, 2, 3 (Mode 1: 8-bit UART is the *de facto* standard)
> - Baud rate generation using Timer 1
> - RS-232 voltage level translation (MAX 232 driver IC)
> - Programming the 8051 for full-duplex serial I/O

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Tunnel and the Train"

Imagine two cities connected by a **single-lane railway tunnel** instead of an eight-lane highway:

- **Parallel Communication** = 8 trucks driving side by side on an 8-lane highway. They all arrive at the *same time*, but the highway needs lots of land, lots of fuel (more wires = more current), and is expensive to build.
- **Serial Communication** = 1 train carrying 8 cargo containers, one behind the other, through a single tunnel. It takes *longer* to deliver the goods, but needs only **one track** (one wire), is cheap, and can travel *very long distances* without signal degradation.

**Key Takeaway:** Serial = slower per-byte but **cheaper, longer-range, and EMI-resistant** — perfect for connecting MCUs to PCs, sensors, and other MCUs.

### 1.3 Key Terminology Snapshot

| Term | Meaning |
|---|---|
| **Baud Rate** | Number of **signal changes per second** (bits per second in simple binary schemes). Standard values: **9600**, **19200**, **115200**. |
| **UART** | Hardware block that converts parallel byte ↔ serial bit stream. |
| **Asynchronous** | No shared clock line; synchronization is via start/stop bits. |
| **Synchronous** | Shared clock line; used in Mode 0 of 8051. |
| **Full-Duplex** | Can transmit AND receive simultaneously. |
| **RS-232** | Voltage standard: Logic 1 = **−3V to −25V**, Logic 0 = **+3V to +25V**. |
| **TTL/CMOS** | MCU levels: Logic 1 = +5V, Logic 0 = 0V. (Needs MAX 232 converter!) |
| **Frame** | Complete packet: 1 Start bit + 8/9 Data bits + (Parity) + 1/2 Stop bits. |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why Serial Communication Matters in 8051 Design**
> 1. **PC Interfacing:** Modern PCs have NO parallel printer ports, but ALWAYS have USB. USB-to-UART bridges (CP2102, CH340, FT232) make serial the *default* debug channel.
> 2. **Sensor Networks:** GPS, GSM, Bluetooth (HC-05), RFID — all speak UART.
> 3. **Multi-MCU Systems:** Multiple 8051s can be networked with only **two wires** (TX, RX) per link.
> 4. **Long Distance:** RS-232 works reliably up to **15 meters**; RS-485 up to **1200 meters**.
