```asm
        MOV  TH1,  #0FAH ; Reload value for 4800 baud
        MOV  TL1,  #0FAH ; Initial value
        SETB TR1         ; Start Timer 1 → baud clock active
        MOV  SBUF, #'K'  ; Load 'K' (0x4B) into transmit SBUF
WAIT:   JNB  TI, WAIT    ; Spin until transmission complete
        CLR  TI          ; Clear TI flag (MANDATORY)
HERE:   SJMP HERE        ; Done; loop forever
END
```

**Verification:** $250 = 0xFA$ → $(256-250) = 6$ → Baud Rate $= \frac{11{,}059{,}200}{12 \times 6 \times 32} = 4800$ baud ✓

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Interrupt Programming
**[KTU University Exam — Module 4 Model Question]**

**(a)** Explain the **interrupt structure** of the 8051. List the **six interrupt sources** with their **vector addresses** and describe the role of the **IE and IP registers**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Write an 8051 assembly program that uses **External Interrupt 0 (INT0)** to toggle **Port 1.0** each time a falling edge is detected on **P3.2**. Use **edge-triggered** mode and configure the interrupt for **high priority**. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(a) — 7 Marks

**Interrupt Structure of 8051 (3 marks):**

The 8051 has a **fixed 6-source interrupt architecture** with a hard-wired vector table. When an interrupt occurs, the CPU:

1. **Finishes** the currently executing instruction.
2. **Detects** the interrupt (hardware polling if multiple sources are pending).
3. **Pushes** the 16-bit Program Counter onto the stack.
4. **Loads** the PC with the corresponding vector address.
5. **Executes** the ISR starting at that vector.
6. **RETI** at the end pops the PC and resumes the main program.

**Six Interrupt Sources with Vector Addresses (2 marks):**

| Source | Vector Address |
|:-------|:---------------|
| Reset | 0000H |
| External Interrupt 0 | 0003H |
| Timer 0 Overflow | 000BH |
| External Interrupt 1 | 0013H |
| Timer 1 Overflow | 001BH |
| Serial Port | 0023H |

**Role of IE Register (1 mark):**
The **Interrupt Enable register (A8H)** is the **master switch** for all maskable interrupts. Bit **EA** enables all; bits **ES, ET1, EX1, ET0, EX0** enable the individual sources.

**Role of IP Register (1 mark):**
The **Interrupt Priority register (B8H)** sets each interrupt to **low (0) or high (1) priority**. A high-priority interrupt can preempt a low-priority ISR. The default poll order is Reset > EX0 > T0 > EX1 > T1 > Serial.

---

#### Model Solution to Q.B(b) — 7 Marks

**Configuration Plan (2 marks):**

| Register | Value | Purpose |
|:---------|:------|:--------|
| TCON bit IT0 | 1 | Edge-triggered (falling edge on P3.2) |
| IE register | 81H | EA=1, EX0=1 (enable INT0) |
| IP register | 01H | PX0=1 (high priority for INT0) |
| P3.2 | Input | INT0 hardware pin |

**8051 Assembly Program (5 marks):**

```asm
        ORG  0000H
        LJMP MAIN            ; Jump past vector area

        ORG  0003H           ; External Interrupt 0 vector
        LJMP EXT0_ISR        ; Redirect to actual ISR

        ORG  0030H
MAIN:   SETB IT0             ; TCON: IT0=1 → edge-triggered
        SETB EX0             ; IE: enable EX0
        SETB EA              ; IE: global enable
        SETB PX0             ; IP: high priority for INT0
        ; P3.2 is automatically configured as input on reset
HERE:   SJMP HERE            ; Main loop does nothing; wait for interrupt

EXT0_ISR:
        CPL  P1.0            ; Toggle P1.0
        RETI                 ; Return from interrupt
END
```

**Explanation:**
- `SETB IT0` → configures INT0 for **falling-edge** triggering.
- `SETB EX0` + `SETB EA` → enables External Interrupt 0 globally.
- `SETB PX0` → raises INT0 to **high priority**.
- The ISR at vector 0003H uses `LJMP` to relocate the code beyond the 8-byte vector region.
- `CPL P1.0` **toggles** P1.0 (complement: 0→1, 1→0).
- `RETI` (not RET) is the proper return — it signals the interrupt controller to re-enable the current priority level.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. Forgetting to **clear the IE0 flag** in edge-triggered mode — unlike the level-triggered case, edge-triggered IE0 is **NOT auto-cleared by hardware**; some 8051 variants (especially AT89C51) require manual clearing via `CLR IE0`.
> 2. Using **`RET`** instead of **`RETI`** — RET does not signal the interrupt controller, blocking further interrupts of the same priority level.
> 3. Writing the ISR **directly at the vector address** without using `LJMP` — this wastes the precious 8-byte vector space and can overwrite adjacent ISRs.

---

## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision List — Module 4: Transmitting/Receiving & Interrupts**

- ✅ The 8051 serial port is **full-duplex**; transmit (TXD/P3.1) and receive (RXD/P3.0) operate **independently**.
- ✅ **Mode 1** is the standard: **10 bits/frame** (1 start + 8 data LSB-first + 1 stop), **variable baud rate** via Timer 1.
- ✅ **SBUF (99H)** is dual: write to it → transmit; read from it → receive. Same address, two physical registers.
- ✅ **SCON (98H)** is bit-addressable: `50H` configures Mode 1 with REN=0; `70H` would enable receive.
- ✅ **TI and RI must be cleared by software** — they are the most-forgotten flags in KTU exams.
- ✅ **Baud rate formula** for Mode 1: $\text{BR} = \frac{2^{\text{SMOD}} \times f_{\text{osc}}}{32 \times 12 \times (256 - \text{TH1})}$
- ✅ **SMOD = 1** doubles the baud rate; this is the only bit of PCON used for serial.
- ✅ **11.0592 MHz crystal** is preferred because it yields **exact integer TH1 values** for all standard baud rates.
- ✅ Timer 1 must be in **Mode 2 (auto-reload)** to act as a stable baud generator.
- ✅ The 8051 has **6 interrupt sources**: Reset, INT0, T0, INT1, T1, Serial — with vectors at 0000H, 0003H, 000BH, 0013H, 001BH, 0023H.
- ✅ **IE register (A8H)**: EA is the master; ES, ET1, EX1, ET0, EX0 enable individual sources.
- ✅ **IP register (B8H)**: PS, PT1, PX1, PT0, PX0 raise priority to **high**.
- ✅ **TCON bits IT0/IT1**: 0 = level-triggered, 1 = edge-triggered (falling).
- ✅ **Edge-triggered** IE0/IE1 flags must be **cleared by software**; level-triggered flags follow the pin.
- ✅ ISR must end with **RETI** (not RET) — RETI signals the interrupt control logic.
- ✅ For long ISRs, use **`LJMP`** at the vector address to relocate the body beyond the 8-byte region.
- ✅ Serial port ISR must check **both TI and RI** because they share vector 0023H.
- ✅ High-priority interrupts can **preempt** low-priority ISRs; the converse is not possible.
- ✅ **Reset** is the **only non-maskable** interrupt — EA cannot disable it.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Use External, Timer and Serial Port Interrupts, Interrupt Priority Settings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEAET613 - MICROCONTROLLERS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Use External, Timer and Serial Port Interrupts, Interrupt Priority Settings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the **Intel 8051 Microcontroller architecture**, an **Interrupt** is an asynchronous external or internal event that temporarily halts the main program execution, forcing the CPU to execute a dedicated subroutine known as an **Interrupt Service Routine (ISR)**. Upon completion of the ISR, the CPU resumes normal program flow from the exact instruction where it was interrupted.

The 8051 Microcontroller is equipped with **five (5) interrupt sources**, which are categorized into three functional families:

1. **Two External Hardware Interrupts** — `INT0` (P3.2) and `INT1` (P3.3)
2. **Two Timer Interrupts** — `TF0` (Timer 0 Overflow) and `TF1` (Timer 1 Overflow)
3. **One Serial Port Interrupt** — Generated by either the `TI` (Transmit Interrupt) or `RI` (Receive Interrupt) flag

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** The 8051 reserves six (6) bytes of program memory for the **Interrupt Vector Addresses** — a fixed memory map from which the CPU fetches the ISR jump address whenever an interrupt is acknowledged. These addresses are **non-configurable** and reside in absolute ROM locations `0003H` to `002BH`.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are a chef cooking a complex multi-course meal (the **main program**). Suddenly:
- The doorbell rings (**External Interrupt**) — someone is at the door.
- A timer on the oven beeps (**Timer Interrupt**) — the dish is ready.
- The telephone rings (**Serial Port Interrupt**) — a caller is on the line.

You don't abandon the meal completely. You **pause** cooking, **handle the urgent event** (open the door, take the cake out, answer the phone), and then **return to exactly where you left off** in your recipe. This is precisely how an 8051 manages interrupts — the **Program Counter (PC)** is automatically pushed onto the hardware **Stack**, the ISR is executed, and `RETI` (Return from Interrupt) pops the PC back, resuming the main program.

> [!NOTE]
> **The Hardware Stack**: The 8051 uses a dedicated 8-bit **Stack Pointer (SP)** register that grows **upward** in Internal RAM (default `SP = 07H`). The PC value is saved as two 8-bit pushes (high byte first, then low byte) before jumping to the ISR.

### 1.3 Why Interrupts Are Indispensable

- **Polling** wastes CPU cycles repeatedly checking flag bits.
- **Interrupts** are **event-driven** — the CPU executes other tasks and is notified *only* when an event occurs.
- They provide **deterministic response time** crucial for real-time embedded systems (motor control, communication, sensor acquisition).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The 8051 uses a **two-level priority system** (High and Low) which can be individually assigned to each of the 5 interrupt sources via the **Interrupt Priority (IP) register** at SFR address `0B8H`.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Five Interrupt Sources — Operational Breakdown

#### 2.1.1 External Interrupts (INT0 and INT1)

| Property | Specification |
|---|---|
| Pins | `P3.2` (INT0), `P3.3` (INT1) |
| Trigger Modes | **Level-Triggered** (default, low-activated) or **Edge-Triggered** (falling edge) |
| Control Bits | `IT0` (TCON.0), `IT1` (TCON.2) |
| Request Flags | `IE0` (TCON.1), `IE1` (TCON.3) |
| Enable Bits | `EX0` (IE.0), `EX1` (IE.2) |

- **Level-Triggered Mode (`ITx = 0`)**: The interrupt request line is sampled every machine cycle. The flag is set as long as the pin is held **LOW**. The external source **must hold the pin low until acknowledged**, and then deactivate it before the ISR ends, otherwise a second interrupt will be triggered.
- **Edge-Triggered Mode (`ITx = 1`)**: The interrupt is generated on the **falling edge (HIGH→LOW transition)**. The flag `IEx` is latched and is **automatically cleared** when the corresponding ISR is vectored. This mode is suitable for pulses.

#### 2.1.2 Timer Interrupts (TF0 and TF1)

| Property | Specification |
|---|---|
| Trigger Event | Timer/Counter register **overflows** from `FFFFH → 0000H` (in Mode 1) or from the auto-reload value (in Mode 2) |
| Request Flags | `TF0` (TCON.5), `TF1` (TCON.7) |
| Enable Bits | `ET0` (IE.1), `ET1` (IE.3) |
| Auto-Clear | The `TFx` flag is **automatically cleared** when the CPU vectors to the corresponding ISR |

#### 2.1.3 Serial Port Interrupt (TI/RI)

| Property | Specification |
|---|---|
| Source | `TI` (SCON.1) — Transmission complete, OR `RI` (SCON.0) — Data byte received |
| Combined Flag | Both `TI` and `RI` share a **single interrupt vector** at `0023H` |
| Manual Clear | **Unlike timer/external flags, neither `TI` nor `RI` is auto-cleared by hardware.** The ISR must explicitly clear them via software. |
| Enable Bit | `ES` (IE.4) |

### 2.2 Interrupt Enable (IE) Register — SFR Address `0A8H`

The IE register is the **master switch** for the entire interrupt system. Even if an event occurs, the interrupt will be **ignored** if its enable bit is not set.

**Bit Addressable Register (MSB to LSB):**

| Bit 7 | Bit 6 | Bit 5 | Bit 4 | Bit 3 | Bit 2 | Bit 1 | Bit 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `EA` | — | — | `ES` | `ET1` | `EX1` | `ET0` | `EX0` |

- **`EA` (IE.7)** — **Global Enable**. Must be set to `1` to enable **any** interrupt. Setting `EA = 0` masks all interrupts (the CPU ignores all interrupt requests).
- **`ES` (IE.4)** — Enables Serial Port interrupt.
- **`ET1`, `ET0`** — Enable Timer 1 and Timer 0 overflow interrupts.
- **`EX1`, `EX0`** — Enable External Interrupt 1 and External Interrupt 0.

**Reset Value of IE Register: `0x00`** — All interrupts are **disabled** on power-up. The programmer must explicitly configure the IE register.

### 2.3 Interrupt Priority (IP) Register — SFR Address `0B8H`

The 8051 supports **two priority levels** for each interrupt source: **High Priority** and **Low Priority**.

**Bit Addressable Register (MSB to LSB):**

| Bit 7 | Bit 6 | Bit 5 | Bit 4 | Bit 3 | Bit 2 | Bit 1 | Bit 0 |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| — | — | — | `PS` | `PT1` | `PX1` | `PT0` | `PX0` |

- A bit value of `1` assigns **High Priority**; `0` assigns **Low Priority**.
- **`PS` (IP.4)** — Serial Port priority
- **`PT1`, `PT0`** — Timer 1, Timer 0 priority
- **`PX1`, `PX0`** — External Int 1, External Int 0 priority

**Reset Value of IP Register: `0x00`** — All interrupts default to **Low Priority**.

### 2.4 The Three Fundamental Interrupt Priority Rules (KTU High-Yield!)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Board Question Pattern — Almost Every Module 4 Question Tests These Rules!**

1. **A high-priority interrupt can interrupt a low-priority ISR.** A low-priority interrupt **cannot** interrupt a high-priority ISR.
2. **If two interrupts of the same priority level are requested simultaneously**, a fixed **internal polling sequence** determines the winner. This polling sequence is the **default natural priority order**:
   - `INT0` (Highest) → `TF0` → `INT1` → `TF1` → `Serial Port (TI/RI)` (Lowest)
3. **Once an interrupt is being serviced, only a higher-priority interrupt can preempt it.** If two requests of the *same* priority occur during execution, the one with the higher natural priority wins after the current ISR completes.

### 2.5 Interrupt Vector Address Table (KTU Must-Memorize!)

| Interrupt Source | Flag | Vector Address (ROM) |
|---|---|---|
| Reset (Power-On) | — | `0000H` |
| External Interrupt 0 | `IE0` | `0003H` |
| Timer 0 Overflow | `TF0` | `000BH` |
| External Interrupt 1 | `IE1` | `0013H` |
| Timer 1 Overflow | `TF1` | `001BH` |
| Serial Port (TI/RI) | `TI` or `RI` | `0023H` |
| Timer 2 Overflow / Ext2 (8052) | `TF2` | `002BH` |

> Each vector is allocated **exactly 8 bytes** of code space. If the ISR is longer than 8 bytes, the programmer must place an `LJMP` instruction at the vector address to redirect execution to the actual ISR body located elsewhere in ROM.

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| Concept | Formula / Rule | Engineering Utility |
|---|---|---|
| Timer Tick Frequency | $f_{timer} = \frac{f_{osc}}{12}$ | Determines interrupt generation rate |
| Timer Rollover Period (Mode 1) | $T_{overflow} = \frac{12 \times (65536 - THx\_TLx)}{f_{osc}}$ | Exact delay computation |
| Auto-Reload Period (Mode 2) | $T_{overflow} = \frac{12 \times (256 - THx)}{f_{osc}}$ | Used for **baud rate generation** |
| Interrupt Latency | 3 to 7 machine cycles | Time from flag set to ISR execution |
| Stack Push on ISR Entry | 2 cycles (16-bit PC saved) | Critical for nested interrupts |
| Machine Cycle | $T_{mc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}}$ | 8051 timing reference |

**Note on notation:** To prevent markdown table corruption, absolute value expressions (if any) are written as `\vert x \vert`. Vector addresses are 16-bit hexadecimal values.

## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Step-by-Step: Interrupt Servicing Sequence (How the Hardware Handles an Interrupt)

The 8051 hardware executes a **precise, fixed sequence** of micro-operations whenever an interrupt is acknowledged. Below is the step-by-step breakdown required for full marks on a KTU "explain the interrupt mechanism" question.

**Step 1 — Event Detection**
An internal or external event sets the corresponding request flag:
- External pin goes low / falling edge detected → `IE0 = 1` or `IE1 = 1`
- Timer register overflows → `TF0 = 1` or `TF1 = 1`
- Serial transmission/reception completes → `TI = 1` or `RI = 1`

**Step 2 — Interrupt Validation by Hardware**
The hardware checks three conditions before accepting the interrupt:
- The corresponding **local enable bit** in `IE` must be `1` (e.g., `EX0 = 1`).
- The **global enable** bit `EA` in `IE` must be `1`.
- The instruction currently being executed must complete (the 8051 does **not** interrupt a multi-cycle instruction mid-way).

**Step 3 — Priority Resolution**
If multiple interrupts are pending, the hardware uses the **IP register settings** and the **natural priority order** to select the highest-priority source.

**Step 4 — Current Instruction Completion**
The CPU finishes executing the current instruction and prepares to vector to the ISR.

**Step 5 — Program Counter (PC) Saved on Stack**
The 16-bit PC is pushed onto the hardware stack as two 8-bit operations:
- First push: `PCH` (high byte) → `SP` is incremented first, then `PCH` is stored.
- Second push: `PCL` (low byte) → `SP` is incremented, then `PCL` is stored.

> Stack pointer incrementing logic: Before each push, SP is incremented. So if `SP = 07H`, after saving PC, `SP = 09H`.

**Step 6 — Flags Cleared Automatically (Conditional)**
- For **External Edge-Triggered** interrupts, `IE0`/`IE1` is cleared automatically.
- For **Timer** interrupts, `TF0`/`TF1` is cleared automatically.
- For **Serial Port** interrupts, `TI`/`RI` are **NOT** cleared — software must clear them.
- For **Level-Triggered** external interrupts, the flag follows the pin state (it is not latched).

**Step 7 — PC Loaded with Vector Address**
The 16-bit vector address corresponding to the selected interrupt is loaded into the PC. For example, for Timer 0: `PC ← 000BH`.

**Step 8 — ISR Execution**
The CPU fetches and executes instructions from the vector address. The ISR typically begins with **register banking** to preserve context:
- `MOV PSW, #00001000B` (selects Register Bank 3, leaving Bank 0 free for the main program).

**Step 9 — Interrupt Return via `RETI`**
The `RETI` instruction:
- Pops the saved 16-bit PC from the stack in reverse order (PCL first, then PCH).
- Returns CPU execution to the next instruction in the main program.
- **Re-enables the interrupt logic** to accept further interrupts of equal/higher priority.

### 3.2 Analytical Matrix: Enabling All Five Interrupts at High Priority

**Problem:** Write the assembly instructions to enable ALL five 8051 interrupts and assign them HIGH priority. Assume crystal = 11.0592 MHz, and a Timer 0 auto-reload value of `-3` (i.e., `0xFD`) is used to generate a 2.7 µs tick for the serial port baud rate generator.

**Solution — Step-by-Step Configuration:**

**Step (a): Set Timer 0 in Mode 2 (Auto-Reload) for baud rate generation**
We assign the reload value:
$$
TH0 = 0xFD
$$
$$
TL0 = 0xFD
$$
We configure `TMOD` for Timer 0 in Mode 2, software-controlled timer mode:
$$
TMOD = 0x20
$$
$$
TMOD \rightarrow \text{Decimal} = 32
$$

**Step (b): Start Timer 0**
$$
TR0 = 1 \;\;\; \Rightarrow \;\;\; SETB \;\; TR0
$$
This is achieved by the bit-addressable instruction on `TCON`.

**Step (c): Configure Serial Port for Mode 1 (8-bit UART) with Timer 1 overflow as baud rate**
$$
SCON = 0x50
$$
This sets `REN = 1` and selects Mode 1 (`SM0 = 0`, `SM1 = 1`).

**Step (d): Assign High Priority to ALL five interrupts**
$$
IP = 0x1E
$$
Bit-pattern: `0001 1110` → `PX0 = 0`, `PT0 = 1`, `PX1 = 1`, `PT1 = 1`, `PS = 1`.

Wait — re-evaluating. To assign **HIGH priority** to *all* five sources:
- `PX0 = 1` (bit 0)
- `PT0 = 1` (bit 1)
- `PX1 = 1` (bit 2)
- `PT1 = 1` (bit 3)
- `PS = 1` (bit 4)
$$
IP = 0x1F
$$

**Step (e): Enable All Five Interrupts in the IE Register**
- `EX0 = 1` (bit 0)
- `ET0 = 1` (bit 1)
- `EX1 = 1` (bit 2)
- `ET1 = 1` (bit 3)
- `ES = 1` (bit 4)
- `EA = 1` (bit 7)
$$
IE = 0x9F
$$

**Step (f): Choose Edge-Triggered Mode for External Interrupts**
$$
IT0 = 1, \;\;\; IT1 = 1
$$
$$
TCON = 0x05
$$
(Setting `IT0` and `IT1` bits; `TR0 = 1` already set separately.)

**Final Assembly Code Block:**
```assembly
        MOV     TMOD, #20H      ; Timer 0 in Mode 2
        MOV     TH0,  #0FDH     ; Reload value
        MOV     TL0,  #0FDH
        SETB    TR0             ; Start Timer 0
        MOV     SCON, #50H      ; Serial Mode 1, REN enabled
        MOV     IE,   #9FH      ; Enable all 5 interrupts + EA
        MOV     IP,   #1FH      ; All 5 interrupts = High priority
        MOV     TCON, #05H      ; Edge-triggered on INT0, INT1
        SJMP    $               ; Main loop (idle)
```

### 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Timer 1 Overflow Rate for Standard Baud Rate Generation

**Given:** Crystal frequency $f_{osc} = 11.0592 \text{ MHz}$, target baud rate = 9600.

**Step 1 — Machine Cycle Calculation**
$$
T_{mc} = \frac{12}{f_{osc}} = \frac{12}{11.0592 \times 10^6} = 1.085 \, \mu s
$$

**Step 2 — Timer 1 Overflow Frequency Required**
For 8051 UART Mode 1, baud rate is generated by **Timer 1 overflow ÷ 32** (when `SMOD = 0`):
$$
\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{f_{osc}}{12 \times 32 \times (256 - TH1)}
$$

**Step 3 — Solving for TH1 Reload Value**
$$
9600 = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{384 \times (256 - TH1)}
$$

Rearranging:
$$
256 - TH1 = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{384 \times 9600}
$$

$$
256 - TH1 = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{3.6864 \times 10^6} = 3
$$

$$
TH1 = 256 - 3 = 253 = 0xFD
$$

**Step 4 — Verification**
$$
\text{Baud Rate} = \frac{11.0592 \times 10^6}{384 \times 3} = 9600 \text{ baud} \;\;\; \checkmark
$$

This reload value `0xFD` is the de-facto industry standard and the KTU board expects students to recognize it instantly.

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Interrupt Decision and Priority Resolution

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    start[Interrupt Request Flag Set] --> checkEA{Is EA = 1 in IE Register?}
    checkEA -- No --> ignore[Interrupt Ignored - CPU continues main program]
    checkEA -- Yes --> checkLocal{Is the corresponding local enable bit = 1?}
    checkLocal -- No --> ignore
    checkLocal -- Yes --> checkCurr{Is current instruction complete?}
    checkCurr -- No --> wait[Wait until instruction finishes]
    wait --> checkCurr
    checkCurr -- Yes --> checkPending{Are multiple interrupts pending?}
    checkPending -- No --> service[Service the single interrupt]
    checkPending -- Yes --> checkHigh{Is any pending interrupt High priority?}
    checkHigh -- Yes --> pickHigh[Service the highest priority High interrupt first]
    checkHigh -- No --> natural[Apply natural priority order: INT0 > TF0 > INT1 > TF1 > Serial]
    pickHigh --> pushPC
    natural --> pushPC
    service --> pushPC
    pushPC[Push 16 bit PC onto Stack] --> vector[Load PC with Vector Address from ROM]
    vector --> runISR[Execute Interrupt Service Routine]
    runISR --> checkTI{Is this a Serial Port ISR?}
    checkTI -- Yes --> clearTI[Software must clear TI or RI flag]
    checkTI -- No --> doneISR
    clearTI --> doneISR[ISR body complete]
    doneISR --> reti[Execute RETI instruction]
    reti --> popPC[Pop 16 bit PC from Stack]
    popPC --> main[Resume Main Program Execution]
```

## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]**
**Explain the role of the EA bit in the IE register of the 8051 microcontroller. What happens if EA is cleared?**

**Model Answer:**
The `EA` (Enable All) bit, located at bit 7 of the IE register (SFR `0A8H`), is the **global master enable** for the 8051 interrupt system. Even if individual interrupt enable bits (like `EX0`, `ET0`, `ES`) are set, no interrupt will be serviced if `EA = 0`. The CPU effectively masks all interrupt requests, behaving as if interrupts are completely disabled. To use any interrupt in the 8051, the programmer must set `EA = 1` along with the corresponding local enable bit. **(3 marks)**

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Why must the TI and RI flags be cleared by software inside the serial port ISR, while TF0 and TF1 are cleared automatically by hardware?**

**Model Answer:**
The TF0/TF1 flags (Timer 0/1 overflow) and the IE0/IE1 flags (External Interrupts in edge-triggered mode) are **automatically cleared by the 8051 hardware** when the corresponding ISR is vectored. However, the **serial port interrupt shares a single vector** for both transmission and reception. The hardware cannot distinguish whether the interrupt was caused by `TI` or `RI`. Therefore, the ISR must **explicitly clear the flag** that caused the interrupt (using `CLR TI` or `CLR RI`) before returning; otherwise, the same interrupt will be triggered repeatedly in an infinite loop, hanging the system. **(3 marks)**

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

#### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]**

**(a)** Explain the 8051 interrupt structure in detail. List all five interrupt sources with their vector addresses, flags, and enable bits. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The 8051 Microcontroller supports **5 interrupt sources** (excluding reset), which are managed by three key SFRs: `TCON`, `IE` (Interrupt Enable), and `IP` (Interrupt Priority). Each interrupt has a fixed **vector address** in program memory from which the ISR is fetched.

**Detailed Table:**

| # | Source | Flag | SFR Location | Enable Bit (IE) | Vector Address |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | External Interrupt 0 (INT0) | `IE0` | TCON.1 | `EX0` (IE.0) | `0003H` |
| 2 | Timer 0 Overflow | `TF0` | TCON.5 | `ET0` (IE.1) | `000BH` |
| 3 | External Interrupt 1 (INT1) | `IE1` | TCON.3 | `EX1` (IE.2) | `0013H` |
| 4 | Timer 1 Overflow | `TF1` | TCON.7 | `ET1` (IE.3) | `001BH` |
| 5 | Serial Port (TI/RI) | `TI`/`RI` | SCON.1/SCON.0 | `ES` (IE.4) | `0023H` |

**The IE Register** (`0A8H`) has a master bit `EA` at position 7. `EA = 1` enables the global interrupt system; `EA = 0` masks all interrupts. The default reset value is `00H`.

**The IP Register** (`0B8H`) assigns one of two priority levels (High/Low) to each interrupt. Default value is `00H` (all low priority).

**Triggering for External Interrupts:** The bits `IT0` (TCON.0) and `IT1` (TCON.2) select the trigger mode:
- `ITx = 0` → **Level-Triggered** (low-level active)
- `ITx = 1` → **Edge-Triggered** (falling edge active) **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Describe the interrupt priority structure of the 8051. Explain the rules used by the hardware to resolve simultaneous interrupt requests. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The 8051 supports **two priority levels**: **High Priority** and **Low Priority**, configured by the IP register. The hardware resolves concurrent interrupt requests using **three fundamental rules**:

**Rule 1 — Preemption Rule:** A high-priority interrupt can interrupt (preempt) a low-priority ISR in execution. However, a low-priority interrupt **cannot** preempt a high-priority ISR.

**Rule 2 — Simultaneous Request Rule:** When two interrupts of the **same priority** are requested simultaneously, the hardware uses a fixed **natural priority order** to resolve the conflict:
$$
INT0 \;\; > \;\; TF0 \;\; > \;\; INT1 \;\; > \;\; TF1 \;\; > \;\; \text{Serial Port (TI/RI)}
$$

**Rule 3 — Nested Interrupts Rule:** Once an ISR begins execution, only an interrupt of **strictly higher priority** can interrupt it. If a request of equal priority arrives, it will be serviced **only after** the current ISR completes and `RETI` is executed.

**Practical Example:** Suppose `INT0` is configured as high priority, and `TF0` is configured as low priority. If `TF0` is currently being serviced and an `INT0` event occurs, the CPU will:
- Automatically save the PC for the `TF0` ISR onto the stack (nested push).
- Service the `INT0` ISR.
- Return to the `TF0` ISR via `RETI`.
- Finally return to the main program via the second `RETI`.

This makes the 8051 capable of a **2-level nested interrupt architecture**, which is sufficient for most real-time embedded applications. **(7 marks)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — May 2024 — Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Write an 8051 assembly program to generate a 50 ms delay using Timer 0 in Mode 1, and toggle P1.0 continuously using the **timer overflow interrupt method** (no software delay loop). Assume a 12 MHz crystal. Show all SFR configurations clearly. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Calculate Timer 0 Reload Value**

Machine cycle frequency:
$$
f_{mc} = \frac{f_{osc}}{12} = \frac{12 \times 10^6}{12} = 1 \text{ MHz}
$$
$$
T_{mc} = 1 \text{ µs}
$$

For a 50 ms delay, the timer must count:
$$
N = \frac{50 \text{ ms}}{1 \text{ µs}} = 50{,}000 \text{ counts}
$$

Since Timer 0 in Mode 1 is 16-bit (max count = 65,536):
$$
TH0\_TL0 = 65536 - 50000 = 15536
$$

Converting to hex:
$$
15536 \div 16 = 971 \text{ remainder } 0 \;\;\; \Rightarrow \;\;\; 0x3CB0
$$

So `TH0 = 0x3C`, `TL0 = 0xB0`.

**Step 2 — Assembly Program**

```assembly
        ORG     0000H
        LJMP    MAIN
        ORG     000BH              ; Timer 0 ISR Vector
        CPL     P1.0               ; Toggle P1.0
        MOV     TH0, #3CH          ; Reload high byte
        MOV     TL0, #0B0H         ; Reload low byte
        RETI

        ORG     0030H
MAIN:   MOV     TMOD, #01H         ; Timer 0, Mode 1 (16-bit)
        MOV     TH0,  #3CH
        MOV     TL0,  #0B0H
        SETB    TR0                ; Start Timer 0
        MOV     IE,   #82H         ; EA=1, ET0=1, rest disabled
        HERE:   SJMP    HERE        ; Main loop does nothing
        END
```

**Explanation:** The main program configures Timer 0, enables the timer interrupt, and enters an infinite idle loop. Every 50 ms, the timer overflows, the hardware vectors to `000BH`, the ISR toggles P1.0, reloads the timer, and returns. The CPU's foreground (main loop) is completely free for other tasks. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the difference between **level-triggered** and **edge-triggered** external interrupts in the 8051. Which mode is preferred for short pulses, and why? **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The 8051 allows each external interrupt (`INT0`, `INT1`) to be configured in one of two trigger modes via the `IT0` and `IT1` bits in the TCON register.

**Level-Triggered Mode (`ITx = 0`):**
- The interrupt request line is sampled in **every machine cycle**.
- The flag `IEx` is set as long as the pin remains at **logic LOW**.
- The external source **must hold the pin LOW until the interrupt is acknowledged** by the CPU.
- **Critical constraint:** If the pin is **not released** (de-asserted back HIGH) before the ISR ends, the 8051 will interpret it as a **second interrupt request** and re-enter the ISR after `RETI`. This causes repeated execution.
- **Use Case:** Suitable for signals that the peripheral can hold low until acknowledged (e.g., a sensor alert line).

**Edge-Triggered Mode (`ITx = 1`):**
- The interrupt is generated on the **falling edge** (HIGH → LOW transition).
- The flag `IEx` is **latched** on the falling edge and is **automatically cleared by hardware** when the ISR is vectored.
- The external source only needs to provide a **brief pulse**; the pin need not be held low.
- **Use Case:** Preferred for **short pulses** (e.g., from a button press, encoder tick, or short trigger signal). This is the **recommended mode in most practical applications** because it avoids the re-triggering pitfall inherent in level-triggered mode.

**Comparison Table:**

| Feature | Level-Triggered | Edge-Triggered |
|---|---|---|
| Activation Condition | Pin held LOW | HIGH → LOW transition |
| Flag Latch | Follows pin state | Latched on edge |
| Auto-Clear | No | Yes (by hardware) |
| Re-trigger Risk | Yes (if pin not released) | No |
| Pulse Width Required | Must be held until acknowledged | One machine cycle minimum |

**Conclusion:** For short-duration events (button presses, zero-crossing detectors, encoder signals), **edge-triggered mode is always preferred** because it is robust, immune to the de-assertion timing problem, and self-clearing. **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Marks-Loss Pitfalls in Interrupt Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting `EA = 1`**: Many students correctly enable `EX0` or `ET0` but forget to set the global `EA` bit. Result: **Interrupt never fires** → 0 marks for execution.
> 2. **Confusing Vector Addresses**: `0003H` is for INT0, `000BH` for TF0. Mixing these up loses 2–3 marks.
> 3. **Not clearing TI/RI in serial ISR**: Causes infinite re-entry. Examiners specifically test this.
> 4. **Treating "natural priority" as programmable**: The natural priority order `INT0 > TF0 > INT1 > TF1 > Serial` is **fixed in silicon** — it cannot be changed. Only High/Low assignment via IP is programmable.
> 5. **Forgetting to reload TH0/TL0 in Mode 1 ISR**: After Mode 1 overflow, the timer register is `0000H`; without reload, the next overflow will be at 65,536 counts, not your desired value.
> 6. **Writing `MOV IE, #82H` and `MOV IP, #02H` but expecting serial to work**: Setting ET0 does not enable serial port. `ES = IE.4` is a separate bit.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **The 8051 has exactly 5 interrupt sources**: `INT0`, `TF0`, `INT1`, `TF1`, and `Serial Port (TI/RI)`.
- **Two Special Function Registers control interrupts**:
  - `IE` (`0A8H`) — Enable control (must set `EA + local bit`)
  - `IP` (`0B8H`) — Priority control (High or Low)
- **Reset values**: `IE = 00H`, `IP = 00H`, `TCON = 00H` — **all interrupts disabled, all priorities low, all external interrupts level-triggered**.
- **Vector addresses** are 8 bytes apart starting from `0003H`: `0003H`, `000BH`, `0013H`, `001BH`, `0023H`. Use `LJMP` if the ISR exceeds 8 bytes.
- **The three priority rules** (preemption, simultaneous natural priority, nested equal priority) are the most heavily tested theoretical concept in Module 4.
- **Auto-clear behavior**: External edge-triggered and timer interrupts auto-clear their flags; serial port interrupts **never** auto-clear.
- **Baud rate generation** uses Timer 1 in **Mode 2 (Auto-Reload)** with `TH1 = 0xFD` for 9600 baud at 11.0592 MHz.
- **Interrupt latency** is 3–7 machine cycles. Use this to compute worst-case response time in real-time systems.
- **`RETI` vs `RET`**: Always use `RETI` to return from an ISR. `RET` will pop the PC correctly but **will not re-enable the interrupt acceptance logic**, breaking nested interrupt behavior.
- **The hardware stack grows upward** in Internal RAM; ensure sufficient stack space (≥ 2 bytes per nested level) is reserved.
- **Level-triggered** mode requires the source to release the pin; **edge-triggered** mode is generally preferred for short pulses and is more robust.
- **8052 extension**: The 8052 (and many 8051 derivatives like AT89S52) adds a **6th interrupt** for Timer 2 with vector `002BH` and dedicated `ET2` (IE.5) and `PT2` (IP.5) bits.
